questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is/are aromatic alcohol?
questions.solution:
The aromatic alcohols are those compounds in which the hydroxyl group is not directly attached to the nucleus but is linked to a carbon atom situated in a side-chain.

The compound-I and compound-II are the phenols, these are not the aromatic alcohols as -OH group is directly attached to benzene ring.

The compounds II and III are benzyl alcohols and these are aromatic alcohols as -OH group is attached to the carbon atom which itself attached to benzene ring.

$\mathrm{Pd}+\mathrm{CuCl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PdCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{CuCl}$
$4 \mathrm{CuCl}+\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{CuCl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{PdCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow$
Propane
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}+\mathrm{Pd}+2 \mathrm{HCl}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The most reactive amine towards reactions with dil. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
More be the ability of N-atom of -NH2 group to donate lone pair of electrons to HCl (dil.), more be the reactivity of amine.

In (B) two alkyl groups are attached to the -NH2 (having electron releasing nature) which increases the electron donating ability of N-atom of -NH2. In aqueous medium, secondary amine is most basic. Hence, it is most reactive.  In (D), due to resonance, lone pair of electrons get delocalised and its basic strength is very less.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A tetrapeptide is made of naturally occuring alanine, serine, glycine and valine. If the $C$-terminal amino acid is alanine and N -terminal amino acid is chiral, the number of possible sequence of the tetrapeptide is
questions.solution:
Since glycine (gly) is achiral, alanine (Ala) is the C-terminal amino acid, therefore N -terminal amino acid can be valine (val) or serine (ser). In the view of these condition, the following four sequences for the tetrapeptide is possible.
(i) Val-gly-Ser-Ala
(ii) Val-Ser-gly-Ala
(iii) Ser-gly-Val-Ala
(iv) Ser-Val-gly-Ala

questions.solution:

- Lactose is composed of galactose and glucose

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The helical structure of protein is stabilized by
questions.solution: The $\alpha$-helix structure is formed when the chain of $\alpha$-amino acids coils as a right handed screw (called $\alpha$-helix) because of the formation of hydrogen bonds between amide groups of the same peptide chain, i.e., NH group in one unit is linked to carbonyl oxygen of the third unit by hydrogen bonding. This hydrogen bonding between different units is responsible for holding helix in a position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound with the formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$ forms a yellow crystalline solid with phenylhydrazine and gives a mixture of sorbitol and mannitol when reduced with sodium. Which among the following could be the compound?
questions.solution: Since the compound forms a yellow crystalline solid, i.e. osazone with phenylhydrazine, it may be an aldohexose or a ketohexose. Further, since on reduction, compound forms a mixture of sorbitol and mannitol, it must be a ketohexose, i.e. fructose. Recall that glucose on reduction gives only one alcohol glucitol (Sorbitol)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The night-blindness is developed due to deficiency of vitamin
questions.solution:
Night blindness is developed due to deficiency of vitamin-A.

Vitamin A deficiency leads to a disease known as night blindness. People with night blindness have a normal vision when adequate light is present but poor vision in the dark.

Vitamin A is necessary for the production of retinal, a component of rhodopsin.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.solution:
The transfer RNA anticodon for messanger RNA codon G-C-A is

C-G-U, because in anticodon of RNA,

G replaced by C
C replaced by G
A replaced by U

Here, C(cytosine) forms triple bond with G (guanine) and A (adenine) forms double bond with U (uracil).

questions.solution: Ketoses on reduction produce a new chiral carbon leading to the formation of two isomeric alcohols which are diastereomeric as well as $\mathrm{C}-2$ epimers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A sequence of how many nucleotides in messenger RNA makes a codon for an amino acid?
questions.solution: The sequence of bases in mRNA are read in a serial order in groups of three at a time. Each triplet of nucleotides (having a specific sequence of bases) in known as codon. Each codon specifies one amino acid. Further since, there are four bases. therefore, $4^{3}=64$ triplets or codons are possible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The secondary structure of a protein refers to
options: fixed configuration of the polypeptide backbone
questions.solution: The secondary structure of a protein refers to the shape in which a long peptide chain can exist. There are two different conformations of the peptide linkage present in protein are $\alpha$ -helix and $\beta$ -conformation. The $\alpha$ -helix always has a right handed arrangement. In $\beta$ -conformation all peptide chains are streched out to nearly maximum extension and then laid side by side held together by intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The structure resembles the pleated folds of drapery and therefore is known as $\beta$ -pleated shelt.

Electronic configuration of Fe .
$\begin{aligned}
{ }_{26} \mathrm{Fe} & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^6 4 s^2 \\
\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5
\end{aligned}$
Number of unpaired electrons, $n=5$
Spin only magnetic moment, $\mu_s=\sqrt{n(n+2)} \mathrm{BM}$
$\begin{gathered}
\mu_s=\sqrt{5(5+2)} \\

questions.solution:
The b.o in $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{O}^{-}$is 1

The b.o in $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{O}^{-}$is $1.5$

The b.o. in $\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{Cl}=\mathrm{O}$ is $\frac{5}{3}=1.66$

The b.o. in $\mathrm{O}=\underset{\mathrm{O}}{\stackrel{\mathrm{O}}{\|}} \mathrm{l}-\mathrm{O}^{-}$is $\frac{7}{4}=1.75$

The bond length increases as b.o. decreases.

In O2, molecule, last two electrons are present in anti bonding

O28+8=16=σ1 s2,σ*1 s2,σ2 s2,σ*2 s2, σ2pz2,π2px2=π2py2,π*2px1=π*2py1

questions.solution:
Valence electrons in A=3

Valence electron in B=6

Thus, A is electropositive and B is electronegative, A can lose three electrons and B can gain two electrons to attain stable configuration.

Hence, A exist as A3+ and B as B2-

∴ Compound formed is A2B3

Which of the following are isoelectronic species?

II. NH2-

III. NH4+

IV. NH3
questions.solution:
I. Number of electrons in C+H3=6+3-1=8

II. Number of electrons in NH2-=7+2+1=10

III. Number of electrons in NH4+=7+4-1=10

IV. Number of electrons in NH3=7+3=10

Since, species with same number of electrons are called isoelectronic species, Hence, IInd,IIIrd and IVth are isoelectronic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bond enthalpies of $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{X}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HX}$ are in the ratio 2: 1: 2 . If enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{HX}$ is $-50 \mathrm{~kJ}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1},$ the bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{X}_{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HX}$
Let the bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{X}$ bond be $x$.
$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}(\mathrm{HX})=-50$
$=\frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}}+\frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{X}}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{X}}$
$=\frac{1}{2} 2 x+\frac{1}{2} x-2 x=\frac{-x}{2}$
$\therefore x=50 \times 2=100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct bond order in the following species is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$ ion - Total number of electrons
$(16-1)=15$
E.C.:
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \\
\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y} \\
\text { Bond order }=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2}=\frac{10-5}{2}=\frac{5}{2}=2 \frac{1}{2}
\end{array}
$$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$ (Super oxide ion): Total number of
electrons $(16+1)=17$
E.C.:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { \sigma1s }^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \\
\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1} \\
\text { Bond order }=\frac{\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}\right)}{2}=\frac{10-7}{2}=\frac{3}{2}=1 \frac{1}{2}
\end{array}
$$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2+}$ ion: Total number of electrons $=(16-2)=14$
E.C.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \\
\text { Bond order }=\frac{\left(N_{b}-N_{a}\right)}{2}=\frac{10-4}{2}=\frac{6}{2}=3
\end{array}
$$
So bond order: $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}<\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}<\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The ratio $\frac{K_P}{K_C}$ for the reaction
$\mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ are related as
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{P}}-\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{R}}=1-1 \frac{1}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}$
$\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}$ for the given reaction $=-\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For an isomerisation reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, the temperature dependence of equilibrium constant is given by
$\log _e K=4.0-\frac{2000}{T}$
The value of $\Delta S^{\circ}$ at Hook is, therefore
questions.solution:
van't Hoff equation giving variation of equilibrium constant, $K$ with temperature $T$ is
$\log _e K=-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T}+\frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$
Given, $\quad \log _e K=-\frac{2000}{T}+4.0$
$\therefore \quad \Delta S^{\circ} / R=4 \Rightarrow \Delta S^{\circ}=4 R$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The ratio $\frac{K_{p}}{K_{c}}$ for the reaction

$\mathrm{CO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(g)$ is:
questions.solution:
$K_{P}=K_{C}(R T)^{\Delta n_{\text {g }}}$

For the reaction

$\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \leftrightharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$

$\Delta n_{g}=1-\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}} ; \quad \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{RT}}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> are mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. At equilibrium for the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> are formed, the equilibrium constant for the reaction will be,
questions.solution:
The initial concentrations of A, B, C, and D are 4M, 4M, 0M, and 0M respectively.

The equilibrium concentrations of A,B,C and D are 4−2=2M, 4−2=2M, 2M and 2M respectively.

                          A   +       B  ⇌  C   +   Dinitial conc        4              4          0           0at equilibrium 4-2   4-2      2            2

The expression for the equilibrium constant is =[C][D]​[A][B]

∴ Keq=2×22×2=1

questions.q: An equilibrium mixture at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> pressure, respectively. N2O4g⇌2NO2g

Pressure at equilibrium

If volume of the container is doubled, the pressure will reduced to half

N2O4⇌2N2O

New pressure,

0.0282-p1.12+2p

Kp=1.12+2p20.282-p=4.32

On solving, we get

p=0.045

∴ pN2O4=0.14-0.045=0.095 atm

pNO2=0.55+2×0.045=0.64 atm

questions.q: Which of the following according to LeChatelier's principle is correct?
options: Increase in temperature favours the endothermic reaction
Increase in temperature favours the exothermic reaction
Increase in pressure shifts the equilibrium in that side in which number of gaseous moles increases
All of the above are true
questions.solution: According to Le-Chatelier's principle increase in temperature favours the endothermic reaction while decrease in temperature favour the exothermic reaction. Increase in pressure shifts the equilibrium in that side in which number of gaseous moles decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If 1.0 mole of $I_{2}$ is introduced into 1.0 litre flask at $1000 \mathrm{~K},$ at equilibrium $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=10^{-6}\right),$ which one is correct?
questions.solution: $K_{C}=\frac{(2 x)^{2}}{(1-x)}=10^{-6}$ It shows that $(1-x)<2 x$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+(1 / 2) \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} / \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta n},$

$\Delta \mathrm{n}=1-\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)=1-\frac{3}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}}=(\mathrm{RT})^{-1 / 2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The reaction, $\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is
exothermic and reversible. A mixture of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$, $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{l})$ is at equilibrium in a closed container. Now a certain quantity of extra $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is introduced into the container, the volume remaining the same. Which of the following is/are true?
options: The pressure inside the container will not change.
questions.solution: Byaddition of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$, equilibrium will shift to RHS which is exothermic. Hence temp, will increase.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the equilibrium constant of the reaction $2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$ is $0.25,$ then the equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}$ would be
questions.solution:
When the reaction is reversed,
$$
\mathrm{K}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{1}{0.25}=4
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction $2 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_3$, the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{O}_2$ is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The rate appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_3$ is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_3$
Rate of reaction $=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]}{d t}=-\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]}{d t}$
Rate of appearance of $\mathrm{SO}_3$,
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]}{d t} & =2 \times\left[\frac{-d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}\right]=2 \times\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right) \\
& =4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
If for a first order reaction, the value of $A$ and $E_a$ are $4 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $98.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
respectively, then at what temperature will its half life period be 10 minutes?
questions.solution:
According to Arrhenius equation,
$\log k=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303 R T}...(i)$
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction,
$k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{600 s}=1.1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
Substitute the value of $k$ in Eq. (i)
$log \left(1.1 \times 10^{-3}\right)=\log \left(4 \times 10^{13}\right)-\frac{98.6 \times 10^3}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times T}$
Solving for $T$,
$T=311.15 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the chemical reaction $A \rightarrow B$, what is the order of the reaction? Given that, the rate of reaction doubles if the concentration of $A$ is increased four times.
questions.solution:
We know, $r_1=k[A]^{\mathrm{n}}$    ....(i)
If conc. of $A$ is increased four times rate of reaction on doubles.
$2 r_1=k[4 A]^{\mathrm{n}}$     ....(ii) 
Divide Eq. (ii) by (i).
$\frac{2 r_i}{r_1}=\frac{k(4 A)^n}{k(A)^n}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2=(4)^{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{n}=(2)^{2 \mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow n=0.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the activation energy of a reaction, whose rate constant doubles on raising the temperature from 300 K to 600 K .
questions.solution:
$\log \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right]$
or, $\log \left(\frac{2 k}{k}\right)=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.3} \times \frac{[600-300]}{300 \times 600}$
$E_a \approx 3.45 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ products, If the concentration of the reactant is doubled rate of the reaction remains unchanged. The order of the reaction with respect to A is
questions.solution:
$r_1=k[A]^n$ and $r_2=k[2 A]^n$
$\because \quad \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{r}_2 \quad \therefore \mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{k}(2[\mathrm{~A}])^{\mathrm{n}}$
or, $\quad 2^n=1$
or, $2^{\mathrm{n}}=2^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=0$
It is a zero order reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a first order reaction, the concentration of the reactant, decreases from 0.8 M to 0.4 M in 15 minutes. The time taken for the concentration to change from 0.1 M to 0.025 M is
questions.solution: As the concentration of reactant decreases from 0.8 to 0.4 in 15 minutes, hence the $t_{1 / 2}$ is 15 minutes. To fall the concentration from 0.1 to 0.025 , we need two half lives, i.e., 30 minutes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the decomposition of azomethane, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ a first order reaction, the variation in partial pressure with time at $600 \mathrm{~K}$ is given as


The half life of the reaction is .
questions.solution:
For first order reaction
$\begin{aligned}
& k=\frac{1}{t} \ln \left(\frac{P_0}{P}\right) \Rightarrow \operatorname{In}\left(\frac{P_0}{P}\right)=k t \\
& \Rightarrow \ln \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)=-k t
\end{aligned}$
On comaring with straight line equation $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{k}=\text { slope }=3.465 \times 10^4 \\
& \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{0.693}{3.465 \times 10^4}=2 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~s}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Plots showing the variation of the rate constant $(k)$ with temperature $(T)$ are given below. The plot that follows Arrhenius equation is
questions.solution: As per Arrhenius equation $\left(k=A \mathrm{e}^{-E_a / R T}\right)$, the rate constant increases exponentially with temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a 1 st order reaction, reactant concentration $C$ varies with time $t$ as :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{XmL}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas effuses through a hole in a container in 5 seconds. The time taken for the effusion of the same volume of the gas specified below under identical conditions is :
questions.solution:
Under identical conditions, $\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}}$

As rate of diffusion is also inversely proportional to time, we will have, $\frac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{\overline{\mathrm{M}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}}$

(A) Thus, For $\mathrm{He}, t_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{2}}(5 s)=5 \sqrt{2} s \neq 10 s$;

(B) $\mathrm{For} \mathrm{O}_{2}, t_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}(5 s)=20 s$

(C) For $\mathrm{CO}, t_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{28}{2}}(5 s) \neq 25 s$;

(D) For $\mathrm{CO}_{2}, t_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{44}{2}}(5 s) \neq 55 s$

#

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A radioactive isotope having a half - life period of 3 days was received after 12 days. If $3 \mathrm{~g}$ of the isotope is left in the container, what would be the initial mass of the isotope?
questions.solution:
Given $t_{1 / 2}=3$ Total time $\mathrm{T}=12$

No. of half lives $(n)=\frac{12}{3}=4$ $\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}} 

\therefore\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{4}=\frac{3}{\mathrm{~N}}$

$\frac{3}{\mathrm{~N}}=\frac{1}{16}$

$N=48$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Given the hypothetical reaction mechanism

A⟶IB⟶IIC⟶IIID→IV E and the rate as

Species formed Rate of its information
B 0.002 mol/h per mole of A
C 0.030 mol/h per mole of B
D 0.011 mol/h per mole of C
E 0.420 mol/h per mole of D

The rate determining step is
questions.solution: Among the formation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math>, the slowest step is the rate determining step. As formation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>002</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> per mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is the slowest step. Hence, step-<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></mfenced></math> is the rate determining step.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the chemical reaction

2O3⟶3O2, the reaction proceeds as follows

O3⇌O2+O (Fast)

O+O3⟶2O2, (Slow)

the rate law expression should be given as
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Let k be the rate constant of given reaction

2O3⟶k3O2

From the slowest step r=k'OO3 ... i

To eliminate O, (From fast step)

keq=O2OO3

O=keq.O3O2

Now, substituting value of O in Eq i

r=k'keqO3O3O2

Let k'keq=k

Thus, r=kO32O2-1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reversible reaction, A⇌B, which one of the following statement is wrong from the given energy profile diagram?
options: Activation energy of forward reaction is greater than backward reaction
The threshold energy is less than that of activation energy
The energy of activation of forward reaction is equal to the sum of heat of reaction and the energy of activation of backward reaction
questions.solution:
where, Ea= activation energy of forward reaction

Ea'= activation energy of backward reaction

The above energy profile diagram shows that Ea>Ea'

The potential energy of the product is greater than that of the reactant, so the reaction is endothermic.

Ea=Ea'+ΔE

Et=Ea or Et>Ea'

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Under the same reaction conditions, initial concentration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>386</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> of a substance becomes half in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> through first order and zero order kinetics, respectively. Ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> of the rate of constants for first order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced></math> and zero order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced></math> of the reaction is
questions.solution:
Given that, 

Under the same reaction conditions, initial concentration of 1.386 mol dm-3 of a substance becomes half in 40 s and 20 sfor first order and zero order kinetics, respectively.

For first order reaction,

t1/2=ln2k1=40 s...i

For zero order reaction, t1/2=A02k0=20 s....ii

Divide eqn. (i) by (ii), we get

12=[A]02k0×k1ln2

k1k0=ln2[A]0=0.6931.386=0.5 mol-1dm3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a reaction triples when temperature changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>20</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>50</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The energy of activation for the reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>314</mn><msup><mi>JK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Arrhenius equation is given by,
log10K2K1=Ea2.303×RT2-T1T1T2

Given,

K2K1=3;R=-8.314 JK-1mol-1

T1=20+273=293 K
and T2=50+273=323 K

Substituting the given values in Arrhenius equation,

log103=Ea8.314×2.303323-293323×293

Ea=2.303×8.314×323×293×0.47730

=28811.8 J mol1

=28.8118kJmol-1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following expression is correct for the rate of reaction given below ?

5Br-aq+BrO3-aq+6H+aq⟶3Br2aq+3H2Ol
questions.solution:
The given chemical reaction is

5Br-aq+BrO3-aq+6H+aq⟶3Br2aq+3H2Ol

Rate law expression for the given reaction can be written as

-15ΔBr-Δt=-ΔBrO3-Δt

-16ΔH+Δt=+13ΔBr2Δt

⇒-ΔBr-Δt=-5ΔBrO3-Δt=-56ΔH+Δt

ΔBr-Δt=56ΔH+Δt

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For an endothermic reaction, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ΔH</mi></math> represent the enthalpy of reaction in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the minimum value for energy of activation (for forward reaction) will be
questions.solution:
We know that, ΔH=Ef-Eb

where, Eb and Ef= activation energy for backward and forward respectively.

Thus,Ef=ΔH+Eb, means Ef>ΔH.

 

ΔH=heat of reaction (endothermic)

Ea=energy of activation

Thus, Ea>ΔH

Heat is absorbed in an endothermic reaction. Thus, for an endothermic reaction, where ΔH represent the enthalpy of reaction in kJ mol-1, the minimum value for energy of activation (for forward reaction) will be more than ΔH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A graph is plotted between log K versus 1T for calculation of activation energy Ea. The correct plot is
questions.solution:
According to Arrhenius equation,

k∝T and k=Ae−Ea​/RT

logk=logA-Ea2.303×R​​×1T​

y=mx+c, the equation of straight line

The value of log k should increase uniformily with T or decrease with 1T.

               

From this, that the slope is negative, which means that angle with x - axis is greater than 90° also that the point on which the straight line of graph intercepts the y - axis is log A which is positive.

Hence all the conclusions point out towards option b as it has negative slope and a positive y intercept.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the reaction :

$\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(a q)+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(a q) \rightarrow$

$\mathrm{S}(s)+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q)$

The rate equation for this reaction is rate $=k\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]$

Which of these mechanisms is/are consistent with this rate equation?

A. $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-}$ (slow)

$\mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{S}$

B. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HS}^{-}$ (fast equilibrium)

$\mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{HS}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{S}$ (Slow)
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the reaction, $\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q} \longrightarrow ? \mathrm{R}+\mathrm{S}$ The time taken for $75 \%$ reaction of $P$ is twice the time taken for $50 \%$ reaction of $\mathrm{P}$. The concentration of $\mathrm{Q}$ varies with reaction time as shown in the figure. The overall order of the reaction is
questions.solution:
For $\mathrm{P}$, if $\mathrm{t}_{50 \%}=\mathrm{x}$ then $\mathrm{t}_{75 \%}=2 \mathrm{x}$

This is true only for first order reaction. So, order with respect to $\mathrm{P}$ is $1 .$ Further the graph shows that concentration of $\mathrm{Q}$ decreases with time. So rate, with respect to $\mathrm{Q},$ remains constant. Hence, it is zero order wrt Q. So, overall order is $1+0=1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The unit of rate constant for reactions of second order is
questions.solution: Unit of rate constant for second order reaction is $\mathrm{L} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a first order reaction with time the concentration of the reactant decreases
questions.solution:
For first order reaction $[\mathrm{A}]=\left[\mathrm{A}_{0}\right] \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{k}}$

$\therefore$ The concentration of reactants will exponentially decreases with time.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The reduction of peroxydisulphate ion by I ion

is expressed by $\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-}+3 \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{3}^{-}$. If

rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ is $9 / 2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol}$ lit $^{-1}$

$\mathrm{s}^{-1},$ what is the rate of formation of $2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ during same time?
questions.solution:
$\frac{1 d\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{2}=\frac{1}{3}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left(\mathrm{I}^{-}\right)}{d t}\right)$

$\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{9}{2} \times 10^{-3}$

$\therefore \quad \frac{d\left[\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}\right]}{d t}=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{Lit}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A gaseous reaction $\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Y}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$

There is increase in pressure from $100 \mathrm{~mm}$ to 120 $\mathrm{mm}$ in 5 minutes. The rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{X}_{2}$ is
questions.solution:
The increase in pressure shows the increase in conc. of $Z$. Rate of appearance of $Z=$

$$

\frac{120-100}{5}=4 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}

$$

Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{X}_{2}=2 \times$ rate of appearance of $Z$ $=2 \times 4 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}=8 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two substances $\mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{S}$ decompose in solution independently, both following first order kinetics. The rate constant of $\mathrm{R}$ is twice that of $\mathrm{S}$. In an experiment, the solution initially contained 0.5 millimoles of $\mathrm{R}$ and 0.25 of $\mathrm{S}$. The molarities of $\mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{S}$ will be equal just at the end of time equal to
questions.solution:
Substance $\mathrm{R}$ Substance $\mathrm{S}$ $2 \mathrm{k} \quad \mathrm{k}$ rate constant $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}$

where $\mathrm{n}=$ number of half life period

Amount of $\mathrm{R}$ left $=\frac{0.5}{(2)^{\mathrm{T} / \mathrm{t}_{/ 2}}}$

Amount of $\mathrm{S}$ left $=\frac{0.25}{(2)^{\mathrm{T} / 2 \mathrm{t} / 2}}$

Equating both $\frac{0.5}{0.25}=\frac{(2)^{\mathrm{T} / \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}}{(2)^{\mathrm{T} / 2 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}}$

or $2=(2)^{\mathrm{T} / \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{T}=2 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}$ is half life of $\mathrm{S}$ and

twice the half -life of $\mathrm{R}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Velocity constant of a reaction at $290 \mathrm{~K}$ was found to be $3.2 \times 10^{-3}$. At $300 \mathrm{~K}$ it will be
questions.solution: The velocity constant doubles for every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At high pressure, the entire surface gets covered by a monomolecular layer of the gas follows
questions.solution: At high pressure the extent of adsorption follows zero order kinetics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
If rate and rate constant for above reaction are $2.40 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ respectively, then calculate the concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$
questions.solution:
The reaction is of first and for a first order reaction, rate, $R=k\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$ $2.4 \times 10^{-5}=3 \times 10^{-5} \times\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$
$\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{3 \times 10^{-5}}=0.8 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
During the kinetic study of the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}$, following results were obtained:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline \text { Run } & {[\mathrm{A}] / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}} & {[\mathrm{~B}] / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}} & \begin{array}{l}
\text { Initial rate of } \\
\text { formation of } \\
\mathrm{D} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}
\end{array} \\
\hline \text { I } & 0.1 & 0.1 & 6.0 \times 10^{-1} \\
\hline \text { II } & 0.3 & 0.2 & 7.2 \times 10^{-1} \\
\hline \text { III } & 0.3 & 0.4 & 28.8 \times 10^{-1} \\
\hline \text { IV } & 0.4 & 0.1 & 24.0 \times 10^{-1} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Based on the above data which one of the following is correct?
questions.solution: In case of (II) and (III) Keeping concentration of [A] constant, when the concentration of $[\mathrm{B}]$ is doubled, the rate quadruples. Hence it is second order with respect to $\mathrm{B}$. In case of $\mathrm{I} \&$ IV Keeping the concentration of [B] constant, when the concentration of $[\mathrm{A}]$ is increased four times, rate also increases four times. Hence, the order with respect to $\mathrm{A}$ is one. Hence Rate $=k[\mathrm{~A}][\mathrm{B}]^{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activation energy for a simple chemical reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is $E_{a}$ in forward direction. The activation energy for reverse reaction

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The following data are for the decomposition of ammonium nitrite in aqueous solution :
The order of reaction is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Volume of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ formed in successive five minutes are $2.75 \mathrm{cc}, 2.40 \mathrm{cc}$ and $2.25 \mathrm{cc}$ which is in decreasing order. So rate of reaction is dependent on concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$. As decrease is not very fast so it will be first order reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The chemical reaction $2 \mathrm{O}_{3} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{O}_{2}$ proceeds as follows:
rate law expression should be:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{O}_{3} \stackrel{\text { Fast }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{O}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{3} \stackrel{\text { Slow }}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \\
\mathrm{k}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right][\mathrm{O}]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]} \\
\text { Rate }=\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right][\mathrm{O}]
\end{array}
$$
put [O] from (ii)
$$
\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{k}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right] \mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_{3}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{-1}
$$
Note: Intermediates are never represented in rate law equation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which one of the following arrangements represents the correct order of electron gain enthalpy (with negative sign) of the given atomic species?
questions.solution:
The amount of energy released when an electron is added to an isolated gaseous atom to produce a monovalent anion is called electron gain enthalpy.

Electron affinity value generally increase on moving from left to right in a period however there are exceptions of this rule in the case of those atoms which have stable configuration. These atoms resist the addition of extra electron, therefore the low value of electron affinity $\underset{-1.48}{\mathrm{O}}<\underset{-2.0}{\mathrm{~S}}<\underset{-3.6}{\mathrm{~F}}<\underset{-3.8}{\mathrm{Cl}}$

On the other hand $\mathrm{Cl}$, because of its comparatively bigger size than $\mathrm{F}$, allow the addition of an extra electron more easily.

questions.q: The screening effect of $d$ -electrons is
questions.solution:
The screening effect of inner electron of the nucleus causes the decrease in ionization potential, therefore the order of the screening effect is

$$

f<d<p<s

$$

Hence, the screening effect of $d$ -electron is less than $p$ -electron.

questions.q:
The ions $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ are

isoelectronic. Their ionic radii show:
options: A decrease from $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ and then increase from $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ to $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$
An increase from $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ and then decrease from $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ to $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$
questions.solution: Amongst isoelectronic species, ionic radii of anion is more than that of cations. Further size of anion increase with increase in -ve charge and size of cation decrease with increase in $+$ ve charge. Hence ionic radii decreases from $\mathrm{O}^{-}$ to $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The type of isomerism present in nitropentammine chromium (III) chloride is
questions.solution:
The chemical formula of nitropenta ammine chromium(III) chloride is:
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$
It can exist in following two structures
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$ and
Pentaamminenitrochromium (III) chloride
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{ONO}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$
Pentaamminenitritochromium (III) chloride
Therefore, the type of isomerism found in this compound is linkage isomerism as $\mathrm{NO}_2$ group is linked through N as $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ or through O as ONO.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify, from the following, the diamagnetic, tetrahedral complex
questions.solution: (a) Ni in $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{Cl})_4\right]^{2-}$ exist as $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ ion. Cl is a weak field ligand (high spin). It will not cause pairing of electrons. Hence, configuration of $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{Cl})_4\right]^{2-}$ is Tetrahedral with two unpaired electrons (i.e., paramagnetic) and has $s p^3$ hybridisation. (b) In $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-},\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3^{2-}$ is a bidentate ligand thus, give octahedral structure. (c) In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$, Ni exist as $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ ion. $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$is a strong field ligand (low spin). Causes pairing of electrons of $3 d$ orbital. Hence, the structure of is square planar and it is diamagnetic. (d) In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$, Ni has zero oxidation state. i.e., CO is a strong field ligand causes rearrangement and pairing of electrons of $3 d$ and $4 s$ orbital. Structure of $\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{CO}_4\right)$ is tetrahedral with $s p^3$ hybridisation and it is diamagnetic. Hence, (4) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the ligands $\mathrm{NH}_3$, en, $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$and $\mathrm{CO}$ the correct order of their increasing field strength, is :
questions.solution:
Ligands can be arranged in a series in the orders of increasing field strength as given below:
Weak field ligands:
$\mathrm{I}^{-} < \mathrm{Br}^{-} < \mathrm{S}^{2-} < \mathrm{SCN} < \mathrm{Cl}^{-} < \mathrm{N}_3^{-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$ $ < $ Urea, OH $ < $ oxalate
Strong field ligands
$\mathrm{O}^{--} < \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{NCS}^{-} < \text {EDTA } < \mathrm{Py}, \mathrm{NH}_3 < $ en $=\mathrm{SO}_3^{-} < $bipy, Phen $ < \mathrm{NO}_2^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_3^{-}$ $ < \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5^{-} < \mathrm{CN}^{-} < \mathrm{CO}$
Such a series is termed as spectrochemical series. It is an experimentally determined series based on the absorption of light by complexes with different ligands.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the octahedral complex, which of the following <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math>-electron configuration will give maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CFSE</mi></math>
questions.solution:
Δ0=-0.4×nt2g+0.6×neg

where, n=number of electrons in nt2g and neg.

CFSE of given α -electrons configuration are as follows

a. For high spin, d6;

Δ0CFSE=-0.4×4+0.6×2=-0.4

b. For low spin, d4;

Δ0CFSE=-0.4×4+0.6×0=1.6

c. For low spin d5

Δ0CFSE=-0.4×5+0.6×0=-2.0

d. For high spin, d7

Δ0CFSE=-0.4×5+0.6×2=-0.8

Neglecting -ve sign (as it indicates that energy is released).

Hence, maximum Δ0 CFSE value is shown by low spin d5 configuration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order for the wavelength if absorption in the visible region.
questions.solution:
When central metal ion is same (here Ni2+ ), the absorption of colour depends on the ligand.

According to spectrochemical series, various ligands are as follows

I<Br-<Cl-<NO3-<F-<H2O

<NH3<NO2-<CN-<CO

Thus, H2O is the weakest ligand among these, therefore the absorbed energy will be lowest in
NiH2O62+, so it will absorb highest wavelength (red light).

E∝1λ

∴ The order of increasing wavelength is

NiNO264-<NiNH362+<NiH2O62+

Among the given option (a) is a diamagnetic complex.

(a) CoNH363+⇒Co27⇒Co3+=3d64s0.

Thus, has no unpaired electrons and is a diamagnetic complex.

(b) NiCl42-⇒Ni28⇒Ni2+=3d84s0 

Thus, has two unpaired electrons is paramagnetic.

(c) CuCl42-⇒Cu29⇒Cu2+=3d94s0

Thus, has one unpaired electrons and is paramagnetic.

(d) FeH2O63+⇒Fe26⇒Fe3+=3d54s0

Thus, has five unpaired electrons and is paramagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following lanthanoid is diamagnetic?

Atomic no's Sm=62, Ce=58, Eu=63, Yb=70
questions.solution:
Electronic configuration of the given species are

a. Sm2+Z=62=Xe4f6 b. Eu2+Z=63=Xe4f7

c. Yb2+Z=70=Xe4f14 d. Ce2+Z=58=Xe4f15d1

On the basis of above electronic configuration, only Yb2+Z=70 has no unpaired electrons i.e. has fully-filled orbitals. Thus, it is diamagnetic in nature.

questions.solution:
Electronic configuration of Mn is Z=25=Ar3d54s2

∴ Electronic configuration of Mn4+ is =Ar3d3

Thus, Mn4+ has 3-unpaired electron and its spin only magnetic moment μ is, μ=nn+2 BM

where, n= number of unpaired electrons.

μ=3(3+2). BM⇒μ=15=3.87BM≈4BM

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pH of 0.5 L of 1.0 M NaCl solution after electrolysis for 965 s using 5.0 A current is,
questions.solution:
The reaction involved is,
$\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { Electrolysis }} \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{NaOH}$
Amount of NaCl present in 0.5 L of $1.0 \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{NaCl}=0.5$ mol
Quantity of electricity passed $=965 \times 5$ coulombs.
$\therefore 4825$ coulombs will decompose NaCl .
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{4825}{96500} \mathrm{~mol} \\
& =0.05 \mathrm{~mol}
\end{aligned}$
NaOH formed in the solution will also be 0.05 mol .
Volume of solution $=0.5 \mathrm{~L}$
$\therefore$ Molarity of NaOH in the solution
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{0.05 \mathrm{moles}}{0.5 \mathrm{~L}}=0.1 \mathrm{M} \\
& =10^{-1} \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \therefore & \mathrm{pOH} & =1 \\ \text { or } & \mathrm{pH} & =14-1=13\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the molarity of a solution containing 5 g of NaOH dissolved in the product of $\mathrm{H}_2-\mathrm{O}_2$ fuel cell operated at 1 A current for 595.1 hours. (Assume $\mathrm{F}=96500 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol}$ of electron and molecular weight of NaOH as $\left.40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$.
questions.solution:
The overall reaction of $\mathrm{H}_2-\mathrm{O}_2$ fuel is,
$2 \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Thus $4 \mathrm{~F}=4 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$ produce water $=2 \mathrm{~mol}=36 \mathrm{~g}$
$\begin{aligned}
\text { Charge actually passed } & =i \times t \\
& =1 \times 595.1 \times 60 \times 60 \\
& =2142360 \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { Water produced }=\frac{36}{4 \times 96500} \times 2142360 \\
& \cong 200 \mathrm{~g} \text { or } 200 \mathrm{~mL} \\
& \therefore \text { Molarity of } \mathrm{NaOH} \text { solution }=\frac{5}{40} \times \frac{1}{200} \times 1000 \\
& =0.625 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The conductivity of a weak acid HA of concentration $0.001 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ is $2.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$.
If $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{HA})=190 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the ionization constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}\right)$ of $\mathrm{HA}$ is equal to _______ x $10^{-6}$.
questions.solution:
$\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=1000 \times \frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{M}}$
$=1000 \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001}=20 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}=\frac{20}{190}=\left(\frac{2}{19}\right)$
$\underset{0.001(1-\alpha)}{\mathrm{HA}} \rightleftharpoons \underset{0.001 \alpha}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}+\underset{0.001 \alpha}{\mathrm{A}^{-}}$
$K_{\mathrm{a}}=0.001\left(\frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}\right)=\frac{0.001 \times\left(\frac{2}{19}\right)^2}{1-\left(\frac{2}{19}\right)}$
$=12.3 \times 10^{-6}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Electrode potential data are given below:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{(\mathrm{s})} ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=-1.66 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Br}_2(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Br}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=+1.08 \mathrm{~V}$
Based on the data, the reducing power of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$, $\mathrm{Al}$ and $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$will increase in the order
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{rrrr} & \text { Fe } & \text { A1 } & \text { B } \\ E_{\text {Red }}^{\circ} & 0.77 & -1.66 & 1.08 \\ E_{\text {Oxi }}^{\circ} & -0.77 & 1.66 & -1.08\end{array}$
Hence, reducing power $\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}>\mathrm{Br}$

questions.q: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molar conductance at infinite dilution for the strong electrolytes $\mathrm{NaOH}, \mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ are $248 \times 10^{-4}, 126 \times 10^{-4}$ and $280 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. $\Delta_{\mathrm{m}}{ }^{0} \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ in $\mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ is
questions.solution: $\Delta_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Delta_{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}=248 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^{\circ}+\Delta_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}=126 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{Ba}}^{\circ}{ }^{2+}+\Delta{ }_{2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}=280 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ $\mathrm{NoW}$, $\Delta_{\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}^{\circ}=\Delta_{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}}^{\circ}+2 \Delta{ }^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{NaOH}}^{\circ}-2 \Delta{ }_{\mathrm{NaC} 1}^{\circ}$ $\Delta{ }_{\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}^{\circ}=280 \times 10^{-4}+2 \times 248 \times 10^{-4}-2 \times 126 \times 10^{-4}$ $\Delta_{\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}^{\circ}=524 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
E1, E2 and E3 are the emf of the following three galvanic cells respectively

(i) ZnsZn2+0.1 MCu2+1 MCus

(ii) ZnsZn2+1 MCu2+1 MCus

(iii) ZnsZn2+1 MCu2+0.1 MCus

Which of the following is true?
questions.solution:
Key-idea

Use Nernst equation, i.e.

Eemf=E°cell-0.05912logZn2+Cu2+

For reaction i

E1=E°cell-0.05912log0.11

E1=E°cell+0.05912

For reaction ii

E2=E°cell-0.05912log11=0

E2=E°cell

For reaction iii

E3=E°cell-0.05912log10.1

E3=E°cell-0.05912

Hence, E1>E2>E3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What time in sec is required for depositing all the silver present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ml</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> solution by passing a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>241</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn></math> A (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>96500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
questions.solution:
Given, 125 ml of 1 M AgNO3

which is equal to 13.5 g of Ag

∵ 1000 ml of AgNO3=108 g of Ag

∴ 125 ml of AgNO3=108×1251000=13.5 g

Also, ∵ 108 g of Ag is deposited by =96500 C

∴ 13.5 g of Ag is deposited by =96500×13.5108=12062.5 C

∵ Q=it

∴ t (time) =Q/i=12062.50241.25, t=50 sec

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> of electricity is passed through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of copper sulphate solution. The amount of copper which can be deposited will be
questions.solution:
Cu2++2e-⟶Cu    ...i

According to Faraday's first law, when an electric current is passed through an electrolyte, amount of substance deposited is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

∵1 mol of 63.5 g of Cu is obtained by passing 2F of electricity.

Mass of Cu obtained by passing 1F of electricity =63.52g

∴ Mass of Cu obtained by passing 0.5F of electricity
=63.52×0.5=15.80 g

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>⟶</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mn>0</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2955</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>eq</mi></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
Given that,

For a reaction, 

A+B2+⟶B+A2+,E°=0.2955V

T=25°C, ⇒T=298 K n=2

Using Nernst equation,

E°=2.303RTnFlogkeq

E°=0.0591nlogkeqat 25oC

⇒  0.2955=0.05912logkeq

keq=1010

Therefore, the value of Keq is 1010.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Blocks of magnesium are fixed to the bottom of a ship to
options: prevent the action of water and salt
questions.solution:
Block of magnesium prevents the action of water and salt of iron (of ship) by sacrificing itself.

This is method of protection of ship (i.e. of iron), known as sacrificial protection. 

It prevents corrosive action of water and salt by providing cathodic protection.

How many moles of Fe2+ ions are formed when excess of iron react with 500 mL O.4 N HCl, under inert atmosphere?

∵ Reaction between Fe and HCl is as follows

Fe+2HCl⟶FeCl2(+2)+H2 on reaction

n(moles of Fe) =n (moles of HCl)

n(Fe)= Normality (HCl)× Volume of HCl

nFe=0.4×5001000=0.2 moles

As each Fe consumers 2 electrons to change to Fe2+ ion.

∴ Number of Fe2+ ions produced =0.22=0.1 mole

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\mathrm{Cu}_{\mathrm{aq}}^{+}$ is unstable in solution and undergoes simultaneous oxidation and reduction according to the reaction :

$2 \mathrm{Cu}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})$

choose correct $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ for above reaction if

$\mathrm{E}^{\circ} \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=0.34 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\mathrm{E}^{\circ} \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=0.15 \mathrm{~V}$
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{Cu}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+2}+\mathrm{Cu}$

$2 \mathrm{e}^{-}+\mathrm{Cu}^{+2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu} ; \mathrm{E}_{1}^{\circ}=0.34 \mathrm{~V} ; \ldots$ (i)

$\mathrm{e}^{-}+\mathrm{Cu}^{+2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+} ; \mathrm{E}_{2}^{\circ}=0.15 \mathrm{~V} ; \ldots$ (ii)

$\mathrm{Cu}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu} ; \mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ}=?$ ...(iii)

Now, $\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}_{1}^{\circ}=-2 \times 0.34 \mathrm{~F}$

$\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\circ}=-1 \times 0.15 \mathrm{~F}, \Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}=-1 \times \mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$

Again, $\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\circ}+\Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}$

$\Rightarrow-0.68 \mathrm{~F}=-0.15 \mathrm{~F}-\mathrm{E}_{3} \mathrm{~F}$

$0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ}=0.68-0.15=0.53 \mathrm{~V}$

$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}\right)$

$=0.53-0.15=0.38 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A current of 2.0 A passed for 5 hours through a molten metal salt deposits $22.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal (At wt. $=177$ ). The oxidation state of the metal in the metal salt is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{wt} \times \mathrm{Q}}{96500}$

$\begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{E} . \mathrm{wt} &=\frac{\mathrm{m} \times 96500}{\mathrm{Q}} \\=\frac{22.2 \times 96500}{2 \times 5 \times 60 \times 60}=60.3 \end{aligned}$

Oxidation state $=\frac{\text { At wt. }}{\text { Eq. wt. }}=\frac{177}{60.3}=3$

questions.q:
In a cell that utilises the reaction
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
addition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to cathode compartment, will
options: increase the $\mathrm{E}$ and shift equilibrium to the right.
lower the $E$ and shift equilibrium to the right.
lower the $E$ and shift equlibrium to the left.
increase the $E$ and shift equilibrium to the left.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons$
$$
\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}=\mathrm{E}^{\circ} \mathrm{cell}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}
$$
Addition of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ will increase $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$ will also increase and the equilibrium will shift towards RHS.

questions.q: The number of geometrical isomers possible for the compound, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2$ is
questions.solution:
Although $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2$ has two double bond but one of them has two similar atoms (i.e. H -atom) therefore no geometrical isomerism is possible around the double bond. However each carbon atom of other double bond has two different atoms/groups and hence geometrical isomerism is possible around this double bond.
Hence, only two geometrical isomers are possible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the correct stability order of the given free radicals.
questions.solution:
In compounds (I), (III) and (IV) the -M effect and hyperconjugation effect are inoperative at meta positions. So we should consider the inductive effect of the groups. -I effect will decrease the stability of the free radical by withdrawing the electrons toward it itself. The order of -I effect is \(\mathrm{NO}_2>\mathrm{CN}>\mathrm{OH}\). Therefore (III) is most destabilised.
In compound (II) the OH act as electron donating group through +M effect which is operative at para position. This increase the electron density at para position and stabilise the free radical. Hence compound (1) is most stabilised. The order of stability of free radical is
\(\text { II }>\text { I }>\text { IV }>\text { III }\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $0.20 \mathrm{~g}$ of an organic compound gave $0.12 \mathrm{~g}$ of AgBr By using Carius method, the percentage of bromine in the compound will be
questions.solution:
Given,
Mass of an organic compound $=0.20 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of $\mathrm{AgBr}=0.12 \mathrm{~g}$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{AgBr}=188 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$188 \mathrm{~g}$ of AgBr contains $80 \mathrm{~g}$ of bromine
$\therefore 0.12 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{AgBr}$ will contain $=\frac{80}{188} \times 0.12$
$=0.05 \mathrm{~g}$ of bromine
$\therefore$ Percentage of bromine $=\frac{0.05}{0.20} \times 100$
$=25 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The Lassaigne's extract is boiled with dil. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ before testing for halogens because
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ and $\mathrm{NaCN},$ formed during fusion with metallic sodium, must be removed before adding $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3},$ otherwise black ppt. due to $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ or white precipitate due to $\mathrm{AgCN}$ will be formed and thus white precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ will not be identified easily.

$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}+\underset{\text { Black }}{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}} \downarrow$

$\mathrm{NaCN}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaNO}_{3} + \underset{\text { White }}{\mathrm{Ag} \mathrm{CN}} \downarrow$

$\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}+\underset{\text { White }}{\mathrm{Ag} \mathrm{Cl}} \downarrow$

$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \stackrel{\text { boil }}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \uparrow$

$\mathrm{NaCN}+\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \stackrel{\text { boil }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}+\mathrm{HCN} \uparrow$

Hinsberg test for the detection and distinction of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines in a given sample.

Carbylamine reaction is the reaction of a primary amine, chloroform and a base to synthesize isocyanides.

Azo dye test is not used for the detection of primary, secondary and tertiary aliphatic amines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compound will give blood red colour while doing the Lassaigne's test for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
questions.solution:
When nitrogen and sulphur both are present in the organic compound, they combine during fusion to form sodium thiocyanate (sulphocyanide).

Thus, the compounds containing both N and S give blood red colour in Lassaigne's test due to the formation of FeSCN2. Thus, H2NC6H4SO3H gives blood red colour in Lassaigne's test of nitrogen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order of stability of conformations for NH2-CH2-CH2-OH.
questions.solution:
The anti conformation is most stable in which NH2 and OH groups are far apart as possible and minimum repulsion between two groups occurs.

In fully eclipsed conformation NH2 and OH groups are so close that the steric strain is maximum.

Since, gauche form is stabilised by intermolecular hydrogen bonding, hence it is more stable than anti.

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compound does not follow Huckel's rule?
questions.solution:
An aromatic compound can follow Huckel's rule if it has (4n+2)π-electrons

where, n= integer 0,1,2,3,… etc.

Thus, 

(a) n=0; 4×0+2=2π

∴ follow Huckel's rule

(b) n=1; Number of π-electrons =6

∴ follows Huckels rules.

(c) n=1; Number of π-electrons =6

∴ follows Huckel's rule.

(d) n=1, Number of π-electrons =8

(4n+2=4×1+2=6)

but this compound has 8 electrons which distorts the plane and hence does not follows Huckel's rule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ionization constant of phenol is higher than that of ethanol because :
options: Phenoxide ion is bulkier than ethoxide
Phenoxide ion is stronger base than ethoxide
Phenoxide ion is stabilized through delocalization
Phenoxide ion is less stable than ethoxide
questions.solution:
The acidic nature of phenol is due to the formation of stable phenoxide ion in solution $$ \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{\text {Phenoxide ion }}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} $$ The phenoxide ion is stable due to resonance.
The negative charge is delocalized in the benzene ring which is a stabilizing factor in the phenoxide ion and increase acidity of phenol. wheras no resonance is possible in alkoxide ions $\left(\mathrm{RO}^{-}\right)$ derived from alcohol. The negative charge is localized on oxygen atom. Thus, alcohols are not acidic.

questions.q: The correct nucleophilicity order is
questions.solution:
Nucleophilicity increases with the decrease in electronegativity of the central atom. Since electronegativity follows the order:
$\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{O}>\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{C} ;$ nucleophilicity of the
concerned group will follow the reverse order i.e.,
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}>\mathrm{OH}^{-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The one which is least basic is
questions.solution:
More the electron density on $\mathrm{N}$, higher will be the basicity. Density on $\mathrm{N}$ is influenced by the (i) nature of the group (+I or -I) present in alkyl group or benzene nucleus and (ii) resonance (delocalisation of the electron present on $\mathrm{N}$ ). In $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ :
electron pair is delocalised to the maximum extent due to three benzene rings and hence least available for protonation, thus it will be least basic)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: The chemical composition of 'slag' formed during the smelting process in the extraction of copper is
questions.solution: During the extraction of copper, iron is present in the ore as impurity (FeS). The ore together with a little coke and silica is smelted; FeS present as impurity in the ore is oxidized to iron oxide, which then reacts with silica to form fusible ferrous silicate which is removed as slag. $2 \mathrm{FeS}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2 \uparrow$

questions.q: The reaction that does NOT take place in a blast furnace between $900 \mathrm{~K}$ to $1500 \mathrm{~K}$ temperature range during extraction of iron is :
questions.solution:
At $900-1500 \mathrm{~K}$ (higher temperature range in the blast furnace)
Reaction which take place are:
$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{CO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}$
$\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{CO}_2$
$\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaSiO}_3$ (Slag formation)
$\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{CO}_2$ at $500-800 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: $\Delta_{f} \mathrm{G}^{-}$ vs $\mathrm{T}$ plot in the Ellingham diagram slopes downward for the reaction
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{S}$ increases.

Hence, as the temperature increases, $\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ increases and hence $\Delta \mathrm{G}(\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S})$ decreases. In other words, the slope of the curve for formation of CO decreases. However, for all other oxides, it increases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: The final step for extraction of copper from copper pyrite in bessemer converter involves the reaction
questions.solution:
In Bessemer converter, copper-sulphide is partially oxidised to cuprous oxide, which further reacts with the remaining copper sulphide to give copper and sulphur di oxide, i.e. Cu2 S+2Cu2O⟶6Cu+SO2

This reaction represents auto reduction. Cu2O reacts with Cu2S to give copper metal. The solid metal obtained is called as blister copper and is about 99% pure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: In the context of the Hall-Heroult process for the extraction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi></math>, which of the following statement is incorrect.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are produced in the process.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Al</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CaF</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which lowers the melting point of the mixture and brings conductivity.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Al</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is reduced at the cathode to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi></math>.
questions.solution:
(i) In Hall-Heroult process for extraction of Al, carbon anode is oxidised to CO and CO2.

(ii) When Al2O3 is mixed with CaF2, it lowers the melting point of the mixture and brings conductivity.

(iii) Al3+ is reduced at cathode to form Al.

(iv) Al2O3 serves as the electrolyte, undergoing the redox process. Na3AlF6 although is an electrolyte but serves as a solvent, not electrolyte.

questions.q: Which of the following metal is leached by cyanide process
questions.solution:
Cyanide process, which is also known as MacArthur cyanide process involves the extraction of gold and silver from their ores by dissolving them in a dilute solution of sodium cyanide or potassium cyanide. The leaching is carried out in the presence of air or oxygen to oxidise the metal.

Ag is leached by cyanide process by making the complex using NaCN/KCN as follows

Ag2S+4NaCN⟶2NaAg(CN)2Soluble+Na2S

questions.solution:
Semiconductor materials like Si and Ge are usually purified by zone refining. Zone refining is based on the principle of fractional crystallisation i.e. difference in

states of metal, so that the zones of impurities are formed and finally removed.

questions.solution:
Phenol is manufactured by heating chlorobenzene with 10% aqueous NaOH solution at about 623K under 300 atm and in the presence of copper salt acting as a catalyst to form sodium phenoxide. The sodium salt when treated with dil. HCl, gives phenol. This method is known as Dow's process. 

When phenol is heated with CHCl3 in the presence of aqueous NaOH, an aldehydic group (−CHO) get introduced in the ring at a position ortho to the phenolic group. This reaction is known as the Reimer-Tiemann reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following reactions is expected to readily give a hydrocarbon product in good yields?
questions.solution:
Electrolysis of a concentrated aqueous solution of either sodium or potassium salts of saturated carboxylic acids yields higher alkane at anode.

$$

2 \mathrm{RCOOK} \underset{\text { Oxidation }}{\text { Electrolytic }}{\longrightarrow} \underset{\text { Anode }}{2 \mathrm{RCOO}^{-}}+\underset{\text { Cathod }}{2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}}

$$

At anode $2 \mathrm{RCOO}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{RCOO}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow$

$$

\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}

$$

At Cathode $2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}$

$$

2 \mathrm{~K}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \uparrow

$$

(Kolbe's Method)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which one of the following is correct order of given isotopes?
I. $T_2>D_2>P_2$ (order of boiling point)
II. $T_2>D_2>P_2$ (order of bond energy)
III. $T_2=D_2=P_2$ (order of bond length)
IV. $T_2 < D_2 < P_2$ (order of reactivity with $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ )
questions.solution:
Protium $(P)$, deuterium $(D)$ and tritium $(T)$ are the three isotopes of hydrogen. These isotopes follow the order in different contexts as shown below :
$T_2>D_2>P_2$ [Order of boiling point (BP)]
$T_2>D_2>P_2$ [Order of bond energy $\left.(\mathrm{BE})\right]$
$T_2=D_2=P_2$ [Order of bond length $\left.(\mathrm{BL})\right]$
$T_2 < D_2 < P_2$ [Order of reactivity with $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ ]
Hence, all the given order are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 20 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid is mixed with 50 mL of potassium acetate. $K_\alpha$ of acetic acid $=1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the concentration of potassium acetate if pH of mixture is 4.8 .
questions.solution:
For an acidic buffer,
$\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_\alpha+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}\ldots(i)$
Let the concentration of potassium acetate solution $=x \mathrm{M}$ 20 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid $=20 \times 0.1 \mathrm{millimol}$ $=2$ millimol
50 mL of $x \mathrm{M}$ potassium acetate $=x \times 50 \mathrm{millimol}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =50 x \text { milimol } \\
\mathrm{pH} & =4.8
(Given)\end{aligned}$
Substitute all value in eq. (i),
$\begin{aligned}
4.8 & =-\left(\log 1.8+10^{-5}\right)+\log \frac{50 x}{2} \\
\log 25 x & =0.06 \\
x & =0.04 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following molecules acts as a Lewis acid?
questions.solution:
According to Lewis concept "Acids are electron acceptor and bases are electron donor" Since, electron deficient compounds also have an ability to accept electrons, these are regarded as acids.
Trimethylborane \((\mathrm{CH})_3\) B have incomplete octet thus, act as Lewis acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following molecular hydrides acts as a Lewis acid?
questions.solution: Boron in $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ is electron deficient

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $2 \mathrm{M}$ of formic acid $\left(\mathrm{p} K_a=3.74\right)$ is neutralise by $\mathrm{NaOH}$, at the equivalence point $\mathrm{pH}$ is
questions.solution:
Sodium formate is present at the equivalence point. It is the salt of weak acid $+$ strong base. So, final solution will be basic in nature.
As we know
$\mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{\mathrm{p} K_a}{2}+\frac{\log C}{2}$
where, $C$ is concentration of salt.
Total volume of solution $=100+100=200 \mathrm{~mL}$
Concentration of salt $(C)=2 \times \frac{100}{200}=1 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{3.74}{2}+\frac{\log [1]}{2}$
$\mathrm{pH}=7+1.87+0$
$\mathrm{pH}=8.87$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is $\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]$in a solution that is $0.02 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NH}_3$ $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KOH} ?\left[K_b\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)=1.8 \times 10^{-5}\right]$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
$K_b=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right]}$
$1.8 \times 10^{-5}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right][0.01]}{[0.02]}$
$\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]=3.6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$

$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+\mathrm{Ag} \mathrm{Br} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} }{\mathrm{Na}_{5}\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of a solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>'</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>'</mo></math>and it is mixed with another solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>'</mo></math> having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>'</mo></math>. If both solutions are mixed, the resultant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of the solution will be (Assume concentration and volume remain unchanged after mixing of individual solution)
questions.solution:
Given that,

pH of solution A=3

H+ of A=10-3 M and, pH of solution B=2

H+ of B=10-2 M

Thus, total H+ are =10-3+10-2=1.1×10-3 M

∴ pHfinal=-logH+

=-log1.1×10-3

=3-log1.1

=3-1.04=1.9

Therefore, the resultant pH of the solution will be 1.9

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of a saturated solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ba</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>OH</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn></math> . The value of its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
Given that,

pH of BaOH2=12

∵ pH+pOH=14

∴ pOH=14-pH=14-12=2

Now, pOH=-logOH-

⇒ 2=-logOH

OH-=10-2 M

BaOH2sS mol L-1⇌Ba2+S+2OH-2S

OH-=2S=10-2 M

∴ S=0.5×10-2=5×10-3 M

Ksp=Ba2+OH-2

⇒(5×10-3)(10-2)2=5×10-7M3

Therefore, the value of  Ksp is 5.0×10-7 M3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is an acid, according to
questions.solution:
BF3 is a Lewis acid, as it accepts lone pair of electrons.

It is not an Arrhenius acid because it does not furnish H+ ion in its aqueous solution.

It is also not a Bronsted Lowry acid as if does not give H+ ions in any solvent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the correct increasing order of Bronsted bases?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}<\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}<\mathrm{ClO}_{2}^{-}<\mathrm{ClO}^{-}$ is the correct

increasing order of Bronsted base. With increase in the number of oxygen atoms in the conjugate bases, the delocalisation of the $\pi$ bond becomes more and more extended. This results in decrease in the electron density. Consequently basicity also decreases.

questions.q:
$K_{c}$ for the the reaction, $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right] \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{CN}^{-},$

the equillibrium constant at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $4.0 \times 10^{-19}$ then the silver ion concentration in a solution which was originally 0.1 molar in $\mathrm{KCN}$ and 0.03 molar in $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is:

$\left.\begin{array}{c}2 \mathrm{KCN}+\mathrm{AgNO}=\left[\underset{0.1}{0.03}=\left[\begin{array}{c}\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2} \\ 0\end{array}\right]^{-}\right. \\ (0.1-0.06) & 0\end{array}\right] \begin{array}{c}\mathrm{KNO}_{3}+\mathrm{K}_{0}^{+} \\ (0.03)\end{array}$

$\therefore\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}=\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\underset{\mathrm{a}}{0.03} \mathrm{a} \quad 2 \mathrm{CN}$

$(0.03-\mathrm{a}) \quad \mathrm{a} .04(\mathrm{left} \mathrm{from} \mathrm{KCN}$

$\quad 0.04 \approx 0.04$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=4 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{(0.04)^{2} \times \mathrm{a}}{0.03}$

$\mathrm{a}=7.5 \times 10^{-18}$

Here $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ increases hence, $\mathrm{pH}$ decreases due to which soil fertility will also decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ge (II) compounds are powerful reducing agents whereas $\mathrm{Pb}$ (IV) compounds are strong oxidants. It can be because
options: ionisation potential of lead is less than that of Ge.
ionic radii of $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}$ are larger than that of $\mathrm{Ge}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Ge}^{4+}$
more pronounced inert pair effect in lead has.
questions.solution:
Inert pair effect is more pronounced in heavier members like Pb.
Hence, $\mathrm{Pb}$ (IV) compounds act as strong oxidising agents and are reduced to more stable $\mathrm{Pb}$ (II) compounds.

questions.solution:
