RSCl3 gives cross-linked silicon polymer on hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The order of acidic strength of boron trihalides
questions.solution:
The strength of acidic character of boron trihalides depends upon pπ-pπ back bonding.

In boron trihalides, pπ-pπ -back bonding occurs due to empty orbital of boron and filled orbitals of halogens.

The pπ-pπ back bonding is shown maximum by BF3, as the size of B and F are small and comparatively same. Due to this effect tendency of accepting lone-pair of electrons of boron decreases as size of halogen increases. The order of size of halogens are F<Cl<Br<I. Thus, acidic nature is in order

BF3<BCl3<BBr3<BI3

questions.q: A mixture of Boron trichloride and hydrogen is subjected to silent electric discharge to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> and heated to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>200</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>. The formula of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
When a mixture of BCl3 and H2 is subjected to silent electric discharge, diborane and HCl are formed. Diborane reacts with NH3 at 200°C to give Borazine (inorganic benzene).

2BCl3+6H2→dischargeElectricB2H6x+6HCl

3B2H61+6NH32⟶200°C2B3N3H6Borazine(y)+12H2

options: They are ketones with silyl group <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>SiH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced></math> similar to alkyl, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>SiH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>CO</mi></math>

They are synthetic polymer containing repeated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SiO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mi>units</mi></math>.
They are formed by hydrolysis of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SiCl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>.
questions.solution:
Silicones are synthetic organo-silicon polymers containing repeated R2SiO units. Since the empirical formula is same as that of a ketone R2CO, the name silicon has been given to these materials. They can be formed by hydrolysis of dichlorosilanes R2SiCl2

R2SiCl2+2H2O⟶R2SiOH2+2HCl

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the most basic oxide?
questions.solution: More the oxidation state of the central atom (metal) more is its acidity. Hence $\mathrm{SeO}_{2}(\mathrm{O} . \mathrm{S} .$ of $\mathrm{Se}=+4)$ is acidic. Further for a given O.S., the basic character of the oxides increases with the increasing size of the central atom. Thus $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Sb}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ are amphoteric and $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is basic.

questions.solution:
The structure of PO43- is as follows

In the given molecule, The P atom have 5 valence electrons. The hybridization can be determined by the number of sigma bonds and the number of lone pair.

As phosphorous central atom forms four 4 sigma bonds and has no lone pair of electrons. It uses four hybrid orbital and thus, hybridisation of p-atom is sp3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction 

N2O4g⟶2NO2g
questions.solution:
∵ ΔH=ΔE+ΔngRT

and Δng (for the given reaction) =2-1=+1

∴ ΔH>ΔE

In exothermic reactions, heat from the system is lost to the surrounding. For such reactions, ∆H is negative.

In endothermic reactions, heat is absorbed by the system from the surroundings. For such reactions, ∆H is positive.

questions.solution: The acids which contain $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{H}$ bond have strong reducing properties. Thus $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$ acid is good reducing agent as it contains two P-H bonds. For example, it reduces $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ to metallic silver.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is less acidic among the given halogen compounds?
questions.solution:
Due to stronger-I-effect of $\mathrm{F}$ than that of Cl, $\mathrm{CHF}_{3}$ should be more acidic than $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3} .$ But actually reverse is true.

This is due to : $\mathrm{CCl}_{3}^{-}$ left after the removal of a proton from $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ is stabilised due to presence of $d$ -orbitals in

Cl than: $\mathrm{CF}_{3}^{-}$ left after the removal of a

proton from $\mathrm{CHF}_{3}$ which is not stabilised due to the absence of $d$ -orbitals on $\mathrm{F}$.

questions.q: When $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is passed over heated metal $\mathrm{A}$, its amide is formed. The metal is

questions.solution:
In presence of acidic medium, ionisation of H2S is supressed so less number of S2- ions are produced. So only those sulphides are precipitated which have low solubility product Ksp value.

Thus, in acidic medium, H2S is very feebly ionised giving very small concentration of sulphide ion for precipitation. Therefore, the most insoluble salts CuS and HgS are precipitated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the stoichiometric coefficient of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ in the following balance reaction?
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{SO}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(a q)+\mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}(a q)$
(in acidic solution)
questions.solution:
Oxidation half reaction:
$\mathrm{SO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow \mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}(a q)+3 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+2 e^{-}...(i)$
Reduction half reaction:
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}(a q)+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}(a q)+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)...(ii)$
Multiply eq. (i) by 5 and (ii) by 2 and then add
$\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}(a q)+5 \mathrm{SO}_2(g) & +2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)+\mathrm{H}^{+}(a q) \\
& \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{+}(a q)+5 \mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}(a q)
\end{aligned}$
Thus, the stoichiometric coefficient of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ in the above balance equation is 5 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Volume of $\mathrm{M} / 8 \mathrm{KMnO}_4$ solution required to react completely with $25.0 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of $M / 4 \mathrm{FeSO}_4$ in acidic medium is.
questions.solution:
The balance ionic equation for the reaction is
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+5 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
From the above equation it is clear that 1 mole of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4=5$ moles of $\mathrm{FeSO}_4$.
Apply molarity equation to balance the redox reaction
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_1 V_1}{n_1}\left(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\right) & =\frac{M_2 V_2}{n_2}\left(\mathrm{FeSO}_4\right) \\
\frac{1 \times V_1}{8 \times 1} & =\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{25}{5} \\
V_1 & =\frac{1 \times 25 \times 8}{4 \times 5} \\
V_1 & =10 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \text { or } V_1=10.0 \mathrm{~mL}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is only a redox reaction but not a disproportionation reaction?
questions.solution:
The reaction $\mathrm{P}_4+8 \mathrm{SOCl}_2 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{PCl}_3+2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ $+4 \mathrm{SO}_2$ involves change of oxidation state of P from 0 to +3 and that of $S$ from +4 to +2 .
Thus, it is a redox reaction but not a disproportionation reaction.

Normality N = Weight of the base ×1000Eq. weight×volume

Gram equivalents of KOH i.e. 0.45E=20×0.51000

where, E= Equivalent mass E=0.45×100020×0.5=45

Thus, basicity =9045=2 ∵Basicity=MolecularmassEquivalentmass

$\mathrm{N}=\frac{3 \times 1000}{53 \times 100}=0.566 \mathrm{~N}$

\therefore \frac{0.45}{\mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{wt}}=\frac{20 \times 0.5}{1000}=\mathrm{E} \mathrm{wt}=45 \\

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $1.520 \mathrm{~g}$ of hydroxide of a metal on ignition gave $0.995 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxide. The equivalent weight of metal is:
questions.solution:
Let $E$ be the equivalent weight of the metal
$$
\text { So, } \frac{E+17}{E+8}=\frac{1.52}{0.995}
$$
[17 is equivalent weight of $\mathrm{OH}$ and 8 is equivalent weight of oxygen]
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow 0.995 \mathrm{E}+17 \times 0.995=\mathrm{E} \times 1.52+8 \times 1.52 \\
\Rightarrow 0.525 \mathrm{E}=16.915-12.16=4.755 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\frac{4.755}{0.525}=9
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is thermodynamically is the most stable?
questions.solution:
The thermal stability of carbonates increases down the group. So, the order of thermal stability of carbonates of alkaline earth metals is
$$
\mathrm{BaCO}_3>\mathrm{CaCO}_3>\mathrm{MgCO}_3>\mathrm{BeCO}_3 .
$$
Therefore, $\mathrm{BaCO}_3$ is thermodynamically most stable and $\mathrm{BeCO}_3$ is least stable.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has minimum melting point?
questions.solution:
Ionic compounds have high melting point. Greater the ionic character, more is melting point. $\mathrm{HCl}$ has least ionic character because of maximum electronegativity difference between the two constituent elements, ( $\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}$ ) among $\mathrm{CsF}, \mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HF}$ and $\mathrm{LiF}$

$\therefore \mathrm{HCl}$ has minimum melting point.

questions.solution: $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ being small in size is heavily hydrated and heat of hydration exceeds the lattice energy. Hence $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ is soluble in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of mobility of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is-
questions.solution:
Mobility of alkali ions in aqueous solution ∝1 size of hydrated ion 

The mobility of alkali ions is inversely proportional to the size of ion in hydrated state, because bigger ion can't move faster. Also, small ion get hydrolysed to large exent due to which its size get increased. In other words, smaller ion will form hydrated ion of larger size.

Hence, correct order of mobility is

Rb+>K+>Na+>Li+

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following salt would give <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with hot and dil <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and also decolourises <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Br</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> water?
questions.solution:
When Na2SO3 reacts with hot and dil H2SO4, it gives SO2g which decolourises bromine-water.

The reaction proceed as follows:

(i) Na2SO3+H2SO4⟶Na2SO4+SO2+H2O

(ii) Br2+H2O⟶2HBr+O

(iii) SO2+O⟶SO3 (decolourisation of bromine-water)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following property of alkaline earth metal increases with their atomic number?
options: solubility of their hydroxides in water
solubility of their sulphate in water
questions.solution: The lattice energy of the hydroxides of alkaline earth metal decreases more rapidly than their hydration energy leading to more negative value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ΔH</mi><mtext>sol</mtext></msub></math> down the group. More negative is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ΔH</mi><mtext>sol</mtext></msub></math>, more is the solubility of compounds. Hence the solubility of hydroxides of alkaline earth metal increases with their atomic number.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the incorrect statement.
options: Sodium borohydride reacts very slowly with cold water

Sodium borohydride reacts violently with cold water to give <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math>

Solubility of sodium borohydride in water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>ml</mi></math>
Melting point of sodium borohydride is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>500</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>
questions.solution:
Option (b) is the incorrect statement as reaction of NaBH4 with cold water is very slow. It is not violent. All other are correct statements.

The reaction of sodium borohydride (NaBH4​) with cold water is very slow.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Sodium sulphate is soluble in water but barium sulphate is insoluble because
options: hydration energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is more than of its lattice energy
lattice energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> is more than its hydration energy
questions.solution:
For any solution to become soluble in water hydration energy of the solution should me more than its lattice energy. Here the hydration energy of sodium sulphate is more than lattice energy but in case of barium sulphate lattice energy of barium sulphate is more than its hydration energy.  

For dissolution hydration energy must be more higher (-ve) value than of lattice energy. Thus, (c) is the correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following elements mostly form covalent compounds?
questions.solution:
First member of 1st group i.e. lithium, mostly form covalent compounds due to its small size and comparatively high I.E. (with respect to other members of its group). 

Covalent character depends upon the atomic radii. Smaller the atomic radii, more the covalent character i.e. the covalent character is inversely proportional to atomic radii.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the followings forms the base of talcum powder?
questions.solution: Magnesium hydrosilicate forms base of Talcum powder.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The by product of Solvay-ammonia process is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Thomas slag is
questions.solution: Calcium silicophosphate (a mixture of $\left.\mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \& \mathrm{Ca}_{2} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}\right)$ is called Thomas slag.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following among alkalimetal is most reactive?
questions.solution: Amongst alkali metals, Cs is most reactive because of its lowest IE.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The alkali metals form salt-like hydrides by the direct synthesis at elevated temperature. The thermal stability of these hydrides decreases in which of the following orders?
questions.solution: The stability of alkali metal hydrides decreases from Li to Cs. It is due to the fact that $\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds becomes weaker with increase in size of alkali metals as we move down the group from $\mathrm{Li}$ to $\mathrm{Cs}$. Thus the order of stability of hydrides is $\mathrm{LiH}>\mathrm{NaH}>\mathrm{KH}>\mathrm{RbH}>\mathrm{CsH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has correct increasing basic strength?
questions.solution: The basic character of oxides increases down the group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Beryllium and aluminium exhibit many properties which are similar. But, the two elements differ in
options: exhibiting amphoteric nature in their oxides
questions.solution: The valency of beryllium is +2 while that of aluminium is +3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When a crystal of caustic soda is exposed to air, a liquid layer is deposited because :
questions.solution:
It is hygroscopic and deliquescent and hence absorbs moisture and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to form $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$
$$
2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Sodium peroxide in contact with moist air turns white due to formation of:
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Beryllium resembles much with :
questions.solution: Beryllium resembles with aluminium due to similarity in the size of ions and similarity in electropositive character. This type of resemblance between first element of a group in second period with second element of the next group is termed as diagonal relationship.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The value of $\mathrm{x}$ is maximum for
questions.solution: Because of smaller size, $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions are extensively hydrated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For making good quality mirrors, plates of float glass are used. These are obtained by floating molten glass over a liquid metal which does not solidify before glass. The metal used can be
questions.solution: It is mercury, because mercury exists as liquid at room temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a solid lattice, the cation has left a lattice site and is located at an interstitial position, the lattice defect is :
questions.solution: Frenkel defect is due to dislocation of ion from its usual lattice site to interstitial position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Frenkel and Schottky defects are :
questions.solution: Frenkel and Schottky defects are crystal defects. It arises due to dislodgement of cation or anion from their places in the crystal lattice.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which compound has antifluorite structure?
questions.solution: Anti-fluorite structure refers to an anion array with tetrahedral cations and the compound having $A_2 B$ formula have anti-fluorite structure. So, $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$ has an anti-fluorite structure in which $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions occupy all the tetrahedral voids and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$occupy half of the cubic holes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Element 'B' forms ccp structure and 'A' 'occupies half of the octahedral voids, while oxygen atoms occupy all the tetrahedral voids. The structure of bimetallic oxide is :
questions.solution:
No. of lattice points $=$ No. of octahedral voids $=\frac{1}{2} \times$ No. of tetrahedral

voids in $c c p$ structure

$\therefore$ No. of atoms of $B=4$

No. of atoms of $A=\frac{1}{2} \times$ No. of octahedral voids

$=\frac{1}{2} \times 4=2$

No. of atoms of $O=$ All tetrahedral voids $=2 \times$ No. of lattice points

$=2 \times 4=8$

Hence, $A: B: O=1: 2: 4$

Therefore, the formula of the compound is $A B_{2} O_{4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a monoclinic unit cell, the relation of sides and angles are respectively:
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a compound, atoms of elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> form ccp lattice and those of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> occupy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rd</mi></math> of tetrahedral voids. The formula of the compound can be
questions.solution:
Let atoms of Y in ccp structure =100

Then, number of tetrahedral voids =2×100

∵ Number of X atoms =23 rd of tetrahedral voids

∴ Number of X atoms =23×200=4003

Hence, formula of compound can be =X4Y3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A metallic element has a cubic lattice. Each edge of the unit cell is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></mover></math> and the density of metal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>. The unit cell in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of metal are
questions.solution:
Edge length of the unit cell =2A.=2×10-8 cm

Volume of unit cell =2×10-83=8×10-24 cm3

Mass of one unit cell = volume × density

=8×10-24×25

Number of unit cell in 200 g of metal

= mass of the metal  mass of one unit cell 

=2008×10-24×25=1×1024

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, which have highest melting point?
questions.solution:
Ionic solids are formed by three dimensional arrangements of positive charged ions i.e. cations and negative charged ions i.e. anions. These ions are held together by coulombic(electrostatic) forces and hence, ionic solids are hard and brittle.

Ionic solids have highest melting point due to strong electrostatic force of attraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The non stoichiometric compound $\mathrm{Fe}_{0.94} \mathrm{O}$ is formed when $\mathrm{x} \%$ of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ions are replaced by as many $\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions, $\mathrm{x}$ is
questions.solution:
The number of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions replacing $\mathrm{x} \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ ions $=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{3}$ vacancies of cations

$$

\begin{array}{l}

=x-\frac{2 x}{3}=x / 3 \\

\text { But } x / 3=1-0.94=0.06, x=0.06 \times 3 \\

=0.18=18 \%

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{Al}$ (at. wt 27 ) crystallizes in the cubic system with a cell edge of $4.05 Ã…$. Its density is $2.7 \mathrm{~g}$ per $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$. Determine the unit cell type calculate the radius of the $\mathrm{Al}$ atom
questions.solution:
$\rho=\frac{Z \times M}{N_{0} \times a^{3}}$,

$$

2.7=\frac{Z \times 27}{6.02 \times 10^{23} \times(4.05)^{3} \times 10^{-24}}

$$

$\therefore Z=4$

Hence it is face centred cubic unit lattice.

Again $4 r=a \sqrt{2}=5.727 Ã…$

$\therefore \mathrm{r}=1.432 Ã…$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An elemental crystal has a density of $8570 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The packing efficiency is 0.68 . The closest distance of approach between neighbouring atom is $2.86 Ã…$. What is the mass of one atom approximately?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Due to Frenkel defect, the density of ionic solids
questions.solution: No change in density.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The fraction of total volume occupied by the atoms present in a simple cube is
questions.solution:
Number of atoms per unit cell $=1$ Atoms touch each other along edges. Hence $r=\frac{a}{2}$
$(\mathrm{r}=$ radius of atom and $\mathrm{a}=$ edge length)
Therefore $\%$ $=\frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}}{(2 r)^{3}}=\frac{\pi}{6}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The atomic radius of atom is r. Total volume of atoms present in a $f c c$ unit cell of an element is
questions.solution:
4 atom are present in $f c c$.
$$
\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{V}=4\left[\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}\right]=\frac{16}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A decimolar solution potassium ferrocyanide is $50 \%$ dissociated at 300 K . The osmotic pressure of solution is $\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\alpha=0.5$ (50\% dissociated) The reaction involves is Total moles at equilibrium $=1-\alpha+4 \alpha+\alpha$ $\text { Total }=1+4 \alpha$ $\therefore$ van't Hoff factor $(i)=\frac{1+4 \alpha}{1}=1+4 \times 0.5=3$ Osmotic pressure, $\pi=i C R T$ $\begin{aligned} C & =0.1 \mathrm{M}=10^2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~m}^{-3} \\ \pi & =3 \times 10^2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \\ & =7.483 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2} \end{aligned}$ or $\pi=7.48 \mathrm{~atm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 58.5 g of NaCl and 180 g of glucose were separately dissolved in 1000 mL of water. Identify the correct statement regarding the elevation of boiling point of the resulting solution.
options: NaCl solution will show higher elevation of boiling point.
Glucose solution will show higher elevation of boiling point.
Both the solution will show equal elevation of boiling point.
None will show boiling point elevation.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\text {  } 58.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NaCl} & =\frac{58.5}{58.5} \mathrm{~mol} \\
& =1 \mathrm{~mol} \\
180 \mathrm{~g} \text { of glucose } & =\frac{180}{180}=1 \mathrm{~mol}
\end{aligned}$
Thus, we have 1 M NaCl and 1 M glucose solution.
But NaCl dissociates in the solution and glucose does not dissociates.
Thus particle concentration is more in NaCl .
Hence, NaCl solution will have higher elevation in boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: One molar concentration of a solution represents :
options: 1 mole of solute in 1 kg of solution.
1 mole of solute in 1 L of solution.
1 mole of solvent in 1 kg of solution.
1 mole of solvent in 1 L of solution.
questions.solution: $1 \mathrm{M}= 1$ mole of solute in 1 L solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following substances show the highest colligative properties?
questions.solution: Colligative property depends on the number of particles or ions. Option (d) consists 4 of ions due to which it shows highest colligative property.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A 0.5 molal solution of ethylene glycol in water is used as coolant in a car. If the freezing point constant of water be $1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ per mole, the mixture shall freeze at
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} &\Delta T_f=K_f \times m=1.86 \times 0.5=0.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \text {; } \\ & T_f=-0.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
How much ethyl alcohol must be added to 1 litre of water so that the solution will freeze at $14^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}\right.$ for water $\left.=1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol}\right)$
questions.solution:
$7.5 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\Delta T_f=K_f m$
$\Delta T_f=K_f \frac{n_2 \times 1000}{w_1}$
$\Rightarrow 14=1.86 \times \frac{n_2 \times 1000}{1000}$
$n_2=7.5 \mathrm{~mol}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Liquids A and B form an ideal solution in the entire composition range. At $350 \mathrm{~K}$, the vapor pressures of pure A and pure B are $7 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~Pa}$ and $12 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~Pa}$, respectively. The composition of the vapour is in equilibrium with a solution containing 40 mole percent of $\mathrm{A}$ at this temperature is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}=7 \times 10^{3}$

$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}=12 \times 10^{3}$

$x_{\mathrm{A}}^{\prime}=0.4 ; x_{\mathrm{B}}^{\prime}=1-0.4$

$x_{\mathrm{B}}^{\prime}=0.6$

$\mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}^{\circ}}^{x_{\mathrm{A}}^{\prime}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ} x_{\mathrm{B}}^{\prime}$

$=7 \times 10^{3} \times 0.4+12 \times 10^{3} \times 0.6$

$=(7 \times 0.4+12 \times 0.6) \times 10^{3}=10^{4}$

$x_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}^{\circ}}^{x^{\prime}} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}}=\frac{7 \times 10^{3} \times 0.4}{10^{4}}$

$\therefore x_{\mathrm{A}}=0.28, x_{\mathrm{B}}=1-0.28=0.72$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The vapour pressure of two pure liquids $A$ and $B$ that form an ideal solution, are 400 and $800 \mathrm{~mm}$ of Hg respectivelyat a temperature $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The mole fraction of $A$ in a solution of $A$ and $B$ whose boiling point is $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be
questions.solution:
V.P. of solution at $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=760 \mathrm{~mm}$ [at b.p., V.P. of solution =atompheric pressure] Thus $=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\infty} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{B}}$

or $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} \cdot \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0}\left(1-\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}}\right)\left[\because \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{B}}=1\right]$

or $760=400 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}+800\left(1-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}\right)[\because \mathrm{P}=760 \mathrm{mmofHg}]$

or $-800+760=-400 x_{\mathrm{A}}$

or $-40=-400 \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}}$

or $x_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{40}{400}=0.1$

Thus mole fraction in solution is 0.1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An aqueous solution freezes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>272</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, while pure water freezes at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>273</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>;</mo></math> the molality of solution and boiling point of solution respectively will be -
questions.solution:
Depression in freezing point is given as ΔTf=Kfm

Molality of solution m=ΔTfKf

or m=0.61.86=0.322 m

Elevation in boiling point of solution is given as

ΔTb=kb×m

ΔTb=0.512×0.322

ΔTb=0.165

∴ Boiling point of solution =373+0.16

=373.16 K

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solution will have lowest freezing point?
questions.solution:
Potassium sulphate K2SO4, i=3

Sodium chloride NaCl, i=2

Urea, i=1

Glucose, i=1

Greater the value of i, greater lowering in freezing point and hence, lower will be the freezing temperature.

Therefore, K2SO4 solution has the lowest freezing point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The van't Hoff factor i for an electrolyte which undergoes dissociation and association in solvents are respectively
questions.solution: When an electrolyte dissociates van't Hoff factor i is greater than 1 and when it associates the $\mathrm{i}$ is less than $1.$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the elevation in boiling point of a solution of $10 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute $(\mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{wt}=100)$ in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $\Delta T b$, the ebullioscopic constant of water is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{k}_{b} \times \mathrm{w}}{W \times \Delta T_{b}}$
or $\quad \begin{aligned} k_{\mathrm{b}} &=\frac{\mathrm{m} \times W \times \Delta T_{b}}{1000 \times \mathrm{w}}=\frac{100 \times 100 \times \Delta T_{b}}{1000 \times 10} \\ &=\Delta T_{b} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $1.00 \mathrm{~g}$ of a non-electrolyte solute (molar mass $\left.250 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$ was dissolved in $51.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene. If the freezing point depression constant, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of benzene is $5.12 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the freezing point of benzene will be lowered by
questions.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}=5.12 \times \frac{1}{250} \times \frac{1000}{51.2}=0.4 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Coolant used in car radiator is aqueous solution of ethylene glycol. In order to prevent the solution from freezing at $-0.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How much ethylene glycol must be added to $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water ? $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{Kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following will form the cathode with respect to iron anode in an electrolyte cell?
questions.solution: In electrolytic cell the cathode is of higher reduction potential.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following statements is false?
options: The correct order of osmotic pressure for 0.01 M aqueous solution of each compound is $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}>\mathrm{KCl}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}>$ sucrose.
The osmotic pressure $(\pi)$ of a solution is given by the equation $\pi=\mathrm{MRT}$, where $\mathrm{M}$ is the molarity of the solution.
Raoult's law states that the vapour pressure of a component over a solution is proportional to its mole fraction.
Two sucrose solutions of same molality prepared in different solvents will have the same freezing point depression.
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \times \mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{i}$. Since $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}$ has different values
for different solvents, hence even if the $\mathrm{m}$ is the same $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}$ will be different.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$ in dilute aq. solution containing $7.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of salt per $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $70 \%$. If vapour pressure of water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $760 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$. The vapour pressure of solution is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$
$1-0.71 \quad 0.7 \quad 2 \times 0.7$
$\quad\left(\because \alpha=\frac{70}{100}=0.7\right)$
$\quad \mathrm{n}_{1}=\frac{100}{18}=5.55$
$\frac{\mathrm{p}^{0}-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{p}^{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2} \times \mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}, \frac{760-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}}{760}$
$=\frac{0.042 \times 2.4}{5.55+0.042}$
$\therefore \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{s}}-746 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg} .$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 1 L closed flask contains a mixture of 4 g of methane and 4.4 g of carbon dioxide. The pressure inside the flask at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is [Assume ideal behaviour of gases]
questions.solution:
no. of moles of $\mathrm{CH}_4\left(\mathrm{n}_1\right)=\frac{4}{16}=0.25 \mathrm{~mol}$
no. of moles of $\mathrm{CO}_2\left(\mathrm{n}_2\right)=\frac{4.4}{44}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Total no. of moles $\left(\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{T}}\right)=\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2=0.35 \mathrm{~mol}$
$\therefore \quad P=\frac{0.35 \times 0.082 \times 300}{1}=8.6 \mathrm{~atm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which mode of expression, the concentration of a solution remains independent of temperature?
questions.solution:
Molality-Moles of solute/mass of solvent in kg
$\because \quad$ Molality does not involve volume term.
$\therefore \quad$ It is independent of temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A certain metal sulphide, $M S_2$, is used extensively as a high temperature lubricant. If $\mathrm{MS}_2$ is $40.06 \%$ by mass sulphur, metal $M$ has atomic mass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If 0.2 gram of an organic compound containing carbon, hydrogen and oxygen on combustion, yielded 0.147 gram carbon dioxide and 0.12 gram water. What will be the content of oxygen in the substance?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An ideal gaseous mixture of ethane $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}\right)$ and ethene $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}\right)$ occupies 28 litre at 1 atm and $273 \mathrm{~K}$. The mixture reacts completely with $128 \mathrm{~g}$ $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ to produce $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} .$ Mole fraction at $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ in the mixture is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+3.5 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ;$

$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

Let volume of ethane is $\mathrm{x}$ litre, $22.4 \times 4=3.5 \mathrm{x}+3(28-\mathrm{x})$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=11.2$ litre

at constant $\mathrm{T}$ and $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{V} \propto \mathrm{n} ;$

$\Rightarrow$ Mole fraction of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ in mixture $=\frac{11.2}{28}=0.4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The amount of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ present in 1 litre of $1.5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ solution, is:
questions.solution:
Molecular weight of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=34$ Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=17$

$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~L}$ of $1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{has}=17 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

$\therefore 1$ Lof $1.5 \mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ has $=1.5 \times 17=25.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{of} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$

questions.q: In a compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> are present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> by weight. If molecular weight of compound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>108</mn></math>, the molecular formula of compound is
questions.solution:
Element Ratio (by weight) Atomic weight Mol
Ratio		 Simplest Mol Ratio
C 9 12 912=0.75 0.750.25=3
H 1 1 11=1.00 1.000.25=4
N 3.5 14 3.514=0.25 0.250.25=1

∴ Empirical formula =C3H4 N

The empirical formula mass =3×12+4×1+14=54 g

Given, molecular mass =108 g

Thus, n=10854=2

Hence, molecular formula = (Empirical formula) ×2

C3H4 N×2=C6H8 N2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The empirical formula and molecular mass of a compound are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> respectively. The molecular formula of the compound will be
questions.solution:
As we know that, the Molar mass of Carbon=12

Molar mass of Hydrogen=1

Molar mass of Oxygen=16

Empirical formula mass =CH2O=12+2+1×16=30

Molecular mass =180

n= Molccular Mass  Empirical formula mass =10030=6

∵ Molecular formula =n× empirical formula

∴ Molecular formula =6×CH2O=C6H12O6

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On electrolysis of water, a total of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mole</mi></math> of gases is evolved. The amount of water decomposed is
questions.solution:
∵ Only one mole of total gases are evolved and H2O decomposes as

H2O→decompositionH2+12O2

i.e. total moles =1+12=32 moles by one mole of H2O

Now,

∵ 32 moles of gases are evolved by =1 mole of H2O

∴ 1 mole of gases are evolved by =13/2=23

Hence, 1 mole of total gases are evolved by 23 mole of H2O.

questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>765</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an acid gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>535</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>138</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. Then, the ratio of percentage of carbon and hydrogen is
questions.solution:
% for CO2

∵ 0.765 g of acid gives CO2=0.535 g

∴100 g of acid gives CO2=0.535×1000.765

% of CO2=70.00

% for carbon 

∵ 44 g of CO2 gives, C=12 g

∴ 70 g of CO2 gives, C=12×7044

=19.09 g

∴ % of carbon =19.00

Similarly, 

(i) for hydrogen 

% of H2O

⇒ 0.138×1000.765

=18.03=18.00

% of Hydrogen

⇒ 2×1818=2.00

Hence ratio of carbon and hydrogen is 19:2

questions.q:
$1.25 \mathrm{~g}$ of a sample of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ is

dissolved in $250 \mathrm{ml}$ solution. $25 \mathrm{ml}$ of this solution neutralises $20 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. The $\%$ of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$

in this sample is

Let the amount of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ present in the mixture be $\mathrm{xg} . \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ will not react with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} .$ Then

$\therefore$ Percentage of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}=$

$\frac{1.06 \times 100}{1.25}=84.8 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
The concerned chemical reactions are

(i) $\underset{64 \mathrm{~kg}}{\mathrm{CaC}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}+\underset{\text { Ethyne, } 26 \mathrm{~kg}}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}}$

(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \underset{\text { Ethylene }, 28 \mathrm{~kg}}{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}$

$$

\text { (iii) } \mathrm{nC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow\left[-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\right]_{\mathrm{n}}

$$

$\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{n} \times 28 \mathrm{~kg} & \mathrm{n} \times 28 \mathrm{~kg} \text { polythene } \\ \text { or } 28 \mathrm{~kg} & \text { or } 28 \mathrm{~kg}\end{array}$

Thus $64 \mathrm{~kg}$ of $\mathrm{CaC}_{2}$ gives $26 \mathrm{~kg}$ of acetylene which in turn gives $28 \mathrm{~kg}$ of ethylene whose $28 \mathrm{~kg}$ gives $28 \mathrm{~kg}$ of the polymer, polythene.

questions.q: A compound of Xe and $\mathrm{F}$ is found to have $53.5 \%$ of Xe. What is oxidation number of Xe in this compound?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Xe}=53.5 \% \therefore \mathrm{F}=46.5 \%$

Relative number of atoms Xe

$$

=\frac{53.5}{131.2}=0.4 \text { and } \mathrm{F}=\frac{46.5}{19}=2.4

$$

Simple ratio $\mathrm{Xe}=1$ and $\mathrm{F}=6 ;$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$

O. $\mathrm{N}$.of $\mathrm{Xe}$ is +6

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $3.92 \mathrm{~g}$ of ferrous ammonium sulphate react completely with $50 \mathrm{ml} \frac{\mathrm{N}}{10} \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution. The percentage purity of the sample is
questions.solution:
Eq of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ used $=\frac{50 \times 1}{1000 \times 10}=0.005$

$\therefore$ Eq of FAS reacted $=0.005$

$\therefore$ weight of FAS needed

$$

=0.005 \times 392=1.96 \mathrm{~g}

$$

Thus percentage purity of FAS is $50 \%$

questions.solution:
$\quad$ Molarity $(\mathrm{M})=\frac{\mathrm{wt} \times 1000}{\text { mol. wt. } \times \mathrm{vol}(\mathrm{mL})}$

$2=\frac{\mathrm{wt} .}{63} \times \frac{1000}{250}$

$\mathrm{wt}=\frac{63}{2} \mathrm{~g}$

wt. of $70 \%$ acid $=\frac{100}{70} \times 31.5=45 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equal weights of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{KCl}$ are dissolved separately in equal volumes of solutions. Molarity of the two solutions will be :
options: That of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ will be less than that of $\mathrm{KCl}$
That of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ will be more than that of $\mathrm{KCl}$ solution
That of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ will be about half of that of $\mathrm{KCl}$ solution
questions.solution: When equal weights of different solutes are present in equal volumes of solution the molarity is inversely related to molecular mass of the solute. Mol. mass of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ is less than $\mathrm{KCl}$. Hence, molarity of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ solution will be more.

According to Dulong and Pettit's law
Atomic weight $\times$ Specific heat $=6.4$ (approx) This law is applicable only to solid elements but it fails to explain very high specific heat of diamond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In van der Waal's equation of state for a nonideal gas, the term that accounts for intermolecular forces is
questions.solution: $\left(P+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V-b)=R T$; Here $\left(P+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)$ represents the intermolecular forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Kinetic theory of gases proves
questions.solution: Kinetic theory of gases proves all the given gas laws.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How will raise in temperature affects the viscosity of liquids and gases?
options: In case of liquids, decreases and in case of gases, increases.
In case of liquid, increases and in case of gases, decreases.
questions.solution:
In case of liquids, cohesive forces decrease and hence, their viscosity decreases while increasing the temperature.
However, in case of gases, with the increase in temperature kinetic energy of gaseous molecules increases and the molecules become more mobile.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The given graph represents the variation of compressibility factor $(Z)=\frac{p V}{n R T}$, for three real gases $A, B$ and $C$. Identify the only incorrect statement.
options: For the gas $A, a=0$ and its dependence on $p$ is linear at all pressure.
For the gas $B, b=0$ and its dependence on $p$ is linear at all pressure.
For the gas $C$, which is typical real gas for which neither. $a$ nor $b=0$. By knowing the minima and point of the intersection, with $Z=1, a$ and $b$ can be calculated.
At high pressure the slope is positive for all real gases.
questions.solution:
From the graph it is clear that, the value of ' $Z$ ' decreases with increase of pressure. We can explain as follows on the basis of van der Waals' equation.
At high pressure, when ' $p$ ' is large, $V$ will be small and one cannot ignore ' $b$ ' in comparison to $V$. However, the team $a / V^2$ may be considered negligible in comparison to ' $p$ ' in van der Waals' equation.
$\left(p+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V-b)=n R T$
$p(V-b)=n R T$
$\Rightarrow \quad p V-p b=n R T$
or    $\frac{p V}{n R T}=1+\frac{p b}{n R T}$
or $Z=1+\frac{p b}{n R T}$
Thus, $Z$ is greater than 1. As pressure is increased (at constant $T$ ), the factor $\frac{p b}{n R T}$ increases. This explains why after minima in the curves, $Z$ increase continuously with pressure. Hence, the only incorrect statement is (b).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two vessels of volumes $16.4 \mathrm{~L}$ and $5 \mathrm{~L}$ contain two ideal gases of molecular existence at the respective temperature of $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and exert 1.5 and 4.1 atmospheres respectively. The ratio of the number of molecules of the former to that of the later is
questions.solution:
Given conditions $\mathrm{V}_{1}=16.4 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=5 \mathrm{~L}$

$\mathrm{P}_{1}=1.5 \mathrm{~atm}, \mathrm{P}_{2}=4.1 \mathrm{~atm}$

$\mathrm{T}_{1}=273+27=300 \mathrm{~K}$

$\mathrm{T}_{2}=273+227=500 \mathrm{~K}$

Applying gas equation, $\frac{\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}}$

$\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}}$

$\therefore \frac{1.5 \times 16.4 \times 500}{4.1 \times 5 \times 300}=\frac{2}{1}$

questions.solution:
Using PM=dRT

P1MP2M = d1RT1d2RT2768760 = 1.35×303d2×273d2 = 1.35×303×760273×768d2 = 1.48 kg/m3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Four gases <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> have almost same values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> but their <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> values <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> are van der Waal s' constant) are in the order <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo><</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>. At a particular temperature, among the four gases, the most easily liquifiable one is
questions.solution: Higher the values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>'</mo></math> more will be the tendency to get liquefy. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>'</mo></math> is highest for gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>. Thus, it is the most liquefiable gas among the given gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
One moles each of four ideal gases are kept as follows. 

I. 5 L of gas (A) at 2 atm pressure

II. 2.5 L of gas (B) at 2 atm pressure

III. 1.25 L of gas (C) at 2 atm pressure

IV. 2.5 L of gas (D) at 2.5 atm pressure

Which of the above gases is kept at highest temperature.
questions.solution:
∵ For ideals gas

pV=nRT

or T=pVnR

∵ n=1= constant

R=0.083= constant

∴ T∝PV

Thus, 

The gas that shows highest product of p×V, has highest temperature.

for
(A) p×V=2×5=10 atm L

(B) p×V=2×2.5=5 atmL

(C) p×V=2×1.25=2.5 atmL 

(D) p×V=2.5×2.5=6.25 atm L

Hence gas (A) show highest temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At what temperature, the rate of effusion of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ would be 1.625 times than that of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
questions.solution:
$\quad r \propto U$ and $U=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{\text { M }}}$

$\frac{\mathrm{r}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1} \mathrm{M}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2} \mathrm{M}_{1}}}$ or $\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{SO}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1} \times 64}{323 \times 28}}=1.625$

or $\mathrm{T}_{2}=373 \mathrm{~K}$

$\overline{\mathrm{KE}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{kT}=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{8.313}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \times 298=6.17 \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{~J}$

(Average Kinetic energy $\left.\overline{\mathrm{KE}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{T}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{T}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Increasing order of rms velocities of $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{HBr}$ is
questions.solution:
RMS velocity of molecules depends on mass. If mol. wt. increases, rms velocity of melocules decreases. $\mathrm{rms} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{wt}}}$

The order of increasing $\mathrm{m} . \mathrm{wt}$. is

$\mathrm{H}_{2}<\mathrm{N}_{2}<\mathrm{O}_{2}<\mathrm{HBr}$

Order of $V_{\mathrm{mx}}$ of molecules. $\mathrm{HBr}<\mathrm{O}_{2}<\mathrm{N}_{2}<\mathrm{H}_{2}$

its molecules are relativelysmaller in size

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The degeneracy of hydrogen atom that has energy equal to $\frac{-R_{\mathrm{H}}}{9}$ is (where, $R_{\mathrm{H}}=$ Rydberg constant)
questions.solution:
The energy is given as, $E=-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{9}...(i)$
We know that,
$\begin{aligned}
E_n & =-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2} ...(ii)\\
n^2 & =9 \\
n & =3
\end{aligned}$
When $n=3, l=0,1,2$
$(3 s, 3 p, 3 d \text { sub-shells) }$
Orbitals present (i.e., degeneracy),
$3 s=1,3 p=3,3 d=5$
Total number of orbital present $=3+1+5=9$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the de-broglie wavelength of a particle of mass $(m)$ is 100 times its velocity. Then, its value in terms of its mass $(m)$ and plank constant $(h)$ is
questions.solution:
Let the wavelength of given particle $=x$
Velocity $(v)=\frac{x}{100}$
Using wavelength formula
$\begin{aligned}
 \lambda = \frac{h}{m v} \\
\therefore &  x  =\frac{h}{m\left(\frac{x}{100}\right)} \\
x & =\frac{100 h}{m x} \\
x^2 & =100 \frac{h}{m} \\
\Rightarrow \quad x & =10 \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The energy of second orbit of hydrogen atom is $-5.45 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$. What is the energy of first orbit of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ ion (in J$)$ ?
questions.solution:
$E_n=-13.6\left[\frac{z^2}{n^2}\right] e V$
$\Rightarrow \quad E_1=-13.6\left[\frac{3^2}{1^2}\right] \mathrm{eV}=-122.4 \mathrm{eV}$
$=-122.4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}=-1.962 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A photon of wavelength $3000 Ã…$ strikes a metal surface. The work function of the metal is 2.13 eV. What is the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectron?
$\left(\mathrm{h}=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\lambda=3000 Ã…=3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}, \phi=2.13 \mathrm{eV}$
K.E. $=h v-h v_0=h v-\phi=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi$
$=\frac{\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8\right)}{3 \times 10^{-7}}-\left(2.13 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)$
$=\frac{1.98 \times 10^{-25}}{3 \times 10^{-7}}-\left(3.408 \times 10^{-19}\right)$
$=\left(6.60 \times 10^{-19}\right)-\left(3.408 \times 10^{-19}\right)$
$=3.192 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}=2.0 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A stream of electrons from a heated filament was passed between two charged plates kept at a potential difference $V$ volt. If 'e' and $m$ are charge and mass of an electron, respectively, then the value of $h / \lambda$ (where $\lambda$ is wavelength associated with electron wave) is given by:
questions.solution:
As electron of charge ' $e$ ' is passed through ' $V$ ' volt, kinetic energy of electron will be eV .
Wavelength of electron wave $(\lambda)=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \cdot \mathrm{KE}}}$
$\lambda=-\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{eV}}} \quad \therefore \frac{h}{\lambda}=\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{eV}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the radius of the $3 \mathrm{rd}$ Bohr's orbit of hydrogen atom is $r_3$ and the radius of $4^{\text {th }}$ Bohr's orbit is $r_4$. Then:
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & r=0.529 \times \frac{n^2}{Z} Å \\ & r_3=0.529 \times \frac{3^2}{1} \Rightarrow r_4=0.529 \times \frac{4^2}{1} \\ & \frac{r_4}{r_3}=\frac{4^2}{3^2}=\frac{16}{9} \Rightarrow r_4=\frac{16 r_3}{9}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The Bohr orbit radius for the hydrogen atom $(n=1)$ is approximately $0.530 Å$. The radius for the first excited state $(n=2)$ orbit is (in $Å$ )

Given : Radius of hydrogen atom $=0.530$
$Å$, Number of excited state $(n)=2$ and atomic number of hydrogen atom $(Z)=1$. We know that the Bohr radius
$(r)=\frac{n^2}{Z} \times \text { radius of atom }=\frac{(2)^2}{1} \times 0.530$
$=4 \times 0.530=2.12 Å$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The probability density plots of $1 s$ and $2 s$ orbitals are given in figure.


The density of dots in a region represents the probability density of finding electrons in the region.
On the basis of above diagram which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: $1 s$ and $2 s$ orbitals are spherical in shape.
The probability of finding the electron is maximum near the nucleus.
The probability of finding the electron at a given distance is equal in all directions.
The probability density of electrons for $2 s$ orbital decreases uniformly as distance from the nucleus increases.
questions.solution: The probability density of electrons in $2 s$ orbital first increases then decreases and after that it increases again as distance increases from nucleus.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The frequency of radiation emitted when the electron falls from $\mathrm{n}=4$ to $\mathrm{n}=1$ in a hydrogen atom will be (Given : ionization energy of $\mathrm{H}=2.18 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}$ atom ${ }^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{h}=6.625 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$ )
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{v} &=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~h}} \times \mathrm{IE} \times\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}{ }^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}{ }^{2}}\right] \\ &=\frac{2.18 \times 10^{-18}}{6.625 \times 10^{-34}} \times\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{16}\right]=3.08 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.q:
Calculate the wavelength (in nanometer) associated with a proton moving at $1.0 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.
(Mass of proton $=1.67 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $\left.\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \lambda &=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27} \times 1 \times 10^{3}} \\ &=3.97 \times 10^{-10} \text { meter }=0.397 \text { nanometer } \approx 0.40 \mathrm{~nm} \end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{RZ}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=\mathrm{R} \times 2^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{l}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{R} ; \lambda=\frac{1}{3 \mathrm{R}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The wavelength corresponding to maximum energy for hydrogen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>91</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The corresponding wavelength for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion is
questions.solution:
Accordinq to Rydberg's equation

v¯=1λ=RZ21nL2-1nH2

where,

v¯= wave number

λ= wavelength

R= Rydberg constant =109677 cm-1

Z= atomic number

nL= lower energy state

nH= higher energy state.

i.e. 1λ∝Z2

Thus, λHe+λH=Z2HZ2He+

To find λHe+=?

Given, λH=91.2 nm

ZH=1

ZHe=2

∴ λHe+=1×91.24=22.8 nm

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a homonuclear molecule, which of the following set of orbitals is degenerated?
questions.solution:
Orbitals of equal energy are called degenerated orbitals. Homonuclear molecules are the molecules composed of only one type of element and contain following set of degenerated orbitals:

As, energy of,

 σ2s>σ1s

π2px≈2py

π*2px<σ*2pz

σ2px<σ*2pz

Thus, only π2px and π2py are degenerate orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the photon of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pm</mi></math> strikes an atom and one of its inner bound electron is ejected out with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, what is the energy with which it is bound to the nucleus?
questions.solution:
Total energy E of any photon is given by the relation : E=hcλ

Where, h= Planck's constant =6.6×10-34 Js

c= Velocity of light =3×108 m/s

λ= wavelength =1.5×10-10 m, i.e, 150 pm

Thus, E=hcλ=6.6×10-34×3×1081.5×10-10; E=1.32×10-15 J and, energy of ejected electron E'

E'=12mv2,

where,

m= mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg

v= velocity of electron

=1.5×107 m/s

E'=12mv2=12×9.1×10-31×1.5×1072

E'=1.024×10-16

Thus, total energy of photon = Binding energy of electron

B + energy of ejected electron E'

Thus, 1.32×10-15=B+E

∴ B=E-E'=1.32×10-15-1.024×10-16

=1.2176×10-15 J

=1.2176×10-151.6×10-19 eV=7.6×103 eV

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>53</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> metre is the radius of smallest electron orbit in hydrogen like atom, then the atom is
questions.solution:
For hydrogen like atom, the radius of nth orbit is given by,

rn=n2Zr0

Where, r0=0.53×10-10 m

and rn=0.53×10-10 m4

At ground state, n=1 

Hence, the atom is Be3+ [∵Z=4]

So, the atom is triply ionised beryllium Be3+.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> with an accuracy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>005</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. Certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, mass of electron,  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>)
questions.solution:
Given that velocity of electron, v=600 m/s

Accuracy of velocity =0.005%

According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle

Δx.mΔv=h4π

∆v=0.005% or 600 m s-1

=600×0.005100=0.03

∆x×9.1×10-31×0.03=6.6×10-344×3.14

Hence, ∆x=6.6×10-344×3.14×0.03×9.1×10-31

=1.92×10-3 m

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The orbital angular momentum of an electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> orbital is
questions.solution:
For s-subshell l=0

Orbital angular momentum (mvr)=h2πl(l+1)

For 2s orbital, l (azimuthal quantum number) =0
∴ orbital angular momentum =h2π0(0+1)

=h2π0=0

Thus, the orbital angular momentum of an electron in 2s -orbital is Zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Adsorption of a gas follows Freundlich adsorption isotherm. $x$ is the mass of the gas adsorbed on mass $m$ of the adsorbent. The plot of $\frac{x}{m}$ versus $\log p$ is shown in the given graph.
$\frac{x}{m}$ is proportional to :
questions.solution:
According to Freundlich adsorption isotherm $\frac{x}{m} \propto p^{\frac{1}{n}}, \frac{x}{m}=k p^{\frac{1}{n}}$

Slope $=\frac{2}{3}$

$\log \frac{x}{m}=\log k+\frac{1}{n} \log p$

Slope $=\frac{1}{n}=\frac{2}{3}$

$\frac{x}{m} \propto p^{\frac{2}{3}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry

options: The adsorption is accompanied by a decrease in enthalpy
The adsorption increases with increase of temperature
questions.solution: The adsorption of methylene blue on activated charcoal is an example of physiosorption which is exothermic, multilayer and does not have energy barrier.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Freundlich adsorption isotherm, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is
options: always <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> in case of physical adsorption
always <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> in use of chemical adsorption
questions.solution:
Freundlich isotherm is related with the pressure of gas adsorbed, as follows xm=k·p1/n

where, xm= amount of gas x adsorbed over the surface of mass m.

p= pressure of gas

k= freundlich constant

Thus, value of 'n' is always greater than 1 and therefore value of 1/n lies between 0 and 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following graph is correctly represented according to Freundlich isotherm.
questions.solution:
According to Freundlich adsorption isotherm,
x/m=k×P1n
where, x= mass of gas adsorbed on mass 'm' of the absorbent at pressure p.

k and n= constants.

Taking logarithm on both sides, we get
logx/m=logk+1nlogp

This is equation of a straight line with slope of 1n and intercept of logk.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following will be the most effective in the coagulation of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ soil?
questions.solution:
According to Hardy-Schulze rule, coagulation power of ions is directly proportional to charge on ion. $\because \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ is positively charged colloid. $\therefore$ It will be coagulated by anion.
(a) $\mathrm{Mg}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$
(b) $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
(c) $\mathrm{NaCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
(d) $\mathrm{KCN} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{CN}^{-}$
Because $\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}$ has highest charge among the given anions, therefore, $\mathrm{Mg}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2}$ is the most effective in the coagulation of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ solution.

questions.solution:
According to Freundlich adsorption isotherm, at intermediate pressure, extent of adsorption $$ \frac{x}{m}=k \mathrm{P}^{1 / n} \text { or } \log \frac{x}{m}=\log k+\frac{1}{n} \log \mathrm{P} $$
plot of $\log \frac{x}{m}$ vs log $\mathrm{P}$ is linear with slope $=\frac{1}{n}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What would be the amount of heat absorbed in the cyclic process shown below?
questions.solution:
In a cyclic process, internal energy change $=0$
According to $1^{\text {st }}$ law of thermodynamics
$\Delta U=\Delta q+\Delta W$
As, $\Delta U=0, \quad \Delta q=-\Delta W$
From the graph, work done $=\Delta W=$ Area under curve Here, area of circle $=\pi r^2$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\pi\left(\frac{25-5}{2}\right)^2=\pi \times 10^2 \\
\therefore \quad \Delta q & =100 \pi \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The bond dissociation energy of $X_2, Y_2$ and $X Y$ are in the ratio of $1: 0.5: 1 . \Delta H$ for the formation of $X Y$ is $-200 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. The bond dissociation energy of $X_2$ will be
questions.solution:
Let the bond dissociation energy of $X_2=a \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\text { i.e. } \mathrm{BE}\left(X_2\right)=a \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Then, $\mathrm{BE}\left(\mathrm{Y}_2\right)=0.5 a$
and $\mathrm{BE}(X Y)=a \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Given, $\frac{1}{2} X_2+\frac{1}{2} Y_2 \longrightarrow X Y, \Delta H=-200 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_r H & =\mathrm{BE}(\text { Rreactants })-\mathrm{BE}(\text { Product }) \\
\Delta_r H & =\left[\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BE}\left(X_2\right)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{BE}\left(Y_2\right)\right]-\mathrm{BE}(X Y) \\
\therefore-200 & =\frac{a}{2}+\frac{0.5 a}{2}-a \\
a & =\frac{200}{0.25}=800 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following relation is not correct?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{U}+\mathrm{PV}$ (By definition)
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta(\mathrm{PV})$ at constant pressure
$\Delta H=\Delta U+P \Delta V$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The standard Gibbs energy $\left(\Delta G^0\right)$ for the following reaction is
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{B}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=10^{12} \mathrm{at} \\ & 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
$\left(\mathrm{K}_c=\right.$ equilibrium constant $)$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}^{2+}+\mathrm{B}$
$\mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$
$=-8.314 \times 298 \times 2.303 \times \log 10^{12}=68.47 \mathrm{~kJ} /$ mol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The combustion of benzene (L) gives $\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(L)$. Given that heat of combustion of benzene at constant volume is $-3263.9 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-}$ ${ }^1$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; heat of combustion (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$ ) of benzene at constant pressure will be :
$\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{I})+\frac{15}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
$\Delta n_g=6-\frac{15}{2}=-\frac{3}{2}$
$\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n_{\mathrm{g}} R T$
$=-3263.9+\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right) \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3} \times 298$
$=-3263.9+(-3.71)=-3267.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following:
questions.solution: $\Delta U=q+w$ For a adiabatic process, $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{w}=-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ex}} \Delta \mathrm{v}$ For free expansion of ideal gas $\mathrm{p}_0=0$ As $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{C} \Delta \mathrm{T}=0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given that bond energies of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{Cl}$ are $430 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $240 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively and $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}$ for $\mathrm{HCl}$ is $-90 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{HCl}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta H_{\mathrm{HCl}}=\sum \text { B.E. of reactant }-\sum \text { B.E. of products } \\
&-90=\frac{1}{2} \times 430+\frac{1}{2} \times 240-\text { B.E. of } \mathrm{HCl} \\
& \therefore \quad \text { B.E. of } \mathrm{HCl}=215+120+90=425 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If enthalpies of formation of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure are 52,394 and $-286 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively, the change in enthalpy for combustion of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4, \mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ are $52,-394$ and $-286 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively.
(Given)
The reaction is
$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
change in enthalpy,
$(\Delta H)=\Delta H_{\text {products }}-\Delta H_{\text {reactants }}$
$=2 \times(-394)+2 \times(-286)-(52+0)$
$=-1412 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Standard entropy of $X_2, Y_2$ and $X Y_2$ are 60,40 and $50 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, respectively. For the reaction, $\frac{1}{2} X_2+\frac{3}{2} Y_2 \longrightarrow X Y_3, \Delta H=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}$, to be at equilibrium, the temperature will be
questions.solution:
$\Delta S=S\left(X Y_3\right)-\frac{1}{2} S\left(X_2\right)-\frac{3}{2} S\left(Y_2\right)$
$=50-30-60=-40 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
$\Delta H=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}=-3000 \mathrm{~J}$
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
At equilibrium,
$\Delta G=0$
$T=\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{-3000}{-40}=750 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an adiabatic process, no transfer of heat takes place between system and surrounding. Choose the correct option for free expansion of an ideal gas under adiabatic condition from the following.
questions.solution:
Free expansion, $W=0$
Adiabatic process, $q=0$
$\Delta U=q+W=0$, this means that internal energy remains constant. Therefore, $\Delta T=0$ in ideal gas there is no intermolecular attraction.
Hence, when such a gas expands under adiabatic conditions into a vacuum, no heat is absorbed or evolved, since no external work is done to separate the molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Work out the heat change (cal) when $40 \mathrm{~g}$ of He gas at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ undergoes isothermal and reversible compression from initial pressure of $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ to $10 \mathrm{~atm}\left(\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{q} &=-\mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{rev}}=-\left(-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}}\right) \\ &=2.303 \times \frac{40}{4} \times 2 \times 300 \log \frac{1}{10}=-13.82 \mathrm{kcal} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the combustion reaction at $298 \mathrm{~K}$

$2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})+1 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s})$

Which of the following alternatives is correct?
options: $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ has no relation with each other
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{nRT}$

$\Delta \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{R}}$

Now, $\Delta \mathrm{n}=2-\frac{5}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{RT}$

Thus, $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\Delta \mathrm{H}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{RT}$

$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}>\Delta \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The heat of reaction for

C10H8s+12O2g⟶10CO2g+4H2O(l) at constant volume is -1228.2 kcal at 250C. The heat of reaction at constant pressure and same temperature is
questions.solution:
Heat of reaction at constant pressure is represented by ΔH

Heat of reaction at constant volume is represented by ΔU

C10H8s+12O2g⟶10CO2g+4H2O

We known that ΔH=ΔE+ΔngRT

Δng=10-12=-2

ΔH=-1228.2×103+-2×2×298

=-1229393 Cal

=-1229.39 kcal

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Enthalpy of combustion of methane and ethane are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>210</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>368</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> respectively. The enthalpy of combustion of decane is
questions.solution:
Given, 

CH4+2O2⟶CO2+2H2O; ΔH=-210 kcal/mol    ...(i)

C2H6+72O2⟶2CO2+3H2O; ΔH=-368 kcal/mol    ...(ii)

On subtracting Eq. (i) from Eq (ii), we get

CH2+32O2⟶CO2+H2O; ΔH=-158 kcal/mol

∴ Enthalpy of combustion of one CH2 unit

=-158 kcal/mol

ΔHcombC10H22=ΔHcombCH4+9×ΔHcombCH2

=-210+9×-158

=-1632 kcal

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the heat of a reaction at constant pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi></math> and entropy change for the reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cal</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mo>deg</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, it is predicted that the reaction at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mn>0</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Heat at constant pressure means enthalpy. i.e., ΔG=ΔH-TΔS

ΔH=2.5×103cal

ΔS=7.4caldeg-1

T=298 K

ΔG=ΔH-TΔS

=-25×103-298×7.4

=-4705.2 cal

As the value of ΔG is −4705.2 cal, which is a negative value, so this reaction will be spontaneous. 

Hence, the process is spontaneous.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following reactions does not involve absorption of energy?

I. Og+e-⟶O-g

II. Sg+e-⟶S-g

III. O-g+e-⟶O2-g

IV. Clg+e-⟶Cl-g
questions.solution:
Reaction (I),(II) and (IV) are of exothermic nature, thus do not involve absorption energy.

The process O-g+e-⟶O2-g​ involves absorption of energy.

When a neutral atom gains an electron, energy is released. When an anion (having unit negative charge) gains an electron, energy is absorbed. This is due to the repulsion between the additional electron and negatively charged anion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following show an increase in entropy?

I. Boiling of water

II. Melting of ice

III. Freezing of water

IV. Formation of hydrogen gas from water
questions.solution:
Entropy is the measure of disorderliness. It means as disorderliness increases entropy increases.

Melting of ice leads to the formation of liquid water where the particles are in random motion which leads to an increase in entropy.

Option (c) is the correct option as in (i),(ii) and (iv) the state changes towards more random state, thus entropy increases.

questions.solution:
Heat needed too be supplied

$$

\text { per } m o l=330+580+1820+2740

$$

$=5470 \mathrm{~kJ}$

For the dissociation reaction, $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{g}) \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=162 \mathrm{Kcal}$

heat of atomisation of $\mathrm{H}$ is

$162=2 \times \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}}-\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}$

$\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{162}{2} \quad\left(\because \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=0\right)$

questions.q: At a constant volume the specific heat of a gas is 0.075 and its molecular weight is $40 .$ The gas is:

$\begin{aligned} & q=\frac{c}{m} \\ & \Rightarrow C_{v}=q \times m \\ &=0.075 \times 40 \\ &=3.0 \mathrm{cal} \\ & C_{P}-C_{v}=R \\ & C_{P}=C_{V}+R \\ &=3+2=5 \\ & \frac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}=\frac{5}{3}=1.66 \end{aligned}$
Monoatomic gas

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is correct regarding $\Delta \mathrm{H}:$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
According to thermodynamics's Ist law
$$
\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{E}+\mathrm{nRT}
$$
Where $\Delta \mathrm{H}=$ enthalpy of reaction at constant pressure $\Delta \mathrm{E}=$ heat of reaction at constant volume $\mathrm{R}=$ molar gas constant
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{T}=\text { temperature of the reaction } \\
\mathrm{n}=(\text { no. of moles of product })-(\text { no. of moles }
\end{array}
$$
of reactant.) From reaction, $\mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{R}}=2-4=-2$
Hence, $\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{E}-2 \mathrm{RT}$.
