questions.q: The maximum area of rectangle inscribed in a circle of diameter $R$ is
questions.solution: According to question, Diameter $=R$ Diagonals, $d_1=d_2=R$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Max Area of rectangle (any quadrilateral) } & =\frac{1}{2} d_1 \times d_2 \\ = & \frac{1}{2} \times R \times R=\frac{R^2}{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the function $f(x)=\frac{|x-1|}{x^2}$, then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution: Given, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x-1}{x^2} ; & x \geq 1 \\ \frac{-x+1}{x^2} ; & x \lt 1\end{aligned}\right.$ $\begin{aligned} & f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{x^2} ; & x \geq 1 \\ -\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x^2} ; & x \lt 1\end{cases} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{-1}{x^2}+\frac{2}{x^3} ; & x \geq 1 \\ \frac{1}{x^2}-\frac{2}{x^3} ; & x \lt 1\end{cases} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{2-x}{x^3} ; & x \geq 1 \\ \frac{x-2}{x^3} ; & x \lt 1\end{cases} \end{aligned}$ By observation, $f^{\prime}(x)$ will be Decreasing in $(0,1) \cup(2, \infty)$ and increasing in $(-\infty, 0) \cup(1,2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The maximum volume (in cu. units) of the cylinder which can be inscribed in a sphere of radius 12 units is
questions.solution: $12^2=r^2+\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2 \Rightarrow V=\pi r^2 h$ $\Rightarrow \quad V=\pi\left(144-\frac{h^2}{4}\right) h$ $\Rightarrow \quad V=144 \pi h-\frac{\pi}{4} h^3 \Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d h}=144 \pi-\frac{3 \pi}{4} h^2$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d V}{d h}=0 \Rightarrow 144 \pi=\frac{3 \pi}{4} h^2$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^2=48 \times 4 \Rightarrow h=8 \sqrt{3}$ $\therefore \quad 12^2=r^2+48 \Rightarrow r^2=96$ Volume $=\pi r^2 h=\pi \times 96 \times 8 \sqrt{3}=768 \sqrt{3} \pi \mathrm{~cm}^3$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the angle made by the tangent at the point $\left(x_0, y_0\right)$ on the curve $x=12(t+\sin t \cos t), y=12$
$(1+\sin t)^2, 0 \lt t \lt \frac{\pi}{2}$, with the positive x -axis is $\frac{\pi}{3}$, then $y_0$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given equation of curve
$x=12(t+\sin t \cos t), y=12(1+\sin t)^2$
Differentiate w.r.t ' $t$ ',
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d x}{d t}=12\left(1+\cos ^2 t-\sin ^2 t\right) \\
& \frac{d x}{d t}=12(1+\cos 2 t) \text { and } \frac{d y}{d t} \\
& =24(1+\sin t) \cos t \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2(1+\sin t) \times \cos t}{1+\cos 2 t}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The altitude of a cone is 20 cm and its semivertical angle is $30^{\circ}$. If the semi-vertical angle is increasing at the rate of $2^{\circ}$ per second, then the radius of the base is increasing at the rate of
questions.solution: Let $\theta$ be the semi-vertical angle and $r$ be the radius of the cone at time $t$. the radius of the cone at time $t$. Then, $r=20 \tan \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d r}{d t}=20 \sec ^2 \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d r}{d t}=20 \sec ^2 30^{\circ} \times 2$ $\left[\because \theta=30^{\circ}\right.$ and $\left.\frac{d \theta}{d t}=2\right]$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d r}{d t}=20 \times \frac{4}{3} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}=\frac{160}{3} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point of inflexion for the curve $y=(x-a)^n$, where n is odd integer and $n \geq 3$ is
questions.solution:
Here $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=n(n-1)(x-a)^{n-2}$
Now, $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=0 \Rightarrow x=a$
Differentiating equation of the curve $n$ times,
we get, $\frac{d^n y}{d x^n}=n$ !
$\therefore \quad$ at $x=a, \frac{d^n y}{d x^n} \neq 0$ and $\frac{d^{n-1} y}{d x^{n-1}}=0$,
where $n$ is odd.
Therefore $(a, 0)$ is the point of inflexion

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The population $p(t)$ at time $t$ of a certain mouse species satisfies the differential equation $\frac{d p(t)}{d t}=0.5 \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{t})-450$. If $p(0)=850$, then the time at which the population becomes zero is :
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is
$\frac{d p(t)}{d t}=0.5 p(t)-450$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d p(t)}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} p(t)-450$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d p(t)}{d t}=\frac{p(t)-900}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \frac{d p(t)}{d t}=-[900-p(t)]$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \frac{d p(t)}{900-p(t)}=-d t$
Integrate both the side, we get :
$-2 \int \frac{d p(t)}{900-p(t)}=\int d t$
Let $900-p(t)=u \Rightarrow-d p(t)=d u$
$\therefore \quad$ We have; $2 \int \frac{d u}{u}=\int d t \Rightarrow 2 \ln u=t+c$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \ln [900-p(t)]=t+c$ when $t=0, p(0)=850$
$2 \ln (50)=c \quad \Rightarrow 2\left[\ln \left(\frac{900-p(t)}{50}\right)\right]=t$
$\Rightarrow \quad 900-p(t)=50 e^{\frac{t}{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad p(t)=900-50 e^{\frac{t}{2}}$
let $p\left(t_1\right)=0$
$0=900-50 e^{\frac{t_1}{2}} \quad \therefore t_1=2 \ln 18$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\log (1+x)-\frac{2 x}{2+x}$ is increasing on
questions.solution:
Given $f(x)=\log (1+x)-\frac{2 x}{2+x}$
$\begin{aligned}
& f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x}-\frac{(2+x)(2)-2 x}{(2+x)^2} \\
& =\frac{1}{1+x}-\frac{4}{(2+x)^2} \\
& =\frac{(2+x)^2-4-4 x}{(1+x)(2+x)^2}=\frac{x^2}{(1+x)(2+x)^2}>0 \text { for all } \\
& x \in(0, \infty)
\end{aligned}$
Thus, given function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is increasing on $(0$, $\infty)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\cos x, g(x)=\cos 2 x, h(x)=\cos 3 x$ and $I(x)$ $=\tan x$, then which of the following option is correct?
options: $h(x)$ is neither increasing nor decreasing in $(0, \pi / 2)$
questions.solution:
(a) Let $f(x)=\cos x$, then $f^{\prime}(x)=-\sin x$.
In interval $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), f^{\prime}(x) < 0$
Therefore, $f(x)$ is strictly decreasing on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
(b) Let $f(x)=\cos 2 x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=-2 \sin x 2 x$
In interval $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), f^{\prime}(x) < 0$
Because $\sin 2 x$ will either lie in the first or second quadrant which will give a positive value.
Therefore, $f(x)$ is strictly decreasing on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
(c) Let $f^{\prime}(x)=\cos 3 x$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=-3 \sin 3 \mathrm{x}$. In Interval $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right), \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) < 0$
Because $\sin 3 \mathrm{x}$ will either lie in the first or second quadrant which will give a positive value.
Therefore, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ irstrictly decreasing on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{3}\right)$.
When $\mathrm{x} \in\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$
Because $\sin 3 x$ will lie in the third quadrant. Therefore, $f(x)$ is not strictly decreasing on
$\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
(d) Let $f(x)=\tan x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\sec ^2 x$.
In Interval $\mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$
Therefore, $f(x)$ is not strictly decreasing on
$\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The interval in which the function $f(x)=$ $\frac{4 x^2+1}{x}$ is decreasing is :
questions.solution:
Given $f(x)=\frac{4 x^2+1}{x}$ Thus $f^{\prime}(x)=4-\frac{1}{x^2}$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ will be decreasing if $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) < 0$
Thus $4-\frac{1}{x^2} < 0 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^2}>4 \Rightarrow \frac{-1}{2} < x < \frac{1}{2}$
Thus interval in which $f(x)$ is decreasing, is $\left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve given by $x+y=e^{x y}$ has a tangent parallel to the Y-axis at the point
questions.solution:
$\because x+y=e^{x y}$
Differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$1+\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x y}\left[y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}\left(1-x e^{x y}\right)=y e^{x y}-1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y e^{x y}-1}{1-x e^{x y}} \because \frac{d y}{d x}=\infty$,
as tangent is parallel to $\mathrm{Y}$-axis
$\Rightarrow 1-x e^{x y}=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{xy}}=1$
This holds, when $x=1$ and $y=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
questions.solution:
Since, $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{1+(\sin \mathrm{x}+\cos \mathrm{x})^2}(\cos \mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x})$
$=\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}$
$f(x)$ is increasing if $f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\frac{\pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow-\frac{3 \pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A running track of $440 \mathrm{ft}$ is to be laid out enclosing a football field, the shape of which is a rectangle with a semi-circle at each end. If the area of the rectangular portion is to be maximum, then the lengths of its side are
questions.solution: Perimeter $=440 \mathrm{ft}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 x+\pi r+\pi r=440$ $2 x+2 \pi r=440$ $$ \begin{aligned} A & =\text { Area of the rectangular portion }=x 2 r \\ A & =x \frac{(440-2 x)}{\pi}=\frac{1}{\pi}\left(440 x-2 x^2\right) \end{aligned} $$ Let $\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{\pi}(440-4 x)=0$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x=110 \text { for which } \frac{d^2 A}{d x^2} < 0 \\ & \Rightarrow A \text { is maximum when } x=110 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 r=\frac{440-2 x}{\pi}=\frac{440-220}{22 / 7}=70 \\ & \therefore \quad r=35 \mathrm{ft} \text { and } x=110 \mathrm{ft} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A spherical balloon is filled with $4500 ~\pi$ cubic meters of helium gas. If a leak in the balloon causes the gas to escape at the rate of $72 ~\pi$ cubic meters per minute then the rate (in meters per minute) at which the radius of the balloon decreases $49 \mathrm{~min}$ after the leakage began is
questions.solution:
$V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \Rightarrow 4500 \pi=\frac{4 \pi r^3}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad r=15 \mathrm{~m}$
After $49 \min =(4500-49.72) \pi$
$=972 \pi \mathrm{m}^3$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & 972 \pi & =\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \\ \Rightarrow & & r^3 & =3 \times 243=3 \times 3^5\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} r & =9 \\ \frac{d v}{d t} & =4 \pi r^2\left(\frac{d r}{d t}\right) \\ 72 \pi & =4 \pi \times 9 \times 9\left(\frac{d r}{d t}\right) \\ \frac{d r}{d t} & =\left(\frac{2}{9}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points at which the tangent passes through the origin for the curve $y=4 x^{3}-2 x^{5}$ are
questions.solution:
The equation of the given curve is

$\begin{array}{l}

y=4 x^{3}-2 x^{5} \\

\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=12 x^{2}-10 x^{4}

\end{array}$

Therefore, the slope of the tangent at point $(x, y)$ is $12 x^{2}-10 x^{4}$. The equation of the tangent at $(x, y)$ is given by

$\mathrm{Y}-y=\left(12 x^{2}-10 x^{4}\right)(\mathrm{X}-x) \ldots \text { (i) }$

When, the tangent passes through the origin $(0,0)$, then $\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{Y}=0$ Therefore, eq. (i) reduce to

$\begin{array}{l}

-y=\left(12 x^{2}-10 x^{4}\right)(-x) \\

\Rightarrow y=12 x^{3}-10 x^{5}

\end{array}$

Also, we have $y=4 x^{3}-2 x^{5}$

$\begin{array}{l}

\therefore 12 x^{3}-10 x^{5} \\

=4 x^{3}-2 x^{5} \\

\Rightarrow 8 x^{5}-8 x^{3}=0 \\

\Rightarrow x^{5}-x^{3}=0 \\

\Rightarrow x^{3}\left(x^{2}-1\right)=0 \\

\Rightarrow x=0, \pm 1

\end{array}$

When, $x=0$

$y=4(0)^{3}-2(0)^{5}=0$

When, $x=1$,

$y=4(1)^{3}-2(1)^{5}=2$

When, $x=-1$,

$y=4(-1)^{3}-2(-1)^{5}=-2$

Hence, the require points are $(0,0),(1,2)$ and $(-1,-2)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the radius of a sphere is measured as $9 \mathrm{~cm}$ with an error of $0.03 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the approximating error in calculating its volume.
questions.solution:
Let $r$ be the radius of the sphere and $\Delta r$ be the error in measuring the radius. Then, $r=9 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\Delta r=0.03 \mathrm{~cm}$

Let $V$ be the volume of the sphere. Then,

$\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}=4 \pi r^{2} \\

\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}}\right)_{r=9}=4 \pi \times 9^{2}=324 \pi

\end{array}$

Let $\Delta \mathrm{V}$ be the error in $\mathrm{V}$ due to error $\Delta \mathrm{r}$ in $r$.

Then,

$\begin{array}{l}

\Delta \mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dr}} \Delta r \\

\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{V}=324 \pi \times 0.03=9.72 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{3}

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f(x)=\left(\frac{e^{2 x}-1}{e^{2 x}+1}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}

\because \mathrm{f}(x)=\left(\frac{e^{2 x}-1}{e^{2 x}+1}\right) \\

\therefore \mathrm{f}(-x)=\frac{e^{-2 x}-1}{e^{-2 x}+1}=\frac{1-e^{2 x}}{1+e^{2 x}} \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(-x)=\frac{-\left(e^{2 x}-1\right)}{e^{2 x}+1}=-\mathrm{f}(x)

\end{array}$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}(x)$ is an odd function.

Again, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\frac{4 e^{2 x}}{\left(1+e^{2 x}\right)^{2}}>0, \forall x \in R$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x)$ is an increasing function.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the tangent at $P(1,1)$ on $y^{2}=x(2-x)^{2}$ meets the curve again at $Q$, then $Q$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}

y^{2}=x(2-x)^{2} \Rightarrow y^{2}=x^{3}-4 x^{2}+4 x \ldots \text { (i) } \\

\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=3 x^{2}-8 x+4 \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{3 x^{2}-8 x+4}{2 y} \\

\Rightarrow\left[\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right]_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{3-8+4}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}

\end{array}$

$y^{2}=x(2-x)^{2} \Rightarrow y^{2}=x^{3}-4 x^{2}+4 x \ldots$ (i) $\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=3 x^{2}-8 x+4$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{3 x^{2}-8 x+4}{2 y}$ $\Rightarrow\left[\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right]_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{3-8+4}{2}=-\frac{1}{2}$ $\therefore$ Equation of tangent at $\mathrm{P}$ is: $y-1=-\frac{1}{2}(x-1)$ $\Rightarrow x+2 y-3=0$ $\Rightarrow x+2 y-3=0$

Using $y=\frac{3-x}{2}$ in (i), we get: $\left(\frac{3-x}{2}\right)^{2}$

$\begin{array}{l}

=x^{3}-4 x^{2}+4 x \\

\Rightarrow 4 x^{3}-17 x^{2}+22 x-9=0 \text {...(ii) }

\end{array}$

which has two roots 1,1 (Because of (ii) being tangent at $(1,1)$ ).

Sum of 3 roots $=\frac{17}{4}$

$\begin{array}{l}

\therefore 3 \mathrm{rd} \text { root }=\frac{17}{4}-2=\frac{9}{4} \\

\text { Then, } y=\frac{3-\frac{9}{4}}{2}=\frac{3}{8} \\

\therefore \mathrm{Q} \text { is }\left(\frac{9}{4}, \frac{3}{8}\right)

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the function $f(x)=|x-1| /x^{2},$ then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{|x-1|}{x^{2}}=\left\{\begin{array}{l}\frac{1-x}{x^{2}}, x<1, x \neq 0 \\ \frac{x-1}{x^{2}}, x>1\end{array}\right.$

Clearly, $f(x)$ is continuous for all $x \in R$ except at $x=0$

$f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}

\frac{x-2}{x^{3}}, x<1, x \pm 0 \\

\frac{2-x}{x^{3}}, x>1

\end{array}\right.$

$f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow x<0$ or $1<x<2$

$f^{\prime}(x)<0 \Rightarrow 0<x<1$ or $x>2$

Hence, $f(x)$ is increasing in $(-\infty, 0) \cup(1,2)$ and

decreasing in (0,1)$\cup(2, \infty)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $x+y=3-\cos 4 \theta$ and $x-y=4 \sin 2 \theta$ then the greatest of $x y$ is
questions.solution:
$x=\frac{3-\cos 4 \theta+4 \sin 2 \theta}{2}$

$=\frac{3-\left(1-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)+4 \sin 2 \theta}{2}=(1+\sin 2 \theta)^{2}$

$y=\frac{3-\cos 4 \theta-4 \sin 2 \theta}{2}$

$=\frac{3-\left(1-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)+4 \sin 2 \theta}{2}=(1-\sin 2 \theta)^{2}$

$\therefore x y=\left(1-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta\right)^{2}=\cos ^{4} 2 \theta \leq 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the tangent to the curve $y=b e^{-x/a}$ at the point where it crosses the $y$ -axis is
questions.solution:
$y=b e^{-x / a}$ meets the $y$ -axis at $(0, b)$. Again,

$\frac{d y}{d x}=b e^{-x / a}\left(-\frac{1}{a}\right)$

$\operatorname{At}(0, b), \frac{d y}{d x}=b e^{0}\left(-\frac{1}{a}\right)=-\frac{b}{a}$

Therefore, required tangent is

$y-b=-\frac{b}{a}(x-0) \text { or } \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose that $f(0)=-3$ and $f^{\prime}(x) \leq 5$ for all values of $x$ Then, the largest value which $f(2)$ can attain is
questions.solution:
Using LMVT in [0,2] $\frac{f(2)-f(0)}{2-0}=f^{\prime}(c),$ where $c \in(0,2)$

$\frac{f(2)+3}{2} \leq 5 \Rightarrow f(2) \leq 7$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> are differentiable function in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>g</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math>, then for some <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Let

hx=fx-2gx

h0=f0-2g0

h0=2-20   ∵f0=2,g0=0

h0=2

h1=f1-2g1

h(1)=6-2(2)    ∵f1=6,g1=2

h1=2

h0=h1

h'x=f'x-2g'x

By Rolle's theorem h'c=0

∴ f'c-2g'c=0,f'c=2g'c

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least value of the function f(x)=∫0x3sinx+4cosxdx in the interval 5π4,4π3 is
questions.solution:
We have, fx=∫0x3sinx+4cosxdx

f'x=3sinx+4cosx=5sinx+tan-143

5π4<x<4π3

π<x+tan-14/3<2π

∵ f'x<0, i.e. fx is decreasing.

∵ least value of fx is 5π4,4π3=f4π3

least fx=∫04π/33sinx+4cosxdx

=-3cosx+4sinx04π/3

=-3-12-1+4-32

=9-432

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The difference of maximum and minimum values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></math> is
questions.solution:
We have, 

fx=x2e-x2

f'x=2xe-x2-2x3e-x2

f'x=2xe-x21-x2

f'x=0,x=0,-1,1

f'-h<0,f'h>0, So minimum at x=0

f'1-h>0,f'1+h<0, So maximum at x=1

f'-1-h>0,f'-1+h<0, So maximum at x=-1

Minimum fx=f0=0

Maximum fx=e-1=1e

Since, fx≥0∀x

So, difference between maximum and minimum values

=1e-0=1e

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance between the origin and the normal to curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

y=e2x+x2

at x=0,y=1

dydx=2e2x+2x

dydxx=0=2

∴ Equation of normal at (0,1) is

y-1=-12(x)

⇒ x+2y-2=0

∴ Distance from (0,0) from normal =25

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An inverted conical flask is being filled with water at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>sec</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The height of the flask is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and the radius of the base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. How fast is the water level rising when the level is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Let depth of water at time t be h and the radius of the base of water level be r.

From similar ∆OMP and ∆ONQ, we have

OMON=PMQN

⇒h10=r5

⇒r=5h10

⇒r=h2

We have, V=13πr2h=13πh22h

⇒V=πh312

On differentiating both sides, we get

dVdt=π12·3h2dhdt=πh24dhdt

Given, dVdt=3 cm3 sec-1 when h=4 cm, so we get

3=π×424dhdt

⇒dhdt=34π cm sec-1

Hence, the water level is rising at 34π cm sec-1

∴ Option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For which interval the given function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is decreasing?
questions.solution:
Given, fx=-2x3-9x2-12x+1

f'x=-6x2-18x-12

To be decreasing f'x<0

⇒-6x2-18x-12<0

⇒x2+3x+2>0

⇒x+2x+1>0

Therefore, either x<-2 or x>-1

x∈-1,∞ or -∞,-2

Hence, option (d) correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>21</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>60</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>41</mn></math>, then
questions.solution:
Given, fx=-2x3+21x2-60x+41

On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

f'x=-6x2+42x-60

⇒f'x=-6x2-7x+10

⇒f'x=-6x-2x-5

⇒x<2 or x>5, f'x<0 i.e., fx is decreasing

and 2<x<5, f'x>0 i.e., fx is increasing

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Rolle's theorem is not applicable for the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>|</mo></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>]</mo></math> because
questions.solution:
We have,

f(x)=|x|

We know that the function is not differentiable at the sharp edges. Hence, fx is not differentiable at x=0.  Therefore, Rolle's theorem is not applicable for the function

f(x)=|x| in [-1,1]

∵ f'(0) does not exist.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></math> intersect at angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
Clearly, the point of intersection of curves is (0,1).

Now, slope of tangent of first curve

m1=dydx=axloga

⇒dydx(0,1)=m1=loga

Slope of tangent of second curve,

m2=dydx=bxlogb

⇒  m2=dydx(0,1)=logb

∴ tanα=m1-m21+m1m2=loga-logb1+logalogb

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>[</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>]</mo></math> is
questions.solution:
Given, fx=x+sin2x

⇒ f'(x)=1+2cos2x

For maximum or minimum value, f'x=0

⇒ 1+2cos2x=0⇒cos2x=-12

⇒ 2x=2nπ±2π3

⇒ x=nπ±π3

⇒ x=π3,2π3,4π3,5π3

Find f0, fπ3,f2π3, f4π3, f5π3, f(2π)

⇒ f0=0,fπ3=π3+32,f2π3=2π3-0.8

⇒f4π3=4π3+0.8,f5π3=5π3-0.8

⇒ f2π=2π+0=2π

∴ Maximum value of fx=2π

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Line joining the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> is a tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, then
questions.solution:
Equation of line joining the points (0,3) and (5,-2) is y=3-x.

If this line is tangent to y=ax(x+1), then

(3-x)(x+1)=ax should have equal roots. 

Thus, (a-2)2+12=0

⇒ No value of a⇒a∈ϕ

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The shortest distance between the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Shortest distance between two curves occured along the common normal.

∴ Normal to y2=4x at m2,2m is

y+mx-2m-m3=0

Normal to y2=2x-3 at m22+3,m is

y+mx-3-m-m32=0

Both normals are same, if

-2m-m3=-4m-12m3⇒m=0,2

So, points will be (4,4) and (5,2) or (4,-4) and (5,-2).

Hence, shortest distance will be 

(1+4)=5 unit.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The set of all values of a for which the function $f(x)=\left(a^{2}-3 a+2\right)\left(\cos ^{2} x / 4-\sin ^{2} x / 4\right)+(a-1) x+$

$\sin 1$ does not possess critical points is
questions.solution:
\(f(x)=\left(a^{2}=-3 a+2\right)\left(\cos ^{2} x / 4-\sin ^{2} x / 4\right)+(a-1) x+\sin 1\)

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=(\mathrm{a}-1)(\mathrm{a}-2) \cos \mathrm{x} / 2+(\mathrm{a}-1) \mathrm{x}+\sin 1\)

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=-\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{a}-1)(\mathrm{a}-2) \sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+(\mathrm{a}-1)\)

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=(\mathrm{a}-1)\left[1-\frac{(\mathrm{a}-2)}{2} \sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}\right]\)

If \(f(x)\) does not possess critical points, then \(f^{\prime}(x)\) = 0 for any \(x \in R\)

\(\Rightarrow(a-1)\left[1-\frac{(a-2)}{2} \sin \frac{x}{2}\right]\) = 0 for any \(x \in R\)

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1\) and \(1-\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}-2}{2}\right) \sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=0\)

must not have any solution in \(\mathrm{R}\).

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1\) and \(\sin \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{a}-2}\) is not solvable in \(\mathrm{R}\).

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a} =  1\) and \(\left|\frac{2}{\mathrm{a}-2}\right|>1\left[\right.\) For \(\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}+\sin 1 \therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=1\)

\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1\) and \(0<\mathrm{a}<4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a} \in(0,1) \cup(1,4)\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the $x$ -coordinate of the point on the curve $f(x)=\sqrt{x}(7 x-6),$ where the tangent is parallel to $\mathrm{x}$ -axis?
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\sqrt{x}(7 x-6)=7 x^{3 / 2}-6 x^{1 / 2}$

$f^{\prime}(x)=7 \times \frac{3}{2} x^{1 / 2}-6 \times \frac{1}{2} x^{-1 / 2}$

When tangent is parallel to $x$ axis $f^{\prime}(x)=0$

or, $\quad \frac{21}{2} \mathrm{x}^{1 / 2}-3 \mathrm{x}^{-1 / 2}=0$

$\frac{21}{2} \sqrt{x}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{x}}$

or, $7 x=2 \Rightarrow x=\frac{2}{7}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Consider the following statements in respect of the function $f(x)=x^{3}-1, x \in[-1,1]$

I. $f(x)$ is increasing in [-1,1] 

II. $f(x)$ has no root in (-1,1) . 

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution: Since $f(x)$ is an increasing function in [-1,1] and it has a root in (-1,1) $\therefore \quad$ Only statement $I$ is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: At an extreme point of a function $f(x),$ the tangent to the curve is
options: inclined at an angle $45^{\circ}$ to the $\mathrm{x}$ -axis
inclined at an angle $60^{\circ}$ to the $x$ -axis
questions.solution: At an extreme point of a function $f(x)$, slope is always zero. Thus, At an extreme point of a function $f(x)$, the tangent to the curve is parallel to the x-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve $y=x e^{x}$ has minimum value equal to
questions.solution:
Let $y=x e^{x}$. Differentiate both side w.r.t. 'x'.

$$

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(1+\mathrm{x})

$$

Put $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(1+\mathrm{x})=0 \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=-1

\end{array}

$$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(1+\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x}+2)$

$$

\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)_{(\mathrm{x}=-1)}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}+0>0

$$

Hence, $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}}$ is minimum function and

$$

\mathrm{y}_{\min }=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}} \text { . }

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line which is parallel to X-axis and crosses the curve $y=\sqrt{x}$ at an angle of $45^{\circ}$, is
questions.solution:
Given equation of a line parallel to X-axis is $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{k}$

Given equation of the curve is $y=\sqrt{x}$, On solving equation of line with the equation of curve, we get $x=k^{2}$ Thus the intersecting point is $\left(\mathrm{k}^{2}, \mathrm{k}\right)$ It is given that the line $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{k}$ intersect the curve $y=\sqrt{x}$ at an angle of $\pi / 4$. This means that the slope of the tangent to

$$

\mathrm{y}=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}} \text { at }\left(\mathrm{k}^{2}, \mathrm{k}\right) \text { is } \tan \left(\pm \frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\pm 1

$$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{\left(\mathrm{k}^{2}, \mathrm{k}\right)}=\pm 1 \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}\right)_{\left(\mathrm{k}^{2}, \mathrm{k}\right)}=\pm 1 \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\pm \frac{1}{2}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real roots of the equation e $\mathrm{e}^{(x-1)}+\mathrm{x}$ $-2=0$ is
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=e^{x-1}+x-2$

check for $x=1$ Then, $\mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{e}^{0}+1-2=0$

So, $x=1$ is a real root of the equation $f(x)=0$ Let $x=\alpha$ be the other root such that $\alpha>1$ or

$\alpha<1$. Consider the interval $[1, \alpha]$ or $[\alpha, 1]$

Clearly $\mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{f}(\alpha)=0$

By Rolle's theorem $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0$ has a root in $(1, \alpha)$ or in $(\alpha, 1)$. But $f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x-1}+1>0,$ for all $x$. Thus

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \neq 0,$ for any $\mathrm{x} \in(1, \alpha)$ or $\mathrm{x} \in(\alpha, 1)$

which is a contradiction. Hence, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0$ has no real root other than 1 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Tangents are drawn from the origin to the curve $\mathrm{y}=\cos \mathrm{x} .$ Their points of contact lie on

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The slope of the tangent to the curve $y=e^{x}$ cos $x$ is minimum at $x=\alpha, 0 \leq a \leq 2 \pi$, then the value of $\alpha$ is
questions.solution:
Let $m$ be the slope of the tangent to the curve

$y=e^{x} \cos x$

Then, $m=\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)$

Diff. w.r.t 'x'

$$

\begin{array}{r}

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\cos \mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(-\sin \mathrm{x}-\cos \mathrm{x}) \\

=-2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}

\end{array}

$$

and $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}(\sin \mathrm{x}+\cos \mathrm{x})$

Put $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \Rightarrow \sin \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=0, \pi, 2 \pi$

Clearly, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}>0$ for $\mathrm{x}=\pi$

Thus, $y$ is minimum at $x=\pi$.

Hence the value of $\alpha=\pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}$ has a local minimum at
questions.solution: $\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}$ is of the form $x+\frac{1}{x} \geq 2$ and equality holds for $x=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}\), then
questions.solution:
Let us assume the functions $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ and $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})$ given by $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\tan \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x},$ for

$0<\mathrm{x}<\frac{\pi}{2}$

Now, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-1$ and

$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=1-\cos \mathrm{x}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})>0$ and $\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0, \forall \mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})>\mathrm{f}(0)$ and $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})>\mathrm{g}(0) \forall \mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \tan \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}>0$ and $\mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x}>0,$

$\forall \mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \tan \mathrm{x}>\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{x}>\sin \mathrm{x}, \forall \mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \quad \sin \mathrm{x}<\mathrm{x}<\tan \mathrm{x}, \forall \mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)$ be a polynomial of degree three satisfying $\mathrm{f}(0)=-1$ and $\mathrm{f}(1)=0 . \mathrm{Also}, 0$ is a stationary point of $f(x)$ If $f(x)$ does not have an extremum at $x=0$, then the value of $\int \frac{f(x)}{x^{3}-1} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$

Put $x=0$ and $x=1$

Then, we get $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=0$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=-1$ and $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=1$

It is given that $x=0$ is a stationary point of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$, but it is not a point of extremum.

Therefore, $f(0)=0=f^{\prime \prime}(0)$ and $f^{\prime \prime \prime}(0)=0$

Now, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{3}+\mathrm{bx}^{2}+\mathrm{cx}+\mathrm{d}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)=3 a x^{2}+2 b x+c$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

f^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 a x+2 b \text { and } f^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 a \\

f^{\prime}=0, f^{\prime \prime}(0)=0 \text { and } f^{\prime \prime \prime}(0)=0 \neq 0

\end{array}

$$

$\Rightarrow c=0, b=0$ and $a \neq 0$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{c}=0$ and $\mathrm{d}=-1$

Put these values in $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ we get $f(x)=x^{3}-1$

Hence, $\int \frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{x}^{3}-1} \mathrm{dx}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}-1}{\mathrm{x}^{3}-1} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{x}=\int 1 \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The interval in which the function $2 \mathrm{x}^{3}+15$ increases less rapidly than the function $9 x^{2}-12 x,$ is $-$
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=2 x^{3}+15$ and $g(x)=9 x^{2}-12 x$ then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=6 \mathrm{x}^{2} \forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is increasing function $\forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$

$$

\text { Also, } g^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow 18 x-12>0 \Rightarrow x>\frac{2}{3}

$$

Thus, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ and $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})$ both increases for $\mathrm{x}>\frac{2}{3}$

$$

\text { Let } F(x)=f(x)-g(x), F^{\prime}(x)<0

$$

$(\because \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ increases less rapidly than the function $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}))$

$$

\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{x}^{2}-18 \mathrm{x}+12<0 \Rightarrow 1<\mathrm{x}<2

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The fuel charges for running a train are proportional to the square of the speed generated in miles per hour and costs ' 48 per hour at 16 miles per hour. The most economical speed if the fixed charges i.e. salaries etc. amount to 300 per hour is
questions.solution:
Let the speed of the train be $\mathrm{v}$ and distance to be covered be so that total time taken is $\mathrm{s} / \mathrm{v}$ hours. Cost of fuel per hour $=\mathrm{kv}^{2}(\mathrm{k}$ is constant) Also $48=\mathrm{k} .16^{2}$ by given condition $\therefore \mathrm{k}=\frac{3}{16}$

$\therefore$ Cost to fuel per hour $\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{v}^{2}$. Other charges per hour are 300 . Total running cost,

$$

\begin{array}{r}

C=\left(\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{v}^{2}+300\right) \frac{\mathrm{s}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~s}}{16} \mathrm{v}+\frac{300 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{v}} \\

\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dv}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~s}}{16}-\frac{300 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=40

\end{array}

$$

$\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{dv}^{2}}=\frac{600 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{v}^{3}}>0 \therefore \mathrm{v}=40$ results in minimum

running cost

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of all lines having slope 2 which are tangent to the curve $y=\frac{1}{x-3}, x \neq 3,$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of the given curve is $\mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}-3}, \mathrm{x} \neq 3$

The slope of the tangent to the given curve

at any point $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ is given by $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{-1}{(\mathrm{x}-3)^{2}}$

For tangent having slope $2,$ we must have

$2=\frac{-1}{(x-3)^{2}}$

$$

\Rightarrow 2(x-3)^{2}=-1 \Rightarrow(x-3)^{2}=-\frac{1}{2}

$$

which is not possible as square of a real number cannot be negative. Hence, there is no tangent to the given curve having slope 2 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=(\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-2))^{2}$ is increasing in the set
questions.solution:
$$ \text { } \begin{array}{l} \text { Here, } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=(\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-2))^{2} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=4 \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-2)(\mathrm{x}-1) \end{array} $$ For $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ as increasing, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$ $$ \text { So, } 4 x(x-1)(x-2)>0 \Rightarrow x(x-1)(x-2)>0 $$
From the above figure required interval is, (0,1)$\cup(2, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a^{2} x^{4}+b^{2} y^{4}=c^{4}$, then the maximum value of xy is
questions.solution:
If the sum of two positive quantities is a constant, then their product is maximum, when they are equal. $\therefore \mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{x}^{4} \cdot \mathrm{b}^{2} \mathrm{y}^{2}$ is maximum when

$$

a^{2} x^{4}=b^{2} y^{4}=\frac{1}{2}\left(a^{2} x^{4}=\frac{1}{2}\left(a^{2} x^{4}+b^{2} y^{4}\right)=\frac{c^{4}}{2}\right.

$$

$\therefore$ maximum value of $a^{2} x^{4} \cdot b^{2} y^{4}=\frac{c^{4}}{2} \cdot \frac{c^{4}}{2}=\frac{c^{8}}{4}$

Maximum value of $x y=\left(\frac{c^{8}}{4 a^{2} b^{2}}\right)^{1 / 4}=\frac{c^{2}}{\sqrt{2 a b}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x^{\alpha} \log x$ and $f(0)=0,$ then the value of $\alpha$ for which Rolle's theorem can be applied in [0,1] is
questions.solution:
For Rolle's theorem in $[\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}], \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{b}),$
$\operatorname{In}[0,1] \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{f}(1)=0$
$\because$ the function has to be continuous in [0,1] $\Rightarrow f(0)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x^{\alpha} \log x=0 \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log x}{x^{-\alpha}}=0$
Applying L.H. Rule $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1 / x}{-\alpha x^{-\alpha-1}}=0$
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-x^{\alpha}}{\alpha}=0 \Rightarrow \alpha>0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}-1}{\mathrm{a}^{2}+1} \mathrm{x}^{3}-3 \mathrm{x}+5$ is a decreasing
function of $\mathrm{x}$ in $\mathbf{R}$, then the set of possible values of a (independent of $\mathrm{x}$ ) is
questions.solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=3\left(\frac{a^{2}-1}{a^{2}+1}\right) x^{2}-3$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})<0$ for all $\mathrm{x}$ if $\mathrm{a}^{2}-1 \leq 0 \Rightarrow-1 \leq \mathrm{a} \leq 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the normal to the curve $y=f(x)$ at the point (3,4) makes an angle $3 \pi / 4$ with the positive $\mathrm{x}$ -axis, then $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(3)=$
questions.solution:
Slope of normal to $y=f(x)$ at (3,4) is $\frac{-1}{f^{\prime}(3)}$.
$\text { Thus, } \begin{array}{c}
\frac{-1}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(3)}=\tan \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\
=-\cot \frac{\pi}{4}=-1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(3)=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(f(x)=\sin x-k x-c\), where \(k\) and \(c\) are constants, decreases always when
questions.solution:
Let \(f(x)=\sin x-k x-c\) where \(k\) and \(c\) are constants. \(f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-k\)
\(\therefore f\) decreases if \(\cos x \leq k\)
Thus, \(f(x)=\sin x-k x-c\) decrease always when \(k \geq 1\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum value of \(f(x)=\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x\) in the interval \(\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\) is
questions.solution:
Let \(y=\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}=4 \sin ^3 x \cos x+4 \cos ^3 x(-\sin x) \\
& =4 \sin x \operatorname{cox}\left(\sin ^2 x-\cos ^2 x\right) \\
& =(2 \sin 2 x)(-\cos 2 x)=-\sin 4 x \\
& \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow \sin 4 x=0 \Rightarrow 4 x=0, \pi, 2 \pi, 3 \pi
\end{aligned}\)
or \(\mathrm{x}=0, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \ldots \ldots \ldots, \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{4}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve \(y-e^{x y}+x=0\) has a vertical tangent at
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& y-e^{x y}+x=0 \\
& \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{x y}\left(y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)+1=0 \\
& \text {i.e., } \frac{d y}{d x}-y(x+y)-x(x+y) \frac{d y}{d x}+1=0 \\
& \text {i.e., }[1-x(x+y)] \frac{d y}{d x}=y(x+y)-1
\end{aligned}\)
for the vertical tangents
\(1-x(x+y)=0\) i.e., \(\quad y=\frac{1-x^2}{x}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{x}=1\) and \(\mathrm{y}=0\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{x}^3-3 \mathrm{x}^2-12 \mathrm{x}+4\), has
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=2 x^3-3 x^2-12 x+4 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=6 x^2-6 x-12=6\left(x^2-x-2\right) \\
& =6(x-2)(x+1)
\end{aligned}\)
For maxima and minima \(\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=0\)
\(\therefore \quad 6(\mathrm{x}-2)(\mathrm{x}+1)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2,-1\)
Now, \(\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=12 \mathrm{x}-6\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { At } \mathrm{x}=2 ; \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=24-6=18 > 0 \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=2, \text { local min. point } \\
& \text { At } \mathrm{x}=-1 ; \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=12(-1)-6=-18  < 0 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{x}=-1 \text { local max. point }
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\) \(\left\{\begin{array}{l}a x^2+1, x > 1 \\ x+a, x \leq 1\end{array}\right.\). Then \(f(x)\) is derivable at \(x=1\), if
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \operatorname{Lf}^{\prime}(1)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h} \\
& =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-h+a)-(1+a)}{-h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h}{-h}=1 \\
& \operatorname{Rf}^{\prime}(1)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\
& =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[a(1+h)^2+1\right]-(1+a)}{h} \\
& =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(a h+2 a)=2 a
\end{aligned}\)
Since f' (1) exists, \(\therefore L f^{\prime}(1)=R f^{\prime}(1) \Rightarrow a=1 / 2\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a circular plate is heated uniformly, its area expands \(3 c\) times as fast as its radius, then the value of \(c\) when the radius is 6 units, is
questions.solution:
Let \(A\) sq. units in the area measure when the radius is \(r\) units.
their \(A=\pi r^2\)
Differentiate both side w.r.t ' \(t\) '
\(\frac{d A}{d t}=2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}\) ...(i))
We have, \(\frac{d A}{d t}=3 c \frac{d r}{d t}\)
From eqn (i), we get
\(3 c \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}=2 \pi r \cdot \frac{d r}{d t} \Rightarrow 3 c=2 \pi r\)
Now, \(c=\frac{2}{3} \pi(6)=4 \pi\) when \(r=6\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\tan \mathrm{x}-4 \mathrm{x}\) is strictly decreasing on
questions.solution:
\(f(x)=\tan x-4 x \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\sec ^2 x-4\)
When \(\frac{-\pi}{3}  < \mathrm{x} < \frac{\pi}{3}, 1 < \sec \mathrm{x} < 2\)
Therefore, \(1 < \sec ^2 x < 4\)
\(\Rightarrow-3 < \left(\sec ^2 x-4\right) < 0\)
Thus, for \(\frac{-\pi}{3} < \mathrm{x} < \frac{\pi}{3}, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) < 0\)
Hence, \(\mathrm{f}\) is strictly decreasing on \(\left(\frac{-\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{3}\right)\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a flagstaff of 6 metres high placed on the top of a tower throws a shadow of \(2 \sqrt{3}\) metres along the ground, then the angle (in degrees) that the sun makes with the ground is
questions.solution:
Accordingly, \(\tan \theta=\frac{h}{x}=\frac{h+6}{x+2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{6}{2 \sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}\)
[Since the triangles \(A E C\) and \(B D C\) are similar] \(h \cot \alpha=(h-100) \cot \beta\) \(\therefore h=\frac{100 \cot \beta}{\cot \beta-\cot \alpha}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(M(\alpha)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 0 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\); \(M(\beta)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \beta & 0 & \sin \beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin \beta & 0 & \cos \beta\end{array}\right]\) then \([M(\alpha) M(\beta)]^{-1}\) is equal to -
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left.[\mathrm{M}(\alpha) \mathrm{M}(\beta)]^{-1}=\mathrm{M}(\beta)^{-1} \mathrm{M}(\alpha)^{-1}\right] \\
& \operatorname{Now~M(\alpha )^{-1}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\cos \alpha & \sin \alpha & 0 \\
-\sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right] \\
& =\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\cos (-\alpha) & -\sin (-\alpha) & 0 \\
\sin (-\alpha) & \cos (-\alpha) & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right]=\mathrm{M}(-\alpha) \\
& M(\beta)^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\cos \beta & 0 & -\sin \beta \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
\sin \beta & 0 & \cos \beta
\end{array}\right] \\
& =\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\cos (-\beta) & 0 & \sin (-\beta) \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
-\sin (-\beta) & 0 & \cos (-\beta)
\end{array}\right]=\mathrm{M}(-\beta) \\
& {[\mathrm{M}(\alpha) \mathrm{M}(\beta)]^{-1}=\mathrm{M}(-\beta) \mathrm{M}(-\alpha)} \\
&
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The set of points of discontinuity of the function \(1 / \log |\mathrm{x}|\) is -
questions.solution:
Let \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\log |x|}\)
The points of disconinuity of \(f(x)\) are those points where \(f(x)\) is undefined or infinite. It is undefined when \(x=0\) and is infinite when \(\log |x|=0,|x|=1\), i.e. \(x= \pm 1\).
\(\therefore \quad\) Set of points of discontinuity \(=\{-1,0,1\}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum value of the function \(y=x^4-2 x^2+1\) in the interval \(\left[\frac{1}{2}, 2\right]\) is
questions.solution:
\(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^4-2 \mathrm{x}^2+1\right)=4 \mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{x}^2-1\right)\)
For \(\max\). or \(\min , \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0\)
\(4 \mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{x}^2-1\right)=0 ;\), either \(\mathrm{x}=0\) or \(\mathrm{x}= \pm 1\)
\(x=0\) and \(x=-1\) does not belong to \(\left[\frac{1}{2}, 2\right]\)
\(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=12 x^2-4 \therefore\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{x=1}\)
\(=12(1)^2-4=8 > 0\)
\(\therefore \quad\) there is minimum value of function at \(\mathrm{x}=1\)
\(\therefore \quad\) minimum value is
\(y(1)=1^4-2(1)^2+1=1-2+1=0\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of \(a\) in order that \(f(x)=\sin x-\cos x-a x+b\) decreases for all real values is given by
questions.solution:
We have; \(f(x)=\sin x-\cos x-a x+b\)
\(\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x+\sin x-a\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})  < 0 ~\forall~ \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}\)
\(\Rightarrow(\cos x+\sin x) < a ~\forall~ x \in R\)
As the max. value of \((\cos x+\sin x)\) is \(\sqrt{2}\)
The above is possible when \(a \geq \sqrt{2}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A person standing on the bank of a river observes that the angle subtended by a tree on the opposite bank is \(60^{\circ}\). when he retreats 20 feet from the bank, he finds the angle to be \(30^{\circ}\). The breadth of the river in feet is :
questions.solution:
et \(h\) be the height of tree PQ and breadth of river PS be \(x \mathrm{~ft}\). Angle of elevation subtended by a tree is \(60^{\circ}\). Also, when he retreats 20 feet, the angle becomes \(30^{\circ}\).
Also, in \(\triangle \mathrm{PQS}\), \(\begin{aligned} & \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{h}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow h=\sqrt{3} x \end{aligned}\) and in \(\triangle \mathrm{PQR}\), \(\begin{aligned} & \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{h}{x+20} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{h}{x+20} \\ & \Rightarrow x+20=\sqrt{3} h \\ & \Rightarrow x+20=3 x \Rightarrow 2 x=20 \Rightarrow x=10 \end{aligned}\) Hence breadth of river is \(10 \mathrm{ft}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $y=m x$ bisects the area unclosed by lines $x=0, y=0$ and $x=\frac{3}{2}$ and the curve $y=1+4 x-x^2$. Then, the value of $m$ is
questions.solution: Given, $y=1+4 x-x^2$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=4-2 x=0 \Rightarrow x=2 \text{[point of maxima]}\\ & \Rightarrow \quad y_{\max }=1+4 \times 2-4=5 \end{aligned}$ when, $x=0, y=1$ $\therefore$ Graph according to question and above information $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \text { If } y=m x \text { bisects the area bounded } \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A_1=A_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \int_0^{3 / 2}\left(1+4 x-x^2\right) d x=2 \int_0^{3 / 2} m x d x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[x+2 x^2-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^{3 / 2}=\left[m x^2\right]_0^{3 / 2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}+2 \times \frac{9}{4}-\frac{27}{8} \times \frac{1}{3}=m \times \frac{9}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad m=\frac{13}{6}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a, c, b$ are in GP, then the area of the triangle formed by the lines $a x+b y+c=0$ with the coordinates axes is equal to
questions.solution: Given $a, c, b$ are in GP $\Rightarrow \quad c^2=a b$...(i) According to question, $\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Area of } \triangle O A B & =\left|\frac{1}{2} \times O A \times O B\right| \\ = & \left|\frac{1}{2} \times\left(\frac{-c}{b}\right) \times\left(\frac{-c}{a}\right)\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{c^2}{a b}=\frac{1}{2} [by Eq. (i)] \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area enclosed by the curves $y=x^3$ and $y=\sqrt{x}$ is
questions.solution: Given curves are $y=x^3$ and $y=\sqrt{x}$ Both curves intersects at $x=0$ and $x=1$ Shaded region is required area $\begin{aligned} A & =\left|\int_0^1\left(x^3-\sqrt{x}\right) d x\right|=\left|\left[\frac{x^4}{4}-\frac{2 x^{3 / 2}}{3}\right]_0^1\right| \\ & =\left|\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{2}{3}\right]\right| \\ & =\frac{5}{12} \text { sq. unit } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the curves $x$ $=y^2-2$ and $x=y$ is
questions.solution: Given, $x=y^2-2$ and $x=y$. On solving, $x=y^2-2$ and $x=y$, we get $(-1,-1)$ and $(2,2)$. Area of the shaded region, $\begin{aligned} & A=\int_{-1}^2 y d y-\int_{-1}^2\left(y^2-2\right) d y \\ & =\left[\frac{y^2}{2}-\frac{y^3}{3}+2 y\right]_{-1}^2=\left(\frac{4}{2}-\frac{8}{3}+4\right)-\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}-2\right) \\ & =\frac{10}{3}+\frac{7}{6}=\frac{27}{6}=\frac{9}{2} . \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the area bounded by the curves $y=a x^2$ and $x$ $=a y^2,(a\gt0)$ is 3 sq units, then the value of $a$ is
questions.solution: We have given, $y=a x^2$ ....(i) and $x=a y^2$ ......(ii) Put the value of $y$ by Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get $x=a \times a^2 x^4 \Rightarrow x^4 a^3-x=0$ $x\left(x^3 a^3-1\right)=0 \Rightarrow x=0, \frac{1}{2}$ When, $x=0 \Rightarrow y=0$ and $x=\frac{1}{a} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{a}$ Here, points of intersection of curves $y=a x^2$ and $x=a y^2$ are $(0,0)$ and $\left(\frac{1}{a}, \frac{1}{a}\right)$. $\therefore \quad$ Required area $A=\int_{x=a}^{x=b}\left[f_2(x)-f_1(x)\right] d x$ $3=\int_0^{1 / a}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{a}}-a x^2\right) d x$ $3=\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \times \frac{2}{3} x^{3 / 2}-\frac{a x^3}{3}\right]_0^{1 / a}$ $3=\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{a}}\left[\left(\frac{1}{a}\right)^{3 / 2}\right]-\frac{a}{3}\left[\left(\frac{1}{a}\right)^3\right]$ $3=\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{a}} \times \frac{1}{a \sqrt{a}}-\frac{a}{3} \times \frac{1}{a^3}$ $3=\frac{2}{3 a^2}-\frac{1}{3 a^2} \Rightarrow 3=\frac{2-1}{3 a^2} \Rightarrow 9 a^2=1$ $a^2=\frac{1}{9} \Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The area of the region
$\mathbf{R}=\left\{(x, y): 5 x^2 \leq y \leq 2 x^2+9\right\} \text { is : }$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Required area }=2 \int_0^{\sqrt{3}}\left(2 x^2+9-5 x^2\right) d x \\ & =2\left[9 x-x^3\right]_0^{\sqrt{3}}=2[9 \sqrt{3}-3 \sqrt{3}]=12 \sqrt{3} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area enclosed between the curve $y=\log _e(x+e)$ and the coordinate axes is
questions.solution:
Required area $A=\int_{1-e}^0 y d x=\int_{1-e}^0 \log _e(x+e) d x$
put $x+e=t \Rightarrow d x=d t$ also when $x=1-e, t=$ 1 and when $x=0, t=e$
$\therefore A=\int_1^e \log _e t d t=\left[t \log _e t-t\right]_1^e$
$e-e-0+1=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $(y-2)^2=(x-1)$, the tangent to the parabola at the point $(2,3)$ and the $X$-axis is
questions.solution:
The equation of the parabola is $y^2-4 y-x+5=0$ The equation of tangent at $(2,3)$ is $$ \begin{aligned} 3 y-2(y+3)-\frac{x+2}{2}+5 & =0 \\ 2 y-x-4 & =0 \end{aligned} $$
$\therefore$ Required area $A$ is given by $$ \begin{aligned} & A=\int_0^3\left(x_2-x_1\right) d y \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\int_0^3\left[\left\{(y-2)^2+1\right\}-\{2 y-4\}\right] d y \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=\int_0^3\left(y^2-6 y+9\right) d y=\int_0^3(3-y)^2 d y \\ & =-\left[\frac{(3-y)^3}{3}\right]_0^3=9 \\ & \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area enclosed by the curves $y=\sin x+\cos x$ and $y=|\cos x-\sin x|$ over the interval $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $y=\sin x+\cos x, x \in[0, \pi / 2]$ $\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x-\sin x$ $\begin{aligned} y & =|\cos x-\sin x| \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{cc}\cos x-\sin x & x \in[0, \pi / 4] \\ \sin x-\cos x & x \in[\pi / 4, \pi / 2]\end{array}\right]\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Required area $\begin{aligned} &=\int_0^{\pi / 4}|(\sin x+\cos x)-(\cos x-\sin x)| d x+\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2}|2 \cos x| d x \end{aligned}$ $=\int_0^{\pi / 4}|2 \sin x| d x+\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2}|2 \cos x| d x$ $=2(-\cos x)_0^{\pi / 4}+2(\sin x)_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2}$ $=2\left[-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$
$\begin{aligned}= & 2\left(2-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ = & 2(2-\sqrt{2}) \\ = & 4-2 \sqrt{2} \\ = & 2 \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{2}-1)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the curves $x+2 y^{2}=0$ and $x+3 y^{2}=1$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $x+2 y^{2}=0$ $\Rightarrow y^{2}=-\frac{x}{2} \ldots \text { (i) }$ a parabola with vertex $(0,0)$ and $x+3 y^{2}=1$ $\Rightarrow y^{2}=\frac{1-x}{3}=-\left(\frac{x-1}{3}\right) \ldots$ (ii), a parabola with vertex $(1,0)$ Solving (i) and (ii), we get $y=\pm 1$
$\mathrm{A}=\int_{-1}^{1}\left(\left(1-3 y^{2}\right)-\left(-2 y^{2}\right)\right) d y$ $=2 \int_{0}^{1}\left(1-y^{2}\right) d y=2\left[y-\frac{y^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1}$ $=\frac{4}{3}$ sq.units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by curves $(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}=1$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$ is
questions.solution:
Given circles are $x^{2}+y^{2}=1 \ldots$ (i) and $(x-1)^{2}+y^{2}=1 \ldots$ (ii) Centre of (i) is $\mathrm{O}(0,0)$ and radius $=1$
Both these circle are symmetrical about $x$-axis solving (i) and (ii), we get, $-2 x+1=0$ $\Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}$ $\begin{array}{l} \text { then } y^{2}=1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=34 \\ \Rightarrow y=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{array}$ $\therefore$ The points of intersection are $\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Q}\left(\frac{1}{2},-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$ It is clear from the figure that the shaded portion in region whose area is required. $\therefore$ Required area = area OQAPO $=2 \times$ area of the region OLAP $=2 \times($ area of the region $O L P O+$ area of $L A P L$ ) $=2\left[\int_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^{2}} d x+\int_{\frac{1}{2}}^{1} \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \mathrm{dx}\right]$ $=2\left[\frac{(x-1) \sqrt{1-(x-1)^{2}}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}(x-1)\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}+2\left[\frac{x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x\right]_{\frac{1}{2}}^{1}$ $=-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)-\sin ^{-1}(-1)+0+\sin ^{-1}(1)-\left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ $=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$ sq. units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The line $y=m x$ bisects the area enclosed by lines $x$ $=0, \mathrm{y}=0$ and $\mathrm{x}=3 / 2$ and the curve $\mathrm{y}=1+4 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}$

Then the value of $\mathrm{m}$ is
questions.solution:
$y=1+4 x-x^{2}=5-(x-2)^{2}$
We have $\int_{0}^{3 / 2}\left(1+4 x-x^{2}\right) \mathrm{dx}=2 \int_{0}^{3 / 2} \operatorname{mx} \mathrm{dx}$ $=\frac{3}{2}+2\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)-\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{27}{8}\right)=\mathrm{m} \cdot \frac{9}{4}$ On solving we get $\mathrm{m}=\frac{13}{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>[</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>]</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>[</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>]</mo><mo>)</mo></math> (where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>]</mo></math> is the greatest integer function) lying inside the region bounded by the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then
questions.solution:
[P+1]=[P]+1, Let [P]=n, then n is integer

∴ ([P+1],[P])=(n+1,n) lie inside the region of circles

S1=x2+y2-2x-15=0,C1=(1,0),r1=4

and S2=x2+y2-2x-7=0,C2=(1,0),r2=22

Both circles are concentric. 

∴ (n+1)2+n2-2(n+1)-7>0

and (n+1)2+n2-2(n+1)-15<0⇒4<n2<8

Which is not possible for any integer.

questions.q: The area under the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis, is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A wire $34 \mathrm{~cm}$ long is to be bent in the form of a quadrilateral of which each angle is $90^{\circ} .$ What is the maximum area which can be enclosed inside the quadrilateral?
questions.solution:
Let one side of quadrilateral be $\mathrm{x}$ and another side be $y$ so, $2(x+y)=34$

or $(x+y)=17$

We know from the basic principle that for a given perimeter square has the maximum area, $\mathrm{so}, \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}$ and putting this value in equation (i)

$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}=\frac{17}{2}$

Area $=x \cdot y=\frac{17}{2} \times \frac{17}{2}=\frac{289}{4}=72.25$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y):|x| \leq|y|\right.$ and $x^{2}$ $\left.+y^{2} \leq 1\right\}$ is
questions.solution: Required area $=4$ (Area of the shaded region in first quadrant) $$ =4 \int_{0}^{1 \sqrt{2}}\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right) d x=4 \int_{0}^{1 \sqrt{2}}\left(\sqrt{1-x^{2}}-x\right) d x $$ $=4\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1} x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1 / \sqrt{2}}$ $=4\left[\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{4}\right]$ $=4\left[\frac{1}{4}+\frac{\pi}{8}-\frac{1}{4}\right]=\frac{4 \pi}{8}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ sq units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The area bounded by the $x$ -axis, the curve $y=f(x)$ and the lines $\mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{b},$ is equal to $\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^{2}+1}-\sqrt{2}$

for all $b>1,$ then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\int_{1}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^{2}+1}-\sqrt{2}$

Differentiate with respect to $\mathrm{b}$

$$

f(b)=\frac{b}{\sqrt{b^{2}+1}} \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $n$ is a positive integer, then $2.4^{2 n+1}+3^{3 n+1}$ is divisible by:
questions.solution:
Let $P(n)=2 \cdot 4^{2 n+1}+3^{3 n+1}$
Then $P(1)=2.4^3+3^4=209$, which is divisible by 11 but not divisible by 2,7 or 27 .
Further, let $P(k)=2.4^{2 k+1}+3^{3 k+1}$ is divisible by 11 , that is,
$2.4^{2 k+1}+3^{3 k+1}=11 q$ for some integer q. Now
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{k}+1) & =2 \cdot 4^{2 \mathrm{k}+3}+3^{3 \mathrm{k}+4} \\
& =2 \cdot 4^{2 \mathrm{k}+1} \cdot 4^2+3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1} \cdot 3^3 \\
& =16 \cdot 2 \cdot 4^{2 \mathrm{k}+1}+27 \cdot 3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1} \\
& =16 \cdot 2 \cdot 4^{2 \mathrm{k}+1}+(16+11) \cdot 3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1} \\
& =16\left[2 \cdot 4^{2 \mathrm{k}+1}+3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1}\right]+11 \cdot 3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1} \\
& =16 \cdot 11 \mathrm{q}+11 \cdot 3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1} \\
& =11\left(16 \mathrm{q}+3^{3 \mathrm{k}+1}\right)=11 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
where $m=16 q+3^{3 k+1}$ is another integer.
$\therefore \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{k}+1)$ is divisible by 11 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution set of $(x-2)^{x^3-6 x+8}>1$ is
questions.solution:
Clearly $x>2$. Write the given inequality
as
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(x^2-6 x+8\right) \log (x-2)>0 \Leftrightarrow(x-2)(x-4) \log (x-2)> \\
& 0 \\
& \Leftrightarrow(x-4) \log (x-2)>0 \quad[\therefore x>2]
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: $2^{3 n}-7 n-1$ is divisible by
questions.solution:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The greatest positive integer, which divides

$\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(\mathrm{n}+2)(\mathrm{n}+3)$ for all $\mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N},$ is
questions.solution: The product of $\mathrm{r}$ consecutive integers is divisible byr! . Thus $n(n+1)(n+2)(n+3)$ is divisible by $4 !=24$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The set of all real $x$ satisfying the inequality

$\frac{3-|x|}{4-|x|} \geq 0,$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{3-|x|}{4-|x|} \geq 0$

$\Rightarrow 3-|x| \leq 0$ and $4-|x|<0$

or $3-|x| \geq 0$ and $4-|x|<0$

$\Rightarrow|x| \geq 3$ and $|x|>4$

or $|x| \leq 3$ and $|x|<4$

$\Rightarrow|x|>4$ or $|x| \leq 3$

$\Rightarrow x \in(-\infty,-4) \cup[-3,3] \cup(4, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If x satisfies $|3 x-2|+|3 x-4|+|3 x-6| \geq 12$, then
questions.solution:
Dividing $\mathrm{R}$ at $\frac{2}{3}, \frac{4}{3}$ and $2,$ analyse 4 cases. When $\mathrm{x} \leq \frac{2}{3},$ the inequality becomes $2-3 x+4-3 x+6-3 x \geq 12$

implying $-9 x \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \leq 0$

when $\mathrm{x} \geq 2$ the ineqality becomes $3 x-2+3 x-4+3 x-6 \geq 12$

Implying $9 \mathrm{x} \geq 24 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \geq 8 / 3$

The inequality in invalid in the other two sections.

$\therefore$ either $x \leq 0$ or $x \geq 8 / 3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $x$ and $y$ be two natural numbers such that $x y=12(x+y)$ and $x \leq y$. Then the total number of pairs $(x, y)$ is
questions.solution:
$x y-12 x-12 y=0 \Rightarrow(x-12)(y-12)=144$
Now 144 can be factorised into two factors $x$ and $y$ where $x \leq y$ and the factors are (1,144),(2,72),(3,48),(4,36),(6,24),(8,18)
(9,16),(12,12)
Thus there are eight solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Prabhat wants to invest the total amount of $Rs 15,000$ in saving certificates and national saving bonds. According to rules, he has to invest at least $Rs 2000$ in saving certificates and $Rs 2500$ in national saving bonds. The interest rate is $8 \%$ on saving certificate and $10 \%$ on national saving bonds per annum. He invest $Rs$ x in saving certificate and $Rs$ y in national saving bonds. Then the objective function for this problem is
questions.solution:
The function is given by profit function
$$
=x \cdot \frac{8}{100}+y \times \frac{10}{100}=0.08+0.10 y .
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x^n$ in the expansion of $\frac{e^{7 x}+e^x}{e^{3 x}}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{e^{7 x}+e^x}{e^{3 x}} \Rightarrow e^{4 x}+e^{-2 x}$
Series of $e^a=1+\frac{a}{1!}+\frac{a^2}{2!}+\frac{a^3}{3!} \ldots$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow e^{4 x}+ & e^{-2 x}=\left(1+\frac{4 x}{1!}+\frac{(4 x)^2}{2!}+\frac{(4 x)^3}{3!}+\ldots\right) \\
& +\left(1+\frac{(-2 x)}{1!}+\frac{(-2 x)^2}{2!}+\ldots \ldots\right)
\end{aligned}$
Here, coefficient of
$\begin{aligned}
& x^2 \equiv \frac{4^2}{2!}+\frac{(-2)^2}{2!} \\
& x^3  \equiv \frac{4^3}{3!}+\frac{(-2)^3}{3!} \\
& \vdots \\
& x^n=\frac{4^n}{n!}+\frac{(-2)^n}{n!}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The coefficient of the highest power of $x$ in the expansion of 
$\left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)^8+\left(x-\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)^8$
questions.solution:
Since $\left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)^8+\left(x-\sqrt{x^2-1}\right)^8$
$\begin{array}{r}=2\left\{{ }^8 C_0 x^8+{ }^8 C_2 x^6\left(x^2-1\right)+{ }^8 C_4 x^4\left(x^2-1\right)^2\right. \\ \left.+{ }^8 C_6 x^2\left(x^2-1\right)^3+{ }^8 C_8 x^0\left(x^2-1\right)^4\right\}\end{array}$
So coefficient of highest power of $x$
$=2\left\{{ }^8 C_0+{ }^8 C_2+{ }^8 C_4+{ }^8 C_6+{ }^8 C_8\right\}$
$=(1+1)^8+(1-1)^8=2^8=256$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the $17^{\text {th }}$ and the $18^{\text {th }}$ terms in the expansion of $(2+a)^{50}$ are equal, then the coefficient of $x^{35}$ in the expansion of $(a+x)^{-2}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $17^{\text {th }}$ and $18^{\text {th }}$ terms in the expansion $(2+a)^{50}$ are equal
$\therefore T_{17}=T_{18}$
$\Rightarrow{ }^{50} C_{16}(2)^{34}(a)^{16}={ }^{50} C_{17}(2)^{33}(a)^{17}$
$\Rightarrow a=\frac{{ }^{50} C_{16}}{{ }^{50} C_{17}} \times 2=1$
$\therefore$ Now, coefficient of $x^{35}$ in the expansion
$(1+x)^{-2}=-36$
$\because$ Coefficient of $x^r$ is $(r+1)$ in $(1-x)^{-2}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x^{18}$ occurs in the rth term in the expansion of $\left(x^4+\frac{1}{x^3}\right)^{15}$, then what is the value of $r$ ?
questions.solution:
In the expansion of $\left(x^4+\frac{1}{x^3}\right)^{15}$, let $T_r$ is the $r^{\text {th }}$ term
$\begin{aligned}
T_r & ={ }^{15} C_{r-1}\left(x^4\right)^{15-r+1}\left(\frac{1}{x^3}\right)^{r-1} \\
& ={ }^{15} C_{r-1} x^{64-4 r-3 r+3}=15_{C_{r-1}} x^{67-7 r}
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{x}^{18}$ occurs in this term
$\Rightarrow 18=67-7 r \Rightarrow 7 r=49 \Rightarrow r=7 .$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The middle term in the expansion of $\left(\frac{10}{x}+\frac{x}{10}\right)^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
General term $=T_{r+1}$
$={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_r\left(\frac{10}{x}\right)^{10-r}\left(\frac{x}{10}\right)^r$
Here $n=10$, which is an even number.
Now, $\left[\frac{10}{2}+1\right]^{\text {th }}$ term i.e. $6^{\text {th }}$ term is the middle term.
Hence, middle term $=T_6$
$\mathrm{T}_{5+1}={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_5\left(\frac{10}{x}\right)^{10-5}\left(\frac{x}{10}\right)^5$
$={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_5\left(\frac{10}{x}\right)^5\left(\frac{x}{10}\right)^5={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_5$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of terms in the expansion of $(1+5 \sqrt{2} x)^9+(1-5 \sqrt{2} x)^9$, is
questions.solution:
We have,
$(1+5 \sqrt{2} x)^9+(1-5 \sqrt{2} x)^9$
$=2\left\{{ }^2 C_0+{ }^9 C_2(5 \sqrt{2} x)^2+\ldots+{ }^9 C_8(5 \sqrt{2} x)^8\right\}$
Clearly, it has 5 terms.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the sum of the coefficients in the expansion of $(x+y)^n$ is 1024 , then the value of the greatest coefficient in the expansion is
questions.solution:
Given, sum of the coefficient $=1024$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { i.e. } & 2^n=1024=2^{10} \\ \Rightarrow & n=10\end{array}$
Since, $n$ is even, so greatest coefficient
$={ }^n C_{n / 2}={ }^{10} C_5=252$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of the term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{3}}+\frac{3}{2 x^{2}}\right)^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
The $(r+1)$ th term in the expansion of $\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{3}}+\frac{3}{2 x^{2}}\right)^{10}$ is given by

$\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{T}_{r+1}={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{3}}\right)^{10-r}\left(\frac{3}{2 x^{2}}\right)^{r} \\

={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r} \frac{x^{5-\left(\frac{r}{2}\right)}}{3^{5-\left(\frac{r}{2}\right)}} \cdot \frac{3^{r}}{2^{r} x^{2 r}} \\

={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r} \frac{3^{\left(\frac{3 r}{2}\right)-5}-5-\left(\frac{5 r}{2}\right)}{2^{r}} x

\end{array}$

$\mathrm{T}_{r+1}={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r}\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{3}}\right)^{10-r}\left(\frac{3}{2 x^{2}}\right)^{r}$ $={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r} \frac{x^{5-\left(\frac{r}{2}\right)}}{3} \cdot \frac{3^{r}}{2^{r} x^{2 r}}$ $={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{r} \frac{3^{\left(\frac{3 r}{2}\right)-5}-\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)-\left(\frac{5 r}{2}\right)}{2^{r}} x$ For $\mathrm{T}_{r+1}$ to be independent of $x$, we must have $5-\left(\frac{5 r}{2}\right)=0$ or $r=2$.

Thus, the 3 rd term is independent of $x$ and is equal to ${ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{2} \frac{3^{3-5}}{2^{2}}=\frac{10 \times 9}{2} \times \frac{3^{-2}}{4}=\frac{5}{4}$

questions.q: If a, b, c are three natural numbers in AP and a + $\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=21$ then the possible number of values of the ordered triplet $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ is
questions.solution:
$\quad$ Let $a=b-d$ and $c=b+d$, then $a+b+c=21$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=7$

So, the equation is $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}=14$

$\therefore$ No. of solution $=$ coeff. of $x^{14}$ in $\left(x+x^{2}+\ldots .\right)$ $={ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_{12}=13$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of values of satisfying the equation ${ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}-1}-{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}}={ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}-1}-{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}}$ is
questions.solution:
${ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}-1}-{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}}={ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}-1}-{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}}$

$\Rightarrow{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}-1}+{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}}={ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}-1}+{ }^{39} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow{ }^{40} \mathrm{C}_{3 \mathrm{r}}={ }^{40} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}^{2}=3 \mathrm{r}$ or $\mathrm{r}^{2}=40-3 \mathrm{r}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=0,3$ or -8,5

3 and 5 are the values as the given equation is not defined by $r=0$ and $r=-8$. Hence, the number of values of $\mathrm{r}$ is 2 .
