questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the third term in the expansion of $\left[\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{\log _{10} \mathrm{x}}\right]^{5}$

is $10^{6},$ then $\mathrm{x}$ may be
questions.solution:
Put $\log _{10} x=y,$ the given expression becomes $\left(x+x^{y}\right)^{5}$

$\mathrm{T}_{3}={ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{3}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{y}}\right)^{2}=10 \mathrm{x}^{3+2 \mathrm{y}}=10^{6}$ (given)

$\Rightarrow(3+2 \mathrm{y}) \log _{10} \mathrm{x}=5 \log _{10} 10=5$

$\Rightarrow(3+2 \mathrm{y}) \mathrm{y}=5$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=1,-\frac{5}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \log _{10} \mathrm{x}=1$ or $\log _{10} \mathrm{x}=-\frac{5}{2}$

$\therefore \mathrm{x}=10$ or $\mathrm{x}=(10)^{-5 / 2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let $\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{5 \mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}}=\mathrm{a}_{0}+\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \mathrm{a}^{3}+\ldots .$

$\begin{aligned} &=\frac{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}}+\frac{\mathrm{e}^{5 \mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}}=\mathrm{a}_{0}+\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\ldots . \\=& \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{a}_{0}+\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \mathrm{x}^{3}+\ldots . \end{aligned}$

By using $\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}=1+\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 !}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}}{3 !}+{ }_{--}--$ and

$\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}=1-\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2 !}-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}}{3 !}+---$

$\mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=2\left[1+\frac{(2 \mathrm{x})^{2}}{21}+\frac{(2 \mathrm{x})^{4}}{41}+\ldots .\right]$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

=a_{0}+a_{1} x+a_{2} x^{2}+a_{3} a^{3}+\ldots . \\

=a_{1}=a_{3}=a_{5}=\ldots=0

\end{array}

$$

Hence, $2 \mathrm{a}_{1}+2^{3} \mathrm{a}_{3}+2^{5} \mathrm{a}_{5}+\ldots .=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{4}$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x+x^{2}+x^{3}\right)^{11},$ is
questions.solution:
We have coefficient of $x^{4}$ in $\left(1+x+x^{2}+x^{3}\right)^{11}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

=\text { coefficient of } \mathrm{x}^{4} \text { in }\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{11}(1+\mathrm{x})^{11} \\

=\text { coefficient of } \mathrm{x}^{4} \text { in }(1+\mathrm{x})^{11}+\text { coefficient } \\

\text { of } \mathrm{x}^{2} \text { in } \\

\text { 11. }(1+\mathrm{x})^{11}+\text { constant term is }{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{2} \cdot(1+\mathrm{x})^{11} \\

={ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{4}+11 .{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{2}+{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{2}=990

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{T}_{0}, \mathrm{~T}_{1}, \mathrm{~T}_{2} \ldots \ldots \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}$ represent the terms in the

expansion of $(x+a)^{n}$, then $\left(\mathrm{T}_{0}-\mathrm{T}_{2}+\mathrm{T}_{4}-\ldots \ldots .\right)^{2}+$

$\left(\mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{3}+\mathrm{T}_{5}-\ldots . .\right)^{2}=$
questions.solution:
From the given condition, replacing $a$ by a and $-a i$ respectively, we get

$(x+a i)^{n}=\left(T_{0}-T_{2}+T_{4}-\ldots \ldots\right)+i\left(T_{1}-T_{3}+T_{5}-\ldots .\right)$ ...(i)

and

$(x-a i)^{n}=\left(T_{0}-T_{2}+T_{4}-\ldots \ldots\right)-i\left(T_{1}-T_{3}+T_{5}-\ldots\right)$...(ii)

Multiplying (ii) and

(i) we get required result i.e.,

$$

\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{n}=\left(T_{0}-T_{2}+T_{4}-\ldots .\right)^{2}+\left(T_{1}-T_{3}+T_{5}-\ldots\right)^{2}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of the middle term in the expansion of $(2+3 \mathrm{x})^{4}$ is:
questions.solution:
When exponent is $n$ then total number of terms are $\mathrm{n}+1 .$ So, total number of terms in $(2+3 x)^{4}=5$

Middle term is 3rd. $\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{3}={ }^{4} \mathrm{C}_{2}(2)^{2} \cdot(3 \mathrm{x})^{2}$

$$

=\frac{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1}{2 \times 1 \times 2} \times 4 \times 9 \mathrm{x}^{2}=216 \mathrm{x}^{2}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{C}_{0}, \mathrm{C}_{1}, \mathrm{C}_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}$ denote the binomial

coefficientsin the expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}}$, then the value of $\mathrm{C}_{0}+\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}+\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)+\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}+\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}\right)+\ldots$

$+\left(C_{0}+C_{1}+\ldots . .+C_{n-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_{0}+\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}+\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)+\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}+\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}\right)+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \\ &+\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}+\mathrm{C}_{1}+\ldots \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}-1}\right) \\ &=\mathrm{n} \mathrm{C}_{0}+(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{C}_{1}+(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{C}_{2}+\ldots \ldots \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}-1} \\ &=\mathrm{C}_{1}+2 \mathrm{C}_{2}+3 \mathrm{C}_{3}+4 \mathrm{C}_{4} \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{n} .2^{\mathrm{n}-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The coefficient of $x^{20}$ in the expansion of
$\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{40} \cdot\left(x^{2}+2+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)^{-5}$ is
questions.solution:
Expression $=\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{40} \cdot\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{-10}$
$=\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{30} \cdot x^{10}$
The coefficient of $x^{20}$ in $x^{10}\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{30}$ $=$ the coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{10}$ in $\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{30}$
$={ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{5}={ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{30-5}={ }^{30} \mathrm{C}_{25}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For \(n \in \mathrm{N}, x^{n+1}+(x+1)^{2 n-1}\) is divisible by
questions.solution:
For \(n=1\), we have;
\(x^{n+1}+(x+1)^{2 n-1}=x^2+(x+1)=x^2+x+1 \text {, }\)
which is divisible by \(x^2+x+1\)
For \(n=2\), we have; \(x^{n+1}+(x+1)^{2 n-1}\)
\(=x^3+(x+1)^3=(2 x+1)\left(x^2+x+1\right) \text {, }\)
which is divisible by \(x^2+x+1\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The term independent of \(x\) in the expansion of \(\left(9 \mathrm{x}-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}\right)^{18}, \mathrm{x} > 0\), is a times the corresponding binomial coefficient. Then a is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the binomial \(\left(2^{1 / 3}+3^{-1 / 3}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}\), if the ratio of the seventh term from the beginning of the expansion to the seventh term from its end is \(1 / 6\), then \(n\) equal to
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}+1}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} a^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}} \cdot \mathrm{b}^{\mathrm{r}}\) where \(\mathrm{a}=2^{1 / 3}\) and \(\mathrm{b}=3^{-1 / 3}\)
\(\mathrm{T}_7\) from beginning \(={ }^n \mathrm{C}_6 a^{\mathrm{n}-6} \mathrm{~b}^6\) and \(\mathrm{T}_7\) from end \(={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_6 \quad \mathrm{~b}^{\mathrm{n}-6} \quad \mathrm{a}^6\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-12}}{\mathrm{~b}^{\mathrm{n}-12}}=\frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow 2^{\frac{\mathrm{n}-12}{3}} \cdot 3^{\frac{\mathrm{n}-12}{3}}=6^{-1} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}-12=-3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=9
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: With 17 consonants and 5 vowels the number of words of four letters that can be formed having two different vowels in the middle and one consonant, repeated or different at each end is
questions.solution:
The two letters, the first and the last of the four lettered word can be chosen in \((17)^2\) ways, as repetition is allowed for consonants. The two vowels in the middle are distinct so that the number of ways of filling up the two places is \({ }^5 \mathrm{P}_2=20\).
The no. of different words \(=(17)^2 \cdot 20=5780\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of \(8 \tan ^2 \frac{x}{2}=1+\sec x\) is
questions.solution:
We have, \(8 \tan ^2 \frac{x}{2}=1+\sec x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 8\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{1+\cos x}\right)=1+\frac{1}{\cos x}=\frac{1+\cos x}{\cos x} \\
& \Rightarrow 8 \cos x-8 \cos ^2 x=(1+\cos x)^2 \\
& \Rightarrow 9 \cos ^2 \mathrm{x}-6 \cos \mathrm{x}+1=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(3 \cos \mathrm{x}-1)^2=0 \Rightarrow 3 \cos \mathrm{x}-1=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \cos \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{3}=\cos \alpha \text { (say) }  \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \alpha \\
& \therefore \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \text { where } \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The letters of the word TOUGH are written in all possible orders and these words are written out as in a dictionary, then the rank of the word TOUGH is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { Rank }=(4 ! \times 3)+(3 ! \times 2)+(2 ! \times 2)+1 \\
& =72+12+4+1=89
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the mid-point of a chord of the circle $x^2+y^2=4$, which subtends a right angle at the origin is
questions.solution: According to question, $O C=\sqrt{h^2+k^2}$ In $\triangle O C B$, $\begin{aligned} & \cos 45^{\circ}=\frac{\sqrt{h^2+k^2}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sqrt{h^2+k^2}}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad h^2+k^2=2 \end{aligned}$ Replacing $h \rightarrow x$ and $k \rightarrow y$ $\text { Locus } \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If p and q be the longest and the shortest distance respectively of the point $(-7,2)$ from any point $(\alpha, \beta)$ on the curve whose equation is $x^2+y^2-10 x-14 y-51=0$, then G.M. of p
questions.solution: The centre $C$ of the circle $=(5,7)$ and the radius $=\sqrt{5^2+7^2+51}=5 \sqrt{5}$ $\mathrm{PC}=\sqrt{12^2+5^2}=13 \Rightarrow q=P A=13-5 \sqrt{5}$ and $p=P B=13+5 \sqrt{3}$ $\therefore$ G.M. of $p$ and $q$ $=\sqrt{p q}=\sqrt{(13-5 \sqrt{5})(13+5 \sqrt{5})}$ $=\sqrt{169-125}=2 \sqrt{11}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: From a point $\mathrm{A}(0,3)$ on the circle $(x+2)^2$ $+(y-3)^2=4$, a chord $A B$ is drawn and it is extended to a point $Q$ such that $A Q=2 A B$. Then the locus of $Q$ is
questions.solution: Given equation of circle $(x+2)^2+(y-3)^2=4$ Let the coordinates of Q is $(h, k)$. Coordinate of B which is midpoint of AQ because $\mathrm{AQ}=2 \mathrm{AB}$. Then, $\mathrm{B}=\left(\frac{0+h}{2}, \frac{k+3}{2}\right) \rightarrow\left(\frac{h}{2}, \frac{k+3}{2}\right)$ Point B also satisfy the equation of circle. $(x+2)^2+(y-3)^2=4$ $\left(\frac{h}{2}+2\right)^2+\left(\frac{k+3}{2}-3\right)^2=4$ $\frac{(h+4)^2}{4}+\frac{(k-3)^2}{4}=4$ $(h+4)^2+(k-3)^2=16$ Replace $(h, k)$ by $(x, y)$, then, the required equation is $(x+4)^2+(y-3)^2=16$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the circle with centre $(0,2)$ and radius 2 is $x^2+y^2-m y=0$. The value of $m$ is
questions.solution:
Here $h=0, k=2$ and $r=2$. Therefore, the required equation of the circle is :
$(x-0)^2+(y-2)^2=(2)^2$
or $x^2+y^2-4 y+4=4$ or $x^2+y^2-4 y=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A circle touches both the $y$-axis and the line $x+$ $y=0$. Then the locus of its center is
questions.solution:
Let $(h, k)$ is centre of circle
$\left|\frac{h-k}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=|h|, k^2-h^2+2 h k=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For each parabola $y=x^2+p x+q$, meeting coordinate axes at 3-distinct points, if circles are drawn through these points, then the family of circles must pass through
questions.solution:
Suppose the parabola $y=x^2+p x+q$ cuts $X$-axis at $A(\alpha, 0)$ and $B(\beta, 0)$.
Then, $\alpha, \beta$ are roots of the equation $x^2+p x+q=0$
$$
\therefore \quad \alpha+\beta=-p \text { and } \alpha \beta=q
$$
The parabola $y=x^2+p x+q$ cuts $Y$-axis at $(0, q)$.
Let the equation of the circle passing through $A, B$ and $C$ be
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c  =0 &...(i)\\
\therefore & \alpha^2+2 g \alpha+c  =0 &...(ii)\\
& \beta^2+2 g \beta+c  =0 &...(iii)\\
\text { and } & q^2+2 f q+c  =0 &...(iv)
\end{aligned}
$$
Subtracting Eq. (iii) from Eq. (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl}
\alpha+\beta+2 g & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & g = p / 2
\end{array}
$$
Adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\alpha^2+\beta^2+2 g(\alpha+\beta)+2 c=0 \\
(\alpha+\beta)^2-2 \alpha \beta+2 g(\alpha+\beta)+2 c=0 \\
p^2-2 q-p^2+2 c=0
\end{array}
$$
$$
\left[\because \alpha+\beta=p \text { and } g=\frac{p}{2}\right]
$$
Putting $c=q$ in Eq. (iv), we get $f=-\left(\frac{q+1}{2}\right)$
Substituting the values of $g, f$ and $c$ in Eq. (i), we obtain the equation of family of circles passing through $A, B$ and $C$ as
$$
x^2+y^2+p x-(q+1) y+q=0
$$
Clearly, it passes through $(0,1)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the mid-point of the chord if contact of tangents drawn from points lying on the straight line $4 x-5 y=20$ to the circle $x^2+y^2=9$ is
questions.solution:
Let $P\left(t, \frac{4 t-20}{5}\right)$ be a point on the line
$4 x-5 y=20$. Then the chord of contact of tangents drawn from $P$ to the circle $x^2+y^2=9$ is
$t x+\left(\frac{4 t-20}{5}\right) y=9\quad \quad \dots(i)$
Let $\theta(h, k)$ be the mid-point of this chord of contact, then its equation is also $h x+k y=h^2+k^2\quad \quad \dots(ii)$
$\left[\right.$ using $\left.T=S^{\prime}\right]$
Clearly Eqs. (i) and (ii) represent the same line
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{t}{h}=\frac{4 t-20}{5 k}=\frac{9}{h^2+k^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{t}{h}=\frac{9}{h^2+k^2} \text { and } \frac{t}{h}=\frac{4 t-20}{5 k} \\
& \Rightarrow t=\frac{9 h}{h^2+k^2} \text { and } t=\frac{20 h}{4 h-5 k} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{9 h}{h^2+k^2}=\frac{20 h}{4 h-5 k} \\
& \Rightarrow  h\left\{20\left(h^2+k^2\right)-36 h+45 k\right\}=0 \\
& x=0, \text { or }\left[20\left(x^2+y^2\right)-36 x+45 y\right]=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the circle, which touches the line $y=5$ and passes through $(-1,2)$ and $(1,2)$ is
questions.solution: The centre of the circle is on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining $(-1,2)$ and $(1,2)$, which is the $y$-axis. The ordinate of the centre is given by $\begin{array}{l} (5-y)^{2}=1+(y-2)^{2} \\ \Rightarrow y=\frac{10}{3} \end{array}$ Hence, eq. of the circle is: $\begin{array}{l} x^{2}+\left(y-\frac{10}{3}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{2} \\ \Rightarrow 9 x^{2}+9 y^{2}-60 y+75=0 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a chord of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=8$ makes equal intercets of length a on the coordinate axes, then
questions.solution:
Since the chord makes equal intercepts of length a on the coordinate axes. So, its equation can be written as $\mathrm{x} \pm \mathrm{y}=\pm \mathrm{a}$. This line meets the given circle at two distinct points. So, length of the perpendicular from the centre (0,0) of the given circle must be less than the radius.

i.e. $\left|\pm \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{2}}\right|<\sqrt{8} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^{2}<16 \Rightarrow|\mathrm{a}|<4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the given figure, the equation of the larger circle is $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 y-5=0$ and the distance between centres is 4 . Then the equation of smaller circle is
questions.solution:
We have $x^{2}+y^{2}+4 y-5=0$. Its centre is $\mathrm{C}_{1}(0,-2)$

$\mathrm{r}_{1}=\sqrt{4+5}=3$. Let $\mathrm{C}_{2}(\mathrm{~h}, \mathrm{k})$ be the centre of the

smaller circle and its radius $\mathrm{r}_{2}$. Then $\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}=4$ $\Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{h}^{2}+(\mathrm{k}+2)^{2}}=3+\mathrm{r}_{2}=4$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{2}=1$

But $\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{r}_{2}=1 \quad$ [it touches $\mathrm{x}$ -axis

$\therefore$ From eq $(1), 4=\sqrt{\mathrm{h}^{2}+(1+2)^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow 16=\mathrm{h}^{2}+9 \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}^{2}=7 \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\pm \sqrt{7}$

Since $\mathrm{h}>0 \quad \therefore \mathrm{h}=\sqrt{7}$

Hence, required circle is $(x-\sqrt{7})^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the coordinates at one end of a diameter of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-8 x-4 y+c=0$ are (-3,2) , then the coordinates at the other end are
questions.solution:
The centre of the given circle is $\mathrm{C} \equiv(4,2)$ Let $A \equiv(-3,2)$
If $(\alpha, \beta)$ are the coordinates of the other end of the diameter, then, as the middle ploint of the diameter is the centre, $\therefore \quad \frac{\alpha-3}{2}=4$ and $\frac{\beta+2}{2}=2 \Rightarrow \alpha=11, \beta=2$ Thus, the coordinates of the other end of diameter are (11,2)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the triangle formed by joining the origin to the point of intersection of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> and circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Length of perpendicular from origin to the line x5+2y=35 is

OL=3552+22=359=5

Radius of the given circle =10=OQ=OP

PQ=2QL

=2OQ2-OL2

=210-5=25

Thus, area of ΔOPQ=12×PQ×OL

=12×25×5=5 sq units

Hence, option (c) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Radius of the largest circle which passes through the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and contained in it, is
questions.solution:
Let r be the radius of the largest circle passing through the focus 1,0 of y2=4x

Clearly, centre of the circle will be on X-axis and its coordinates are 1+r,0.

The equation of the circle, is

x-1-r2+y2=r2.

It touches y2=4x. Therefore, the equation x-r-12+4x=r2 must have equal roots.

∴ 41-r2-42r+1=0

⇒r=4

Hence, option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If two distinct chords drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> on the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>y</mi></math> (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mi>q</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math>) are bisected by the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis, then
questions.solution:
Let (t, m) be the other end of the chord drawn from the point (p, q) on the circle

x2+y2=px+qy

Their mid-point is t+p2,m+q2

since, mid-point lies on X-axis i.e. y=0

m+q=0   ...(i)

Also, (t, m) lies on the circle.

∴ t2+m2-pt-qm=0   ...(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get t2-pt+2q2=0

Which is quadratic in t such that, Discriminant >0

⇒ p2-8q2>0⇒p2>8q2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line joining (5,0) to $((10 \cos \theta, 10 \sin \theta)$ is divided internally in the ratio 2: 3 at $P$. If $\theta$ varies, then the locus of $\mathrm{P}$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of integral values of $\lambda$ for which $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\lambda \mathrm{x}+(1-\lambda) \mathrm{y}+5=0$ is the equation of a ircle whose radius cannot exceed $5,$ is
questions.solution:
Radius $\leq 5$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\sqrt{\frac{\lambda^{2}}{4}+\frac{(1-\lambda)^{2}}{4}-5} \leq 5 \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}+(1-\lambda)^{2}-20 \leq 100 \\

\Rightarrow 2 \lambda^{2}-2 \lambda-119 \leq 0 \\

\therefore \frac{1-\sqrt{239}}{2} \leq \lambda \leq \frac{1+\sqrt{239}}{2} \Rightarrow-7.2 \leq \lambda \leq 8.2 \\

\quad(\text { approx. }) \\

\therefore \lambda=-7,-6,-5, \ldots \ldots .7,8, \text { in all } 16

\end{array}

$$ values

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The lengths of the tangent drawn from any point

on the circle $15 x^{2}+15 y^{2}-48 x+64 y=0$ to the two circles $5 x^{2}+5 y^{2}-24 x+32 y+75=0$ and $5 x^{2}+5 y^{2}-48 x$ $+64 y+300=0$ are in the ratio of
questions.solution:
Let \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})\) be a point on the circle

\(15 x^{2}+15 y^{2}-48 x+64 y=0\)

Then the lengths of the tangents from \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})\) to

\(\begin{aligned} &5 x^{2}+5 y^{2}-24 x+32 y+75=0 \\ &5 x^{2}+5 y^{2}-48 x+64 y+300=0 \text { are } \\ &P T_{1}=\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}-\frac{24}{5} h+\frac{32}{5} k+15} \\ &\text { and } P T_{2}=\sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}-\frac{48}{5} h+\frac{64}{5} k+60} \\ &\text { or } P T_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{48}{15} h-\frac{64}{15} k-\frac{24}{5} h+\frac{32}{5} k+15}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{15} k-\frac{24}{15} h+15} \end{aligned}\)

(Since \((\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})\) lies on \(15 \mathrm{x}^{2}-15 \mathrm{y}^{2}-48 \mathrm{x}+64 \mathrm{y}=0\)

\(\begin{aligned} &\left.\therefore \mathrm{h}^{2}+\mathrm{k}^{2}-\frac{48}{15} \mathrm{~h}+\frac{64}{15} \mathrm{k}=0\right) \\ &\text { and } \mathrm{PT}_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{48}{15} \mathrm{~h}-\frac{64}{5} \mathrm{k}-\frac{48}{5} \mathrm{~h}+\frac{64}{5} \mathrm{k}+60} \\ &=\sqrt{-\frac{96}{15} \mathrm{~h}+\frac{128}{15} \mathrm{k}+60}=2 \sqrt{-\frac{24}{15} \mathrm{~h}+\frac{32}{15} \mathrm{k}+15}=2 \mathrm{PT}_{1} \\ &\Rightarrow \mathrm{PT}_{1}: \mathrm{PT}_{2}=1: 2 \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of the chord $x+y=3$ intercepted by the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y-2=0$ is
questions.solution: The centre of the circle is \(\mathrm{C}(1,1)\) and radius of the circle is 2 , perpendicular distance from \(\mathrm{C}\) on \(\mathrm{AB}\), the chord \(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=3\) \(\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{CD}=\left|\frac{1+1-3}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ &\Rightarrow \mathrm{AD}=\sqrt{4-\frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{7}{2}}\left[\mathrm{AD}=\sqrt{\mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{CD}^{2}}\right] \end{aligned}\) Hence, the length of the chord \(\mathrm{AB}=2 \mathrm{AD}=2 \sqrt{\frac{7}{2}}=\sqrt{14}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area of the circle in which a chord of length $\sqrt{2}$ makes an angle $\pi / 2$ at the centre, is
questions.solution: Let $\mathrm{AB}$ be the chord of length $\sqrt{2}$. Let $\mathrm{O}$ be the centre of the circle and let $O C$ be the perpendicular from $\mathrm{O}$ on $\mathrm{AB}$. Then $, \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ In $\Delta \mathrm{OBC},$ we have $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{OB} &=\mathrm{BC} \operatorname{cosec} 45^{\circ} \\ &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{2}=1 \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Area of the circle $=\pi(\mathrm{OB})^{2}=\pi$ sq units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle of intersection of the two circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}=4,$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A pair of tangents are drawn from the origin to the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+20(x+y)+20=0,$ then the equation of the pair of tangent are
questions.solution:
Equation of pair of tangents is given by $\mathrm{SS}_{1}=\mathrm{T}^{2}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\text { or } \quad \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+20(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})+20, \mathrm{~S}_{1}=20 \\

\mathrm{~T}=10(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})+20=0 \\

\therefore \quad \mathrm{SS}_{1}=\mathrm{T}^{2} \\

\Rightarrow \quad 20\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+20(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})+20\right)=10^{2} \\

\quad(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2)^{2} \\

\Rightarrow \quad 4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+4 \mathrm{y}^{2}+10 \mathrm{xy}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{y}^{2}+5 \mathrm{xy}=0

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of the tangent drawn from any point on the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}+2 f y+\lambda=0$ to the circle $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+2 \mathrm{fy}+\mu=0,$ where $\mu>\lambda>0,$ is
questions.solution:
Let the radius of the first circle be $\mathrm{CT}=\mathrm{r}_{1}$. Also, let the radius of the second circle be $\mathrm{CP}=\mathrm{r}_{2}$ In the triangle $\mathrm{PCT}, \mathrm{T}$ is a right angle
$\begin{aligned} \text { So, } P T &=\sqrt{P C^{2}-C T^{2}}=\sqrt{r_{1}^{2}-r_{2}^{2}} \\ &=\sqrt{\left(f^{2}-\lambda\right)-\left(f^{2}-\mu\right)}=\sqrt{\mu-\lambda} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the line $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p$ represents the common chord of the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$ and $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}(\mathrm{a}>\mathrm{b}),$ where $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ lie ont he first circle and $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ lie on the second circle, then AP is equal to
questions.solution: The given circles are concentric with centre at (0,0) and the length of the perpendicular from (0,0) on the given line is $\mathrm{p}$. Let $\mathrm{OL}=\mathrm{p}$ then, $\mathrm{AL}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OA}^{2}-\mathrm{OL}^{2}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^{2}-\mathrm{p}^{2}}$ and $\mathrm{PL}=\sqrt{\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{OL}^{2}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^{2}-\mathrm{p}^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{AP}=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^{2}-\mathrm{p}^{2}}-\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^{2}-\mathrm{p}^{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For what value of \(k\) the circles \(x^2+y^2+5 x+3 y+7=0\) and \(x^2+y^2-8 x+6 y+k=0\) cuts orthogonally
questions.solution:
Let the two circles be \(x^2+y^2+2 g_1 x+2 f_1 y\) \(+c_1=0\) and \(x^2+y^2+2 g_2 x+2 f_2 y+c_2=0\) where \(g_1=5 / 2, f_1=3 / 2, c_1=7, g_2=4, f_2=3\) and \(\mathrm{c}_2=\mathrm{k}\)
If the two circles intersects orthogonally, then
\(\begin{aligned}
& 2~\left(\mathrm{g}_1 \mathrm{~g}_2+\mathrm{f}_1 \mathrm{f}_2\right)=\mathrm{c}_1+\mathrm{c}_2 \Rightarrow 2\left(-10+\frac{9}{2}\right)=7+\mathrm{k} \\
& \Rightarrow 11=7+\mathrm{k} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=-18
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the lines \(3 x-4 y+4=0\) and \(6 x-8 y-7=0\) are tangents to a circle, then the radius of the circle is
questions.solution:
The diameter of the circle is perpendicular distance between the parallel lines (tangents) \(3 x-4 y+4=0\) and \(3 x-4 y-\frac{7}{2}=0\) and so it is equal to
\(\frac{4}{\sqrt{9+16}}+\frac{7 / 2}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{3}{2}\). Hence radius is \(\frac{3}{4}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line \(a x+b y=1\) cuts ellipse \(c x^2+d y^2=1\) only once if
questions.solution:
Clearly \(a x+b y=1\)
i.e \(y=-\frac{a}{b} x+\frac{1}{b}\) is tangent to
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{cx}^2+\mathrm{dy}^2=1 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\frac{1}{c}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{\frac{1}{d}}=1 \\
& \therefore\left(\frac{1}{b}\right)^2=\left(\frac{1}{c}\right)\left(-\frac{a}{b}\right)^2+\left(\frac{1}{d}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 1=\frac{a^2}{c}+\frac{b^2}{d}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If \(|r| > \) land \(x=a+\frac{a}{r}+\frac{a}{r^2}+\ldots\) to \(\infty\),
\(\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{b}-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{r}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{r}^2}-\ldots . \text { to } \infty\)
and \(z=c+\frac{c}{r^2}+\frac{c}{r^4}+\ldots\) to \(\infty\), then \(\frac{x y}{z}=\)
questions.solution:
Since \(|\mathrm{r}| > 1, \frac{1}{|\mathrm{r}|}  < 1\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}}=\frac{\mathrm{ar}}{\mathrm{r}-1}\)
Similarly, \(y=\frac{b}{1-\left(-\frac{1}{r}\right)}=\frac{b r}{r+1}\) and
\(\begin{gathered}
z=\frac{c}{1-\frac{1}{r^2}}=\frac{cr^2}{r^2-1} \quad ...(1)\\
\therefore \quad x y=\frac{a r}{r-1} \times \frac{b r}{r+1}=\frac{a b r^2}{r^2-1} \quad ...(2)
\end{gathered}\)
Dividing (2) by (1), we get
\(\frac{\mathrm{xy}}{\mathrm{z}}=\frac{\mathrm{abr}^2}{\mathrm{r}^2-1} \times \frac{\mathrm{r}^2-1}{\mathrm{cr}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{ab}}{\mathrm{c}}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points represented by the complex number $1+i,-2+3 i, \frac{5}{3} i$ on the argand plane are
questions.solution: According to question, Let find slope of $A B, B C$ and $C A$ Slope of $A B=\frac{1-3}{1-(-2)}=\frac{-2}{3}$ Slope of $B C=\frac{3-\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)}{-2-0}=\frac{-2}{3}$ Slope of $C A=\frac{\frac{5}{3}-1}{0-1}=\frac{-2}{3}$ Since, slope of all lines are same, therefore, points are collinear.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The modulus of the complex number $z$ such that $|z+3-i|=1$ and $\arg (z)=\pi$ is equal to
questions.solution: Let $z=x+i y$ Given, $\quad|z+3-i|=1$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad|(x+3)+(y-1) i|=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{(x+3)^2+(y-1)^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(x+3)^2+(y-1)^2=1\ldots(i) \end{aligned}$ Above is the equation of circle with centre $\equiv(-3,1)$ and $\text { radius }=1$ Also, $\arg (z)=\pi \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{y}{x}\right|=\pi$ $\Rightarrow \quad\left|\frac{y}{x}\right|=\tan \pi=0 \Rightarrow y=0$ By Eq. (i), $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & x & =-3 \\ \therefore & z & =-3+0 i \\ \Rightarrow & \text { Modulus of } z & =3 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z, \bar{z},-z,-\bar{z}$ forms a rectangle of area $2 \sqrt{3}$ square units, then one such $z$ is
questions.solution: Let a complex number, $z=x+i y$ $\Rightarrow \bar{z}=\bar{x}-i y$ Then, vertices of rectangle for $z, \bar{z},-z,-\bar{z}$ are $(x, y),(x,-y),(-x,-y),(-x, y)$ Now, Area of rectangle $=(2 x)(2 y)=4 x y$ It is given that, Area $=2 \sqrt{3}=4 x y \Rightarrow 2 x y=\sqrt{3}$ $\therefore x=\frac{1}{2}, y=\sqrt{3} \quad \therefore z=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{3} i$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_1, z_2, \ldots . . z_n$ are complex numbers such that $\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|=\ldots . .=\left|z_n\right|=1$, then $\left|z_1+z_2+\ldots . .+z_n\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_3\right|=\ldots \ldots .=\left|z_n\right|=1$
$\Rightarrow z_1 \bar{z}_1=z_2 \bar{z}_2=z_3 \bar{z}_3=\ldots \ldots=z_n \bar{z}_n=1$
$\overline{\mathrm{z}}_1=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}}, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_2=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_2}, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_3=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_3}, \ldots \ldots, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{n}}}$
Now, $\left|z_1+z_2+z_3+\ldots \ldots .+z_n\right|$
$=\left|\bar{z}_1+\bar{z}_2+\ldots \ldots+\bar{z}_n\right|$
$\left|\bar{z}_1+\bar{z}_2+\bar{z}_3+\ldots .+\bar{z}_n\right|=\left|\frac{1}{z_1}+\frac{1}{z_2}+\frac{1}{z_3}+\ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{z_n}\right|$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|z_1\right|=2,\left|z_2\right|=3,\left|z_3\right|=4$ and $\left|2 z_1+3 z_2+4 z_3\right|=$ 4 , then absolute value of $8 z_2 z_3+27 z_3 z_1+$ $64 z_1 z_2$ equals
questions.solution:
$\left|8 z_2 z_3+27 z_3 z_1+64 z_1 z_2\right|=\left|z_1\right|\left|z_2\right|\left|z_3\right|$
$\left|\frac{8}{z_1}+\frac{27}{z_2}+\frac{64}{z_3}\right|$
$=(2)(3)(4)\left|\frac{8 \bar{z}_1}{\left|z_1\right|^2}+\frac{27 \bar{z}_2}{\left|z_2\right|^2}+\frac{64 \bar{z}_3}{\left|z_3\right|^2}\right|$
$=24\left|2 \bar{z}_1+3 \bar{z}_2+4 \bar{z}_3\right|$
$=24\left|\overline{2 z_1+3 z_2+4 z_3}\right|$
$=24\left|2 z_1+3 z_2+4 z_3\right|=(24)(4)=96$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The modulus of $\frac{(1+i \sqrt{3})(2+2 i)}{(\sqrt{3}-i)}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{(1+i \sqrt{3})(2+2 i)}{\sqrt{3}-i}=\frac{2+2 \sqrt{3} i+2 i-2 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}-i} \\ & =\frac{(2-2 \sqrt{3})+(2 \sqrt{3}+2) i}{\sqrt{3}-i} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}+i}{\sqrt{3}+i} \\ & =\frac{2 \sqrt{3}-6+2 i-2 \sqrt{3} i+6 i+2 \sqrt{3} i-2 \sqrt{3}-2}{3+1} \\ & =\frac{8 i-8}{4}=-2+2 i \\ & \therefore \text { Modulus }=\sqrt{(-2)^2+(2)^2}=2 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $i^{57}+\frac{1}{i^{25}}$, when simplified has the value
questions.solution: $i^{57}+\frac{1}{i^{25}}=\left(i^4\right)^{14} . i+\frac{1}{\left(i^4\right)^6  .i}=i+\frac{1}{i}=i-i=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|w|=2$, then the set of points $z=w-\frac{1}{w}$ is contained in or equal to the set of points $z$ satisfying
questions.solution:
We have, $|w|=2 \Rightarrow w=2(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta)$
$\therefore \quad z=w-\frac{1}{w}$
$=2(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta)-\frac{1}{2}(\cos \theta-i \sin \theta)$
$\Rightarrow \quad z=\frac{3}{2} \cos \theta+\frac{5}{2} i \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad|z|=\sqrt{\frac{9}{4}+\frac{25}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{17}}{2} < 3$
Hence, option (d) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The smallest positive integral value of $n$ such that 
$\left[\frac{1+\sin \frac{\pi}{8}+i \cos \frac{\pi}{8}}{1+\sin \frac{\pi}{8}-i \cos \frac{\pi}{8}}\right]^n$ is purely imaginary, is equal to
questions.solution:
$\left[\frac{1+\sin \frac{\pi}{8}+i \cos \frac{\pi}{8}}{1+\sin \frac{\pi}{8}-i \cos \frac{\pi}{8}}\right]^n=\left[\frac{1+\cos \alpha+i \sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha-i \sin \alpha}\right]^n$
$\left[\because \alpha=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{8}\right]$
$=\left[\frac{2 \cos ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}+2 i \sin \frac{\alpha}{2} \cos \frac{\alpha}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}-2 i \sin \frac{\alpha}{2} \cos \frac{\alpha}{2}}\right]^n$
$=\left[\frac{\cos \frac{\alpha}{2}+i \sin \frac{\alpha}{2}}{\cos \frac{\alpha}{2}-i \sin \frac{\alpha}{2}}\right]_{-}^n=\left(e^{2 i \frac{\alpha}{2}}\right]^n=e^{i n \alpha}$
$=e^{i n\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}\right)}=\cos \frac{3 n \pi}{8}+i \sin \frac{3 n \pi}{8}$
For $n=4$ we get imaginary part.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z$ is a complex number such that $z^{2}=(\bar{z})^{2}$, then
options: either $z$ is purely real or purely imaginary
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$, then its conjugate $\bar{z}=x-i y$

Given that $z^{2}=(\bar{z})^{2}$

$\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow x^{2}-y^{2}+2 i x y=x^{2}-y^{2}-2 i x y \\

\Rightarrow 4 i x y=0

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\text { If } z_{1}=\sqrt{2}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \text { and } z_{2}=\sqrt{3}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right]$ then $\left|z_{1} z_{2}\right|$ is equal to $\sqrt{m}$. Value of $m$ is
questions.solution:
$z_{1}=\sqrt{2}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\sqrt{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]=1+i$

$\left|z_{1}\right|=\sqrt{2}$

and $z_{2}=\sqrt{3}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right]=\sqrt{3}\left[\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$

$\left|z_{2}\right|=\sqrt{\frac{3}{4}+\frac{9}{4}}=\sqrt{3}$

$\left|z_{1} z_{2}\right|=\left|z_{1}\right|\left|z_{2}\right|=\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3}=6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\left|z_{1}\right|=\left|z_{2}\right|=\ldots \ldots \ldots . .\left|z_{n}\right|=1,$ then the

value of $\left|z_{1}+z_{2}+\ldots \ldots . z_{n}\right|-$

$\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\ldots \ldots .+\frac{1}{z_{n}}\right|$ is,
questions.solution:
$z_{1} \bar{z}_{1}=z_{2} \bar{z}_{2}=\ldots .=z_{n} \bar{z}_{n}=1$

$\Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{z}}_{1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_{1}}, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_{2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_{2}}, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_{3}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_{3}}, \ldots \ldots, \overline{\mathrm{z}}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{n}}}$

$\therefore\left|z_{1}+z_{2}+\ldots .+z_{n}\right|-\left|\frac{1}{z_{1}}+\frac{1}{z_{2}}+\ldots .+\frac{1}{z_{n}}\right|$

$=\left|z_{1}+z_{2}+\ldots+z_{n}\right|-\left|\bar{z}_{1}+\bar{z}_{2}+\ldots .+\bar{z}_{n}\right|=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> for which the loci <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>arg</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>i</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>λ</mi></math> on the argand plane touch each other is
questions.solution:
z=x+iy

argz=tan-1yx

argz=π6

∴ tan-1yx=π6

yx=tanπ6=13

⇒ x=3y

Also given, 

|z-23i|=λ

|x+iy-23i|=λ

x2+y-232=λ2

This is the equation of circle whose centre x-3y=0 is (0,23) and r=λ. Now, the line touches the circle

∴ λ=0-3231+3

λ=62=3

∴ λ=3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>z</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> represents adjacent vertices of a regular polygon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> sides and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>I</mi><mi>m</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
Let z1=reiθ

then, z¯1=re-iθ

z1z¯1=e2iθ=e2πi/n

⇒ θ=πn

Also, Imz1Rez1=tanθ=tanπn=2-1

⇒ tanπ8=2-1

∴ n=8

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> roots of unity are in
questions.solution:
Let x=1

=cos0°+isin0°

Then, the roots are 1, e2πin, e4πin,… which are clearly on GP with common ratio e2πi/n

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi><mi>b</mi></math> satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>arg</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>arg</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>i</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>:</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
questions.solution:
We have,

arg(z-1)=arg(z+3i)

⇒arg((a-1)+ib)=arg(a+(b+3)i)

⇒tan-1ba-1=tan-1b+3a

⇒ ba-1=b+3a

⇒ ab=(a-1)(b+3)

⇒ 3(a-1)=b

⇒ (a-1):b=1:3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> represent the vertices of an equilateral triangle such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced></math> then
questions.solution:
Since, z1=z2=z3.

⇒z1-0=z2-0=z3-0.

Thus, the given points are equidistant from origin, and we know that the point which is equidistant from the vertices of a triangle is the circumcentre of the triangle.

∴ 0 is the circumcentre of an equilateral ∆ABC and we also know that for an equilateral triangle the centroid and circumcentre are same.

∴ x1+x2+x33=0=y1+y2+y33

where, z1=x1+iy1.

⇒x1+x2+x33+iy1+y2+y33=0

⇒x1+iy1+x2+iy2+x3+iy3=0

⇒z1+z2+z3=0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $z_{1}=\sqrt{3}+i \sqrt{3}$ and $z_{2}=\sqrt{3}+i$, then the

complex number $\left(\frac{\mathrm{z}_{1}}{\mathrm{z}_{2}}\right)^{50}$ lies in the :
questions.solution:
$\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right)^{50}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+i}\right)^{50}$

$=\left[\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}(1+i)}{\sqrt{3}+i}\right)^{2}\right]^{25}=\left[\frac{3(2 i)}{3-1+2 \sqrt{3} i}\right]^{25}$

$=\left(\frac{3 i}{1+\sqrt{3} i}\right)^{25}=\frac{3^{25} i^{25}}{\left(-2 \omega^{2}\right)^{25}}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

=-i . \omega\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{25}=-\mathrm{i}\left(\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{i}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{25} \\

=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{25}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{i}\right)

\end{array}

$$

Hence, $\left(\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\right)^{50}$ lies in the first quadrant as both real and imaginary parts of this number are positive.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The root of the equation

$2(1+i) x^{2}-4(2-i) x-5-3 i=0$ which has

greater modulus is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Roots } &=\frac{4(2-i) \pm \sqrt{16(2-i)^{2}+8(1+i)(5+3 i)}}{4(1+i)} \\ &=\frac{4-i}{1+i} \text { or } \frac{-i}{1+i}=\frac{3-5 i}{2} \text { or } \frac{-1-i}{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If complex number $z_{1}, z_{2}$ and 0 are vertices of equilateral triangle, then $z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}-z_{1} z_{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$z_{1}, z_{2}, 0$ are vertices of an equilateral triangle, so we have $z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}+0^{2}=z_{1} z_{2}+z_{2} \cdot 0+0 \cdot z_{1}$

$$

\Rightarrow z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}=z_{1} z_{2} \Rightarrow z_{1}^{2}+z_{2}^{2}-z_{1} z_{2}=0

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $z=x+i y, z^{1 / 3}=a-i b,$ then $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=k\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)$

where $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$z^{1 / 3}=a-i b \Rightarrow z=(a-i b)^{3}$

$\therefore x+i y=a^{3}+i b^{3}-3 i a^{2} b-3 a b^{2}$

$\Rightarrow x=a^{3}-3 a b^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{x}{a}=a^{2}-3 b^{2}$

and $y=b^{3}-3 a^{2} b \Rightarrow \frac{y}{b}=b^{2}-3 a^{2}$

So, $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=4\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $i^{57}+\frac{1}{i^{25}},$ when simplified has the value
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} i^{57}+\frac{1}{i^{25}}=\left(i^{4}\right)^{14} \cdot i &+\frac{1}{\left(i^{4}\right)^{6} \cdot i} \\=i+\frac{1}{i}\left(\because i^{4}=1\right) &=i-i\left(\because \frac{1}{i}=-i\right) \\ &=0 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The complex number $z=z+i y$ which satisfies

the equation $\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1,$ lies on
questions.solution:
$\left|\frac{z-3 i}{z+3 i}\right|=1 \Rightarrow|z-3 i|=|z+3 i|$

[if $\left|z-z_{1}\right|=\left|z+z_{2}\right|,$ then it is a perpendicular bisector of $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ ] Hence, perpendicular bisector of (0,3) and (0,-3) is $X$ -axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the real part of $\frac{\bar{z}+2}{\bar{z}-1}$ is $4, z \neq 1,$ then the locus of the point representing $z$ in the complex plane is
options: a straight line equally inclined to axes
questions.solution:
Real part of $\frac{\bar{z}+2}{\bar{z}-1}$ is given by

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\bar{z}+2}{\bar{z}-1}+\left(\frac{\bar{z}+2}{\bar{z}-1}\right)\right]=4 \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\bar{z}+2}{\bar{z}-1}+\frac{z+2}{z-1}=8 \\

\Rightarrow z \bar{z}-\bar{z}+2 z-2+z \bar{z}+2 \bar{z}-z-2 \\

=8(z \bar{z}-\bar{z}-z+1) \\

\Rightarrow z \bar{z}-\frac{3}{2} z-\frac{3}{2} \bar{z}+2=0

\end{array}

$$

Comparing with the equation $z \bar{z}+\bar{a} z+a \bar{z}+b=0,$ we get $a=-\frac{3}{2}$ and $b$

$=2$. Thus, the locus of $z$ given by the equation

(i) is a circle with centre $=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of $x^{2}-x+1=0,$ then the equation whose roots are $\alpha^{100}$ and $\beta^{100}$ are
questions.solution:
We have $x^{3}+1 \equiv(x+1)\left(x^{2}-x+1\right)$. Therefore, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the complex cube roots of -1 so that we may take $\alpha=-\omega$ and $\beta=-\omega^{2},$ where $\omega \neq 1$ is a cube root of unity.

Thus $\alpha^{100}=(-\omega)^{100}=\omega$ and

$\beta^{100}=\left(-\omega^{2}\right)^{100}=\omega^{2},$ so that the required

equation is $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}+1=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The amplitude of $\sin \frac{\pi}{5}+i\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{5}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\sin \frac{\pi}{5}+\mathrm{i}\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{5}\right)$
$=2 \sin \frac{\pi}{10} \cos \frac{\pi}{10}+\mathrm{i} 2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{10}$
$=2 \sin \frac{\pi}{10}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{10}+\mathrm{i} \sin \frac{\pi}{10}\right)$
For amplitude, $\tan \theta=\frac{\pi}{\sin \frac{\pi}{10}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{10}}=\tan \frac{\pi}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{10}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=\omega-\omega^{2}-2,$ then the value of $x^{4}+3 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-11 x-6$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $x=\omega-\omega^{2}-2$ or $x+2=\omega-\omega^{2}$
Squaring, $x^{2}+4 x+4=\omega^{2}+\omega^{4}-2 \omega^{3}$
$=\omega^{2}+\omega^{3} \omega \cdot-2 \omega^{3}=\omega^{2}+\omega-2 \quad\left[\omega^{3}=1\right]$
$=-1-2=-3 \Rightarrow$
$x^{2}+4 x+7=0$
Dividing $x^{4}+3 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-11 x-6$ by $x^{2}+4 x+7$
we get $x^{4}+3 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-11 x-6=\left(x^{2}+4 x+7\right)\left(x^{2}-x-1\right)+1$
$=(0)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}-1\right)+1=0+1=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(a > 0, a \in R, z=a+2 i\) and \(z|z|-a z+1=0\) then
options: \(z\) is always a positive real number
\(z\) is always a negative real number
such a complex z does not exist
questions.solution: Putting \(z=a+2 i\) in the given equation and comparing imaginary parts, we get \(\mathrm{a}^2+4=\mathrm{a}^2\), which is not possible.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The roots of the equation \(x^2-2 \sqrt{2} x+1=0\) are
questions.solution:
The discriminant of the equation \((-2 \sqrt{2})^2-4(1)(1)=8-4=4 > 0\) and \(a\) perfect square, so roots are real and different but we can't say that roots are rational because coefficients are not rational therefore.
\(\frac{\sqrt{2 \sqrt{2} \pm \sqrt{(2 \sqrt{2})^2-4}}}{2}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \pm 2}{2}=\sqrt{2} \pm 1\)
this is irrational \(\therefore\) the roots are real and different.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the equation \(\frac{1}{x+a}-\frac{1}{x+b}=\frac{1}{x+c}\), if the product of roots is zero, then the sum of roots is
questions.solution:
\(\frac{1}{x+a}-\frac{1}{x+b}=\frac{1}{x+c}\)
or \(x^2+(a+b) x+a b=(b-a) x+(b-a) c\) or \(\mathrm{x}^2+2 \mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{ab}+\mathrm{ca}-\mathrm{bc}=0\) Since product of the roots \(=0\)
\(a b+c a-b c=0 ; a=\frac{b c}{b+c}\)
Thus sum of roots \(=-2 a=\frac{-2 b c}{b+c}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f$ be the function defined by
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
\frac{x^2-1}{x^2-2|x-1|-1}, & x \neq 1 \\
\frac{1}{2}, & x=1
\end{array}\right.$
options: The function is continuous for all values of $x$
The function is continuous only for $x\gt1$
The function is not continuous at $x=1$
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{x^2-1}{x^2-2 x+1} ; & x\gt1 \\ \frac{1}{2} \quad ; & x=1 \\ \frac{x^2-1}{x^2+2 x-3} ; & x \lt 1\end{array}\right.$
$\because \quad x^2+3 x-x-3$
$=x(x+3)-1(x+3)$
Lets Find
LHL, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{x^2-1}{x^2-2 x+1}$ and RHL, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{x^2-1}{x^2+2 x-3}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{(x-1)^2} \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{(x+3)(x-1)} \\
& \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{x+1}{x-1}=\infty \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{x+1}{x+3}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ LHL $\neq f(1) \Rightarrow f(x)$ is not continuous at $x=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{x^2 \log (\cos x)}{\log (1+x)}, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{array}\right.$, then at $x=0, f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x^2 \log (\cos x)}{\log (1+x)}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}-\frac{x \cdot \log (\cos x)}{\frac{\log (1+x)}{x}}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x \cdot \log (\cos x)=0 \cdot \log 1=0$
$\because \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0)$
$\therefore f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$
Now, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$
$\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(h)-f(0)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^2 \log \cos h-0}{\log (1+h)}$
$\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^2 \log (\cos h)}{1}=0$
$\therefore f(x)$ is differentiable at $x=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}4 & -\infty \lt x \lt -\sqrt{5} \\ x^2-1 & -\sqrt{5} \leq x \leq \sqrt{5} \\ 4 & \sqrt{5} \leq x \lt \infty\end{array}\right.$
If $k$ is the number of points where $f(x)$ is not differentiable then $k-2=$
questions.solution:
Here $f(x)$ is continous $\forall x \in \mathbf{R}$
Now At $x=-\sqrt{5}$
L.H.D = 0
R.H.D $=2 x=-2 \sqrt{5}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is not differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=-\sqrt{5}$
At $x=\sqrt{5}$
L.H.D. $=2 x=2 \sqrt{5}$
R.H.D. $=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is not differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=\sqrt{5}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=2$
$k-2=2-2=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{f}$ be the function defined by $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}-2|x-1|-1}, & x \neq 1 \\ 1 / 2, & x=1\end{array}\right.$
options: The function is continuous for all values of x
The function is continuous only for $x>1$
The function is not continuous at $x=1$
questions.solution:
For $x<1, f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+2 x-3}=\frac{x+1}{x+3}$

$\therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{2}$

For $x>1, f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}-2 x+1}=\frac{x+1}{x-1}$

$\therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\infty$

$\therefore$ The function is not continuous at $\mathrm{x}=1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of points at which the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\log |\mathrm{x}|}$ is discontinuous is:
questions.solution:
The function $\log |x|$ is not defined at $x=0$, so, $\mathrm{x}=0$ is a point of discontinuity. Also for $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ to be defined, $\log |\mathrm{x}| \neq 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \neq\pm 1$

Hence, 0,1,-1 are three points of discontinuity.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\frac{x \log \cos x}{\log \left(1+x^{2}\right)} & , x \neq 0 \\ 0 & , x=0\end{array}\right.$ then $f(x)$ is
options: continuous as well as differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=0$
questions.solution:
We have,

$$

\begin{aligned}

\mathrm{Lf}^{\prime}(0) &=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{f}(0-\mathrm{h})-\mathrm{f}(0)}{-\mathrm{h}}=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\mathrm{h} \log \cosh }{-\mathrm{h} \log \left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)} \\

=& \lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log \cosh }{\log \left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)}\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right)

\end{aligned}

$$

$\begin{aligned} &=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\tan \mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~h} /\left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)}=-1 / 2 \\ \mathrm{Rf}^{\prime}(0) &=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{f}(0+\mathrm{h})-\mathrm{f}(0)}{\mathrm{h}}=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{h} \log \cosh }{\mathrm{h} \log \left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)} \\ &=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log \cos \mathrm{h}}{\log \left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)}\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \\ &=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\tan \mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~h} /\left(1+\mathrm{h}^{2}\right)}=\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned}$

Since $\mathrm{Lf}^{\prime}(0)=\operatorname{Rf}^{\prime}(0),$ therefore $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is dif-

ferentiable at $x=0$

Since differentiability $\Rightarrow$ continuity, there-fore $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If a function $f(x)$ is given by

$f(x)=\frac{x}{1+x}+\frac{x}{(x+1)(2 x+1)}$

$+\frac{x}{(2 x+1)(3 x+1)}+\ldots, \infty$, then at $x=0, f(x)$
questions.solution:
Let

$f(x)=\frac{x}{1+x}+\frac{x}{(x+1)(2 x+1)}+\frac{x}{(2 x+1)(3 x+1)}+\ldots \infty$

$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=1}^{n} \frac{x}{[(r-1) x+1](r x+1)}$

$=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{[(\mathrm{r}-1) \mathrm{x}+1]}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{rx}+1}\right]$

$=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[1-\frac{1}{n x+1}\right]=1$

For $x=0,$ we have $f(x)=0$

Thus, we have $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}1, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{array}\right.$

Clearly, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0^{+}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \neq \mathrm{f}(0)$

So, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is not continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be a function such that $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y), \forall x, y \in \mathbb{R}$

If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=0$, then which one of the following is incorrect?
options: $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is differentiable only in a finite interval containing zera.
questions.solution:
Let $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y), \forall x, y \in R$

Put $x=0=y$

$\Rightarrow f(0)=f(0)+f(0)$

$\Rightarrow f(0)=0$

Now, $f^{\prime}(0)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}$

$f^{\prime \prime}(0)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(h)}{h}$

Now, $f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x+h)-f(x)}{h}$

$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x)+f(h)-f(x)}{h}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{h})}{\mathrm{h}}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0) \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{f}(0)+\mathrm{C}

\end{array}

$$

But $f(0)=0$

$$

\therefore \mathrm{C}=0

$$

Hence, $f(x)=x f(0), \forall x \in R$

Clearly, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is everywhere continuous and differentiable and $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$ is constant.

$\forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c}(x-1) \sin \frac{1}{x-1} \text { if } x \neq 1 \\ 0 \quad \text { if } x=1\end{array}\right.$

Then which one of the following is true?
options: $f$ is differentiable at $x=0$ but not at $x=1$
$f$ is differentiable at $x=1$ but not at $x=0$
$f$ is neither differentiable at $x=0$ nor at $x=1$
questions.solution:
We have; $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}(x-1) \sin \left(\frac{1}{x-1}\right) & \text { if } x \neq 1 \\ 0 & \text { if } x=1\end{array}\right.$

$R f^{\prime}(1)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}$

$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h \sin \frac{1}{h}-0}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \sin \frac{1}{h}$

which does not exist.

$\therefore f$ is not differentiable at $x=1$ Also $\left.f^{\prime}(0)=\sin \frac{1}{(x-1)}-\frac{x-1}{(x-1)^{2}} \cos \left(\frac{1}{x-1}\right)\right]_{x=0}$

$=-\sin 1+\cos 1$

$\therefore f$ is differentiable at $x=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{l}\frac{1-\sin ^{3} \mathrm{x}}{3 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{x}<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \mathrm{p}, \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{\mathrm{q}(1-\sin \mathrm{x})}{(\pi-2 \mathrm{x})^{2}}, \mathrm{x}>\frac{\pi}{2}\end{array}\right.$

If $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=\frac{\pi}{2},(p, q)=$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{f}\left[(\pi / 2)^{-}\right]=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\sin ^{3}[(\pi / 2)-\mathrm{h}]}{3 \cos ^{2}[(\pi / 2)-\mathrm{h}]}$

$\quad=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos ^{3} \mathrm{~h}}{3 \sin ^{2} \mathrm{~h}}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\mathrm{f}\left[(\pi / 2)^{+}\right]=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{q}[1-\sin \{(\pi / 2)+\mathrm{h}\}]}{[\pi-2\{(\pi / 2)+\mathrm{h}\}]^{2}}$

$=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{q}(1-\cosh )}{4 \mathrm{~h}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{8}$

$\therefore \mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{8} \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{q}=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the function $f(x)=\begin{cases}A x-B, & x \leq 1 \\ 3 x & , 1<x<2 \\ B x^{2}-A, & x \geq 2\end{cases}$
is continuous at $x=2$ and $4,$ then the values of a and $b$ are
questions.solution:
Since $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=2$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}(2)=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2^{+}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \Rightarrow 1=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2^{+}}(\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b})$
$\therefore 1=2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$
Again $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=4$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}(4)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 4^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \Rightarrow 7=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 4^{-}}(\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b})$
$\therefore 7=4 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$
Solving (1) and $(2),$ we get $a=3, b=-5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=(\mathrm{x}-1) \sqrt{|\ell n \mathrm{x}|}\) is at \(\mathrm{x}=1\)
questions.solution: Continuous as well as differentiable, so \( f^{\prime}(1)=0\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of \(\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & 3 \\ -4 & 3 & 6 \\ 2 & -7 & 9\end{array}\right|\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned} & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & 3 \\ -4 & 3 & 6 \\ 2 & -7 & 9\end{array}\right|=1\left|\begin{array}{cc}3 & 6 \\ -7 & 9\end{array}\right|-2\left|\begin{array}{cc}-4 & 6 \\ 2 & 9\end{array}\right|+3 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{cc}-4 & 3 \\ 2 & -7\end{array}\right| \\ & \end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =1(3 \times 9-6(-7))-2(-4 \times 9-2 \times 6)+3 \\
& ((-4)(-7)-3 \times 2) \\
& =(27+42)-2(-36-12)+3(28-6)=231
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(f(x)=\frac{\left(e^x-1\right)^2}{\sin \left(\frac{x}{a}\right) \log \left(1+\frac{x}{4}\right)}\) for \(x \neq 0\), and \(f(0)=12\). If \(\mathrm{f}\) is continuous at \(x=0\), then the value of \(a\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(e^x-1\right)^2}{\sin \left(\frac{x}{a}\right) \log \left(1+\frac{x}{4}\right)} \\
&=\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{\left(e^x-1\right)^2}{x} \cdot x^2}{\frac{x}{a} \cdot \frac{\sin \left(\frac{x}{a}\right)}{\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)} \cdot \frac{\log \left(1+\frac{x}{4}\right)}{\frac{x}{4}} \cdot \frac{x}{4}} \Rightarrow 4 a=12 \\
& \Rightarrow a=3
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{d x}{\left(x^2+a^2\right)\left(x^2+b^2\right)}$ is
questions.solution: Let $\begin{aligned} I & =\int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{\left(x^2+a^2\right)\left(x^2+b^2\right)} d x \\ I & =\frac{1}{a^2-b^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\left(x^2+a^2\right)-\left(x^2+b^2\right)}{\left(x^2+a^2\right)\left(x^2+b^2\right)} d x \\ I & =\frac{1}{a^2-b^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{1}{\left(x^2+b^2\right)}-\frac{1}{\left(x^2+a^2\right)} d x \\ I & =\frac{1}{a^2-b^2}\left[\frac{1}{b} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{b}-\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}\right]_0^{\infty} \\ I & =\frac{1}{a^2-b^2}\left[\frac{1}{b} \times \frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{a} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right] \\ I & =\frac{1}{(a+b)(a-b)}\left[\frac{a-b}{a b}\right] \times \frac{\pi}{2} \\ I & =\frac{\pi}{2 a b(a+b)}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of definite integral $\int_0^{\pi / 2} \log (\tan x) d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\tan x) d x$....(i)
By applying property,
$\begin{aligned}
\int_a^b f(x) d x & =\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x \\
I & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)\right) d x \\
I & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\cot x) d x\ldots(ii)
\end{aligned}$
Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}[\log (\tan x)+\log (\cot x)] d x \\
& 2 I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log 1 d x=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3 x^2}{\log 3 x^2+\log \left(588-84 x+3 x^2\right)} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let
$I=\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3 x^2 d x}{\log 3 x^2+\log \left(588-84 x+3 x^2\right)}$  ...(i)
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3 x^2 d x}{\log 3 x^2+\log 3(14-x)^2} \\ & =\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3(14-x)^2 d x}{\log 3(14-x)^2+\log 3(14-(14-x))^2} \\ & \quad\left[\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x\right]\end{aligned}$
$I=\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3(14-x)^2 d x}{\log 3(14-x)^2+\log 3 x^2}$  ...(ii)
Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 I=\int_5^9 \frac{\log 3 x^2+\log 3(14-x)^2}{\log 3(14-x)^2+\log 3 x^2} d x \\
& 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_5^9 d x=9-5=4 \Rightarrow I=2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integral $\int \frac{x^2\left(x \sec ^2 x+\tan x\right)}{(x \tan x+1)^2} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
(c) We note that
$\frac{d}{d x}(x \tan x+1)=x \sec ^2 x+\tan x$
$\therefore$ integrating by parts with $x^2$ as first function, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& I=\int x^2 \frac{x \sec ^2 x+\tan x}{(x \tan x+1)^2} d x \\
& =x^2\left(-\frac{1}{x \tan x+1}\right)-\int 2 x\left(-\frac{1}{x \tan x+1}\right) d x \\
& =-\frac{x^2}{x \tan x+1}+2 \int \frac{x \cos x}{x \sin x+\cos x} d x \\
& =-\frac{x^2}{x \tan x+1}+2 \log _e|x \sin x+\cos x|+c \\
& \quad\left(\because \frac{d}{d x}(x \sin x+\cos x)=x \cos x\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{r=3}^{\infty} \frac{r^3-8}{r^3+8}$ equals to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{r=3}^{\infty} \frac{r^3-8}{r^3+8}=\left(\frac{3^3-8}{3^3+8}\right)\left(\frac{4^3-8}{4^3+8}\right) \\ & \text {......... }\left(\frac{n^3-8}{n^3+8}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{r}=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{(3-2)\left(3^2+2^2+3.2\right)}{(3+2)\left(3^2+2^2-3.2\right)}\right]\left[\frac{(4-2)\left(4^2+2+4.2\right)}{(4+2)\left(4^2+2^2-4.2\right)}\right] \\ \ldots \ldots \ldots . .\left[\frac{(n-2)\left(n^2+2^n+n .2\right)}{(n+2)\left(n^2+2^2-n .2\right)}\right]\end{array}$
$\begin{gathered}\quad=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{(3-2)(4-2) \ldots \ldots \ldots(n-2)}{(3+2)(4+2) \ldots \ldots \ldots(n+2)}\right] \\ {\left[\frac{\left(3^2+2^2+3.2\right)\left(4^2+2^2+4.2\right) \ldots \ldots .\left(n^2+2^2+n .2\right)}{\left(3^2+2^2-3.2\right)\left(4^2+2^2-4.2\right) \ldots \ldots . .\left(n^2+2^2-n .2\right)}\right]}\end{gathered}$
$=\left[\frac{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3.4 .5 .6 \ldots \ldots \ldots .}{5 \cdot 6.7 .8 \ldots \ldots \ldots . .}\right]\left[\frac{19 \cdot 28 \cdot 39.52 .63 \ldots \ldots \ldots . .}{7 \cdot 12 \cdot 19.28 .39 .52 \ldots \ldots . .}\right]$
$=\frac{2}{7}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)+\sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}+x\right)}{\cos x+\sin x} d x=$
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)+\sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}+x\right)}{\cos x+\sin x} d x$
$\Rightarrow I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}-x\right)+\sin \left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}-x\right)}{\sin x+\cos x} d x$
$\Rightarrow I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)-\sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}+x\right)}{\cos x+\sin x} d x$
Now $I+I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)}{\cos x+\sin x} d x$
$\Rightarrow I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x}{\cos x-\sin x} d x=\frac{\pi}{2 \sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\pi / 4} \cos x e^{\sin x} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Suppose, $I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \cos x e^{\sin x} d x$
Let $\sin x=t \Rightarrow \cos x d x=d t$
$x \rightarrow 0 \Rightarrow t \rightarrow 0$
and $x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow t \rightarrow 1$
So $I=\int_0^1 e^t d t=\left[e^t\right]_0^1=e-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{f}(x)$ is an even function then

$\int_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=2 \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x$

Here $\mathrm{f}(x)=x^{8}-x^{4}+x^{2}+1$ is an even function, therefore $a=4$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Evaluate: $\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{5+4 \cos x} d x$
questions.solution:
We have, $\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{5+4 \cos x} d x$

$=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1}{5+4\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}\right)} d x$

$=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{5\left(1+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}\right)+4\left(1-\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}\right)} d x$

$=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{9+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}} \mathrm{dx}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{9+\tan ^{2} \frac{x}{2}} \mathrm{dx}$

Let $\tan \frac{x}{2}=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{x}{2} d x=d t$

Also, $x=0 \Rightarrow t=0$ and $x=\pi \Rightarrow t=\infty$

$\therefore \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{9+t^{2}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{I}=2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{9+t^{2}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{I}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} \frac{t}{3}\right]_{0}^{\infty}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\tan ^{-1} \infty-\tan ^{-1} 0\right]$

$=\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2}\right)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}\right)}$ is
questions.solution:
$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)\left(x^{2}+b^{2}\right)}=\frac{1}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$

$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left(x^{2}+b^{2}\right)-\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)\left(x^{2}+b^{2}\right)}$

$=\frac{1}{b^{2}-a^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty}\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}+a^{2}}-\frac{1}{x^{2}+b^{2}}\right] d x$

$=\frac{1}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\left[\frac{1}{a} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}-\frac{1}{b} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{b}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$

$=\frac{1}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\left[\frac{\pi}{2 a}-\frac{\pi}{2 b}\right]=\frac{\pi}{2 a b(a+b)}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} x \sin (\pi[x]-x) \mathrm{dx}$ is equal to
questions.solution: In the interval $\frac{\pi}{3}$ to $\frac{\pi}{2},[\mathrm{x}]=1$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{I} &=\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \mathrm{x} \sin (\pi-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \mathrm{x} \sin \mathrm{x} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x} \\ &=[-\mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}+\sin \mathrm{x}]_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2}=1-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Elvaluate $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x$
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin \mathrm{x}}{1+\cos ^{2} \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}$

Let $\cos \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}$ and $-\sin \mathrm{x} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{dt}$

Now, $\mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\cos 0=1$ and

$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_{1}^{0} \frac{\sin \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{t}^{2}}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\sin \mathrm{x}}\right)=-\int_{1}^{0} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^{2}}$

$=\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{1}^{0}=-\left[0-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{2^{\sin x}}{2^{\sin x}+2^{\cos x}} d x$ equals
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{2^{\sin \mathrm{x}}}{2^{\sin x}+2^{\cos \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}$
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{2^{\sin (\pi / 2-\mathrm{x})}}{2^{\sin (\pi / 2-\mathrm{x})}+2^{\cos (\pi / 2-\mathrm{x})} \mathrm{dx}}$
$=\int \frac{2^{\cos \mathrm{x}}}{2^{\cos \mathrm{x}}+2^{\sin \mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx} \Rightarrow 21=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow 1=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the value of \(\int_0^{4 \pi}|\sin x| d x\)
questions.solution:
We know that \(|\sin x|\) is a periodic function of \(\pi\)
\(\text { Hence } \begin{array}{r}
\int_0^{4 \pi}|\sin x| d x=4 \int_0^\pi|\sin x| d x=4 \int_0^\pi \sin x d x \\
=4[-\cos x]_0^\pi=8
\end{array}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\int 4 \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \cos 2 x \cdot \cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}+x\right) d x\)
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \int 4 \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \cos 2 x \cdot \cos \left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}+x\right) d x \\
& =2 \int\left(\cos (2 x+\pi) \cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cos 2 x d x \\
& =2 \int\left(-\cos 2 x-\frac{1}{2}\right) \cos 2 x d x \\
& =\int\left(-2 \cos ^2 2 x-\cos 2 x\right) d x \\
& =-\int(1+\cos 4 x+\cos 2 x) d x \\
& =-x-\frac{\sin 4 x}{x}-\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+c
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Evaluate: \(\int x \tan ^{-1} x d x\)
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \int x \tan ^{-1} x d x=\frac{x^2}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{x^2}{1+x^2} d x \\
& =\frac{x^2}{2} \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} \int\left[1-\frac{1}{1+x^2}\right] d x \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left(x^2+1\right) \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{1}{2} x+c
\end{aligned}\)
