questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Suppose $p, q, r \neq 0$ and system of equation
$\begin{gathered}
(p+a) x+b y+c z=0 \\
a x+(q+b) y+c z=0
\end{gathered}$
$a x+b y+(r+c) z=0$, has a non-trivial solution, then the value of $\frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $|A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c\end{array}\right|$
Also, given that equations has non trivial solution
$|A|=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
p+a & b & c \\
a & q+b & c \\
a & b & r+c
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(p+a)[(q+b)(r+c)-b c]-b[a(r+c)-c a] \\
& +c[a b-a(q+b)]=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(p+a)[q r+q c+b r]-b[a r]+c[-a q]=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad p q r+p q c+p b r+a q r=0
\end{aligned}$
Dividing whole equation by $p q r$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & \frac{p q r}{p q r}+\frac{p q c}{p q r}+\frac{p r b}{p q r}+\frac{q r a}{p q r} & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & 1+\frac{c}{r}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{a}{p} & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r} & =-1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $x$ is a complex root of the equation
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & x & x \\ x & 1 & x \\ x & x & 1\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1-x & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1-x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1-x\end{array}\right|=0$,
then $x^{2007}+x^{-2007}=$
questions.solution:
Expanding the two determinants, we get
$\left(1-3 x^2+2 x^3\right)+\left(3 x^2-x^3\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow x^3+1=0$
$\Rightarrow x=-\omega,-\omega^2,-1$
$x^{2007}+x^{-2007}=-1-1=-2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The equations $x-y+2 z=4$
$3 x+y+4 z=6$
$x+y+z=1$ have
questions.solution:
Given equations, $x-y+2 z=4$
$3 x+y+4 z=6$
$x+y+z=1$
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=1(1-4)+1(3-4)+2(3-1)$
$=-3-1+4=0$
and $\Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}4 & -1 & 2 \\ 6 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=4(1-4)+1(6-4)+2(6-1)$
$=-12+2+10=0$
Now, $\Delta=0$ and $\Delta_1=0$
$\therefore$ These equations have infinitely many solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the system of linear equations $2 x+y-z=7$
$x-3 y+2 z=1 ; x+4 y+\delta z=k$
where $\delta, k \in R$ has infinitely many solutions, then $\delta+k$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
For getting infinite solutions $\mathrm{D}=0, \mathrm{D}_1=$ $\mathrm{D}_2=\mathrm{D}=0$ then check all the three equations
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 & \delta\end{array}\right|=0 \quad \Rightarrow \delta=-3$
And $\Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}7 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -3 & 2 \\ k & 4 & -3\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow k=6$
$\Rightarrow \delta+k=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Given $2 x-y+2 z=2, x-2 y+z=-4$, $x+y+\lambda z=4$, then the value of $\lambda$ such that the given system of equation has no solution is
questions.solution:
Since, the system has no solution.
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & -2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & \lambda\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow 2(-2 \lambda+1)+1(\lambda+1)+2(3)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-4 \lambda+2+\lambda+1+6=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda=9 \Rightarrow \lambda=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $p \neq a, q \neq b, r \neq c$ and the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& p x+a y+a z=0 \\
& b x+q y+b z=0 \\
& c x+c y+r z=0
\end{aligned}$
has a non-trivial solution, then the value of 
$\frac{p}{p-a}+\frac{q}{q-b}+\frac{r}{r-c}$ is
questions.solution:
As the given system of equations has a non-trivial solution.
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}p & a & a \\ b & q & b \\ c & c & r\end{array}\right|=0$
Applying $C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_1$ and $C_3 \rightarrow C_3-C_1$
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p & a-p & a-p \\ b & q-b & 0 \\ c & 0 & r-c\end{array}\right|=0$
Expanding along $C_3$, we get
$(a-p)\left|\begin{array}{cc}b & q-b \\ c & 0\end{array}\right|+(r-c)\left|\begin{array}{cc}p & a-p \\ b & q-b\end{array}\right|=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow(a-p)(-c)(q-b)+(r-c) \\
& \{p(q-b)-b(a-p)\}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \\
& (p-a)(q-b) c+p(r-c)(q-b)+b(r-c)(p-a)=0
\end{aligned}$
Dividing by $(p-a)(q-b)(r-c)$, we get
$\frac{c}{r-c}+\frac{p}{p-a}+\frac{b}{q-b}=0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{p}{p-a}+\frac{q}{q-b}+\frac{r}{r-c}=\frac{q-b}{q-b}+\frac{r-c}{r-c}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Suppose $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r} \neq 0$ and system of equation $(\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{a}) \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{by}+\mathrm{cz}=0$

$a x+(q+b) y+c z=0$

$a x+b y+(r+c) z=0$ has a non-trivial solution,

then value of $\frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r}$ is
questions.solution:
Since the system is homogeneous and has a non-trivial solution

$0=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p & -q & 0 \\ 0 & q & -r \\ a & b & r+c\end{array}\right|$

using $\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{3}$

Expanding along $\mathrm{C}$, we get $\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}[\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{r}+\mathrm{c})+\mathrm{br}]+\mathrm{aqr}=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{pqr}+\mathrm{pqc}+\mathrm{prb}+\mathrm{qra}=0$

$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r}=-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If system of equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>c</mi></math> is inconsistent, then which of the following is correct?
questions.solution:
We have, 

a x+y+z=a   ...(i)

x+by+z=b   ...(ii)

x+y+cz=c   ...(iii)

are inconsistent. 

∴a111b111c=0 and a11bb1c1c≠0

⇒ a111b111c=0

⇒ a(bc-1)-1(c-1)+1(1-b)=0

abc-a-c+1+1-b=0

abc-a-b-c+2=0

and a11bb1c1c≠0

It is possible only when a≠1,b≠1 and c≠1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>∈</mo><msup><mi>R</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> and the system of equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>)</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has infinitely many solutions, the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

1-ax+y+z=0

x+1-by+z=0

x+y+1-cz=0

has many solutions 

∴ 1-a1111-b1111-c=0

⇒ ab+ac+bc=abc

∴ ab+bc+ac3≥a2b2c21/3⇒abc1/3≥3

∴ Minimum of abc=33=27

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Given, x2=sinθcosθ0-cosθsinθ1sinθcosθ2

On expanding sinθcosθ0-cosθsinθ1sinθcosθ2 along C3, we get

x2=0-1sinθcosθsinθcosθ+2sinθcosθ-cosθsinθ=-1sinθcosθ-sinθcosθ+2sin2θ+cos2θ

=-1×0+2×1

⇒x2=2⇒x=±2

If x=2, then

4x2+xsin3π2+5=4×22-2+5

=8-2+5=13-2

If x=-2, then

4x2+xsin3π2+5=4×-22+2+5

=8+2+5=13+2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & \lambda+2 \\ 2 & 4 & 8 \\ 3 & 5 & 10\end{array}\right]$ is singular, then $\lambda=$
questions.solution:
$|\mathrm{A}|=0$ as the matrix $\mathrm{A}$ is singular

$$

\therefore|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

1 & 3 & \lambda+2 \\

2 & 4 & 8 \\

3 & 5 & 10

\end{array}\right|=0

$$

Apply $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-2 \mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-3 \mathrm{R}_{1}$

and expand. $-2(4-3 \lambda)+4(4-2 \lambda)=0$

$$

\Rightarrow 8-2 \lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=4

$$

For $\lambda=4,$ the second and the third column are proportional.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}$ and $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}$ be the roots of $a x^{2}+b x+c$ $=0$ and $\mathrm{px}^{2}+\mathrm{qx}+\mathrm{r}=0$ respectively. If the system of equations $\alpha_{1} \mathrm{y}+\alpha_{2} \mathrm{z}=0$ and $\beta_{1} \mathrm{y}+\beta_{2} \mathrm{z}=0$ has a non-trivial solution, then
questions.solution:
Since $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}$ and $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}$ are the roots of ax $^{2}$ $+b x+c=0$ and $p x^{2}+q x+r=0$ respectively

therefore

$$

\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}=\frac{-b}{a}, \alpha_{1} \alpha_{2}=\frac{c}{a}

$$

and $\beta_{1}+\beta_{2}=\frac{-q}{p}, \beta_{1} \beta_{2}=\frac{r}{p}$

Since the given system of equation has a non-trivial solution

$$

\therefore\left|\begin{array}{ll}

\alpha_{1} & \alpha_{2} \\

\beta_{1} & \beta_{2}

\end{array}\right|=0 \text { i.e. } \alpha_{1} \beta_{2}-\alpha_{2} \beta_{1}=0

$$

or $\quad \frac{\alpha_{1}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\beta_{2}}=\frac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{\beta_{1}+\beta_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_{1} \alpha_{2}}{\beta_{1} \beta_{2}}}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{pb}}{\mathrm{qa}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{pc}}{\mathrm{ra}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{b}^{2}}{\mathrm{q}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{ac}}{\mathrm{pr}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If [ ] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to the real number under consideration and $-1 \leq x<0 ; 0 \leq y<1 ; 1 \leq z<2,$ then the value of the determinant
is
questions.solution: Since, $-1 \leq x<0$ $\begin{array}{ll}\therefore \quad & {[\mathrm{x}]=-1} \\ & 0 \leq \mathrm{y}<1 \quad \therefore[\mathrm{y}]=0 \\ & 1 \leq \mathrm{z}<2 \quad \therefore[\mathrm{z}]=1 \\ \therefore \quad & \text { Given determinant }=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 & 2\end{array}\right|=1=[\mathrm{z}]\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{p} & \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{z} \\ \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{q} & \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{z} \\ \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{r}\end{array}\right|=0,$ then the value of

$\frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y}+\frac{r}{z}$ is
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{p} & \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{z} \\ \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{q} & \mathrm{r}-\mathrm{z} \\ \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{r}\end{array}\right|=0$

Apply $\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{3},$ we get

$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{x} & 0 & -\mathrm{z} \\ 0 & \mathrm{y} & -\mathrm{z} \\ \mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{r}\end{array}\right|=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}[\mathrm{yr}+\mathrm{z}(\mathrm{q}-\mathrm{y})]-\mathrm{z}[0-\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{x})]=0$

[Expansion along first row $]$

$\Rightarrow x y r+z x q+y z p=2 x y z \Rightarrow \frac{p}{x}+\frac{q}{y}+\frac{r}{z}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & (x-3) & (x-3)^{2} \\ 1 & (x-4) & (x-4)^{2} \\ 1 & (x-5) & (x-5)^{2}\end{array}\right|$
questions.solution:
The given determinant vanishes, i.e.,

$$

\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

1 & x-3 & (x-3)^{2} \\

1 & x-4 & (x-4)^{2} \\

1 & x-5 & (x-5)^{2}

\end{array}\right|=0

$$

Expanding along $\mathrm{C}_{1},$ we get

$$

\begin{array}{l}

(x-4)(x-5)^{2}-(x-5)(x-4)^{2}-\left\{(x-3)(x-5)^{2}\right. \\

\left.-(x-5)(x-3)^{2}\right\}+(x-3)(x-4)^{2} \\

-(x-4)(x-3)^{2}=0 \\

\Rightarrow(x-4)(x-5)(x-5-x+4) \\

-(x-3)(x-5)(x-5-x+3) \\

+(x-3)(x-4)(x-4-x+3)=0

\end{array}

$$

$$

\Rightarrow-(x-4)(x-5)+2(x-3)(x-5)-(x-3)

$$

$(x-4)=0$

$$

\Rightarrow-x^{2}+9 x-20+2 x^{2}-16 x+30-x^{2}+7 x-12=0

$$

$\Rightarrow-32+30=0 \Rightarrow-2=0$

Which is not possible, hence no value of $\mathrm{x}$ satisfies the given condition.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equations $2 x+3 y+4=0 ; 3 x+4 y+6=0$ and $4 x+5 y+8=0$ are
questions.solution:
Consider first two equations:
$2 x+3 y=-4 \quad$ and $\quad 3 x+4 y=-6$
We have $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 3 & 4\end{array}\right|=-1 \neq 0$
$$
\Delta_{\mathrm{x}}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
-4 & 3 \\
-6 & 4
\end{array}\right|=2 \quad \text { and } \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{y}}=\left|\begin{array}{cc}
2 & -4 \\
3 & -6
\end{array}\right|=0
$$
$\therefore x=-2$ and $y=0$
Now this solution satisfies the third, so the equations are consistent with unique solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of the determinant 
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}265 & 240 & 219 \\ 240 & 225 & 198 \\ 219 & 198 & 181\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Applying $\mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{3},$ we get
$\begin{aligned}
\text { Det. } &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
25 & 21 & 219 \\
15 & 27 & 198 \\
21 & 17 & 181
\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
4 & 21 & 9 \\
-12 & 27 & -72 \\
4 & 17 & 11
\end{array}\right| \\
=&\left[\begin{array}{llc}
4 & 21 & 9 \\
0 & 90 & -45 \\
0 & -4 & 2
\end{array} \mid\left[\mathrm{By} \mathrm{R}_{2}+3 \mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\right]\right.\\
=4(180-180)=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 5\end{array}\right]\) and \(B=\left[\begin{array}{cc}-1 & -2 \\ 0 & 5 \\ 3 & 1\end{array}\right]\) and \(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{D}=0\) (zero matrix), then \(\mathrm{D}\) matrix will be -
questions.solution:
Let \(D=\left[\begin{array}{ll}a & b \\ c & d \\ e & f\end{array}\right]\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \therefore A+B-C=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 5\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc}-1 & -2 \\ 0 & 5 \\ 3 & 1\end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{b} \\ \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{d} \\ \mathrm{e} & \mathrm{f}\end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ll}1-1-\mathrm{a} & 3-2-\mathrm{b} \\ 3+0-\mathrm{c} & 2+5-\mathrm{d} \\ 2+3-\mathrm{e} & 5+1-\mathrm{f}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right]\end{aligned}\)
\(\begin{array}{ll}-\mathrm{a}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=0,1-\mathrm{b}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=1, \\ 3-\mathrm{c}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=3, & 7-\mathrm{d}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=7, \\ 5-\mathrm{e}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}=5, & 6-\mathrm{f}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=6,\end{array}\)
\(\therefore D=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 3 & 7 \\ 5 & 6\end{array}\right]\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\mathrm{a} > 0, \mathrm{~b} > 0, \mathrm{c} > 0\) are respectively the \(\mathrm{pth}, \mathrm{q}\) th, rth terms of GP., then the value of the determinant \(\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{p} & 1 \\ \log \mathrm{b} & \mathrm{q} & 1 \\ \log \mathrm{c} & \mathrm{r} & 1\end{array}\right|\) is
questions.solution:
Let \(A\) be the 1 st term and \(\mathrm{R}\) the common ratio of G.P., then
\(\begin{aligned}
& a=T_p=A R^{p-1} \\
& \therefore \log \mathrm{a}=\log \mathrm{A}+(\mathrm{p}-1) \log \mathrm{R}
\end{aligned}\)
Similarly, \(\log \mathrm{b}=\log \mathrm{A}+(\mathrm{q}-1) \log \mathrm{R}\)
and \(\log \mathrm{c}=\log \mathrm{A}+(\mathrm{r}-1) \log \mathrm{R}\)
\(\therefore \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}
\log A+(p-1) \log R & p & 1 \\
\log A+(q-1) \log R & q & 1 \\
\log A+(r-1) \log R & r & 1
\end{array}\right|\)
Split into two determinants and in the first take \(\log A\) common and in the second take \(\log \mathrm{R}\) common
\(\Delta=\log A\left|\begin{array}{lll}
1 & p & 1 \\
1 & q & 1 \\
1 & r & 1
\end{array}\right|+\log R\left|\begin{array}{lll}
p-1 & p & 1 \\
q-1 & q & 1 \\
r-1 & r & 1
\end{array}\right|\)
Apply \(\mathrm{C}_1 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_3\) in the second
\(\Delta=0+\log R\left|\begin{array}{lll}
0 & p & 1 \\
0 & q & 1 \\
0 & r & 1
\end{array}\right|=0\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The digits \(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) are such that the three digit numbers \(A 88,6 \mathrm{~B} 8,86 \mathrm{C}\) are divisible by 72 then the determinant \(\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\text { A } & 6 & 8 \\ 8 & B & 6 \\ 8 & 8 & C\end{array}\right|\) is divisible by
questions.solution:
\(R_3 \rightarrow 100 R_1+10 R_2+R_3\)
\(\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
A & 6 & 8 \\
8 & B & 6 \\
8 & 8 & C
\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
A & 6 & 8 \\
8 & B & 6 \\
A 88 & 6 B C & 86 C
\end{array}\right|\)
which is divisible by 72.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(x+1)^2$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x+1}=e^{3 x}(x+1)$
Above is linear differential equation of form
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}+P x=Q \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{IF}=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x+1} d x}=e^{-\ln (1+x)}=\frac{1}{1+x}
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow$ Solution will be
$\begin{aligned}
y \cdot \mathrm{IF} & =\int Q \cdot \mathrm{IF} d x \\
y \cdot \frac{1}{1+x} & =\int e^{3 x}(x+1) \times \frac{1}{(1+x)} d x \\
\frac{y}{1+x} & =\frac{e^{3 x}}{3}+C^{\prime} \\
\frac{3 y}{1+x} & =e^{3 x}+C^{\prime} \quad \quad\left[\because C=3 C^{\prime}\right]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\frac{d y}{d x}-y \log _e 2=2^{\sin x}(\cos x-1) \log _e 2$, then $y$
questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}-y \log _e 2=2^{\sin x}(\cos x-1) \log _e 2$
This is linear differential equation
$\text { I.F. }=e^{-\log _e 2 \int d x}=e^{-x \log _e 2}=2^{-x}$
then general Solution is
$y 2^{-x}=\int 2^{-x} 2^{\sin x}(\cos x-1) \log _e 2 d x+c$
Now let $\sin x-x=t \Rightarrow(\cos x-1) d x=d t$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore y 2^{-x}=\log _e 2 \int 2^t d t+c \\
& \therefore y 2^{-x}=2^t+c \\
& \therefore y=2^{x+t}+c 2^x \\
& \therefore y=2^{\sin x}+c 2^x
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of $\frac{x d y}{d x}-y=x^4-3 x$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { (c) Since } x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^4-3 x \\
& \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x^3-3
\end{aligned}$
Hence $I F=e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=\frac{1}{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions of $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x-1}$, when $y(1)=2$ is
questions.solution:
Since, $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x-1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{y+1}=\frac{d x}{x-1}$
After integrating on both sides, we have
$\log (y+1)=\log (x-1)-\log C$
$C(y+1)=(x-1)$
$C=\frac{x-1}{y+1}$
If $x=1$, then $y=2$, so $C=0$
Therefore, $x-1=0$
Hence, there is only one solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \tan x=y \sec ^2 x+\sin x$, find general solution
questions.solution:
Given that
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \tan x=y \sec ^2 x+\sin x \\
& \tan x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)-y \sec ^2 x=\sin x \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{-\sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right) y=\frac{\sin x}{\tan x} \\
& \text { Here, } P=-\frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan x} \text { and } Q=\cos x \\
& \text { IF }=e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{-\sec ^ 2 x}{\tan x} d x} \\
& \text { IF }=e^{-\log (\tan x)}=e^{\log \cot x}=\cot x \\
& \text { Now, } y \times \mathrm{IF}=\int(Q \times \mathrm{IF}) d x+C \\
& y \cdot \cot x=\int \cos x \cdot \cot x+C \\
& y \cdot \cot x=\int \frac{1-\sin ^2 x}{\sin x}+C \\
& y \cdot \cot x=\log |\operatorname{cosec} x-\cot x|+\cos x+C
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point $\mathrm{P}(x, y)$ is $\frac{y}{x}-\cos ^{2} \frac{y}{x}$, then the equation of a curve passing through $\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
According to the condition,

$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{y}{x}-\cos ^{2} \frac{y}{x} \ldots \text { (i) }$

This is a homogeneous differential equation Substituting $y=v x$, we get

$\begin{array}{l}

v+x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=v-\cos ^{2} v \\

\Rightarrow x \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\cos ^{2} v \\

\Rightarrow \int \sec ^{2} \mathrm{vdv}=-\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}} \\

\Rightarrow \tan v=-\log x+\mathrm{C} \\

\Rightarrow \tan \frac{y}{x}+\log x=\mathrm{C}

\end{array}$

Substituting $\mathrm{x}=1, y=\frac{\pi}{4}$, we get $\mathrm{C}=1$

Thus, we get

$\tan \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)+\log x=1$

which is the required solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\left(\tan ^{-1} y-x\right) d y=\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x$ is
questions.solution:
The given differential equation can be written as $\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}} \ldots$ (i)

Now, eq. (i) is a linear differential equation of the form $\frac{d x}{d y}+\mathrm{P}_{1} x=\mathrm{Q}_{1}$

where $\mathrm{P}_{1}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}$ and $\mathrm{Q}_{1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}$

Therefore, I.F $=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}$

Thus, the solution of the given differential equation is given by $x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} \mathrm{dy}+\mathrm{C} \ldots$ (ii)

Let $\mathrm{I}=\int\left(\frac{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}}\right) e^{\tan ^{-1} y} \mathrm{dy}$

On substituting $\tan ^{-1} y=t$, so that

$\left(\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\mathrm{dt}$, we get

$I=\int t e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-\int 1 \cdot e^{t} d t=t e^{t}-e^{t}=e^{t}(t-1)$

or $\mathrm{I}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)$

On substituting the value of 1 in equation (i1), we get

$x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+\mathrm{C}$

or $x=\left(\tan ^{-1} y-1\right)+\mathrm{C} e^{\tan ^{-1} y}$

which is the general solution of the given differntial equation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The solution of the differential equation

$(x+1) \frac{d y}{d x}-y=e^{3 x}(x+1)^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
The given equation is $\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x+1}=e^{3 x}(x+1)$

I.F. $=e^{\int-\frac{1}{x+1} d x}=e^{-\log (x+1)}=\frac{1}{x+1}$

The solution is

$y\left(\frac{1}{x+1}\right)=\int e^{3 x}(x+1) \cdot \frac{1}{x+1} d x+a$

$\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x+1}=\int e^{3 x} d x+a=\frac{e^{3 x}}{3}+a$

$\Rightarrow \frac{3 y}{x+1}=e^{3 x}+c, c=3 a$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solutions of $(x+y+1) d y=d x$ are
questions.solution:
Putting $x+y+1=u,$ we have $d u=d x+d y$ and the given equation reduces to $u(d u-d x)=$ $d x$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{u d u}{u+1}=d x \quad \Rightarrow \quad u-\log (u+1)=x$

$\Rightarrow \log (x+y+2)=y+$ constant

$\Rightarrow x+y+2=C e^{y}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solution of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>y</mi></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

dydx=ex-yex-ey

⇒ eydydx+eyex=e2x

Put ey=v

∴ eydydx=dvdx

∴ dvdx+vex=e2x

This is linear equation 

I.F==e∫exdx=eex

∴ Required solution is 

v·eex=∫eex·e2xdx+C

v·eex=eexex-1+C

⇒ ey·eex=eexex-1+C

⇒ ey=ex-1+ce-ex

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mn>1</mn></msubsup><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Given,

f'x=fx+∫01fxdx,f0=1   ...(i)

⇒ f''x=f'x+0

⇒ f''xf'x=1

⇒ ∫f''xf'xdx=∫dx

⇒ logf'x=x+C

⇒ f'x=Aex

⇒ fx=Aex+K

f0=A+K=1    ...(ii)

∴ Aex=Aex+K+∫01Aex+kdx

⇒ k+Aex+Kx01=0

⇒ k+Ae-A+K=0

⇒ A(e-1)+2k=0   ...(iii)

From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

A=23-e,k=1-e3-e

∴ fx=2ex3-e+1-e3-e

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have,

dydx=sinx+y+cosx+y

Put x+y=v⇒1+dydx=dvdx

dvdx-1=sinv+cosv

⇒dvdx=sinv+cosv+1

⇒dvsinv+cosv+1=dx

⇒∫1sinv+cosv+1dv=∫dx

⇒∫12tanv21+tan2v2+1-tan2v21+tan2v2+1dv=∫dx

⇒∫1+tan2v22tanv2+1-tan2v2+1+tan2v2dv=x+C

⇒12∫sec2v2tanv2+1dv=x+C

⇒logtanv2+1=x+C

⇒logtanx+y2+1=x+C

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the curve whose slope at any point is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and which passes through the origin is
questions.solution:
We have, dydx=y+2x

⇒dydx-y=2x

Now, IF=e-∫1dx=e-x

y·e-x=∫2xe-xdx+k, k be the constant of integration

=2x∫e-xdx-∫1·-e-xdx+k  [using integration by parts]

⇒y·e-x=-2xe-x-2e-x+k   ...i

As curve (i) passes through 0,0

∴ 0=0-2+k

⇒k=2

Thus, the curve is

ye-x=-2xe-x-2e-x+2

∴ y=2ex-x-1

Hence, option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>y</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, is
questions.solution:
Integrating the given differential equation, we have

dydx=-cos3x3+ex+x33+C1

But y10=1

So, 1=-13+1+C1

⇒C1=13

∴ dydx=-cos3x3+ex+x33+13

Again integrating, we get

y=-sin3x9+ex+x412+13x+C2

But y0=0, so 0=0+1+C2

⇒C2=-1

Thus, y=-sin3x9+ex+x412+13x-1

Hence, option (a) correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, is
questions.solution:
Put x+y=v and 1+dydx=dvdx

Therefore, the differential equation reduces to

dvdx=1+cosv+sinv=2cos2v2+2sinv2cosv2

=2cos2v21+tanv2

⇒ ∫sec2V221+tanv2dv=∫dx

∴ log1+tanx+y2=x+C

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any differentiable function $y$ of $x$, $\frac{d^{2} x}{d y^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=$
questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{-1}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-1\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{-2}\left\{\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)\right\}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(-1)\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{-2}\left\{\frac{d}{d y}\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}\right\}$

$=(-1)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\left\{\frac{d^{2} x}{d y^{2}} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right\}=-\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}\left\{\frac{d^{2} x}{d y^{2}}\right\}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} x}{d y^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}+\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\phi(\mathrm{x})$ is a differential function, then the solution of the differential equation $\mathrm{dy}+\left\{\mathrm{y} \phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})-\phi(\mathrm{x})\right.$

$\left.\phi^{\prime}(x)\right\} d x=0,$ is
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is

$\mathrm{dy}+\left\{y \phi^{\prime}(x)-\phi(x) \phi^{\prime}(x)\right\} d x=0$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{y}=\phi(\mathrm{x}) \phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$

which is a linear differential equation with $\quad P=\phi^{\prime}(x), Q=\phi(x) \cdot \phi^{\prime}(x)$ and

$I \cdot F=e^{\int \phi^{\prime}(x) d x}=e^{\phi(x)}$

$\therefore$ Solution is $y \cdot e^{\phi(x)}=\int \phi(x) \cdot \phi^{\prime}(x) e^{\phi(x)} d x+C$

$\Rightarrow y \cdot e^{\phi(x)}=\int \phi(x) \cdot e^{\phi(x)} \phi^{\prime}(x) d x+C$

$\Rightarrow y \cdot e^{\phi(x)}=\phi(x) e^{\phi(x)}-\int \phi^{\prime}(x) e^{\phi(x)} d x+C$

$\Rightarrow y \cdot e^{\phi(x)}=\phi(x) e^{\phi(x)}-e^{\phi(x)}+C$

$\Rightarrow y=\lceil\phi(x)-1]+C e^{-\phi(x)}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=\left(x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)^{n},$ then $\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The degree of the differential equation satisfying

$\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1+y^{2}}=a(x-y)$ is
questions.solution:
$\quad$ Put $x=\sin \theta$ and $y=\sin \phi$

$\Rightarrow \cos \theta+\cos \phi=a(\sin \theta-\sin \phi)$

$\Rightarrow 2 \cos$

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\theta+\phi}{2} \cos \frac{\theta-\phi}{2}=2 a \cos \frac{\theta+\phi}{2} \sin \frac{\theta-\phi}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \cot \frac{\theta-\phi}{2}=a \Rightarrow \theta-\phi=2 \cot ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \\ \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}-\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{y}=2 \cot ^{-1} \mathrm{a} \\ \text { Differentiate } \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{y}^{2}}} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \end{aligned}$

so the degree is one

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Solution of differential equation

$$

x^{2}=1+\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{-1} \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{-2}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{2 !}+\frac{\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{-3}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}}{3 !}+\ldots \text { is }

$$
questions.solution:
$x^{2}=e^{\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^{-1}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)} \Rightarrow x^{2}=e^{\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}$

$\Rightarrow \ln x^{2}=\frac{y}{x} \frac{d y}{d x}$ or $\int x \ln x^{2} d x=\int y d y$

Put $x^{2}=t \Rightarrow 2 x d x=d t \therefore \frac{1}{2} \int \ln t d t=\frac{y^{2}}{2}$

$C+t \ln t-t=y^{2}$ or $y^{2}=x^{2}\left(\ln x^{2}-1\right)+C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\sin (x+y)=\sin (x-y)$ is
questions.solution:
The equation is,

$\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin (x-y)-\sin (x+y)=2 \cos x \sin (-y)$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{\sin y}+2 \cos x d x=0$

$\Rightarrow \int \cos e c y d y+2 \int \cos x d x=\mathrm{C}$

$\Rightarrow \log \tan \frac{y}{2}+2 \sin x=\mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution to the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y f^{\prime}(x)-y^{2}}{f(x)}$ where $f(x)$ is a given function is
questions.solution:
We have $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} y-\frac{y^{2}}{f(x)}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} y=-\frac{y^{2}}{f(x)}$

Divide by $y^{2}: y^{-2} \frac{d y}{d x}-y^{-1} \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)}=-\frac{1}{f(x)}$

Put $y^{-1}=z \Rightarrow-y^{-2} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}$

$-\frac{d z}{d x}-\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)}(z)=-\frac{1}{f(x)}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}+\frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} z=\frac{1}{f(x)}$

$I F=e^{\int \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)} d x}=e^{\log f(x)}=f(x)$

$\therefore$ The solution is $z(f(x))=\int \frac{1}{f(x)}(f(x)) d x+c$

$\Rightarrow y^{-1}(f(x))=x+c \Rightarrow f(x)=y(x+c)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation whose solution is $A x^{2}+B y^{2}=1$ where $A$ and $B$ are arbitrary constants is of
questions.solution:
$A x^{2}+B y^{2}=1$ ...(1)
$A x+B y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$ ...(2)
$A+B y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+B\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=0$ ...(3)
$x\left\{-B y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-B\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right\}+B y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
Dividing both sides by $-\mathrm{B}$, we get $x y \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}-y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
Which is a DE of order 2 and degree 1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The degree of the differential equation \(\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^{2 / 3}+4-3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+5 \frac{d y}{d x}=0\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^{2 / 3}+4-3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+5 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^2=\left[3 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}-4\right]^3
\end{aligned}\)
It is a differential equation of degree 2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the curve \(y=x|x|, x\)-axis and the ordinates \(x=1, x=-1\) is given by :
questions.solution:
The area of the region bounded by the curve \(y=f(x)\) and the ordinates \(x=a, x=b\) is given by \(\text {Area }=\left|\int_a^b y d x\right|\) According to the question, \(y=x|x|= \begin{cases}x^2, & x \geq 0 \\ -x^2, & x < 0\end{cases}\)
Required area \(=\) area of region \(\mathrm{OAB}+\) area of region \(\mathrm{OCD}\) \(=2 \times\) Area of region \(\mathrm{OAB}\) \(=2 \int_0^1 x^2 d x=\frac{2}{3} s q\). units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area of the triangle formed by the line \(x+y=3\) and angle bisectors of the pair of straight lines \(x^2-y^2+2 y=1\) is
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{x}^2-\mathrm{y}^2+2 \mathrm{y}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}= \pm(\mathrm{y}-1)\) Bisectors of above line are \(x=0 ~\&~ y=1\)
So area between \(x=0, y=1 ~\&~ x+y=3\) is shaded Region shown in figure.
Area \(=1 / 2 \times 2 \times 2=2\) sq. units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}=$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {  Given, } x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(x \sqrt{1+y})^2=(-y \sqrt{1+x})^2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{lr}
\Rightarrow & x^2(1+y)=y^2(1+x) \\
\Rightarrow & x^2-y^2=y^2 x-x^2 y \\
\Rightarrow & (x-y)(x+y)=x y(y-x) \\
\Rightarrow & x+y=-x y \\
\Rightarrow & x+y+x y=0 \\
\Rightarrow & y=\frac{-x}{1+x}=-1+\frac{1}{1+x}
\end{array}$
Differentiating w.r.t, $x$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{(1+x)^2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right)$, then $y^{\prime}(1)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right) \\ y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+\sqrt{x} \cdot x}\right) \\ y & =\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{x})-\tan ^{-1} x \\ & {\left[\because \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-y}{1+x y}\right)=\tan ^{-1} x-\tan ^{-1} y\right] }\end{aligned}$
Differentiating w.r.t $x$
$y^{\prime}(x)=\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1+x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}-\frac{1}{1+x^2}$
Now, $\quad y^{\prime}(1)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=-\frac{1}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: At $x=\frac{\pi^2}{4}, \frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1}(\cos \sqrt{x})+\sec ^{-1}\left(e^x\right)\right)=$
questions.solution:
$\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1}(\cos \sqrt{x})+\sec ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{e}^x\right)\right)$
$=\frac{(-\sin \sqrt{x})}{1+\cos ^2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}\right)+\frac{1}{e^x \sqrt{e^{2 x}-1}} \cdot e^x$
but when $x=\frac{\pi^2}{4}$
$=-\frac{-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{1+\cos ^2 \frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{e^{\pi^2 / 2}-1}}$
$=-\frac{1}{\pi}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{e^{\pi^2 / 2}-1}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The slope of the tangent to the curve $x=3 t^2+1$, $y=t^3-1$ at $x=1$ is:
questions.solution:
Given curve is $x=3 t^2+1$


Second curve is $y=t^3-1$

$\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}=3 t^2 \times \frac{1}{6 t}=\frac{t}{2}$
But from (i) when $x=1$
we have $1=3 t^2+1 \Rightarrow 3 t^2=0 \Rightarrow t=0$
$\therefore$ When $\mathrm{x}=1$ then $\mathrm{t}=0 \quad \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathbf{0}$
Hence, slope of the tangent to the curve $=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y^x=e^{y-x}$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{x}}$
Taking log on both sides, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& \log y^x=\log e^{y-x} \\
& \quad\left(\because \log a^b=b \log a \text { and } \log e=1\right)
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow x \log y=(y-x) \log e \Rightarrow x \log y=y-x \ldots(i)$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\frac{d}{d x}(x \log y)=\frac{d}{d x}(y-x)$
(using product rule)
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow x\left(\frac{1}{y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y(1)=\frac{d y}{d x}-1 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{x}{y}-1\right)=-1-\log y \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left[\frac{y}{(1+\log y) y}-1\right]=-(1+\log y) \\
& \qquad \quad\left[\because \text { from eq. }(i) \cdot x=\frac{y}{(1+\log y)}\right] \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left[\frac{1-1-\log y}{1+\log y}\right]=-(1+\log y)
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{(1+\log y)^2}{-\log y}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(1+\log y)^2}{\log y}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\sqrt{1+\cos ^2\left(\mathrm{x}^2\right)}$, then the value of $f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
On differentiating (i) w.r.t.x, we get
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-2 \sin x^2 \cos x^2}{\sqrt{1+\cos ^2 x^2}}(x)$

Put, $x=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ in (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)=-\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{2}}} \\
& =-\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}}=-\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{6}}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The derivative of $e^{x^3}$ with respect to $\log x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $y=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}^3}, \mathrm{z}=\log \mathrm{x}$
On differentiating w.r.t.x, we get
$\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x^3}\left(3 x^2\right)=3 x^2 e^{x^3} \text { and } \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}$
$\therefore \frac{d y}{d z}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d z}{d x}}=\frac{3 x^2 e^{x^3}}{\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}=3 x^3 e^{x^3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1+\cos 2 \theta}{1-\cos 2 \theta}\right)}$, then $\frac{d y}{d \theta}$ at $\theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}$ is :
questions.solution:
$y=\sqrt{\frac{1+\cos 2 \theta}{1-\cos 2 \theta}}$
$\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{\frac{2 \cos ^2 \theta}{2 \sin ^2 \theta}}=\sqrt{\cot ^2 \theta}$
$\Rightarrow y=\cot \theta$
Differentiate w.r.t. ' $\theta$ ', we get: $\frac{d y}{d \theta}=-\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta$
Now, $\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}\right)_{\theta=\frac{3 \pi}{4}}=-\operatorname{cosec}^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)$
$=-\operatorname{cosec}^2\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=-\operatorname{cosec}^2 \frac{\pi}{4}=-2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\text { If } 2^{x}+2^{y}=2^{x+y} \text {, then } \frac{d y}{d x}=$
questions.solution:
On differentiating

$\begin{array}{l}

2^{x} \log 2+2^{y} \log 2 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}} \\

=2^{x} \cdot 2^{y} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cdot \log 2+2^{y} \cdot 2^{x} \log 2 \\

\Rightarrow 2^{x}+2^{y} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2^{x+y} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+2^{x+y} \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2^{x+y}-2^{x}}{2^{y}-2^{x+y}} \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{2^{x}+2^{y}-2^{x}}{2^{y}-2^{x}-2^{y}}=-2^{y-x}

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=\frac{1-t^{2}}{1+t^{2}}$ and $y=\frac{2 t}{1+t^{2}}$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Let $x=\frac{1-t^{2}}{1+t^{2}}$ and $y=\frac{2 t}{1+t^{2}}$

Put $t=\tan \theta$, we get

$x=\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}$ and $y=\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}$

$\Rightarrow x=\cos 2 \theta$ and $y=\sin 2 \theta$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=-2 \sin 2 \theta$ and $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=2 \cos 2 \theta$

Now, $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \times \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta}=-\frac{x}{y}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+\cos \frac{x}{2}}{1-\cos \frac{x}{2}}}\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have $\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+\cos \frac{x}{2}}{1-\cos \frac{x}{2}}}\right)$

$=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{1+\left(2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{4}-1\right)}{1-\left(1-2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{4}\right)}}\right]$

$=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\left.\frac{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{4}}{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{4}}\right)}\right.$

$=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cot ^{2} \frac{x}{4}}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \cot \frac{x}{4}\right)$

$=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right)\right)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right)=0-\frac{1}{4}=-\frac{1}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x y+y^{2}=\tan x+y,$ then find $\frac{d y}{d x}$ is
questions.solution:
The given relation is $x y+y^{2}=\tan x+y$. Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$, we get

$\frac{d}{d x}(x y)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(y^{2}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)+\frac{d y}{d x}$

or $\left[y \cdot 1+x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right]+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\frac{d y}{d x}$

or $(x+2 y-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x-y$

$\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^{2} x-y}{(x+2 y-1)}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∉</mo><mi>Z</mi></math>, (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mo>·</mo><mo>]</mo></math> denotes greatest integer function), then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, f(x)=xx

∴ x is an integer. 

⇒fx=px  ∵[x]=p

⇒f'x=p=x

∴ f'x=x

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $g$ is the inverse of function $f$ and $f^{\prime}(x)=\sin x$, then $\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Since, $g$ is the inverse of function $f$. Therefore, $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}^{-1}(\mathrm{x})$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}[\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})]=\mathrm{x}$

$\Rightarrow$ fog $(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x},$ for all $\mathrm{x}$

Differentiate both side,w.r.tx

$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\{\operatorname{fog}(\mathrm{x})\}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}),$ for all $\mathrm{x}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}[\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})] \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=1,$ for all $\mathrm{x}$

$\Rightarrow \sin \{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})\} \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=1,$ for all $\mathrm{x}$

(By defn of $\left.f^{\prime}(x)\right)$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{\sin \{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})\}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=1+x+\frac{x^{2}}{2 !}+\frac{x^{3}}{3 !}+\ldots . \infty,$ then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ is equal to
questions.solution: $y=e^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x}=y$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x+y)=f(x) \cdot f(y)$ for all $x, y$ where $f(0) \neq 0$ If $f(5)=2$ and $f^{\prime}(0)=3,$ then $f^{\prime}(5)$ is equal to-
questions.solution:
$f^{\prime}(5)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(5+h)-f(5)}{h}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(5+h)-f(5+0)}{h}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(5) \cdot f(h)-f(5)+f(0)}{h}$
$(\because f(x+y)=f(x), f(y)$ for all $x, y)$
$=\left(\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(h)-f(0)}{h}\right) \cdot f(5)=f^{\prime}(0) \cdot f(5)$
$=3 \times 2=6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=a \sin \theta$ and $y=b \cos \theta,$ then $\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
(b) Given $x=a \sin \theta$ and $y=b \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta$ and $\frac{d y}{d \theta}=-b \sin \theta$
$\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d \theta} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} \tan \theta \Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-b}{a} \sec ^{2} \theta$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The diagonal of a square is changing at the rate of $0.5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$. Then the rate of change of area, when the area is $400 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$, is equal to
questions.solution:
Diagonal $\mathrm{D}=\sqrt{2}$ a
Differentiating w.r.t.
t: $\frac{\mathrm{dD}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\text { at }}$
or $\frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times 0.5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$
Let Area is denoted by $\underline{A}$ $\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{dt}}$
when area $A$ is $400 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ then $\mathrm{a}=20$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \times 20 \times \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{2}}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
For the function
$f(x)=\frac{x^{100}}{100}+\frac{x^{99}}{99}+\ldots \frac{x^{2}}{2}+x+1$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=\mathrm{mf}^{\prime}(0),$ where $\mathrm{m}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\frac{x^{100}}{100}+\frac{x^{99}}{99}+\ldots+\frac{x^{2}}{2}+x+1$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{100 x^{99}}{100}+\frac{99 x^{98}}{99}+\ldots+\frac{2 x}{2}+1+0$
$\left[\because \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{nx}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}^{99}+\mathrm{x}^{98}+\ldots+\mathrm{x}+1$ ...(i)
Putting $\mathrm{x}=1$, we get $f^{\prime}(1)=\underbrace{(1)^{99}+1^{98}+\ldots+1+1}_{100 \text { times }}=\underbrace{1+1+1 \ldots+1+1}_{100 \text { times }}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=100$ ...(ii)
Again, putting $\mathrm{x}=0$, we get $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=0+0+\ldots+0+1 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=1$ ...(iii)
From eqs. (ii) and(iii), we get; $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=100 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)$ Hence, $m=100$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(y=x^{x^2}\), then \(\frac{d y}{d x}\) is equal to
questions.solution:
Consider \(y=x^{x^2} \Rightarrow \ln y=x^2 \ln x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x \ln x+x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{x}=x(1+2 \ln x) \\
& \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{x^2} \cdot x(1+2 \ln x)=x^{x^2+1}(1+2 \ln x)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The eccentricity of the ellipse whose major axis is three times the minor axis is:
questions.solution:
Let a be the major axis and $b$, the minor axis of the ellipse, then 3 minor axis = major axis.

$\Rightarrow 3 b=a$

Eccentricity is given by:

$\begin{array}{l}

b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right) \\

\Rightarrow b^{2}=9 b^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right) \\

\Rightarrow \frac{1}{9}=\left(1-e^{2}\right) \\

\Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{8}{9} \\

\Rightarrow e=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An ellipse has $\mathrm{OB}$ as semi minor axis, $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}$ its focii and the angle FBF' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
$\because \angle \mathrm{FBF}^{\prime}=90^{\circ}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{FB}^{2}+\mathrm{F}^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{2}=\mathrm{FF}^{\prime 2}$ $\therefore\left(\sqrt{a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}}\right)^{2}=(2 a e)^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 2\left(a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}\right)=4 a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}$
Also $e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=1-e^{2}$ $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow 2 e^{2}=1 \\ \Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{ma}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{nb}}=1$ touches the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1,$ then
questions.solution:
The line is $y=-\frac{\text { nb }}{\text { ma }} x-n b$. It will touch the ellipse if $(-n b)^{2}=a^{2}\left(-\frac{n b}{m a}\right)^{2}+b^{2}$

$\left[\mathrm{c}^{2}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{~m}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}\right]$

$\Rightarrow n^{2}=\frac{n^{2}}{m^{2}}+1$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}-1}$ or $\mathrm{n}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{~m}^{2}-1}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the length of the major axis of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is three times the length of minor axis, its eccentricity is
questions.solution:
We have

x2a2+y2b2=1

Given,

Length of the major axis=3Length of the minor axis

⇒2a=32b

⇒a=3b

Now,

e=1-b2a2

⇒e=1-b29b2

⇒ e=1-19=83=223

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn from the centre of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math> on any tangent to it is
questions.solution:
We have,

x2+3y2=6

⇒x26+y22=1

Centre of an ellipse is 0,0.

Let the foot of perpendicular be (h,k).

Equation of tangent in slope form to the standard ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 is

y=mx±a2m2+b2.

Hence, equation of tangent with slope m passing (h,k) is 

y=mx±6m2+2

⇒k=mh±6m2+2, where m=-hk.

⇒k=h-hk±6m2+2

⇒6h2k2+2=h2+k2k

⇒6h2+2k2=h2+k22

So, required locus is

6x2+2y2=x2+y22.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line passing through the extremity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of the major axis and extremity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of the minor axis of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> meets its auxiliary circle at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>. Then, the area of the triangle with vertices at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>M</mi></math> and the origin <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Equation of auxiliary circle is

x2+y2=9   ..i

∴ Equation of AM is x3+y1=1   ...ii

On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

M-125,95

Now, area of ΔAOM=12OA×MN=2710 sq units

Hence, option (d) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>p</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> denote the lengths of the perpendicular from a focus and the centre of an ellipse with semi-major axis of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>, respectively, on a tangent to the ellipse and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> denotes the focal distance of the point, then
questions.solution:
Let the ellipse be x2a2+y2b2=1

and let xcosθa+ysinθb=1  ...(i)

be a tangent to it at point (acosθ,bsinθ). Then, p= length of the perpendicular from S(ae, 0) on Eq. (i)

p=ecosθ-1cos2θa2+sin2θb2

p'= length of the perpendicular from O(0,0) on Eq. (i)

⇒ p'=1cos2θa2+sin2θb2

and r=aecosθ-a

Clearly, rp'=ap

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The eccentricity of an ellipse, with its centre at the origin, is $1 / 2$. If one of the directrices is $\mathrm{x}=4$ then the equation of the ellipse is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{e}=\frac{1}{2} .$ Directrix $, \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}=4$

$$

\therefore \mathrm{a}=4 \times \frac{1}{2}=2 \quad \therefore \mathrm{b}=2 \sqrt{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\sqrt{3}

$$

Equation of ellispe is

$$

\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1 \Rightarrow 3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=12

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
An ellipse has $O B$ as semi minor axis, $F$ and $F^{\prime}$

its focii and the angle $F B F^{\prime}$ is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \because \angle F B F^{\prime}=90^{\circ} \Rightarrow F B^{2}+F^{\prime} B^{2}=F F^{\prime 2} \\ \therefore &\left(\sqrt{a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}}\right)^{2}=(2 a e)^{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left(a^{2} e^{2}+b^{2}\right)=4 a^{2} e^{2} \Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} \ldots(1) \end{aligned}$
Also, $e^{2}=1-b^{2} / a^{2}=1-e^{2}$ (By using equation (i)) $\Rightarrow 2 e^{2}=1 \Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\mathrm{S}$ and $\mathrm{T}$ are the foci of an ellipse and $\mathrm{B}$ is an end of the minor axis. If STB is an equilateral triangle, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
Let eq. of ellipse be $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, $\mathrm{S}$ is $(-\mathrm{ae}, 0), \mathrm{T}$ is $(\mathrm{ae}, 0)$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is $(0, \mathrm{~b})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{SB}=\sqrt{(0+\mathrm{ae})^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}}$ Also $\mathrm{SB}^{2}=\mathrm{ST}^{2} \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{2}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 3 a^{2} e^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)=a^{2}-a^{2} e^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 4 a^{2} e^{2}=a^{2} \Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the ellipse with focus at \(( \pm 5,0)\) and \(x=\frac{36}{5}\) as one directrix is
questions.solution:
We have ae \(=5\) [Since focus is \(( \pm \mathrm{ae}, 0)\)]
and \(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{36}{5} \quad\left[\right.\) since directrix is \(\left.\mathrm{x}= \pm \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}\right]\)
On solving we get \(\mathrm{a}=6\)
\(\text { and } \mathrm{e}=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}^2=\mathrm{a}^2\left(1-\mathrm{e}^2\right)=36\left(1-\frac{25}{36}\right)=11\)
Thus, the required equation of the ellipse is
\(\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{y^2}{11}=1\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the line \(2 x-1=0\) is the directrix of the parabola \(y^2-k x+6=0\), then one of the values of \(k\) is
questions.solution:
Given equation of parabola is \(y^2-k x+6=0\)
\(\Rightarrow y^2=k x-6 \Rightarrow y^2=k\left(x-\frac{6}{k}\right)\)
Now, directrix, \(x-\frac{6}{k}=-\frac{k}{4}\)
\(\Rightarrow x=\frac{6}{k}-\frac{k}{4}\) ...(i)
But directrix is given \(\Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}\) ...(ii)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{6}{k}-\frac{k}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 24-k^2=2 k \\
& \Rightarrow k^2+2 k-24=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(k+6)(k-4)=0 \quad \Rightarrow k=-6, k=4
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $[x]$ denote the greatest integer $\leq x$. If $f(x)=[x]$ and $g(x)=|x|$, then the value of $f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)-g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=[x]$ and $g(x)=|x|$
Now,
$\begin{gathered}
f\left(\frac{-8}{5}\right)=\left[-\frac{8}{5}\right]=-2 \ldots(i)\\
g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)=\left|\frac{8}{5}\right|=\frac{8}{5}\ldots(ii)
\end{gathered}$
Now, $f\left(g\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)-g\left(f\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)=f\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)-g(-2)$
By Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left[\frac{8}{5}\right]-|-2| \\
& =1-2 \\
& =-1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of real solution of $\sqrt{5-\log _2|x|}$ $=3-\log _2|x|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\text {  } & \text {  Let } & \log _2|x| & =t \ldots(i)\\
& \therefore & \sqrt{5-t} & =3-t \\
\Rightarrow & & 5-t & =(3-t)^2 \\
\Rightarrow & & 5-t & =9+t^2-6 t
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad t^2-5 t+4=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(t-4)(t-1)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad t=1 \text { or } 4 \text{[rejected]}\\
& {[\because \sqrt{5-4}=3-4,1 \neq-1]} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \log _2|x|=1 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad|x|=2 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad x= \pm 2
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Total 2 real solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f(x)=\frac{\cos x}{\left[\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right]+\frac{1}{2}}$, where x is not an integral multiple of $\pi$ and [.] denotes the greatest integer function, is
questions.solution: $=-\frac{\cos x}{\left[\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right]+\frac{1}{2}}=-f(x)$ Therefore, $f(x)$ is an odd function.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f: \boldsymbol{R} \rightarrow \boldsymbol{R}$ defined by $f(x)=$ $\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$ is
questions.solution: Given that, $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$ For injective: Let $x_1, x_2 \in \boldsymbol{R}$ such that $f\left(x_1\right)=f\left(x_2\right)$ $\Rightarrow \frac{x_1}{\sqrt{1+x_1^2}}=\frac{x_2}{\sqrt{1+x_2^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{x_1^2}{1+x_1^2}=\frac{x_2^2}{1+x_2^2}$ $\Rightarrow x_1^2+x_1^2 x_2^2=x_2^2+x_1^2 x_2^2 \Rightarrow x_1^2=x_2^2$ $\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$ So, $f(x)$ is injective. For surjective: Let $y=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$ $\Rightarrow y^2\left(1+x^2\right)=x^2 \Rightarrow y^2+y^2 x^2=x^2$ $\Rightarrow x^2\left(1-y^2\right)=y^2 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{y^2}{1-y^2}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{y^2}{1-y^2} \geq 0$ $\therefore y \in(-1,1)$ So, $f(x)$ is not surjective.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f: R \rightarrow R, g: R \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$ are defined by $f(x)=5 x-$ $3, g(x)=x^2+3$, then $g o f^{-1}(3)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $\quad f(x)=5 x-3$ and $g(x)=x^2+3$
Let, $y=f(x), \therefore y=5 x-3$
$y+3=5 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{y+3}{5}$
$\therefore \quad f^{-1}(y)=\frac{y+3}{5} \Rightarrow f^{-1}(x)=\frac{x+3}{5}$
Now, $\quad g(x)=x^2+3$
So, $g \circ f^{-1}(3)=g\left[f^{-1}(3)\right]$
$=g\left(\frac{3+3}{5}\right)=g\left(\frac{6}{5}\right)=\frac{(6)^2}{(5)^2}+3=\frac{36}{25}+3=\frac{111}{25}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The domain of the real valued function
$f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{2 x^2-7 x+5}{3 x^2-5 x-2}}$ is
questions.solution: Given function $f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{2 x^2-7 x+5}{3 x^2-5 x-2}}$ Here, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ should be greater than or equal to 0 . So, $2 x^2-7 x+5=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^2-5 x-2 x+5=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad(x-1)(2 x-5)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=1, \frac{5}{2}$ $3 x^2-5 x-2=0 \Rightarrow 3 x^2-6 x+x-2=0$ $3 x(x-2)+1(x-2)=0 \Rightarrow(x-2)(3 x+1)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=2, \frac{-1}{3}$ When we include $x=\frac{-1}{3}, 2$ then $f(x)$ would give not define value so, we will exclude these values from the domain. When we take values between $\left(-\frac{1}{3}, 1\right)$ and So, domain is $\left(-\infty, \frac{-1}{3}\right) \cup[1,2) \cup\left[\frac{5}{2}, \infty\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a function $\mathbf{f}: \mathbf{R}-\{l\} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}-\{m\}$ defined by $f(x)=\frac{x+3}{x-2}$ is a bijection, then $3 l+2 m=$
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x+3}{x-2}$
$\because \quad f(x)$ is not defined for $x=2$
i.e. domain of $f(x)$ is $\mathrm{R}-\{2\}$
$\therefore l=2$
Now, $y=\frac{x+3}{x-2}$
$x y-2 y=x+3$
$x(y-1)=2 y+3$
$x=\frac{2 y+3}{y-1}$
$y$ can take any value except $y=1$
Co-domain $=\mathrm{R}-\{1\}$
$m=1$
$3 l+2 m=3(2)+2(1)=8$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f(x)=\sin x+\cos x, g(x)=x^2-1$, then $g(f(x))$ is invertible if
questions.solution: Given that, $f(x)=\sin x+\cos x$ $g(x)=x^2-1$ $g[f(x)]=(\sin x+\cos x)^2-1$ $=\sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x+2 \sin x \cos x-1$ $=1+\sin 2 x-1=\sin 2 x$ $\left(\because \sin ^2 x+\cos ^2 x=1, \sin x=2 \sin x \cos x\right)$ Among the given options, $\sin 2 x$ is monotonous (here strictly increasing) in $-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$. So, $g(f(x))$ is invertible in $-\frac{\pi}{4} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{4}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let the function $g:(-\infty, \infty) \rightarrow\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ be given by $g(u)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}$. Then, g is
options: even and is strictly increasing in $(0, \infty)$
odd and is strictly decreasing in $(-\infty, \infty)$
odd and is strictly increasing in $(-\infty, \infty)$
neither even nor odd, but is strictly increasing in $(-\infty, \infty)$
questions.solution:
Given that $g(u)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\therefore \quad g(-u)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(e^{-u}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}=$
$2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{e^u}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}$
$=2 \cot ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}=2\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)\right]-\frac{\pi}{2}$
$=\pi-2 \tan ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}$
$=\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \tan ^{-1}\left(e^u\right)=-g(u)$
$\therefore \quad g$ is an odd function.
Also $g^{\prime}(u)=\frac{2 e^u}{1+e^{2 u}}\gt0, \forall u \in(-\infty, \infty)$
$\therefore g$ is strictly increasing on $(-\infty, \infty)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The domain of the function $f(x)=\sqrt{x-\sqrt{1-x^2}}$ is
questions.solution:
For $f(x)$ to be defined, we must have
$x-\sqrt{1-x^2} \geq 0 \text { or } x \geq \sqrt{1-x^2}>0 \text { or } x^2 \geq 1-x^2$
or $x^2 \geq \frac{1}{2}$.
Now, $x^2 \geq \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow\left(x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow x \leq-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text { or } \quad x \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
Also, $1-x^2 \geq 0$ or $x^2 \leq 1$.
Also, $x^2 \leq 1 \Rightarrow(x-1)(x+1) \leq 0 \Rightarrow-1 \leq x \leq 1$
Thus, $x>0, x^2 \geq \frac{1}{2}$ and $x^2 \leq 1 \Rightarrow x \in\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the function $\mathrm{f}$ in $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{R}-\left\{\frac{2}{3}\right\}$ defined as $f(x)=\frac{4 x+3}{6 x-4}$, then $f^{-1}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given $f(x)=\frac{4 x+3}{6 x-4}$
Let $y=\frac{4 x+3}{6 x-4}$,
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 6 x y-4 y=4 x+3 \Rightarrow x(6 y-4)=3+4 y \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{3+4 y}{6 y-4} \\
& f^{-1}(x)=\frac{3+4 x}{6 x-4}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$, then (fof of) $(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$
fof$=\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{1+x^2}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{2 x^2+1}}$
fofof $=\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{2 x^2+1}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{2 x^2+1}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $g(x)=x^2+x-2$ and $\frac{1}{2} g o f(x)=2 x^2-5 x+2$ then $f(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $\frac{1}{2} go f(x)=2 x^2-5 x+2$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow g(f(x))=4 x^2-10 x+4 \\
& \Rightarrow(f(x))^2+f(x)-2=4 x^2-10 x+4 \\
& \Rightarrow(f(x))^2+f(x)-\left(4 x^2-10 x+6\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+4\left(4 x^2-10 x+6\right)}}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{16 x^2-40 x+25}}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow f(x)=\frac{-1 \pm(4 x-5)}{2}=2 x-3,-2 x+2
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $f(x)=2 x-3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The domain of the function $\mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{12}-x^{9}+x^{4}-x+1}}$ is given by
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{f}(x)$ is defined for $x^{12}-x^{9}+x^{4}-x+1>0$

$\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow x^{4}\left(x^{8}+1\right)-x\left(x^{8}+1\right)+1>0 \\

\Rightarrow\left(x^{8}+1\right) x\left(x^{3}-1\right)+1>0

\end{array}$

If $x \geq 1$ or $x \leq-1$, then the above expres - sion is positive.

If $-1 < x \leq 0$, the above inequality still holds.

If $0 < x < 1$, then

$\begin{array}{l}

x^{12}-x\left(x^{8}+\right)+\left(x^{4}+1\right)>0 \\

\because\left[x^{4}+1>x^{8}+1 \text { and so } x^{4}+1>x\left(x^{8}+1\right)\right]

\end{array}$

The domain of $f=(-\infty, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $[x]$ denote the greatest integer $\leq x$. If $f(x)=$ $[\mathrm{x}]$ and $g(x)=|x|,$ then the value of $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)-\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{f}\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}

&\text { Given that, } f(x)=[x] \text { and } g(x)=|x|\\

&\text { Now, } \mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)=\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)=\left[\frac{8}{5}\right]=1

\end{aligned}$

and $\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{f}\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)=\mathrm{g}\left(\left[-\frac{8}{5}\right]\right)=\mathrm{g}(-2)=|-2|=2$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)-\mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{f}\left(-\frac{8}{5}\right)\right)=1-2=-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The domain of the function $f(x)=\sqrt{x-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$ is
questions.solution:
For $f(x)$ to be defined, we must have $x-\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \geq 0$ or $\quad x \geq \sqrt{1-x^{2}}>0$

$\therefore \quad x^{2} \geq 1-x^{2}$ or $x^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2}$

Also, $1-x^{2} \geq 0$ or $x^{2} \leq 1$.

Now, $x^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow\left(x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \geq 0$

$\Rightarrow x \leq-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ or $x \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

Also, $x^{2} \leq 1 \Rightarrow(x-1)(x+1) \leq 0$

$\Rightarrow-1 \leq x \leq 1$

Thus, $x>0, x^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2}$ and $x^{2} \leq 1$

$\Rightarrow x \in\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right]$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The inverse of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>a</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced></math> (where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>1</mn></math>) is
questions.solution:
Let fx=y, then

ay=x+x2+1

⇒a-y=1x+x2+1

⇒a-y=x-x2+1-1  [rationalising]

∴ ay-a-y=2x

⇒x=12ay-a-y

∴ f-1y=12ay-a-y   ∵ fx=y⇒x=f-1y

⇒f-1x=12ax-a-x  [replacing y by x]

Hence, option (a) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be a function satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, fx+y=fx+2y2+kxy for all x,y∈R

⇒fx+y-fxy=2y+kx for all x∈R

⇒limy→0fx+y-fxy=limy→02y+kx

⇒f'x=kx for all x∈R

⇒fx=kx22+C for all x∈R  [by integration]

But f1=2 and f2=8

∴ 2=k2+C and 8=2k+C

k=4 and C=0

Hence, fx=2x2 for all x∈R

So, option (a) is correct.

options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is continuous for all positive real values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>
questions.solution:
∵ limx→0fx=f0

Let a be any point

Now, at x=a,limx→afx=limh→0fa+h

=limh→0fa+limh→0fh

=fa+f0=fa+0=fa

∴ fx is continuous at x=a, where a is any arbitrary point.

Hence, fx is continuous for all x.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=\frac{a x+b}{c x+d},$ then fof $(x)=x,$ provided that:
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{a x+b}{c x+d}$

fof $(x)=\frac{a\left\{\frac{a x+b}{c x+d}\right\}+b}{c\left\{\frac{a x+b}{c x+d}\right\}+d} \Rightarrow \frac{a^{2} x+a b+b c x+b d}{a c x+b c+c d x+d^{2}}=x$

$\Rightarrow(a c+d c) x^{2}+\left(b c+d^{2}-b c-a^{2}\right) x$

$-a b-b d=0, \quad \forall x \in R$

$\Rightarrow(a+d) c=0, d^{2}-a^{2}=0$ and $(a+d) b=0$

$\Rightarrow a+d=0$

questions.q:
The domain of the function

$f(x)=\frac{\sin ^{-1}(x-3)}{\sqrt{9-x^{2}}}$
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{\sin ^{-1}(x-3)}{\sqrt{9-x^{2}}}$ is defined

if (i) $-1 \leq x-3 \leq 1 \Rightarrow 2 \leq x \leq 4$ and

(ii) $9-x^{2}>0 \Rightarrow-3<x<3$

Taking common solution of (i) and (ii), we get $2 \leq x<3$

$\therefore$ Domain $=[2,3)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The domain of the function $f(x)=\log _{2}\left(-\log _{1 / 2}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{1 / 4}}\right)-1\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$f(x)$ is defined if

$\begin{array}{l} -\log _{1 / 2}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{1 / 4}}\right)-1 > 0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \log _{1 / 2}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{1 / 4}}\right) < -1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1+\frac{1}{x^{1 / 4}} > \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-1} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{x^{1 / 4}} > 1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 0 < x < 1 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$ be a function defined by

$f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n},$ where $m \neq n,$ then
questions.solution:
Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be a function defined by

$f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}$

For any $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \in \mathrm{R}$

Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{y})$

$\Rightarrow \frac{x-m}{x-n}=\frac{y-m}{y-n} \Rightarrow x=y$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}$ is one $-$ one

Let $\alpha \in \mathrm{R}$ such that $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\alpha$

$$

\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{x-m}{x-n} \Rightarrow(x-n) \alpha=x-m

$$

$\Rightarrow x \alpha-n \alpha=x-m$

$$

\Rightarrow x \alpha-x=n \alpha-m

$$

$\Rightarrow x(\alpha-1)=n \alpha-m$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \alpha-\mathrm{m}}{\alpha-1} \cdot$ for $\alpha=1, \mathrm{x} \notin \mathrm{R}$

So, $\mathrm{f}$ is not onto.
