questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance from the point (3,4,5) to the point where the line $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{z-5}{2}$ meets the plane $x+y+z=17$ is
questions.solution:
Anypoint on the line is $(r+3,2 r+4,2 r+5)$. It lies on the plane $x+y+z=17$ $\therefore(r+3)+(2 r+4)+(2 r+5)=17$ i.e $r=1$

Thus the point of intersection of the plane and the line is (4,6,7) Required distance $=$ distance between (3,4,5) and (4,6,7)

$=\sqrt{\left\{(4-3)^{2}+(6-4)^{2}+(7-5)^{2}\right\}}=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of plane containing line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>z</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Equation of plane containing line x-y-1=0 and z-1=0 is (x-y-1)+λ(z-1)=0

⇒ x-y+λz-1-λ=0   ...(i)

Since, this plane is parallel to line x2-z3=1 and y=3

x-22=y-30=z3   ...(ii)

∴ 2(1)+(0)(-1)+3(λ)=0

⇒ λ=-2/3

Putting the value of λ in Eq. (i), we get

x-y-23z-1+23=0⇒3x-3y-2z=1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle between the lines whose direction cosine satisfy the equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
questions.solution:
We have, 

l+m+n=0   ...(i)

and l2=m2+n2   ...(ii)

Putting the value of l in Eq. (ii), we get

(m+n)2=m2+n2

⇒ m2+n2+2mn=m2+n2

⇒ mn=0

⇒ m=0,n=0

when m=0,l=-n

∴ Direction cosines are -12,0,12.

When n=0,l=-m

∴ Direction cosines are -12,12,0.

Angle between lines are 

cosθ=-12×-12+0×12+12×0

cosθ=12,θ=π/3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An equation of plane parallel to plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and at a unit distance from the origin is
questions.solution:
Equation of plane parallel to the plane 

x-2y+2z-5=0 is x-2y+2z+λ=0

Since, this plane is unit distance from origin

∴ 1=λ1+4+4

|λ|=3⇒λ=±3

∴ Equation of plane is

x-2y+2z=3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Reflection of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow><mn>1</mn></mfrac></math> in the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
Given line x-1-1=y-23=z-41 is passes through P(1,2,4).

To find the reflection of line we need one more point on the line clearly M(0,5,5) also lie on line.

Let N(α,β,γ) be the reflection M in the x+y+z=7

∴ α2+β+52+γ+52=7

⇒ α+β+γ=4

Also, MN is perpendicular, i.e. parallel to the normal of the plane

∴ α1=β-51=γ-51=λ

⇒ α=λ,β=λ+5,γ=λ+5

∴ λ+λ+5+λ+5=4

⇒ λ=-2

∴ N=(-2,3,5)

Equation PN is x-1-3=y-21=z-4-1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line makes angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>γ</mi></math> with the coordinate axes. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, cos2α+cos2β+cos2γ=1

⇒cos2α+cos2π2-α+cos2γ=1  [given α+β=π2]

⇒cos2α+sin2α+cos2γ=1

⇒1+cos2γ=1

⇒cos2γ=0

⇒cosγ=0

∴ cosα+cosβ+cosγ2=cosα+sinα2

=1+2sinα·cosα=1+sin2α

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the sum of squares of distances of a point from the planes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>p</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> then locus of the point is
questions.solution:
Let distances of a point Px,y,z from the planes x+y+z=0, x-z=0 and x-2y+z=0 are x+y+z3, x-z2 and x-2y+z6 respectively, then the sum of the squares of distances, is as

⇒x+y+z32+x-z22+x-2y+z62=p2

⇒2x+y+z2+3x-z2+x-2y+z2=6p2

⇒  2x2+2y2+2z2+4xy+4yz+4zx+3x2+3z2-6xz+x2+4y2+z2-4xy-4yz+2xz=6p2

⇒6x2+6y+6z2=6p2

⇒x2+y2+z2=p2

Hence, option (d) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>λ</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> is

When line x-x1a1=y-y1b1=z-z1c1 is perpendicular to plane ax+by+cz+d=0, then

a1a=b1b=c1c

∴ λ2=12=-4-8

⇒λ=1

Hence, option (a) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the planes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>·</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are perpendicular to each other, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

r→·λi^+5j^-k^=5

r→·2i^-λj^+3k^=0

We know that two planes r→·n→1=d1 and r→·n→2=d2 are perpendicular if

n→1·n→2=0

Therefore,

λi^+5j^-k^·2i^-λj^+3k^=0

⇒2λ-5λ-3=0

⇒-3λ-3=0

⇒λ=-1

Therefore, λ2+λ=0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is the angle between the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> equals to
questions.solution:
Let 

a→=i^+j^+k^

b→=4i^-k^

Then,

a→=12+12+12=3

b→=42+02+-42=42

Now,

cosθ=a→·b→a→b→

⇒cosθ=i^+j^+k^·4i^-4k^a→b→

⇒cosθ=4-4a→b→=0

Hence, sinθ=1-cos2θ=1

Therefore, sinθ+cosθ=1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of perpendicular drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>)</mo></math> to line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, is
questions.solution:
Let P be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from A(2,3,4) to the given line l.

Then, x-4-2=y6=z-1-3

Now, any point on the line l is given by

x=4-2λ,y=6λ,z=1-3λ

The coordinates of P are (4-2λ,6λ,1-3λ)

The direction ratios of AP are

(4-2λ-2,6λ-3,1-3λ-4)

i.e. (2-2λ,6λ-3,-3-3λ)

And the direction ratios of l are-2,6 and -3.

Given, AP⊥l

∴ -2(2-2λ)+6(6λ-3)-3(-3-3λ)=0

⇒ λ=1349

∴ AP2=(4-2λ-2)2+(6λ-3)2+(1-3λ-4)2

=22-26λ+49λ2

Put λ=1349, we get

AP2=90949⇒AP=37101

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The image of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math> in the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> is
questions.solution:
Let P(1,6,3) be the given point and L be the foot of perpendicular from P to the given line. The coordinates of a general point on the given line are

x-01=y-12=z-23=λ   (say)

i.e. x=λ

y=2λ+1, z=3λ+2 

If the coordinates of L are (λ,2λ+1,3λ+2), then the direction ratios of PL are (λ-1,2λ-5,3λ-1).

Since, the direction ratios of given line which is perpendicular to PL, are 1,2 and 3. Therefore, (λ-1)1+(2λ-5)2+(3λ-1)3=0,which gives λ=1.

Hence, coordinates of L are (1,3,5).

Let Qx1,y1,z1 be the image of P(1,6,3) in the given line. Then, L is the mid-point of PQ.

Therefore, x1+12=1,y1+62=3,z1+32=5

⇒x1=1,y=0,z=7

Hence, the image of (1,6,3) in the given line is (1,0,7).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distances of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>)</mo></math> from the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> measured along a straight line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>z</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>k</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given equation of plane is x+y+z=5.

The distance measured along the line x=y=z.

Direction ratios of the given line is (1,1,1).

So, the equation of line PQ is

x-11=y+51=z-91=λ

⇒ x=λ+1,y=λ-5,z=λ+9

lies on the plane x+y+z=5

∴ (λ+1)-(-λ+5)+(λ+9)=5

⇒ λ=-10

The coordinate of Q is (-9,-15,-1) and the coordinate of P is (1,-5,9)

PQ=(10)2+(10)2+(10)2=103

∴ 23k=103⇒k=5

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Two lines $L_{1}: x=5, \frac{y}{3-\alpha}=\frac{z}{-2}$

$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \mathrm{x}=\alpha, \frac{\mathrm{y}}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2-\alpha}$ are coplanar. Then, $\alpha \mathrm{can}$ take value (s)
questions.solution:
The equations of given lines can be written

as

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{L}_{1}: \mathrm{x}-5=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{3-\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{-2} \\

\mathrm{~L}_{2}: \mathrm{x}-\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2-\alpha}

\end{array}

$$

Since, these lines are coplanar.

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\text { Therefore, }\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

5-\alpha & 0-0 & 0-0 \\

0 & 3-\alpha & -2 \\

0 & -1 & 2-\alpha

\end{array}\right|=0 \\

\Rightarrow \quad(5-\alpha)(3-\alpha)(2-\alpha)-2=0 \\

\Rightarrow \quad(5-\alpha)\left(6-3 \alpha-2 \alpha+\alpha^{2}-2\right]=0 \\

\Rightarrow \quad(5-\alpha)\left(\alpha^{2}-5 \alpha+4\right]=0 \\

\Rightarrow \quad(5-\alpha)(\alpha-1)(\alpha-4)=0 \\

\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=1,4,5

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line makes the same angle $\theta$ with each of the $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Z}$ -axes. If the angle $\beta$, which it makes with Y-axis, is such that $\sin ^{2} \beta=3 \sin ^{2} \theta,$ then $\cos ^{2} \theta$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $l, \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{n}$ be the direction cosines. Then, $l=\cos \theta, \mathrm{m}=\cos \beta, \mathrm{n}=\cos \theta$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\text { we have } l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1 \\

\Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1 \\

\Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \theta+1-\sin ^{2} \beta=1 \\

\Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \theta-\sin ^{2} \beta=0 \\

\Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \theta-3 \sin ^{2} \beta=0 \\

\quad\left[\because \sin ^{2} \beta=3 \sin ^{2} 0(\text { given })\right] \\

\Rightarrow \tan ^{2} \theta=2 / 3 \\

\therefore \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{1}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}=\frac{1}{1+2 / 3}=\frac{3}{5}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Find the angle between the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z-3}{6}$

and the plane $10 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}-11 \mathrm{z}=3$
questions.solution:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between the line and the normal to the plane. Converting the given equations into vector form, we have $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ and

$$

\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-11 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=3

$$

Here, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}=10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-11 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\sin \phi=\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) \cdot(10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-11 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}} \sqrt{10^{2}+2^{2}+11^{2}}}\right| \\

=\left|\frac{-40}{7 \times 15}\right|=\left|\frac{-8}{21}\right|=\frac{8}{21} \text { or } \phi=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{21}\right)

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the right bisector plane of the segment joining (2,3,4) and (6,7,8) is
questions.solution:
If the given points be $A(2,3,4)$ and $B$ $(6,7,8),$ then their mid-point $\mathrm{N}(4,5,6)$ must lie on the plane. The direction ratios of $\mathrm{AB}$ are $4,4,4,$ i.e. 1,1,1
$\therefore$ The required plane passes through $\mathrm{N}(4,5,6)$ and is normal to $\mathrm{AB}$. Thus its equation is $$ 1(x-4)+1(y-5)+1(z-6)=0 \Rightarrow x+y+z=15 $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coordinates of the point where the line through the points $\mathrm{A}(3,4,1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(5,1,6)$ crosses the XY-plane are
questions.solution:
Equation of the line through the given points is $\frac{x-3}{5-3}=\frac{y-4}{1-4}=\frac{z-1}{6-1}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y-4}{-3}=\frac{z-1}{5}$

Any point on this line can be taken as $(3+$ $2 \lambda, 4-3 \lambda, 1+5 \lambda)$

If this point lies on XY-plane then the z-coordinate is zero

$$

\Rightarrow 1+5 \lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{5}

$$

Thus the required coordinates of the point are

$$

\left(3-\frac{2}{5}, 4-3\left(-\frac{1}{5}\right), 0\right) \equiv\left(\frac{13}{5}, \frac{23}{5}, 0\right)

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the angle between the two planes $2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{z}=5$ and $3 x-6 y-2 z=7$
questions.solution:
The angle between two planes is the angle between their normals. From the equation of the planes, the normal vectors are $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{1}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{2}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$

Therefore, $\cos \theta=\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{1} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{2}}{\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{1}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{N}}_{2}\right|}\right|$

$$

=\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \cdot(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{4+1+4} \sqrt{9+36+4}}\right|=\left(\frac{4}{21}\right)

$$

Hence, $\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{21}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the value of $n$ so that the angle between the lines having direction ratios (1,1,1) and $(1,-1, n)$ is $60^{\circ} ?$
questions.solution:
If $\left(l_{1}, m_{1}, n_{1}\right)$ and $\left(l_{2}, m_{2}, n_{2}\right)$ are the direction ratios then angle between the lines is
$\cos \theta=\frac{l_{1} l_{2}+m_{1} m_{2}+n_{1} n_{2}}{\sqrt{l_{1}^{2}+m_{1}^{2}+n_{1}^{2}} \sqrt{l_{2}^{2}+m_{2}^{2}+n_{2}^{2}}}$
Here $l_{1}=1, m_{1}=1, n_{1}=1$ and
$l_{2}=1, m_{2}=-1, n_{2}=n$ and $\theta=60^{\circ}$
$\therefore \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{1 \times 1+1 \times(-1)+1 \times n}{\sqrt{h^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2} \times} \sqrt{1^{2}+1^{2}+y^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3} \sqrt{2+n^{2}}} \Rightarrow 3\left(2+n^{2}\right)=4 n^{2}$
$\Rightarrow n^{2}=6 \Rightarrow n=\pm \sqrt{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The foot of the perpendicular from the point (7,14,5) to the plane $2 \mathrm{x}+4 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z}=2$ are
questions.solution:
We know that the length of the perpendicular from the point $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right)$ to the plane $a x+b y+c z+d=0$ is $\frac{\left|a x_{1}+b y_{1}+c z_{1}+d\right|}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}$
and the co-ordinate $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ of the foot of the $\perp$ are given by $\frac{\alpha-x_{1}}{a}=\frac{\beta-y_{1}}{b}=\frac{\gamma-z_{1}}{c}$
$=-\left(\frac{a x_{1}+b y_{1}+c z_{1}+d}{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}\right)$ ...(1)
In the given ques, $x_{1}=7, y_{1}=14, z_{1}=5$,
$a=2 b=4, c=-1, d=-2$
By putting these values in (1), we get $\frac{\alpha-7}{2}=\frac{\beta-14}{4}=\frac{\gamma-5}{-1}=-\frac{63}{21}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=1, \beta=2$ and $\gamma=8$
Hence, foot of $\perp$ is (1,2,8)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the coordinates of the point where the line joining the points (2,-3,1) and (3,-4,-5) cuts the plane $2 x+y+z=7$
questions.solution:
The direction ratios of the line are $3-2,-4-(-3),-5-1$ i.e. 1,-1,-6
Hence equation of the line joining the given points is $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+3}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{-6}=r($ say $)$
Coordinates of any point on this line are $(r+2,-r-3,-6 r+1)$
If this point lies on the given plane $2 x+y+z=7$ then $2(r+2)+(-r-3)+(-6 r+1)=7 \Rightarrow r=-1$
Coordinates of any point on this line are $(-1+2,-(-1)-3,-6(-1)+1)$ i.e. (1,-2,7)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Gives the line \(L: \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-3}{-1}\) and the plane \(\pi: x-2 y-z=0\). Of the following assertions, the only one that is always true is
questions.solution:
Since \(3(1)+2(-2)+(-1)(-1)=3-4+1=0\)
\(\therefore\) Given line is \(\perp\) to the normal to the plane i.e., given line is parallel to the given plane.
Also, \((1,-1,3)\) lies on the plane \(x-2 y-z=0\) if \(1-2(-1)-3=0\) i.e., \(1+2-3=0\) which is true \(\therefore\) L lies in plane \(\pi\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the middle points of sides \(\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA} ~\&~ \mathrm{AB}\) of triangle \(A B C\) are respectively \(D, E, F\) then position vector of centre of triangle \(\mathrm{DEF}\), when position vector of \(A, B, C\) are respectively \(\hat{i}+\hat{j}\), \(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{i}}\) is -
questions.solution:
The position vector of points \(D, E, F\) are respectively
\(\frac{\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{j}}{2}+\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{i}+\frac{\mathrm{k}+\mathrm{j}}{2} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{k}}{2}+\mathrm{j}\)
So, position vector of centre of \(\triangle \mathrm{DEF}\)
\(=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{j}}{2}+\mathrm{k}+\mathrm{i} \frac{\mathrm{k}+\mathrm{j}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{k}}{2}+\mathrm{j}\right]=\frac{2}{3}[\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{j}+\mathrm{k}]\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The perpendicular distance of \(P(1,2,3)\) from the line \(\frac{x-6}{3}=\frac{y-7}{2}=\frac{z-7}{-2}\) is
questions.solution:
The point \(\mathrm{A}(6,7,7)\) is on the line. Let the perpendicular from \(\mathrm{P}\) meet the line in \(\mathrm{L}\). Then \(\mathrm{AP}^2=(6-1)^2+(7-2)^2+(7-3)^2=66\) Also \(\mathrm{AL}=\) projection of AP on line
\(\begin{aligned} & \left(\text {actual d.c.'s } \frac{3}{\sqrt{17}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{17}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{17}}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & (6-1) \cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt{17}}+(7-2) \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{17}}+(7-3) \frac{-2}{\sqrt{17}} \\ & =\sqrt{17} \end{aligned}\) \(\therefore \perp\) distance \(\mathrm{d}\) of \(\mathrm{P}\) from the line is given by \(\mathrm{d}^2=\mathrm{AP}^2-\mathrm{AL}^2=66-17=49 \text { so that } \mathrm{d}=7\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solution of the differential equation \(\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=\sin x\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=\sin x\left[\text { Type } \frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q\right] \\
& e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{\log x}=x
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) Sol. is \(\mathrm{yx}=\int \mathrm{x} \sin \mathrm{xdx}+\mathrm{C}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =x(-\cos x)-\int 1 \cdot(-\cos x) d x+C \\
& =-x \cos x+\sin x+C \Rightarrow x(y+\cos x)=\sin x+C
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the line \(\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-k}{2}\) lies in the plane \(2 x-4 y+z=7\), then the value of \(k\) is
questions.solution:
The point \((4,2, k)\) on the line also lies on the plane \(2 x-4 y+z=7\)
So, \(8-8+\mathrm{k}=7 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=7\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of solutions of equations $\sin 9 \theta=\sin \theta$ in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {  Given, } \quad \sin 9 \theta=\sin \theta \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \sin 9 \theta-\sin \theta=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos \left(\frac{9 \theta+\theta}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{9 \theta-\theta}{2}\right)=0 \\
& {\left[\because \sin C-\sin D=2 \cos \left(\frac{C+D}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{C-D}{2}\right)\right]} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 \cos 5 \theta \sin 4 \theta=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \text { Either } \cos 5 \theta=0 \text { or } \sin 4 \theta=0 \text {, also } \theta \in[0,2 \pi] \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 5 \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2} \\
& \text { or } \quad 4 \theta=n \pi \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\pi}{10} \text { or } \theta=\frac{n \pi}{4} \\
& \therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{10}, \frac{3 \pi}{10}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{7 \pi}{10}, \frac{9 \pi}{10}, \frac{11 \pi}{10}, \frac{13 \pi}{10}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \frac{17 \pi}{10}, \frac{19 \pi}{10} \\
& \text { or } \quad 0, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}, 2 \pi \\
& \therefore \text { Total number of solutions }=17 \\
& \left(\because \frac{\pi}{2} \text { and } \frac{3 \pi}{2} \text { are common }\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The range of $(8 \sin \theta+6 \cos \theta)^2+2$ is
questions.solution:
$-10 \leq 8 \sin \theta+6 \cos \theta \leq 10$
$\Rightarrow 0 \leq(8 \sin \theta+6 \cos \theta)^2 \leq 100$
$\Rightarrow 2 \leq(8 \sin \theta+6 \cos \theta)^2+2 \leq 102$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: General solution of $\tan 5 \theta=\cot 2 \theta$ is
questions.solution:
We have $\tan 5 \theta=\cot 2 \theta$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \tan 5 \theta=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \theta\right) \ldots \\
& \left[\because \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\cot \theta\right] \\
& \Rightarrow 5 \theta=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \theta \Rightarrow 7 \theta=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{n \pi}{7}+\frac{\pi}{14} .
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sec ^2 \theta=\frac{4}{3}$, then the general value of $\theta$ is
questions.solution:
We have $\sec ^2 \theta=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \Rightarrow$
$\theta=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{6} \ldots \ldots \ldots$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sum of all the solution of the equation $\cos \theta \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\theta\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}-\theta\right)=\frac{1}{4}, \theta \in[0,6 \pi]$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \cos \theta\left[\cos 120^{\circ}+\cos 2 \theta\right]=1 \\
\Rightarrow & 2 \cos \theta\left(-\frac{1}{2}+2 \cos ^2 \theta-1\right)=1 \\
\Rightarrow & 4 \cos ^3 \theta-3 \cos \theta-1=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \cos 3 \theta=1=\cos 0 \\
\Rightarrow & 3 \theta=2 n \pi \text { or } \theta=\frac{2 n \pi}{3}, n \in z
\end{aligned}
$$
Given the values so that $2 n$ does not exceed 18 .
Hence, the sum $=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \sum_1^9 n=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \times \frac{9(9+1)}{2}=30 \pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y=\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}$, then value of $\frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha}$

$=\frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha} \cdot \frac{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}$

$=\frac{(1+\sin \alpha)^{2}-\cos ^{2} \alpha}{(1+\sin \alpha)(1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha)}$

$=\frac{\left(1+\sin ^{2} \alpha+2 \sin \alpha\right)-\left(1-\sin ^{2} \alpha\right)}{(1+\sin \alpha)(1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha)}$

$=\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}=y$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\sin \left[\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{4}\right], \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}$ is
questions.solution:
$\sin \left[\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{4}\right]=(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \sin \left[(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$

$\left[\because \sin (\mathrm{n} \pi+\theta)=(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \sin \theta\right]$

$\begin{aligned} &=(-1)^{\mathrm{n}}(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \sin \frac{\pi}{4} \\ \therefore \sin &\left.\left[(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \theta\right]=(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \sin \theta\right] \\ &=(-1)^{2 \mathrm{n}} \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=\sin \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least positive non-integral solution of the equation $\sin \pi\left(x^{2}+x\right)=\sin \pi x^{2}$ is
options: irrational of the form $\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{p}}-1}{4},$ where $\mathrm{p}$ is an odd integer
irrational of the form $\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{p}}+1}{4},$ where $\mathrm{p}$ is an even integer
questions.solution:
We have, $\sin \pi\left(x^{2}+x\right)=\sin \pi x^{2}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \pi\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}\right)=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \pi \mathrm{x}^{2}$

$\therefore$ Either $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{x}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{~m} \in \mathrm{I}$

or $\quad \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{k}-\mathrm{x}^{2},$ where $\mathrm{k}$ is an odd integer

$\Rightarrow \quad 2 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{k}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1+8 \mathrm{k}}}{4}$

For least positive non-integral solution

is $x=\frac{1}{2},$ when $k=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> for which the equality <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>α</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, holds for all real <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> which are not integral multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
We have, sinλαsinα-cosλαcosα=λ-1

sinλαcosα-cosλαsinα=λ-1sinαcosα

sinλ-1α=λ-1sinαcosα

sinλ-1α=λ-12sin2α

It is possible, when λ-1=0⇒λ=1

and λ-12=1⇒λ=3

∴ λ has only two solution.

⇒sinx+2cost·cosx=2

For real solution

Hence, option (a) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

sinx-3sin2x+sin3x=cosx-3cos2x+cos3x

⇒sinx+sin3x-3sin2x=cosx+cos3x-3cos2x

⇒2sin2xcosx-3sin2x-2cos2xcosx+3cos2x=0

⇒sin2x(2cosx-3)-cos2x(2cosx-3)=0

⇒(sin2x-cos2x)(2cosx-3)=0

⇒ sin2x=cos2x  [∵cosx≠3/2]

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}+\sin \theta_{3}=3,$ then $\cos \theta_{1}+\cos \theta_{2}$$+\cos \theta^{3}=$
questions.solution:
Since, $\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}+\sin \theta_{3}=3$

$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & \sin \theta_{1}=\sin \theta_{2}=\sin \theta_{3}=1 \Rightarrow \theta_{1}=\theta_{2}=\theta_{3}= \\ & \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \therefore & \cos \theta_{1}+\cos \theta_{2}+\cos \theta_{3}=0\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\tan (\cot x)=\cot (\tan x)$, then $\sin 2 x$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
Given, $\tan (\cot x)=\cot (\tan x)=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan x\right)$

$\Rightarrow \cot x=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan x$

$\Rightarrow \cot x+\tan x=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sin x \cos x}=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sin 2 x}=\frac{n \pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4}$

$\Rightarrow \sin 2 x=\frac{1}{\frac{n \pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{4}{(2 n+1) \pi}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the equation $\sin 2 x+2 \sin x+2 \cos x+1=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\sin 2 \mathrm{x}+2 \sin \mathrm{x}+2 \cos \mathrm{x}+1=0$

$\Rightarrow 1+\sin 2 \mathrm{x}+2(\sin \mathrm{x}+\cos \mathrm{x})=0$

$\Rightarrow(\sin x+\cos x)^{2}+2(\sin x+\cos x)=0$

$\Rightarrow(\sin x+\cos x)(\sin x+\cos x+2)=0$

$\therefore \sin x+\cos x=0$ or $\sin x+\cos x=-2$

But, $\sin x+\cos x=-2$ is inadmissible.

Since, $|\sin x| \leq 1,|\cos x| \leq 1$

$\therefore \sin x+\cos x=0 \Rightarrow \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=0$

$\Rightarrow x+\frac{\pi}{4}=n \pi \Rightarrow x=n \pi-\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $(2 \cos x-1)(3+2 \cos x)=0$ in the interval $0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi$ is
questions.solution:
We have $(2 \cos x-1)(3+2 \cos x)=0$

If $2 \cos x-1=0,$ then $\cos x=\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore x=\pi / 3,5 \pi / 3$

If $3+2 \cos x=0,$ the $\cos x=-3 / 2$

which is not possible.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$\quad$ If $\cos 7 \theta=\cos \theta-\sin 4 \theta,$ then the general value

of $\theta$ is
questions.solution:
$\cos 7 \theta=\cos \theta-\sin 4 \theta$

$\Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=\cos \theta-\cos 7 \theta$

$\Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta=2 \sin 4 \theta \sin 3 \theta$

$\Rightarrow \sin 4 \theta(1-2 \sin 3 \theta)=0$

$\therefore \sin 4 \theta=0$ or $\sin 3 \theta=\frac{1}{2}$

$\Rightarrow 4 \theta=n \pi$ or $3 \theta=n \pi+(-1)^{n} \frac{\pi}{6}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{n \pi}{4}$ or $\frac{n \pi}{3}+(-1)^{n} \frac{\pi}{18}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\sin \theta=-\frac{1}{2}\) and \(\tan \theta=1 / \sqrt{3}\), then \(\theta=\)
questions.solution:
We shall first consider values of \(\theta\) between 0
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { and } 2 \pi \sin \theta=-\frac{1}{2}=-\sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\sin \left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \\
& \text { or } \sin (2 \pi-\pi / 6) \\
& \therefore \theta=7 \pi / 6 ; 11 \pi / 6 \\
& \tan \theta=1 / \sqrt{3}=\tan (\pi / 6) \text { or } \tan (\pi+\pi / 6) \\
& \therefore \theta=\pi / 6,7 \pi / 6
\end{aligned}\)
The value of \(\theta\) which satisfies both the equations is \(7 \pi / 6\)
Hence the general value of \(\theta\) is \(2 n \pi+7 \pi / 6\) where \(\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(12 \cot ^2 \theta-31 \operatorname{coses} \theta+32=0\), then the value of \(\sin \theta\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& 12 \cot ^2 \theta-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+32=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 12\left(\operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta-1\right)-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+32=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 12 \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+20=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 12 \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta-16 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-15 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+20=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad(4 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-5)(3 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-4)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \operatorname{cosec} \theta=\frac{5}{4}, \frac{4}{3} ; \therefore \sin \theta=\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\tan 20^{\circ}+\tan 40^{\circ}+\sqrt{3} \tan 20^{\circ} \tan 40^{\circ}\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{3} & =\tan 60^{\circ}=\tan \left(40^{\circ}+20^{\circ}\right) \\
& =\frac{\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 20^{\circ}}{1-\tan 40^{\circ} \tan 20^{\circ}} \\
\therefore \quad & \sqrt{3}-\sqrt{3} \tan 40^{\circ} \tan 20^{\circ} \\
& =\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 20^{\circ}
\end{aligned}\)
Hence \(\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 20^{\circ}+\sqrt{3} \tan 40^{\circ} \tan 20^{\circ}\) \(=\sqrt{3}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\), then (fof of \()(x)\) is
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^2}}\)
\((f o f)(x)=\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{1+x^2}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{2 x^2+1}}\)
\((fofof)(x)=\frac{\frac{x}{\sqrt{2 x^2+1}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{2 x^2+1}}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^2}}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(y=\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}\), then value of \(\frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha}\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha} \\
& =\frac{1-\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\sin \alpha} \cdot \frac{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha} \\
& =\frac{(1+\sin \alpha)^2-\cos ^2 \alpha}{(1+\sin \alpha)(1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha)} \\
& =\frac{2 \sin \alpha(1+\sin \alpha)}{(1+\sin \alpha)(1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha)} \\
& =\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha+\sin \alpha}=y
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A, B$ and $C$ are the angles of a triangle and $\tan \frac{A}{2}=\frac{1}{3}, \tan \frac{B}{2}=\frac{2}{3}$. Then, $\tan \frac{C}{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=180^{\circ} \text { (Angle sum property of a triangle) } \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}=180^{\circ}-\mathrm{C} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{A+B}{2}=90^{\circ}-\frac{C}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \left(\frac{A}{2}+\frac{B}{2}\right)=\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\frac{C}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\tan A / 2+\tan B / 2}{1-\tan A / 2 \tan B / 2}=\cot C / 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{1}{3}+\frac{2}{3}}{1-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{3}}=\cot C / 2 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-\frac{2}{9}}=\cot C / 2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{9}{7}=\cot C / 2 \Rightarrow \tan C / 2=7 / 9\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sum of all values of $x$ in $[0,2 \pi]$, for which $\sin x+\sin 2 x+\sin 3 x+\sin 4 x=0$, is equal to :
questions.solution:
$(\sin x+\sin 4 x)+(\sin 2 x+\sin 3 x)=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 \sin \frac{5 x}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{3 x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{5 x}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{x}{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 \sin \frac{5 x}{2}\left\{\cos \frac{3 x}{2}+\cos \frac{x}{2}\right\}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 \sin \frac{5 x}{2}\left\{2 \cos x \cos \frac{x}{2}\right\}=0$
$2 \sin \frac{5 x}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{5 x}{2}=0, \pi, 2 \pi, 3 \pi, 4 \pi, 5 \pi, \ldots$
$\Rightarrow x=0, \frac{2 \pi}{5}, \frac{4 \pi}{5}, \frac{6 \pi}{5}, \frac{8 \pi}{5}, 2 \pi$
$\cos \frac{x}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{2}=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow x=\pi ;$
$\cos x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}$
So, sum $=6 \pi+\pi+2 \pi=9 \pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ then
options: $\tan A \tan B+\tan B \tan C+\tan C \tan A=1$
$\cot A+\cot B+\cot C=\cot A \cot B \cot C$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{A} \Rightarrow \tan (\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C})=\cot \mathrm{A}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{\tan \mathrm{B}+\tan \mathrm{C}}{1-\tan \mathrm{B} \tan \mathrm{C}}=\cot \mathrm{A}$

$\Rightarrow \tan \mathrm{A} \tan \mathrm{B}+\tan \mathrm{B} \tan \mathrm{C}+\tan \mathrm{C} \tan \mathrm{A}=1$

$\Rightarrow \tan \mathrm{A} \tan \mathrm{B} \tan \mathrm{C}(\cot \mathrm{C}+\cot \mathrm{A}+\cot \mathrm{B})=1$

$\Rightarrow \cot \mathrm{A}+\cot \mathrm{B}+\cot \mathrm{C}=\cot \mathrm{A} \cot \mathrm{B} \cot \mathrm{C}$

Again $\cos 2 \mathrm{~A}+\cos 2 \mathrm{~B}+\cos 2 \mathrm{C}$

$=2 \cos (\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) \cos (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})+\cos 2 \mathrm{C}$

$=2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-C\right) \cos (A-B)+1-2 \sin ^{2} C$

$=2 \sin C[\cos (A-B)-\sin C]+1$

$=2 \sin \mathrm{C}\left[\cos (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}-(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B})\right]+1$

$\begin{array}{l}

=2 \sin C[\cos (A-B)-\cos (A+B)]+1 \\

=4 \sin A \sin B \sin C+1

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $\sin ^{4} x-(k+2) \sin ^{2} x-(k+3)=0$ possesses a solution if
questions.solution:
We have, $\sin ^{4} x-(k+2) \sin ^{2} x-(k+3)=0$

$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} x=\frac{(k+2) \pm \sqrt{(k+2)^{2}+4(k+3)}}{2}$

$=\frac{(k+2) \pm(k+4)}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} x=k+3\left(\because \sin ^{2} x=-1\right.$ is not possible)

Since $0 \leq \sin ^{2} x \leq 1$

$\therefore 0 \leq \mathrm{k}+3 \leq 1$ or $-3 \leq \mathrm{k} \leq-2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> and let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>13</mn></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>≤</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
questions.solution:
0≤α,β≤π4; α+β≤π2

cos(α+β)=4/5⇒tan(α+β)=3/4

sin(α-β)=5/13⇒α-β∈θ1

and tan(α-β)=5/12

tan2α=tan(α+β+α-β)

=tan(α+β)+tan(α-β)1-tan(α+β)tan(α-β)

=3/4+5/121-3/4×512=5633

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $A$ and $B$ are positive acute angles satisfying $3 \cos ^{2} A+2 \cos ^{2} B=4$ and $\frac{3 \sin A}{\sin B}=\frac{2 \cos B}{\cos A}$

Then the value of $\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B}$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Given, $3 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~A}+2 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~B}=4$

$\Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~B}-1=4-3 \cos ^{2} \mathrm{~A}-1$

$\Rightarrow \cos 2 \mathrm{~B}=3\left(1-\cos ^{2} \mathrm{~A}\right)=3 \sin ^{2} \mathrm{~A} \ldots(1)$

and $2 \cos \mathrm{B} \sin \mathrm{B}=3 \sin \mathrm{A} \cos \mathrm{A}$

$\sin 2 \mathrm{~B}=3 \sin \mathrm{A} \cos \mathrm{A}$

Now, $\cos (\mathrm{A}+2 \mathrm{~B})=\cos \mathrm{A} \cos 2 \mathrm{~B}-\sin \mathrm{A}$

$\sin 2 \mathrm{~B}$

$=\cos A\left(3 \sin ^{2} A\right)-\sin A(3 \sin A \cos A)=0$

[using eqs. (1) and (2)] $\Rightarrow A+2 B=\frac{\pi}{2}$

questions.q: If $\frac{\cos A}{\cos B}=n, \frac{\sin A}{\sin B}=m$, then the value of $\left(m^{2}-n^{2}\right) \sin ^{2} B$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\theta\right)-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-\theta\right)=$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\theta\right)-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-\theta\right) \\ &=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\theta+\frac{\pi}{6}-\theta\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}+\theta-\frac{\pi}{6}+\theta\right) \\ &=\cos \frac{2 \pi}{6} \cos 2 \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 \theta \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An arch of a bridge is semi-elliptical with major axis horizontal. If the length the base is 9 meter and the highest part of the bridge is 3 meter from the horizontal; the best approximation of the height of the arch. 2 meter from the centre of the base is
questions.solution: The equation of the ellipse is $$ \frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1 $$ Where centre is assumed as origin and base as $x$ -axis. Put $x=2,$ we get $$ \frac{16}{81}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{9}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{\sqrt{65}}{3} \approx \frac{8}{3} \mathrm{~m} $$ (approximately)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $x \sin ^{3} \theta+y \cos ^{3} \theta=\sin \theta \cos \theta$ and

$x \sin \theta=y \cos \theta,$ then $x^{2}+y^{2}=$
questions.solution:
$x \sin ^{3} \theta+y \cos ^{3} \theta=\sin \theta \cos \theta$

and $x \sin \theta=y \cos \theta$

Equation (i) may be written as $x \sin \theta \cdot \sin ^{2} \theta+y \cos ^{3} \theta=\sin \theta \cos \theta$

$\Rightarrow y \cos \theta \sin ^{2} \theta+y \cos ^{3} \theta=\sin \theta \cos \theta$

$\Rightarrow y \cos \theta\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)=\sin \theta \cos \theta$

$\Rightarrow y \cos \theta=\sin \theta \cos \theta \therefore y=\sin \theta \quad \ldots$

Putting the value of y from

(iii) in (ii), we get $\mathrm{x} \sin \theta=\sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\cos \theta \quad \ldots$ (iv)

Squaring (iii) and (iv) and adding, we get $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An object is obseved from the points $A, B$ and C lying in a horizontal straight line which passes directly underneath the object. The angular elevation at $\mathrm{B}$ is twice that at $\mathrm{A}$ and at $\mathrm{C}$ three times that at $\mathrm{A} .$ It $\mathrm{AB}=a, \mathrm{BC}=b$, then the height of the object is
questions.solution: Let $\mathrm{ED}=h, \angle \mathrm{EAB}=\alpha$ $\therefore \quad \angle \mathrm{EBD}=2 \alpha, \angle \mathrm{ECD}=3 \alpha$ Now, $\angle \mathrm{DBE}=\angle \mathrm{EAB}+\angle \mathrm{BEA}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 \alpha=\alpha+\angle \mathrm{BEA}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \angle \mathrm{BEA}=\alpha=\angle \mathrm{EAB}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{EB}=a$ Similarly, $\angle \mathrm{EBC}=\alpha$ $\operatorname{In} \Delta \mathrm{EBC}, \frac{\mathrm{BC}}{\sin \alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{EB}}{\sin \left(180^{\circ}-3 \alpha\right)}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{b}{\sin \alpha}=\frac{a}{\sin 3 \alpha} \Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{\sin 3 \alpha}{\sin 3 \alpha}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a}{b}=\frac{3 \sin \alpha-4 \sin ^{3} \alpha}{\sin \alpha}=3-4 \sin ^{2} \alpha$ $\Rightarrow 4 \sin ^{2} \alpha=3-\frac{a}{b}=\frac{3 b-a}{b}$ $\Rightarrow \sin \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{3 b-a}{4 b}}$ In $\Delta \mathrm{EBD}, \sin 2 \alpha=\frac{E D}{E B}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{ED}=\mathrm{a} \cdot 2 \sin \alpha \cdot \cos \alpha$ $\Rightarrow h=2 a \sqrt{\frac{3 b-a}{4 b}}, \sqrt{1-\frac{3 b-a}{4 b}}$ $=2 a \sqrt{\frac{3 b-a}{4 b}} \sqrt{\frac{b-a}{4 b}}$ $=\frac{a}{2 b} \sqrt{(a+b)(3 b-a)}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sin ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \phi=1 / 2, \cos ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \phi=3 / 2,$ then $\cos ^{2}(\theta-\phi)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Using cosine formula 
$2 \sin (\theta+\phi) \cos (\theta-\phi)=1 / 2$ ...(i)
$2 \cos (\theta+\phi) \cos (\theta-\phi)=3 / 2$ ...(ii)
Squaring (1) and (2) and then adding $\cos ^{2}(\theta-\phi)=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{9}{4}=\frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow \cos ^{2}(\theta-\phi)=\frac{5}{8}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\frac{\cos \theta}{1-\tan \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\cot \theta}\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\cos \theta}{1-\tan \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\cot \theta}=\frac{\cos \theta}{1-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}} \\
= & \frac{\cos ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}-\frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}=\cos \theta+\sin \theta
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cup \mathrm{B})=\frac{2}{3}, \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{6}\) and \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{1}{3}\) then
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A} \cup \mathrm{B})=\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})-\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{AB})\)
\(\Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{3}+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})-\frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{2}\)
Now, \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{AB})=\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})~ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B}), \mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) are independent events.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the function \(f:(-\infty, \infty) \rightarrow B\) defined by \(f(x)=-x^2+6 x-8\) is bijective, then \(B=\)
questions.solution:
Since the function \(f\) is bijective, therefore \(f\) is onto. Therefore range of \(f=B\).
Let \(y=-x^2+6 x-8\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^2-6 \mathrm{x}+(8+\mathrm{y})=0\)
\(\Rightarrow x=\frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36-4(8+y)}}{2}=\frac{6 \pm \sqrt{4-4 y}}{2}\)
For \(x\) to be real, \(4-4 y \geq 0 \Rightarrow y \leq 1\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{B}=\) range of \(\mathrm{F}=(-\infty, 1]\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathbf{a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{b}=x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+(1-x) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{c}=y \hat{\mathbf{i}}+x \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(1+x-y) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$. Then, $[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{~ c}]$ depends on
questions.solution:
Here, $[a b c]=|a \cdot(b \times c)|$
Now, according to question,
$\begin{aligned}
{\left[\begin{array}{lll}
a & b c
\end{array}\right] } & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 0 & -1 \\
x & 1 & 1 - x \\
y & x & 1+x-y
\end{array}\right| \\
& =1(1+x-y-x(1-x))-1\left(x^2-y\right) \\
& =1+x-y-x+x^2-x^2+y \\
& =1 \\
& =\text { Independent of } x \text { and } y .
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let ABC be a triangle and be $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ the position vectors of $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Let D divide $B C$ in the ratio $3: 1$ internally and $E$ divide AD in the ratio $4: 1$ internally. Let BE meet AC in F . IfE divides BF in the ratio $3: 2$ internally then the position vector of $F$ is
questions.solution:
Here we are given that
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$
Now P.V of Di.e $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OD}}=\frac{1 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}+3 \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}}{1+3}$
$\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OD}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{4} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OE}}=\frac{4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}}{4+1}=\frac{\frac{4(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})}{4}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OE}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{5} \\ & \text { Now, } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OE}}=\frac{2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OF}}}{2+3} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OF}}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OE}}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OF}}=\frac{\frac{5(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})}{5}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OF}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{3} \Rightarrow P . V . \text { of } \mathrm{F} \text { is } \frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$, then the value of
$|\hat{i} \times(\vec{a} \times \hat{i})|^2+|\hat{j} \times(\vec{a} \times \hat{j})|^2+|\hat{k} \times(\vec{a} \times \hat{k})|^2$
is equal to
questions.solution:
$\hat{i} \times(\bar{a} \times \hat{i})=(\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i}) \bar{a}-(\hat{i} \cdot \bar{a}) \hat{i}=\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Similarly, } \hat{j} \times(\bar{a} \times \hat{j})=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k} \\
& \hat{k} \times(\bar{a} \times \hat{k})=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j} \\
& \Rightarrow \|\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}|^2+|2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}|^2+|2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}|^2 \\
& =5+8+5=18
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A vector perpendicular to the plane containing the vectors $\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ and $3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$ is inclined to the vector $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ at an angle
questions.solution:
A vector perpendicular to the plane is
$\begin{aligned}
& (\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \times(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \\
& =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\
1 & -2 & -1 \\
3 & -2 & -1
\end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k} \\
& \Rightarrow \text { unit vector } \hat{a}=\frac{-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+16}}=\frac{-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{2 \sqrt{5}}
\end{aligned}$
Angle between the unit vector and $\vec{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$=\cos ^{-1} \frac{\vec{r} \cdot \hat{a}}{|\vec{r}| \cdot|\hat{a}|}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{15}}=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{14}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{j}-\vec{k}$, then $\vec{b}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{j}-\vec{k}$,
Let $\bar{b}=b_1 \hat{i}+b_2 \hat{j}+b_3 \widehat{k}$
Now, $\hat{j}-\widehat{k}=\bar{a} \times \bar{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \widehat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ b_2 & b_2 & b_3\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow b_3-b_2=0, b_1-b_3=1, b_2-b_1=-1$
$\Rightarrow b_3=b_2, b_1=b_2+1$
Also, $\bar{a} \cdot \bar{b}=1$
$\Rightarrow b_1+b_2+b_3=1$
$\Rightarrow 3 b_2+1=1$
$\Rightarrow b_2=0$
$\Rightarrow b_1=1, b_3=0$
Thus, $\bar{b}=\hat{i}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: 107. $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{v}}$ are two non-collinear unit vectors such that $\left|\frac{\hat{\mathbf{u}}+\hat{\mathbf{v}}}{\mathbf{2}}+\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}\right|=1$. Then the value of $|\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given that, $\left|\frac{\hat{\mathbf{u}}+\hat{\mathbf{v}}}{2}+\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}\right|=1$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \left|\frac{\hat{\mathbf{u}}+\hat{\mathbf{v}}}{2}+\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}\right|^2=1 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{2+2 \cos \theta}{4}+\sin ^2 \theta=1 \\
 & \quad[\because \hat{\mathbf{u}} \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}})=\hat{\mathbf{v}}(\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}})=0] \\
\Rightarrow & \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}=\cos 2 \theta \\
\Rightarrow & \theta=n \pi \pm \frac{\theta}{2}, n \in z \\
\Rightarrow & \theta=\frac{2 \pi}{3}
\end{array}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad|\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \hat{\mathbf{v}}|=\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}=\sin \frac{\pi}{3}=\left|\frac{\hat{\mathbf{u}}-\hat{\mathbf{v}}}{2}\right|$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}$ is a vector of magnitude 50 , collinear with the vector $\vec{b}=6 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}-\frac{15}{2} \hat{k}$ and makes an acute angle with the positive direction of $Z$ - axis, then $\vec{a}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Since $\vec{a}=m \vec{b}$ for some scalar $m$ i.e.,

$\begin{array}{l}

\vec{a}=m\left(6 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}-\frac{15}{2} \hat{k}\right) \\

\Rightarrow|a|=|m| \sqrt{36+64+\frac{225}{4}} \\

\Rightarrow 50=\frac{25}{2}|m| \\

\Rightarrow|m|=4 \\

\Rightarrow m=\pm 4

\end{array}$

Since, a makes an acute angle with the positive direction of $Z$ - axis, so its $z$ component must be positive and hence, $m$ must be $-4$.

$\therefore a=-4\left(6 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-\frac{15}{2} \hat{k}\right)=-24 \hat{i}+32 \hat{j}+30 \hat{k}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{s}$ arenon-zero constant vectors and the scalar $b$ is chosen such that $|\vec{r}+b \vec{s}|$ is minimum, then the value of $|b \vec{s}|^{2}+|\vec{r}+b \vec{s}|^{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
For minimum value $|\vec{r}+b \vec{s}|=0$. Let $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{s}$ are anti-parallel so $b \vec{s}=-\vec{r}$

$\therefore|b \vec{s}|^{2}+|\vec{r}+b \vec{s}|^{2}=|-\vec{r}|^{2}+|\vec{r}-\vec{r}|^{2}=|\vec{r}|^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are non-coplaner vectors such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">×</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">c</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">;</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">c</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">×</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">;</mo><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">×</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">c</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">→</mo></mover></math>, then which of the following is not TRUE?
questions.solution:
Given, 

a→×b→=c→

b→×c→=a→

c→×a→=b→

(b→×c→)·a→=a→·a→

(c→×a→)·b→=b→·b→

a→2-b→2=(b→×c→)·a→-(c→×a→)·b→

=[c→a→b→]-[b→c→a→]=0

∴ |a→|2-|b→|2=0

|a→|-|b→|=0

⇒|a→|=|b→|

We know that, 

a→×b→  b→×c→  c→×a→=[a→b→c→]2

a→b→c→=a→b→c→2

⇒a→b→c→=1

|a→||b→||c→|=1

∴ |a→|=|b→|=|c→|≠2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>p</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is normal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
a→+pb→ is normal to c→

∴ a→+pb→·c→=0

1-pi^+2+2pj^+3+pk^·3i^+j^=0

3-3p+2+2p=0⇒p=5

questions.q: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∆</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>E</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>F</mi></math> are the mid-points of the sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is the altitude of a parallelopiped determined by the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and the base is taken to be the parallelogram determined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>c</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><msup><mi>h</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
questions.solution:
Volume of the parallelopiped =a^ b^ c^

⇒ (area of the base parallelogram) ×h=a^ b^ c^

⇒a^×b^h=a^ b^ c^

⇒-5i^+3j^+2k^h=11124-1113

⇒38h=4⇒h=438

⇒38h2=42⇒19h2=8

Hence, option (c) is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover></math> are two non-collinear unit vectors. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>u</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>a</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi>a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">·</mo><mover><mi>b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>b</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">a</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo mathvariant="italic">×</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">b</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>|</mo></math> is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, a^=b^=1

Given, v→=a^×b^=a^b^sinθn^=sinθn^

where, θ is the angle between a^ and b^. 

∴   v→=sinθ

Now, u→=a^-a^·b^b^=a^-a^b^cosθb^

u→=a^-cosθb^

∴ u→=a^2+-cosθb^2-2a^-cosθb^cosθ

⇒ u→=1+cos2θ-2cos2θ

⇒u→=1-cos2θ=sin2θ

∴ u→=sinθ

So, v→=u→

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $a, b$ and $c$ be three vectors satisfying $a \times b=$ $(a \times c),|a|=|c|=1,|b|=4$ and $|b \times c|=\sqrt{15}$. If $b-$

$2 \mathrm{c}=\lambda \mathrm{a},$ then $\lambda$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $\theta$ be the angle between $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$. Given, $|\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c}|=\sqrt{15}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow|\mathrm{b} \| \mathrm{c}| \sin \theta=\sqrt{15} \\

\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{|\mathrm{~b}| \mathrm{c} \mid} \\

\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sqrt{\frac{15}{4 \times 1}}=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4} \\

\quad \therefore \cos \theta=\sqrt{1-\frac{15}{16}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{16}}=\frac{1}{4}

\end{array}

$$

Now given, $\mathrm{b}-2 \mathrm{c}=\lambda \mathrm{a} \Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{b}-2 \mathrm{c}^{2}\right|=\mid \lambda \mathrm{a}^{2}$

$$

\Rightarrow|\mathrm{b}|^{2}+4|\mathrm{c}|-4(\mathrm{~b} \cdot \mathrm{c})=\lambda^{2}|\mathrm{a}|^{2}

$$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow 16+4-4|\mathrm{~b} \| \mathrm{c}| \cos \theta=\lambda^{2} \\

\Rightarrow 20-16 \cos \theta=\lambda^{2} \\

\Rightarrow 20-4=\lambda^{2} \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}=16\left(\therefore \cos \theta=\frac{1}{4}\right) \\

\Rightarrow \lambda=\pm 4

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the middle points of sides $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CA} \& \mathrm{AB}$ of triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ are respectively $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{F}$ then position vector of centre of triangle $\mathrm{DEF}$, when position vector of $A, \quad B, \quad C$ are respectively $\hat{i}+\hat{j}, \hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{k}+\hat{i}$ is
questions.solution:
The position vector of points $D, E, F$ are respectively $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{i}+\frac{\hat{k}+\hat{j}}{2}$ and $\frac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{2}+\hat{j}$
So, position vector of centre of $\Delta \mathrm{DEF}$ $=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{2}+\hat{k}+\hat{i} \frac{\hat{k}+\hat{j}}{2}+\frac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{2}+\hat{j}\right]$ $=\frac{2}{3}[\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle between any two diagonal of a cube is
questions.solution:
for a unit cube unit vector along the diagonal $$ \mathrm{OP}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) $$
unit vector along the diagonal $\mathrm{CD}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$ $\therefore \cos \theta=\frac{1}{3}(1+1-1)=\frac{1}{3} \quad \therefore \tan \theta=2 \sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ are three unit vectors such that $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0},$ where $\overrightarrow{0}$ is null vector, then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}$ is :
questions.solution:
We have $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}$

$|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|=0 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|^{2}=0$

$\Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}$

$+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0$

$\Rightarrow 1+1+1+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0$

$\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\bar{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\bar{c} \cdot \bar{a}=-\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If vectors $2 \mathrm{i}-\mathrm{j}+\mathrm{k}, \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j}-3 \mathrm{k}$ and $3 \mathrm{i}+\mathrm{aj}+5 \mathrm{k}$ are

coplanar, then the value of a is
questions.solution:
If given vectors are coplanar, then there exists two scalar quantities $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}$ such that $2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}=x(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+y(3 \hat{i}+a \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$

Comparing coefficient of $\hat{i}, \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k}$ on both sides of (1) we get $x+3 y=2,2 x+a y=-1,-3 x+5 y=1 \ldots(2)$

Solving first and third equations, we get $\mathrm{x}=1 / 2, \mathrm{y}=1 / 2$

Since the vectors are coplanar, therefore these values of $x$ and $y$ will satisfy the equation $2 x+a y=-1$

$\therefore 2(1 / 2)+a(1 / 2)=-1 \Rightarrow a=-4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The unit vector perpendicular to the vectors $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and $3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$ is $-$
questions.solution: Unit vector perpendicular to both the given vectors is, $\frac{(6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \times(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})}{|(6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \times(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})|}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}}{7}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a$.b $=a$.c and $a \times b=a \times c$, then correct statement is
questions.solution:
$a . b .=a \cdot c \Rightarrow a \cdot(b-c)=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=0 \mathrm{or} \mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}=0$ or $\mathrm{a} \perp(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=0$ or $\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{c}$ or $\mathrm{a} \perp(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})$
Also a $x b=a x c \Rightarrow a \times(b-c)=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=0$ or $\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}=0$ or a $\|(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})$
$\Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=c$ or $a \|(b-c)$
Observing to ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) we find that $\mathrm{a}=0$ or $\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{c}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two vectors \(\vec{A}\) and \(\vec{B}\) are such that \(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}|\). The angle between the two vectors will be-
questions.solution:
\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}|=| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \mid\)
\(\Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{A}^2+\mathrm{B}^2+2 \mathrm{AB} \cos \theta}=\sqrt{\mathrm{A}^2+\mathrm{B}^2-2 \mathrm{AB} \cos \theta}\)
Squaring both the sides, we get
\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}^2+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}^2+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cos \theta=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}^2+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}^2-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cos \theta\)
or \(4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cos \theta=0\) or \(\cos \theta=0\) (since the scalar or dot product is zero). Therefore angle between \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}\) to \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}\) is \(90^{\circ}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of differential equation \(2 x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=3\) represents a family of
questions.solution:
We have, \(2 x \frac{d y}{d x}=y+3 \Rightarrow \frac{2}{y+3} d y=\frac{d x}{x}\) integrating, \(2 \ln (y+3)=\ln x+\ln c=\ln c x\)
\(\Rightarrow \ln (y+3)^2=\ln \mathrm{cx} \Rightarrow(y+3)^2=\mathrm{cx}\)
which is a family of parabolas.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \((\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^2+(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2=676\) and \(|\vec{b}|=2\) then \(|\vec{a}|\) is equal to
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} &(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^2+(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^2=676 \\ &(|\vec{a}| \cdot|\vec{b}| \sin \theta \hat{n})^2+(|\vec{a}| \cdot|\vec{b}| \cos \theta)^2=676 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2 b^2 \sin ^2 \theta+a^2 b^2 \cos ^2 \theta=676\left[(\hat{n})^2=1\right] \\ & a^2 b^2\left(\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta\right)=676 \Rightarrow a^2=\frac{676}{b^2}=\frac{676}{4} \\ &|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{\frac{676}{4}} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|=\frac{26}{2} \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|=13\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which one of the following is the unit vector perpendicular to both \(\vec{a}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}\) and \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}\) ?
questions.solution:
According to question \(\mathrm{a}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}\) and \(\mathrm{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}\)
Then, \(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|\)
\(\begin{gathered}
=\hat{i}[1+1]-\hat{j}[-1-1]+\hat{k}[1-1]=2(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \\
\quad \text { and }|\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}|=\sqrt{4+4}=2 \sqrt{2}
\end{gathered}\)
\(\therefore \text { Required unit vector }= \pm \frac{2(\hat{i}+\hat{j})}{2 \sqrt{2}}= \pm \frac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}\)

questions.solution:
Rising half to peak
$t=T / 6$
$\mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \pi}{6 \omega}=\frac{\pi}{3 \omega}=\frac{\pi}{300 \pi}=\frac{1}{300}=3.3 \mathrm{~ms}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, rms value of current (Irms) through the resistor $R$ is :
questions.solution:
(a) Given,
Capacitive reactance, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=100 \Omega$
Inductive reactance, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=200 \Omega$
Resistance, $R=100 \Omega$
Impedance, $\mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^2}$
$Z=\sqrt{100^2+(200-100)^2}=100 \sqrt{2} \Omega$
RMS value of current,
$i_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{V_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{200 \sqrt{2}}{100 \sqrt{2}}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown in the figure, the $\mathrm{AC}$ source gives a voltage $V=20 \cos (2000 t)$ neglecting source resistance, the voltmeter and ammeter reading will be
questions.solution:
Given, $R_1=8 \Omega$
$\begin{aligned} R_2 & =2 \Omega \\ L & =5 \mathrm{mH} \\ C & =50 \mu \mathrm{F}\end{aligned}$
and $\quad V_0=20 \cos (2000 t)$
Impedance, $Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}$
As, here, $X_L=\omega L=2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}=10 \Omega$
Similarly, $X_L=\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}=10 \Omega$ $\because \quad X_L=X_C$, Hence, $i_{\max }=\frac{V_0}{Z}=\frac{20}{(8+2)}=2 \mathrm{~A}$
Hence, $\quad i_{\operatorname{rms}}=\frac{i_{\max }}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=1.41 \mathrm{~A}$ and
$$
\begin{aligned}
V & =R_2 i_{\mathrm{rms}}=1.41 \times 2 \\
& =282 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: An alternating voltage $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_{0} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$ is applied across a circuit. As a result, a current $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0} \sin \left(\omega \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ flows in it. The power consumed per cycle is
questions.solution:
The phase angle between voltage $V$ and current $I$ is $\frac{\pi}{2}$.

Therefore, power factor $\cos \phi=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0$.

Hence the power consumed is zero.

Voltage $V$ and current $i$ in $A C$ circuit are given by $\mathrm{V}=50 \sin (50 \mathrm{t})$ volt, $\mathrm{i}=50 \sin \left(50 \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \mathrm{mA}$

The power dissipated in the circuit is

Given $V=50 \sin (50$ t) $V$ Maximum voltage, $\mathrm{V}_{0}=50 \mathrm{~V}$

$$

i=\left(50 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \mathrm{mA}

$$

Maximum current, $i_{0}=50 \mathrm{~mA}=50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$

Power dissipated, $\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}$

$=\frac{50 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=\frac{2500 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=1.25 \mathrm{~W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following transitions of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion will give rise to spectral line which has same wavelength as the spectral line in hydrogen atom?
questions.solution:
Let the transition of electron in hydrogen atom $(Z=1)$ gives wavelength $\lambda_1$ for transition from $n_2$ to $n_1$, then
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^2\left(\frac{1}{m^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right)$
Here, $\lambda=\lambda_1, Z=1, m=n_1, n=n_2$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R(1)^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
Now, if electron makes transition in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion from $n_4$ to $n_3$ and gives wavelength $\lambda_2$, then
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\lambda_2} & =R(2)^2\left(\frac{1}{n_3^2}-\frac{1}{n_4^4}\right) \\
& =R\left[\frac{1}{\left(\frac{n_3}{2}\right)^2}-\frac{1}{\left(\frac{n_4}{2}\right)^2}\right]
\end{aligned}$
Given that, $\quad \lambda_1=\lambda_2$
$\Rightarrow R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)=R\left[\frac{1}{\left(\frac{n_3}{2}\right)^2}-\frac{1}{\left(\frac{n_4}{2}\right)^2}\right]$
On comparing beth sides, we get
$n_1=\frac{n_3}{2} \text { and } n_2=\frac{n_4}{2}$
The value of $n_1, n_2, n_3$ and $n_4$ must be an integer, as they denote principal quantum number.
If $n_3=2$ and $n_4=4$, then the given condition is satisfied.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the shortest wavelength of Balmer series to the shortest wavelength of Lyman series for hydrogen atom is:
questions.solution:
Wavelength of H -atom is
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{Rz}^2\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)$
Shortest wavelength for Balmer series:
$\frac{1}{\lambda_B}=\mathrm{Rz}^2\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)$
Shortest wavelength for Lyman series:
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=\mathrm{Rz}^2\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)$     ....(ii)
Dividing eq. (i) and (ii),
$\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}: \lambda_1=4: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum excitation energy of an electron revolving in the first orbit of hydrogen is
questions.solution:
Energy of electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit
$E_n=\frac{-13.6 e V}{n^2}$
Excitation energy of electron from $n=1$ to $n=2$
$E=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{1}\right]=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{1}\right]=10.2 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular momentum for the electron in Bohr's orbit is L. If the electron is assumed to revolve in second orbit of hydrogen atom, then the change in angular momentum will be:
questions.solution:
From the Bohr's quantization rule
Angular momentum $\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}, \mathrm{L}_1=\frac{1 . \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}=\mathrm{L}$ ( $n=1$ for first orbit)
in ( $n=2$ for second orbit) $L_2=\frac{2 h}{2 \pi}=2 L$
Hence, change in angular momentum $\mathrm{L}_2-\mathrm{L}_1=2 \mathrm{~L}-$ $\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $12.5 \mathrm{eV}$ electron beam is used to bombard gaseous hydrogen at room temperature. The number of spectral lines emitted will be:
questions.solution:
If we assume electron in hydrogen atom takes energy $12.09 \mathrm{eV}$ from the incoming radiation, the maximum excited state of electron will be $n=3$. So, number of spectral lines is $\frac{3(3-1)}{2}=3$.
Here we assume some part of energy $12.5 \mathrm{eV}$ $-12.09 \mathrm{eV}=0.41 \mathrm{eV}$ get lost due to collision.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> energy is given to electron in third orbit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>-atom, then final energy of electron when it ionise from third orbit, is
questions.solution:
Given, total energy given to electron in third orbit,

E=12 eV

Energy of third orbit of H-atom, E'=-13.6n2

=-13.632=-1.51 eV

lonisation energy of electron of third orbit, E''=-E'

=--1.51 eV⇒E''=1.51 eV

Hence, final energy of electron when it comes out of H-atom from third orbit

Ef=E-E''

=12-1.51=10.49 eV

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of hydrogen is about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. Its kinetic energy in this state is
questions.solution:
We have, Kinetic energy of electron,

K=Ze28πε0·r

also, Potential energy of electron,

U=-14πε0·Ze2r

∴ Total energy

E=K+U

=Ze28πε0·r-Ze24πε0·r

E=-Ze28πε0r or K=-E=--3.4

=3.4 eV

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the series limit wavelength of Lyman series for the hydrogen atom is $912 Å,$ then the series limit wavelength for Balmer series of hydrogen atoms is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]$

For limiting wavelength of Lyman series

$$

n_{1}=1, n_{2}=\infty \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{L}}=R

$$

For limiting wavelength of Balmer series $n_{1}=2, n_{2}=\infty$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\frac{1}{\lambda_{B}}=R\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \Rightarrow \lambda_{B}=\frac{4}{R} \\

\therefore \lambda_{B}=4 \lambda_{L}=4 \times 912 \AA

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One of the lines in the emission spectrum of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ has the same wavelength as that of the $2^{\text {nd }}$ line of Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum. The electronic transition corresponding to this line is $\mathrm{n}=12 \rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{x} .$ Find the value of $\mathrm{x}$
questions.solution:
For $2^{\text {nd line of Balmer series in hydrogen }}$ spectrum

$$

\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}(1)\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{R}

$$

$$

\text { For } \mathrm{Li}^{2+}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R} \times 9\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}-\frac{1}{12^{2}}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{16}\right]

$$

which is satisfied by $n=12 \rightarrow n=6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation $d=2 \mathrm{~m}$ has a capacitance of $4 \mu \mathrm{~F}$. The new capacitance of the system if half of the space between them is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant $\mathrm{K}=3$ (as shown in figure) will be:
questions.solution:
We have, $C_i=\frac{A \epsilon_0}{d}=4 \mu F$
$C_f=\frac{A \epsilon_0}{d-t+\frac{t}{k}}=\frac{A \epsilon_0}{d-\frac{d}{2}+\frac{d}{2 \times 3}}$
$=\frac{A \in_0}{d\left(1-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}\right)}$
$=\frac{4 \mu F}{\frac{2}{3}}=6 \mu F$

& \phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\text {in }}}{\epsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\epsilon_0} \quad \because \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV} \\
& \therefore \phi=\frac{\mathrm{CV}}{\epsilon_0}
