questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit given below, the charge in $\mu \mathrm{C},$ on the capacitor having $5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is
questions.solution:
Potential difference across the branch de is

$6 \mathrm{~V}$. Net capacitance of de branch is $2.1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ So, $q=C V$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=2.1 \times 6 \mu \mathrm{C} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=12.6 \mu \mathrm{C}$

Potential across $3 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitance is $\mathrm{V}=\frac{12.6}{3}=4.2 \mathrm{volt}$

Potential across 2 and 5 combination in parallel is $6-4.2=1.8 \mathrm{~V}$

So, $q^{\prime}=(1.8)(5)=9 \mu C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a capacitor is fully charged as shown in the following figure, then current drawn from the cell is
questions.solution:
When capacitor is fully charged, then it acts like a open switch, therefore 8 kΩ resistor has no use. So, rest of the resistances 4 kΩ, 7 kΩ and 9 kΩ will be in series. Hence, current drawn from cell

I=104+7+9×103=1020×103=0.5 mA

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is discharging through a resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. At what time the charge on the capacitor is equal to one half of its initial value?
questions.solution:
The charge on the capacitor at time t in a discharging circuit is given by q=q0e-t/τ

where, τ=RC= time constant
∴               q=q0e-t/RC

when,   q=q02

then  q02=q0e-t/RC

12=e-t/RC

Taking natural log on both side loge12=log0e-t/RC

-loge2=-tRClogee

⇒t=RCloge2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A parallel plate capacitor C with plates of unit area and separation d is filled with a liquid of dielectric constant K=2, the level of liquid isd3,initially. 
Suppose, the liquid level decreases at a constant speed v, the time constant as a function of time is
questions.solution:
We have, the time constant, τ=RC'    …i

Now, C'=C1C2C1+C2

=Aε0d-xKAε0xAε0d-x+KAε0x

∴  C'=KAε0x+K(d-x)

Putting the value of C' in Equation. (i), we get

τ=RKAε0d3-vt+kd-d3+vt∵x=d3-vt

Given, A=1 and K=2

∴  τ=3×2ε0×Rd-3vt+6d-2d+6vt=6Rε05d+3vt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the given circuit diagram,

E=5 V, r=1 Ω, R2=4 Ω, R1=R3=1 Ω and C=3 μF

Then, what will be the numerical value of charge on each plates of the capacitor.
questions.solution:
Current will flow only through the branch containing resistance R2

∴ i=ER2+r=54+1=1 A

Potential difference across R2

=1×4=4 V

If q be the charge on each plate of capacitor, then

qc+qc=4

or 2qc=4

or 2q3×10-6=4

q=6 μC

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi></math>. The separation between the plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The space between the plates is now filled with two dielectrics constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> while the other one has dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>d</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. Capacitance of the capacitor is now

C=C1C2C1+C2   ...(i)

where, C1=K1ε0Ad3    ∵C=ε0Ad   ...(ii)

C2=K2ε0A2d3    ...(iii)

Ceq=3K1ε0Ad×K2ε0A·32d3K1ε0Ad+3K2ε0A2d

=9K1K2ε02A22d2×dε0A×18

It is given

ε0Ad=9 pF

Using given values

Ceq=40.5 pF

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure below, what is the potential difference between the point $A$ and $B$ and between $B$ and $C$ respectively in steady state
options: $\quad V_{A B}=75 V, V_{B C}=25 V$
$\quad V_{A B}=25 V, V_{B C}=75 V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor of area ' $A$ ' plate separation ' $d$ ' is filled with two dielectrics as shown. What is the capacitance of the arrangement?
questions.solution:
$$ c_{1}=\frac{(A / 2) \varepsilon_{0}}{d / 2}=\frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{d}, c_{2}=K \frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{d}, c_{3}=K \frac{A \varepsilon_{0}}{2 d} $$
$$ \therefore \quad c_{e q .}=\frac{c_{1} \times c_{2}}{c_{1}+c_{2}}+c_{3}=\frac{(3+\mathrm{K}) \mathrm{K} A \varepsilon_{0}}{2 d(\mathrm{~K}+1)} $$ $\left(\because C_{1}\right.$ and $C_{2}$ are in series and resultant of these two in parallel with $C_{3}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $\mathrm{C}$ is connected to a battery and is charged to a potential difference $\mathrm{V}$. Another capacitor of capacitance $2 \mathrm{C}$ is similarly charge to a potential difference $2 \mathrm{~V}$. The charging battery is now disconnected and the capacitors are connected in parallel to each other in such a way that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of the configuration is
questions.solution:
From the figure. The net charge shared between the capacitors $$ Q^{\prime}=Q_{2}-Q_{2}=4 C V-C V=3 C V $$
The two capacitors will have some potential, say $\mathrm{V}^{\prime}$. The net capacitance of the parallel combination of the two capacitors $$ C^{\prime}=C_{1}+C_{2}=C+2 C+3 C $$ The potential of the capacitors $$ \mathrm{V}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{CV}}{3 \mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{V} $$ The electrostatic energy of the capacitors $$ \mathrm{E}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{V}^{\prime 2}=\frac{1}{2}(3 \mathrm{C}) \mathrm{V}^{2}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three capacitors $\mathrm{C}_{1}=1 \mu \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{C}_{2}=2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}=3$ $\mu \mathrm{F}$ are connected as shown in figure, then the equivalent capacitance between points A and $\mathrm{B}$ is
questions.solution: $C_{\mathrm{eq}}=(1+2+3) \mu \mathrm{F}=6 \mu \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical capacitors, have the same capacitance $\mathrm{C}$. One of them is charged to potential $\mathrm{V}_{1}$ and the other to $\mathrm{V}_{2}$. The negative ends of the capacitors are connected together. When the positive ends are also connected, the decrease in energy of the combined system is -
questions.solution:
Initial energy of combined system

$$

\mathrm{U}_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}_{2}^{2}

$$

Final common potential, $\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}}{2},$

Final energy of system,

$$

\mathrm{U}_{2}=2 \times \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}

$$

Hence loss of energy $=\mathrm{U}_{1}-\mathrm{U}_{2}$

$$

=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{V}_{1}-\mathrm{V}_{2}\right)^{2}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ are charged to $120 \mathrm{~V}$ and $200 \mathrm{~V}$ respectively. It is found that by connecting them together the potential on each one can be made zero. Then
questions.solution: For potential to be made zero, after connection $120 \mathrm{C}_{1}=200 \mathrm{C}_{2} \quad\left[\because C=\frac{q}{v}\right]$ $\Rightarrow 3 C_{1}=5 C_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the potential of a capacitor having capacity $6 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is increased from $10 \mathrm{~V}$ to $20 \mathrm{~V}$, then increase in its energywill be
questions.solution:
Capacitance of capacitor (C) $=6 \mu \mathrm{F}$ $=6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$; Initial potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)=10 \mathrm{~V}$ and
final potential $\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}\right)=20 \mathrm{~V}$.
The increase in energy $(\Delta \mathrm{U})$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}^{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}^{2}\right) \\
=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(6 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left[(20)^{2}-(10)^{2}\right] \\
=\left(3 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times 300=9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J} .
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Calculate the area of the plates of a one farad parallel plate capacitor if separation between plates is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and plates are in vacuum

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball falling freely from a height of $4.9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, hits a horizontal surface. If $e=\frac{3}{4}$, then the ball will hit the surface, second time after
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\text {Velocity on hitting the surface } & =\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 4.9} \\
& =9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
Velocity after first bounce, $v=\frac{3}{4} \times 9.8$
Time taken from first bounce to the second bounce
$=\frac{2 v}{g}=2 \times \frac{3}{4} \times 9.8 \times \frac{1}{9.8}=1.5 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two bodies of mass 1 kg and 3 kg have position vectors $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+k$ and $-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ respectively. The magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of this system will be similar to the magnitude of vector :
questions.solution:
Position of COM of a mass - system is given as,
$\vec{r}_{\mathrm{com}}=\frac{m_1 \vec{r}_1+m_2 \vec{r}_2}{m_1+m_2}=\frac{1(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+3(-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}}{1+3}$
$=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$\left|\vec{r}_{c m}\right|=|-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}|=\sqrt{(2)^2+(1)^2+(1)^2}=\sqrt{6}$
Only option (1) magnitude is
$\sqrt{1^2+(-2)^2+1^2}=\sqrt{6}$
So option (1) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio $16: 9$. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is:
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { K.E. }=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{p}_1^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}_1} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{p}_2^2}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1}=\frac{16}{9} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{9}{16}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides with a stationary particle of mass $2 \mathrm{~m}$. After collision, they stick together and continue to move together with velocity
questions.solution:
Applying conservation of linear momentum
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{f}}(\because \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{mv})$
$m v_1+2 m v_2=(m+2 m) v^{\prime}$
$m v^1+2 m \times 0=(3 m) v^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{mv}=3 \mathrm{mv}^{\prime} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Three bricks each of length $L$ and mass $M$ are arranged as shown from the wall. The distance of the centre of mass of the system from the wall is
questions.solution: $x_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}, x_{2}=\mathrm{L}, x_{3}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~L}}{4}$ $\therefore x_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}+m_{3} x_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{M} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}+\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{L}+\mathrm{M} \times \frac{5 \mathrm{~L}}{4}}{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{M}}=\frac{11 \mathrm{~L}}{12}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A nucleus splits into two nuclear parts which have their velocity ratio equal to $2: 1 .$ What will be the ratio of their nuclear radius?
questions.solution:
As momentum is conserved, therefore

$\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}_{1}}{\mathrm{~A}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{1}}{\mathrm{~A}_{2}}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}=1: 2^{1 / 3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: An object of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is projected with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, to the horizontal. At the highest point of its path, the projectile explodes and breaks up into two fragments of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The fragments separate horizontally after the explosion, which releases internal energy such that the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mi>E</mi></math> of the system af the highest point is doubled. The separation between the two fragments when they reach the ground is
questions.solution:
Given, m=5 kg,v=20 m s-1,θ=60°

Vertical component of velocity, vy=v sin 60°

=20×32=103 m s-1

Time taken to reach the highest point = Time taken to reach the ground from highest point.

t=v sin θg=vyg=1039.8=1.77 s

At the highest point, m splits up into two parts of masses m1=1 kg and m2=4 kg

If their velocities are v1 and v2 respectively, then applying the principle of conservation of linear momentum, we get

m1v1+m2v2=mv cos θ

v1+4v2=5×20×12  ∵    θ=60°

v1+4v2=5×10=50     ....i

Initial KE=12 m v cos θ2

=12×5×102=250 J

Final KE=2 (initial KE)=2×250=500 J

∴  12m1v12+12m2v22=500

or   12×1×v12+12×4×v22=500

or   v12+4v22=1000     ....ii

Solving Eq. (i) and (ii), we get

v1=30 m s-1, v2=5 m s-1

Hence, the separation between the two fragments

=v1-v2×t=30-5×1.77 m=44.25 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A trolley having mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moves with uniform speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kmh</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> on a frictionless track. A child of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> runs on the trolley from one end to the other (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away)  with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> relative to the trolley in a direction opposite to its inotion and ultimately jumps out of the trolley. With how much velocity has the trolley moved from the time the child begins to run?
questions.solution:
since, no external force is acting on the system, we can apply conservation of linear momentum.

Speed of 200 kg trolley =36×100060×60=10 m s-1

If u be the initial velocity of trolley, vb be the absolute velocity of the boy after the beginning of journey of the boy, their relative velocity is 4.

So, v'-vb=4⇒vb=v'-4

Now, applying law of conservation of momentum,

Momentum before the boy begins tu run
= Momentum after the beginning of boy's running

⇒  220×10=200v'+20v'-4

2200=220v'-80⇒220v'=2280

v'=2280220=10.36 m s-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Consider elastic collision of a particle of mass m moving with a velocity u with another particle of the same mass at rest. After the collision the projectile and the struck particle move in directions making angles $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ respectively with the initial direction of motion. The sum of the angles $\theta_{1}+\theta_{2},$ is :
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A neutron moving with speed v makes a head on collision with a hydrogen atom in ground state kept at rest. The minimum kinetic energy of the neutron for which inelastic collision takes place is
questions.solution:
Let speed of neutron before collision $=\mathrm{V}$ Speed of neutron after collision $=\mathrm{V}_{1}$ Speed of proton or hydrogen atom after collision $=\mathrm{V}_{2}$ Energy of excitation $=\Delta \mathrm{E}$ From the law of conservation of linear momentum, $\mathrm{mv}=\mathrm{mv}_{1}+\mathrm{mv}_{2}$

And for law of conservation of energy,

$$

\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{2}^{2}

$$

From squaring eq. (i), we get

$$

v^{2}=v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}+2 v_{1} v_{2}

$$

From squaring eq. (ii), we get

$$

v^{2}=v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}+\frac{2 \Delta E}{m}

$$

From eqn (3) \& (4)

$$

\begin{aligned}

\therefore 2 \mathrm{v}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{2} &=\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}} \\

\therefore\left(\mathrm{v}_{1}-\mathrm{v}_{2}\right)^{2} &=\left(\mathrm{v}_{1}+\mathrm{v}_{2}\right)^{2}-4 \mathrm{v}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{2}=\mathrm{v}^{2}-\frac{4 \Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}

\end{aligned}

$$

As, $v_{1}-v_{2}$ must be real, $v^{2}-\frac{4 \Delta E}{m} \geq 0$

$$

\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \geq 2 \Delta \mathrm{E}

$$

The minimum energy that can be absorbed by the hydrogen atom in the ground state to go into the excited state is $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$. Therefore, the maximum kinetic energy needed is

$$

\frac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^{2}=2 \times 10.2=20.4 \mathrm{eV}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A rifle man, who together with his rifle has a mass of $100 \mathrm{~kg}$, stands on a smooth surface and fires 10 shots horizontally. Each bullet has a mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ and a muzzle velocity of $800 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The velocity which the rifle man attains after firing 10 shots is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A man of mass $100 \mathrm{~kg}$. is standing on a platform of mass $200 \mathrm{~kg}$. which is kept on a smooth ice surface. If the man starts moving on the platform with a speed $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$ relative to the platform then calculate with what velocity relative to the ice the platform will recoil?
questions.solution:
Consider the situation shown in figure.Suppose the man moves at a speed w towards right and the the platform recoils at a speed \(\mathrm{V}\) towards left, both relative to the ice. Hence, the speed of the man relative to the platform is
\(\mathrm{V}+\mathrm{w}\). By the question,
\(\mathrm{V}+\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{v}\), or \(\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{V} \ldots\) (i)
Taking the platform and the man to be the system, there is no external horizontal force on the system. The linear momentum of the system remains constant. Initially both the man and the platform were at rest. Thus,
\(0=\mathrm{MV}-\underset{m v}{\operatorname{mor}} \mathrm{MV}=\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{V})[\mathrm{Using}(\mathrm{i})]\)
or, \(V=\frac{m v}{M+m}\)
Hence, the velocity relative to the ice is \(V=\frac{m v}{M+m}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A spring is compressed between two toy carts of mass $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$. When the toy carts are released, the springs exert equal and opposite average forces for the same time on each toy cart. If $v_{1}$ and $\mathrm{v}_{2}$ are the velocities of the toy carts and there is no friction between the toy carts and the ground, then :
questions.solution:
Applying law of conservation of linear momentum
$\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2}=0$
$\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}$ or $\frac{\mathrm{v}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A system consists of three particles, each of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and located at (1,1),(2,2) and (3,3) . The co-ordinates of the centre of mass are
questions.solution:
The coordinates of C.M of three particle are
\begin{array}{c}
\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{x}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3} \mathrm{x}_{3}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3}} \& \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{y}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3} \mathrm{y}_{3}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3}} \\
\text { here } \mathrm{m}_{1}=\mathrm{m}_{2}=\mathrm{m}_{3}=\mathrm{m} \\
\text { so } \mathrm{x}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}+\mathrm{x}_{3}\right) \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{m}}=2
\end{array}
$$
y=\frac{\left(y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}\right) m}{m+m+m}=2
$$
so coordinates of $\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{M}$. of three particle are (2,2)
$\mathrm{V}=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A particle of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving eastwards with a speed $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ collides with another particle of the same mass moving north-wards with the same speed $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The two particles coalesce on collision. The new particle of mass $20 \mathrm{~kg}$ will move in the north-east direction with velocity
questions.solution:
Here $\hat{i} m v+\hat{j} m v=2 m \vec{V}$
That is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}})$
Hence $V=\frac{v}{2} \times \sqrt{2}=\frac{v}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot\left[\right.$ Here $\left.v=5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right]$
$$
\text { So, } V=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, $E_1=E_2=E_3=2 \mathrm{~V}$ and $R_1=R_2=4 \Omega$, then current flowing through the branch $A B$ is
questions.solution: The given circuit can be redrawn as, where, equivalent emf of the combination, $\begin{aligned} E_{\text {eq }} & =\frac{E_1 R_1+E_2 R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\ & =\frac{2 \times 4+2 \times 4}{4+4}=2 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$ and $R_{e q}=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \Omega$ Net emf of the circuit, $\begin{aligned} E^{\prime} & =E_2+E_{\mathrm{eq}} \\ & =2+2 \\ & =4 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Current flowing through branch $A B$, $I=\frac{E^{\prime}}{R_{e q}}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \mathrm{~A}$ Clearly from figure, it is clear that direction of current is from point $A$ to $B$ clockwise is loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the following circuit diagram, when $3 \Omega$ resistor is removed, then equivalent resistance of the network
questions.solution: The equivalent Wheatstone's bridge network of the given circuit is shown in figure. Here, The points $B$ and $D$ are at the same potential as the bridge is balanced. So, the $3 \Omega$ resistance in $B D$ arm is ineffective and can be omitted from the circuit. Hence, equivalent resistance remains same when $3 \Omega$ resistor is removed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting wire is stretched by applying a deforming force, so that its diameter decreases to $40 \%$ of original value. The percentage change in its resistance will be
questions.solution:
On stretching, volume of wire remains constant.
So,
$V=A l$ or $l=\frac{V}{A}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & R=\rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}=\frac{\rho V}{A^2}=\frac{\rho V}{\frac{\pi^2 D^4}{16}}=\frac{16 \rho V}{\pi^2 D^4} \\
\therefore & \frac{\Delta R}{R}=-4 \frac{\Delta D}{D}=-4(-0.4)=1.6 \%
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of resistance $160 \Omega$ is melted and drawn in wire of one-fourth of its length. The new resistance of the wire will be
questions.solution:
Let
Initial length $=l_1$
Final length $=l_2$
Initial area $=\mathrm{A}_1$
Final area $=A_1$
$\therefore$ Volume remains same
$\therefore \mathrm{A}_1 \mathrm{l}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \mathrm{l}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}_1 \mathrm{l}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \frac{\mathrm{l}_1}{4}$
$\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{~A}_1=\mathrm{A}_2$
Initial resistance, $\mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\rho l_1}{A_1}=160 \Omega$ (given)
Final resistance, $\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\mathrm{\rho l}_2}{\mathrm{~A}_2}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{l}_2 \mathrm{~A}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2 \mathrm{l}_1}=\frac{l_1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{~A}_1}{4 \mathrm{~A}_1 l_1}$
$\Rightarrow R_2=\frac{1}{16} R_1=\frac{1}{16} \times 160=10 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five cells each of emf $E$ and internal resistance $r$ send the same amount of current through an external resistance $R$ whether the cells are connected in parallel or in series. Then the ratio $\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Given : Number of cells, $n=5$, emf of each cell $=E$
Internal resistance of each cell $=r$
In series, current through resistance $R$
$I=\frac{n E}{n r+R}=\frac{5 E}{5 r+R}$
In parallel, current through resistance $R$
$I^{\prime}=\frac{E}{\frac{r}{n}+R}=\frac{n E}{r+n R}=\frac{5 E}{r+5 R}$
According to question, $I=I^{\prime}$
$\therefore \frac{5 E}{5 r+5 R}=\frac{5 E}{r+5 R} \Rightarrow 5 r+R=r+5 R$
or $R=r \quad \therefore \frac{R}{r}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ratio of thermal energy released in two resistor $R$ and $3 R$ connected in parallel in an electric circuit is :
questions.solution:
Thermal energy is given by
$\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{P} \times \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{R}} \times \mathrm{t}$
Here, voltage $\mathrm{V}$ is same.
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{H}_1}{\mathrm{H}_2}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{V}^2 \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{R}}}{\frac{\mathrm{V}^2 \mathrm{t}}{3 \mathrm{R}}}=3: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a wire is $5 \Omega$. It's new resistance in ohm if stretched to 5 times of its original length will be :
questions.solution:
Let resistance of a wire $R$ and length 1 .
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}}=5 \Omega$
$\therefore \quad$ Volume of wire is constant in stretching
$V_i=V_f \Rightarrow A_i \ell_i=A_f \ell_f$
$\mathrm{A} \ell=\mathrm{A}^{\prime}(5 \ell) \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{5}$
$R_f=\frac{\rho \ell_f}{A_f}=\frac{\rho(5 \ell)}{\left(\frac{A}{5}\right)}=25\left(\frac{\rho \ell}{A}\right)=25 \times 5=125 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement shown in figure, when the switch $S_2$ is open, the galvanometer, shows no deflection for $l=50 \mathrm{~cm}$ when the switch $S_2$ is closed, the galvanometer shows no deflection for $l=0.416 \mathrm{~m}$. The internal resistance $(r)$ of $6 \mathrm{~V}$ cell is
questions.solution:
When $S_2$ open.
Assume resistance of $X Y=R$.
Resistance of wire per unit length, $x=\frac{R}{L}=R \Omega \mathrm{m}^{-1}$
$\because \quad I=E_0 / R$
Now, the potential drop across $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ length is $6 \mathrm{~V}$, So
$$
\begin{array}{cc} 
& \frac{E_0}{R} \times R \times \frac{50}{100}=6 \\
\Rightarrow & E_0=12 \mathrm{~V}
\end{array}
$$
When $S_2$ closed, potential drop across $0.416 \mathrm{~cm}$ length,
$$
V_1=\frac{E_0}{R} \times R \times 0.416=12 \times 0.416 \approx 5 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Hence, $\quad E-I r=5 \mathrm{~V}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & & 6-I r & =5 \\
& \because & I & =\frac{5}{10} \\
\therefore & & 6-5 & =\frac{5}{10} r \\
\Rightarrow & & r & =2 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electric current $I$ enters and leaves a uniform circular wire of radius $r$ through diametrically opposite points. A charged particle $q$ moves along the axis of circular wire passes through its centre at speed $v$.
The magnetic force on the particle when it passes through the centre has a magnitude.
questions.solution:
Force on a moving charged particle in uniform magnetic field
$$
F=B q v \sin \theta
$$
Since, charge particle moves along the axis of circular current carrying loop, therefore, $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$
When $\theta=0^{\circ} ; F=B q v \sin 0^{\circ}$
$$
F=0
$$
When $\theta=180^{\circ}, F=B q u \sin 180^{\circ}$
$$
F=0
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of a certain material is stretched slowly by ten per cent. Its new resistance and specific resistance become respectively:
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}}\)

Now, \(1=1+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{111}{10}\)

and therefore, \(\mathrm{A}=\frac{10 \mathrm{~A}}{11}\)

So \(\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{p} \times\left(\frac{111}{10}\right)}{\frac{10 \mathrm{~A}}{11}}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{A}} \times \frac{(11)^{2}}{(10)^{2}}=1.21 \mathrm{R}\)

Now resistance becomes \(1.21\) times of initial and specific resistance is the intrinsic property so remains same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A moving coil galvanometer has resistance of $10 \Omega$ and full scale deflection of $0.01 \mathrm{~A}$. It can be converted into voltmeter of $10 \mathrm{~V}$ full scale by connecting into resistance of
questions.solution: $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}-\mathrm{G}=\frac{10}{0.01}-10=990 \Omega$ in series.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of the same material, having radii in the ratio 1: 2 and carry currents in the ratio 4: 1 . The ratio of drift speed of electrons in $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is
questions.solution:
Current flowing through the conductor, $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{n} \mathrm{e} \mathrm{v} \mathrm{A}$. Hence

$\frac{4}{1}=\frac{\text { nev }_{\mathrm{d}_{1}} \pi(1)^{2}}{\operatorname{nev}_{\mathrm{d}_{2}} \pi(2)^{2}}$ or $\frac{v_{\mathrm{d}_{1}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}_{2}}}=\frac{4 \times 1}{1}=\frac{16}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the current in $2 \Omega$ resistor.
questions.solution:
At steady state the capacitor will be fully charged and thus there will be no current in the $1 \Omega$ resistance. So the effective circuit becomes
Net current from the $6 \mathrm{~V}$ battery, $I=\frac{6}{\left(\frac{2 \times 3}{2+3}\right)+\frac{2.8}{1}}=\frac{6}{1.2+2.8}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5 \mathrm{~A}$ Between $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, voltage is same in both resistances, $2 \mathrm{I}_{1}=3 \mathrm{I}_{2}$ where $\mathrm{I}_{1}+\mathrm{I}_{2}=\mathrm{I}=1.5$ $\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}_{1}=3\left(1.5-\mathrm{I}_{1}\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{1}=0.9 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The value of current I as shown in the given circuit diagram is
questions.solution:
The given circuit can be redrawn as

Since, RABRBC=RADRDC=12, hence, it is a balanced Wheatstone bridge, therefore current flowing in arm BD=0

∴    Req=10+20∥6+12=30∥18

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ammeter of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> can measure currents upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The value of shunt resistance to measure current upto <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given, for an ammeter, Ig=1 A, Rg=1.5 Ω

Total current, I=4 A

If Rs be the shunt resistance, then according to the question circuit diagram is given, Rs‖Rg, hence have equal potential difference)

Rs=IgI-Ig×Rg=14-1×1.5=0.5 Ω

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A carbon resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>56</mn><mo>±</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is to be marked with rings of different colours for its identification. The colour code sequence will be
questions.solution:
Given, R=56±5.6 k Ω

=56 k Ω±10 % of 56 k Ω
=56×103±10 % Ω

As per the colour code for carbon resistors, the colour assigned to numbers

5→ Green
6→ Blue
3→ Orange

For 10 % accuracy or tolerance, the colour is silver. Hence, the bands of colours of carbon resistor in sequence are green, blue, orange and silver.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit given below, the value of resistance X, when the potential difference between the points B and D is zero, will be
questions.solution:
P=15+6=21 Ω, Q=8X8+X+3

R=15+6×66+6=18 Ω, S=4+4×44+4=6 Ω

Since potential difference across BD is zero hence it satisfies Wheatstone's Bridge condition,

 ∴PQ=RS, so Q=PSR=21×618=7 Ω

3+8x8+x=7⇒x=8 Ω

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, it is observed that the current I is independent of the value of resistance R5. Then, the resistance value must satisfy
questions.solution:
From the given circuit, we can say that I is independent of resistance R5, so no current flows through R5. This required that the junction of R1 and R2 is at the same potential as the junction of R3 and R4 (Wheatstone's bridge balancing condition)

So, R1R2=R3R4

or R1R4=R3R2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a wire at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. At which temperature, its resistance will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
questions.solution:
We have, Rt=R0 1+αt

Here, 20=R0 1+20α

60=R0 1+500α

⇒   6020=R0 1+500αR0 1+20α

3=1+500 α1+20 α

⇒ 3 1+20 α=1+500 α

⇒ 3+60α=1+500α

⇒ 440 α=2

⇒ α=2440=1220I°C

Also given Rt=25 °C, t= ?

Now, 2025=R0 1+20220R0 1+t220

4 1+t220=5 1+20220

On solving, 

t=80 °C

questions.q: In the circuit shown, the heat produced in 5 Ω resistor is 10 cal s-1. The heat produced per sec in 4 Ω resistor will be
questions.solution:
H=I2Rt

∴ H1H2=l1I22×R1R2

or 10H2=112×54

H2=2 cal

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown in the figure, find the current in $45 \Omega$.
questions.solution: $i=\frac{180}{90}=2 A$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The drift velocity of electrons in silver wire with cross-sectional area $3.14 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ carrying a current of $20 \mathrm{~A}$ is. Given atomic weight of $\mathrm{Ag}=$ $108,$ density of silver $=10.5 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two resistances at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha_{1}$ and $\alpha_{2}$ joined in series act as a single resistance in a circuit. The temperature coefficient of their single resistance will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} R_{1} &=R_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} t\right)+R_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{2} t\right) \\ &=2 R_{0}\left(1+\frac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2} t\right) \end{aligned}$

Comparing with $R=R_{0}(1+\alpha t)$

$\alpha=\frac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a wire is $R$. It is bent at the middle by $180^{\circ}$ and both the ends are twisted together to make a shorter wire. The resistance of the new wire is
questions.solution:
Resistance of wire $(R)=\rho \frac{l}{A}$ If wire is bent in the middle then

$$

l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}, A^{\prime}=2 A

$$

$\therefore$ New resistance, $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}}=\frac{\rho \frac{l}{2}}{2 A}=$

$\frac{\rho l}{4 A}=\frac{R}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A constant voltage is applied between the two ends of a uniform metallic wire. Some heat is developed in it. The heat developed is doubled if
options: both the length and the radius of the wire are halved.
both the length and the radius of the wire are doubled.
the radius of the wire is doubled.
the length of the wire is doubled.
questions.solution:
The heat produced is given by

$$

H=\frac{V^{2}}{R} \text { and } R=\frac{\rho \ell}{\pi r^{2}}

$$

$\therefore H=V^{2}\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\rho \ell}\right)$

or $\quad H=\left(\frac{\pi V^{2}}{\rho}\right) \frac{r^{2}}{\ell}$

Thus heat $(H)$ is doubled if both length $(\ell)$ and radius $(r)$ are doubled.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire $X$ is half the diameter and half the length of a wire $Y$ of similar material. The ratio of resistance of $\mathrm{X}$ to that of $\mathrm{Y}$ is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{R} \propto \frac{\ell}{\mathrm{D}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{Rx}}{\mathrm{Ry}}=\frac{2}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For the circuit (figure), the current is to be measured. The ammeter shown is a galvanometer with a resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}=60.00 \Omega$ converted to an ammeter by a shunt resistance $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}=0.02 \Omega$. The value of the current is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}=60.00 \Omega,$ shunt resistance, $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}=0.02 \Omega$

Total resistance in the circuit is $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}+3=63 \Omega$ Hence, $\mathrm{I}=3 / 63=0.048 \mathrm{~A}$

Resistance of the galvanometer converted to an ammeter is,

$$

\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}} \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}+\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}}=\frac{60 \Omega \times 0.02 \Omega}{(60+0.02) \Omega}=0.02 \Omega

$$

Total resistance in the circuit $=0.02+3$

$$

=3.02 \Omega

$$

Hence, $\mathrm{I}=3 / 3.02=0.99 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What should be the characteristic of fuse wire?
options: High melting point, high specific resistance.
Low melting point, low specific resistance.
High melting point, low specific resistance.
Low melting point, high specific resistance.
questions.solution: Fuse wire should be such that it melts immediatley when strong current flows through the circuit. The same is possible if its melting point is low and resistivity is high.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown in figure potential difference between points $A$ and $B$ is $16 \mathrm{~V}$. the current passing through $2 \Omega$ resistance will be
questions.solution:
$\therefore 4 \mathrm{i}_{1}+2\left(\mathrm{i}_{1}+\mathrm{i}_{2}\right)-3+4 \mathrm{i}_{1}=16 \mathrm{~V}$

Using Kirchhoff's second law in the closed loop we have

$$

9-i_{2}-2\left(i_{1}+i_{2}\right)=0

$$

Solving equations (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{i}_{1}=1.5 \mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{2}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

$\therefore$ current through $2 \mathrm{~W}$ resistor $=2+1.5=3.5 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three voltmeters $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ having resistances $\mathrm{R}, 1.5 \mathrm{R}$ and $3 \mathrm{R},$ respectively, are connected as shown. When some potential difference is applied between $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y},$ the voltmeter readings are $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}$ respectively. Then -
questions.solution:
$V_{A}=I R$
$V_{B}=\left(\frac{2 I}{3}\right) 1.5 R=I R \quad V_{C}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) 3 R=I R$
$\therefore \quad V_{A}=V_{B}=V_{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A letter'A' is constructed of a uniform wire with resistance $1.0 \Omega$ per $\mathrm{cm}$, The sides of the letter are $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the cross piece in the middle is 10 $\mathrm{cm}$ long. The apex angle is $60 .$ The resistance between the ends of the legs is close to:
questions.solution: For ADE $\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2 x}+\frac{1}{10} \quad$ or $\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{20 x}{10+2 x}$ $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+20-\mathrm{x}+20-\mathrm{x} \quad \ldots$(i) or $\quad \frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+40=2 \mathrm{x}$ Solving we get $\mathrm{x}=10 \Omega$ Putting the value of $x=10 \Omega$ in equation (i) We get $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{20 \times 10}{10+2 \times 10}+20-10+20-10$ $=\frac{80}{3}=26.7 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Calculate the effective resistance between A and B in following network.
questions.solution:
Equivalent resistance
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=(5+10+15) \|(10+20+30) \\
\text { So, } R_{\text {eq }}=\frac{30 \times 60}{30+60}=20 \Omega
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The power dissipated in the circuit shown in the figure is 30 Watts. The value of $R$ is
questions.solution:
$P=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}}$
$\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{5+R}{5 R} R_{\mathrm{eq}}=\left(\frac{5 R}{5+R}\right) P=30 \mathrm{~W}$
Substituting the values in equation (i)
$$
30=\frac{(10)^{2}}{\left(\frac{5 R}{5+R}\right)} \Rightarrow R=10 \Omega
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following quantities do not change when a resistor connected to a battery is heated due to the current?
questions.solution: Only number of free electrons is constant, other factors are temperature dependent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of a potentiometer wire is $\ell$. A cell of emf $E$ is balanced at a length $\ell / 3$ from the positive end of the wire. If the length of the wire is increased by $\ell / 2 .$ At what distance will be the same cell give a balance point.
questions.solution:
Potential gradient in the first case $=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\ell}$ $$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\ell}{3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\ell}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{3}
$$
Potential gradient in second case
$$
=\frac{E_{0}}{3 \ell / 2} \text { (x) } \frac{2 E_{0}}{3 \ell}
$$
From equations (i) and (ii),
$$
\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{3}-\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{E}_{0}}{3 \ell}\right) \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\ell}{2}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ammeter reads upto 1 ampere. Its internal resistance is $0.81 \mathrm{ohm} .$ To increase the range to 10 A the value of the required shunt is
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which figure shows the correct variation of applied potential difference (V) with photoelectric current (I) at two different intensities of light $\left(\mathrm{I}_1 \lt \mathrm{I}_2\right)$ of same wavelengths:
questions.solution: Given lights are of same wavelength and stopping potential is independent on intensity. Hence stopping potential will remain same. Intensity $\mathrm{I}_2\gt\mathrm{I}_1$, hence saturation current corresponding to $\mathrm{I}_2$ will be greater than that corresponding to $\mathrm{I}_1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The acceptor level of a p-type semiconductor is 6 eV . The maximum wavelength of light which can create a hole would be : Given hc $=1242 \mathrm{eV}$ nm .
questions.solution:
Energy is, $\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1242 \mathrm{~nm}-\mathrm{eV}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1242}{6}=207 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When light is incident on a metal surface the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons
questions.solution: Max. K.E. $=h v-W_0$; so Max. K.E. $\propto v$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the kinetic energy of a free electron doubles, it's de-Broglie wavelength changes by the factor
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength,
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot m \cdot(K \cdot E)}} \quad \therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{K \cdot E}}$
If K.E is doubled, $\lambda$ becomes $\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton and an $\alpha$-particle are accelerated from rest by $2 \mathrm{~V}$ and $4 \mathrm{~V}$ potentials, respectively. The ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is:
questions.solution:
De-Broglie wavelength,
$\begin{aligned}
& \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mq} \Delta \mathrm{V}}} \\
& \therefore \frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_\alpha \mathrm{V}_\alpha \mathrm{q}_\alpha}} \\
& \because \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}=2 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } \mathrm{V}_\alpha=4 \mathrm{~V} \text { (given) } \\
& \mathrm{m}_\alpha=4 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}} \text { and } \mathrm{q}_\alpha=2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\sqrt{\frac{1 \times 2 \times 1}{4 \times 4 \times 2}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda_p: \lambda_\alpha=4: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Proton (p) and electron (e) will have same deBroglie wavelength when the ratio of their momentum is (assume, $m_p=1849 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}$ )
questions.solution:
De Broglie wavelength is $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{e}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}}$
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{p}}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{1}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The energy levels of an atom is shown is figure. Which one of these transitions will result in the emission of a photon of wavelength $124.1 \mathrm{~nm}$ ? Given $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)$
questions.solution:
As $\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{eV})=\frac{1240}{\lambda(\mathrm{nm})}=\frac{1240}{124.1} \simeq 10 \mathrm{eV}$
Only is transition (D), the energy gap is $10 \mathrm{eV}$ So, option (d) is correct

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron moving with a velocity $\frac{c}{3}\left(c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$ is equal to the wavelength of photon. The ratio of the kinetic energies of electron and photon is
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength, $\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$
Here, $\quad \lambda_e=\frac{h}{m_e \frac{c}{3}}$ and $\lambda_p=\frac{h}{m_p c}$
Given, $\quad \lambda_e=\lambda_p$
So, $\quad \frac{h}{m_e \frac{c}{3}}=\frac{h}{m_p c}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m_e}{m_p}=3$
Ratio of KE $=\frac{K_e}{K_p}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_e v_e^2}{\frac{1}{2} m_p v_p^2}=\frac{1}{3}$
$\left(\because \frac{m_e}{m_p}=3, v_e=\frac{c}{3}\right.$ and $\left.v_p=c\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Monochromatic light of wavelength $667 \mathrm{~nm}$ is produced by a helium neon laser. The power emitted is $9 \mathrm{~mW}$. The number of photons arriving per sec on the average at a target irradiated by this beam is:
questions.solution:
$\lambda=667 \times 10^{-9} m, \mathrm{P}=9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~W}$

$\mathrm{P}=\frac{\text { Nhc }}{\lambda}, \mathrm{N}=$ No. of photons emitted/sec.

$\mathrm{N}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3} \times 667 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}=3 \times 10^{16} / \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electrons are accelerated through a potential difference $\mathrm{V}$ and protons are accelerated through a potential difference $4 \mathrm{~V}$. The de - Broglie wavelengths are $\lambda_{e}$ and $\lambda_{p}$ for electrons and protons respectively. The ratio of $\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{p}}$ is given by: (given $m_{e}$ is mass of electron and $m_{p}$ is mass of proton).
questions.solution:
Energy $=$ charge $\times$ potential diff.

$\mathrm{E}_{\text {electron }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{V}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} 4 \mathrm{~V}$

de $-$ Broglie wavelength $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mE}}}$

$\lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} \mathrm{eV}}}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{\mathrm{p}} e 4 \mathrm{~V}}} \because\left(q_{e}=q_{p}\right)$

$\therefore \frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} \mathrm{eV}}}}{\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{\mathrm{p}} e 4 \mathrm{~V}}}}=2 \sqrt{\frac{m_{\mathrm{P}}}{m_{e}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plane electromagnetic wave is incident on a plane surface of area A, normally and is perfectly reflected. If energy E strikes the surface in time $t$ then force exerted on the surface is ( $c=$ speed of light)
questions.solution:
Incident momentum, $p=\frac{E}{c}$

For perfectly reflecting surface with normal incidence

$\Delta p=2 p=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{c} ; \mathrm{F}=\frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{c t}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plane electromagnetic wave is incident on a plane surface of area A, normally and is perfectly reflected. If energy E strikes the surface in timet then average pressure exerted on the surface is $(\mathrm{c}=$ speed of light $)$
questions.solution:
Incident momentum, $\mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{c}}$ For perfectly reflecting surface with normal incidence 

$\Delta \mathrm{p}=2 \mathrm{p}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{c}}$

$\mathrm{F}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{p}}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{ct}}$

$\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{ctA}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>, which is at rest. The collision is head-on and perfectly elastic. The ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>Y</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>X</mi></msub></math> after the collision is,
questions.solution:
For perfectly elastic collision,

total momentum before collision = total momentum after collision

i.e. mu+3m4×0=mvx+3m4vy

⇒4u=4vx+3vy   ...i

Coefficient of restitution,

e=Velocity of separation after collision Velocity of approach before collision = vy-vxux-uy

⇒1=vy-vxu-0   [∵ ux=u and uy=0]

u=vy-vx   ...ii

From equation (i) and (ii), we get

vx=u7 and vy=8u7

∴ λyλx=h34mvyhm×vx=43×vxvy=43u78u7=16

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency and the intensity of incident beam of light falling on the surface of a photoelectric material is increased by a factor of two. This will
options: increase the maximum kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons by a factor of more than two and would increase the photoelectric current by a factor of two.
increase the maximum kinetic energy of the photo electron and would increase the photoelectric current both by a factor of two.
increase the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron by a factor of two and will have no effect on the magnitude of the photoelectric current produced.
not produce any effect on the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons but will increase the photoelectric current by a factor of two.
questions.solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equations,

hv=ϕ+eVs

eVs=hv-ϕ....(i)

Thus, when the frequency of incident light radiation (photons) v increases to 2v, the stopping potential Vs changes to Vs

eVs'=h(2v)-ϕ....ii

On dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (ii), we get

Vs'Vs=2hv-ϕhv-ϕ=2hv-2ϕ+ϕhv-ϕ

=2(hv-ϕ)+ϕhv-ϕ=2+ϕhv-ϕ

∴ V'sVs>2⇒Vs'>2Vs

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When photon of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> strikes the surface of a metal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the elected photoelectrons have maximum kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> and de-Broglie wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math>. The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron liberated from another metal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> by photon of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>T</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>. If the de-Broglie wavelength of these photoelectrons <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math>, then choose the correct statement(s).
questions.solution:
From Einstein photoelectric equation,
E=ϕ0+KEmax

For metal A  4=ϕA+TA....i
For metal B 4.5=ϕB+TA-1.5....ii

From Eq. (i) and (ii), wo got
ϕB-ϕA=2

Now, according to de-Broglie hypothesis,
λA=hmv=h2mTA

Similarly, λB=h2mTB

∴ λAλB=TBTA=TA-1.5TA=1-1.5TA1/2

122=1-1.5TA

On solving, TA=2.0eV

or ϕA=4-TA=4-2=2.0eV

ϕB=6-TA=6-2=4.0eV

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a proton (charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>) accelerated through a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength,

λ=hp=h2mE=h2mqV

⇒ λ=6.6×10-342×1.6×10-27×1.6×10-19×1000

⇒ λ=6.6×10-347.16×10-22=0.9×10-12 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the number of photon emitted per second by a 25 watt source of monochromatic light of wavelength $6600 Å$. What is the photoelectric current assuming $3 \%$ efficiency for photoelectric effect?
questions.solution:
$P_{\text {in }}=25 \mathrm{~W}, \lambda=6600 \AA=6600 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$

$n h v=P$

Number of photons emitted/sec $\begin{aligned} n &=\frac{P}{\frac{h c}{\lambda}}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c}=\frac{25 \times 6600 \times 10^{-10}}{6.64 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\ &=8.28 \times 10^{19}=\frac{25}{3} \times 10^{19} \end{aligned}$

$3 \%$ of emitted photons are producing current

$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & I=\frac{3}{100} \times n e \\ &=\frac{3}{100} \times \frac{25}{3} \times 10^{19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=0.4 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The beam of light has three wavelengths $4144 Ã, 4972 Ã$ and $6216 Ã$ with a total intensity of $3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wm}^{2}$ equally distributed amongst the three wavelengths. The beam falls normally on the area $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ of a clean metallic surface of work function $2.3 \mathrm{eV}$. Assume that there is $\mathrm{n} 0$ loss of light by reflection and that each energetically capable photon ejects one electron. Calculate the number of photoelectrons liberated in $2 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When $0.50 Ã$ X-rays strike a material, the photoelectrons from the $\mathrm{k}$ shell are observed to move in a circle of radius $23 \mathrm{~mm}$ in a magnetic field of $2 \times 10^{-2}$ tesla acting perpendicularly to the direction of emission of photoelectrons. What is the binding energy of k-shell electrons?
questions.solution:
As we know,

$$

\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB}=\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{BR}

$$

The kinetic energy of the photoelectron

$$

\begin{array}{l}

=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{e^{2} B^{2} R^{2}}{m} \\

=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}\left(23 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\right)} \\

=2.97 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~J} \\

=\frac{2.97 \times 10^{-15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=18.36 \mathrm{keV}

\end{array}

$$

Fnergy of the incident $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$

$$

=\frac{12.4}{0.50}=24.8 \mathrm{keV}

$$

Therefore, Binding energy $=24.8-18.6=$ $6.2 \mathrm{keV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The threshold wavelength of the tungsten is 2300 $Ã… .$ If ultraviolet light of wavelength $1800 Ã…$ is incident on it, then the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons would be about -
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{hv}-\mathrm{hv}_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)$

$1.24 \times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{10^{8}}{18}-\frac{10^{8}}{23}\right)=1.49 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.q: Graph betwen stopping potential for most energetic emitted photoelectrons $\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$ with frequency (v) of incident radiation on metal is given below. Value of $\mathrm{AB} / \mathrm{BC}$, in graph is [where $\mathrm{h}=$ plank's constant, $\mathrm{e}=$ electronic charge]
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light having wavelength $300 \mathrm{nm}$ fall on a metal surface. The work function of metal is $2.54 \mathrm{eV}$, what is stopping potential?
questions.solution:
Energy of incident radiation is given by:
\(\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{6.64 * 10^{-34} * 3 * 10^{8}}{300 * 10^{-9}}=6.64 * 10^{-19}=4.05 \mathrm{eV}\)
The work function of the metal is equal to \(2.54 \mathrm{eV}\)
Hence the maximum kinetic energy of an ejected photoelectron would be \(1.59 \mathrm{eV}\)
And the stopping potential needed to stop this would be \(1.59 \mathrm{~V}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an experiment on photoelectric effect photons of wavelength $300 \mathrm{nm}$ eject electrons from a metal of work function $2.25 \mathrm{eV}$. A photon of energy equal to that of the most energetic electron corresponds to the following transition in the hydrogen atom:
questions.solution:
Given $\phi=2.25 \mathrm{eV}$
$$
\lambda=300 \times 100^{-3} \mathrm{~m}
$$
Energy of the given photon,
$$
\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{hv}
$$
$$
=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{663 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}}=6.63 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}=4.13 \mathrm{eV}
$$
Now,
$$
\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi=4.137-2.25=1.88 \mathrm{eV} \rightarrow(1)
$$
So, $1.88 \mathrm{eV}$ energy is used to jump from one orbit to another orbit by electron. Therefore, energy of different orbital of hydrogen
$$
\begin{array}{rrrr}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
\mathrm{E}=-13.6 \mathrm{ev} & -3.4 & -1.51 & -0.85
\end{array}
$$
And from statement (1),
$$
1.18=E_i-E_f
$$
$\left\{E_i \& E_f \quad\right.$ are energy of initial and final orbit $\}$ And also from the above table, we can observe that,
$$
E_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=(-1.51)-(-3.4)=1.89 \mathrm{eV}
$$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_2-\mathrm{E}_3
$$
That the electron jumps from 3rd to 2nd orbit. Therefore, with one photon of 3oonm wavelength hydrogen electron can jump from 3 to 2 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the energy of a photon is $10 \mathrm{eV}$, then its momentum is
questions.solution:
Momentum of a photon $\propto \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{c}}$
$$
=5.33 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron, in a hydrogen-like atom, is in an excited state. It has a total energy of $-3.4 \mathrm{eV}$. The kinetic energy and the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron are respectively
questions.solution:
$E_{n}=-3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
The kinetic energyis equal to the magnitude of total energy in this case. $\therefore \mathrm{K.E}=+3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
The de Broglie wavelength of electron
$\begin{aligned} \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}} &=\frac{6.64 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}} \mathrm{eV} \\ &=0.66 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$

If a uniform magnetic field of $5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$ is applied parallel to plate, what would be theradius of the path followed by electrons ejected normally from the plate with maximum energy?

questions.q: The photoelectric threshold of metal is $2000 \AA$. The energy of the electrons ejected from the surface by ultraviolet light of wavelength $1500 \AA$ is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)=2.0 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Inside a solenoid of radius 0.5 m , magnetic field is changing at a rate of $50 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{s}$.
Acceleration of an electron placed at a distance of 0.3 m from axis of solenoid will be
questions.solution:
The changing magnetic field induce an electric field which will accelerate the electron.
The acceleration of electron will be
$a=\frac{e E}{m} \quad[\because F=q E]$
To find electric field $E$, we will use Faraday's electromagnetic induction.
$\mathrm{emf}, \varepsilon=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}(B \cdot A)$
where, $B$ is the magnetic field and $A$ is the area of cross-section.
$\varepsilon=-A \cdot \frac{d B}{d t}=-\pi r^2 \frac{d B}{d t}$
Now, electric field, $E=-\frac{\mathrm{emf}}{\text { distance }}=-\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{d}$
$=\frac{\pi r^2}{d} \frac{d B}{d t}$
Now, acceleration, $a=\frac{e}{m} \frac{\pi r^2}{d} \frac{d B}{d t}$
$=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} \times \frac{22}{7}$
$\begin{aligned} & \times \frac{(0.5)^2}{(0.3)} \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \\ & =23 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are two long co-axial solenoids of same length $l$. The inner and outer coils have radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ and number of turns per unit length $n_1$ and $n_2$, respectively. The ratio of mutual inductance to the self-inductance of the inner-coil is:
questions.solution:
The rate of mutual inductance is given by
$\mathrm{M}=\mu_0 \mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{n}_2 \pi \mathrm{~m}_1^2$     ....(i)
The rate of self inductance is given by
$\mathrm{L}=\mu_0 \mathrm{n}_1^2 \pi \mathrm{r}_1^2$    .....(ii)
Dividing (i) by (ii)
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{n}_1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular loop of length 2.5 m and width 2 m is placed at $60^{\circ}$ to a magnetic field of 4 T . The loop is removed from the field in 10 sec . The average emf induced in the loop during this time is
questions.solution:
Averageemf, $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\text { Changein flux }}{\text { Time }}=-\frac{\Delta \phi}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$
$=-\frac{0-\left(4 \times(2.5 \times 2) \cos 60^{\circ}\right)}{10}=+1 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $12 \mathrm{~V}$ battery connected to a coil of resistance $6 \Omega$ through a switch, drives a constant current in the circuit. The switch is opened in $1 \mathrm{~ms}$. The emf induced across the coil is $20 \mathrm{~V}$. The inductance of the coil is :
questions.solution:
Induced emf, $\varepsilon=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow 20=-\mathrm{L} \frac{(0-2)}{10^{-3}}$ Hence, inductance of the coil.
$\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an LC oscillator, if values of inductance and capacitance become twice and eight times, respectively, then the resonant frequency of oscillator becomes $\mathrm{x}$ times its initial resonant frequency $\omega_0$. The value of $x$ is:
questions.solution:
The resonance frequency of LC oscillations circuit is
$\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{L}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~L}$
$\mathrm{C}^{\prime} \rightarrow 8 \mathrm{C}$
$\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~L} \times 8 \mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{4 \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}} 9 \omega_0=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
$\omega=\frac{\omega_0}{4}$ So, $x=\frac{1}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop of radius $\frac{10}{\sqrt{\pi}} \mathrm{cm}$ is placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of $0.5 T$. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in $0.5 s$ at a steady rate. The induced emf in the circular loop at $0.25 s$ is:
questions.solution:
As $\left.\varepsilon\right|_{t=0.5 \mathrm{sec}}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$=-\mathrm{A} \frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{dt}} \quad\left[\because \theta=0^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=1\right]$
$=-\pi \times\left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{\pi}}\right)^2 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{0-0.5}{0.5}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~V}=10 \mathrm{mV}$
As $\frac{d B}{d t}=$ constant $\Rightarrow$ Induced emf will not change with time. So, $\left.\mathrm{e}\right|_{0,5 \mathrm{sec}}=\left.\mathrm{e}\right|_{0.25 \mathrm{sec}}=10 \mathrm{mV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal disc of radius $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ is rotated at a constant angular speed of $60 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a plane at right angles to an external field of magnetic induction $0.05 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The emf induced between the centre and a point on the rim will be
questions.solution:
Induced emf produced between the centre and a point on the disc is given by

$\begin{array}{l}

e=\frac{1}{2} \omega \mathrm{BR}^{2} \\

\omega=60 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{B}=0.05 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^{2} \\

\text { and } \mathrm{R}=100 \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~m} \\

\text { We get } e=\frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 0.05 \times(1)^{2}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}

\end{array}$

questions.q:
A conducting square loop of side $\mathrm{L}$ and resistance $\mathrm{R}$ moves in its plane with a uniform velocity v perpendicular to one of its side. A magnetic induction $\mathrm{B}$ constant in time and space, pointing perpendicular and into the plane of the loop exists everywhere.
The current induced in the loop is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A semicircular wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> rotates in uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> about its diameter with angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. If the total resistance of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then the mean power generated per period of rotation is
questions.solution:
The flux linked with coil (made of wire of radius r) of area A and magnetic field B, is given by

ϕ=BA cos θ=B πr22 cos ωt

ϕ=πBr22 cos ωt   [∵    A=πr22 and θ=ωt]

By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, induced emf, e=-dϕdt

Hence, after differentiating,

e=-ddtπBr22 cos ωt=πBr2ω2 sin ωt  (where, R= resistance in the circuit)

∴ Power P=e2R=π2B2r4ω2 sin2ωt4 R

∴ Mean power P¯=π2B2r4ω24 R·sin2ωt¯

=π2B2r4ω24 R·12   ∵    sin2ωt¯=12

=πBr2ω28 R

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a coil of resistance 50 Ω, the induced current developed by changing magnetic flux through it, is shown in figure as a function of time. The magnitude of change in flux through the coil is
questions.solution:
According to the given graph I-t induced current (I) is obtained from the slope. Hence, I=1Rdϕdt

⇒dϕ=Idt×R= area of triangle POS×R=8×0.22×50=40 Wb

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two inductors L1 and L2 are connected in parallel and a time varying current flows as shown in figure. The ratio ot current i1i2 at any ime t Is
questions.solution:
As the inductors are in parallel, induced emf across the two inductors is the same, i.e.

e1=e2

L1di1dt=L2di2dt

On integrating both sides, we get
L1∫di1dt=L2∫di2dt

L1i1=L2i2

⇒i1i2=L2L1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>025</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> with its plane perpendicular to the loop. The radius of the loop is made to shrink at a constant rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The induced emf when the radius is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, is
questions.solution:
Here, magnetic field,B=0.025 T

∴ Magnetic flux ϕ=B·A=B·πr2

Induced emf e=dϕdt

=Bπ2r·drdt

=0.025×π×2×2×10-2×1×10-3

=π×10-6  V

=π μV

questions.q: An inductor of inductance $L=400 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistors of resistance $R_{1}=2 \Omega$ and $R_{2}=2 \Omega$ are connected to a battery of emf $12 \mathrm{~V}$ as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the battery is negligible. The switch $S$ is closed at $t=0 .$ The potential drop across $L$ as a function of time is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $0.04 \mathrm{~T}$ with its plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. The radius of the loop starts shrinking at $2 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{s}$. The induced emf in the loop when the radius is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
questions.solution:
Induced emf in the loop is given by

$\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{B} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}$ where $\mathrm{A}$ is the area of the loop.

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{B} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right)=-\mathrm{B} \pi 2 \mathrm{r} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\

\mathrm{r}=2 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\

\mathrm{dr}=2 \mathrm{~mm}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \\

\mathrm{dt}=1 \mathrm{~s} \\

\mathrm{e}=-0.04 \times 3.14 \times 2 \times 2 \times 10^{-2} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1} \mathrm{~V} \\

=0.32 \pi \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~V} \\

=3.2 \pi \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~V} \\

=3.2 \pi \mu \mathrm{V}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting square loop is placed in a magnetic field $B$ with its plane perpendicular to the field. The sides of the loop start shrinking at a constant rate $\alpha$. The induced emf in the loop at an instant when its side is ' $a$ ' is
questions.solution:
At any time $t$, the side of the square $a=\left(a_{0}\right.$ $-\alpha t),$ where $a_{0}=$ side at $t=0$

At this instant, flux through the square :

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\phi=B A \cos 0^{\circ}=B\left(a_{0}-\alpha t\right)^{2} \\

\therefore \text { emf induced } E=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\

\Rightarrow E=-B .2\left(a_{0}-\alpha t\right)(0-\alpha)=+2 \alpha a B

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil 10 turns and a resistance of $20 \Omega$ is connected in series with B.G of resistance $30 \Omega$. The coil is placed with its plane perpendicular to the direction of a uniform magnetic field of induction $10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$. If it is now turned through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ about an axis in its plane. Find the charge induced in the coil. (Area of a coil $=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ )
questions.solution:
Given: $\mathrm{n}=10$ turns, $\mathrm{R}_{\text {coil }}=20 \Omega, \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{G}}=30 \Omega$, Total resistance in the circuit $=20+30$

$=50 \Omega$

$\mathrm{A}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{2}, \mathrm{~B}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}, \phi_{1}=0^{\circ}, \phi_{2}=60^{\circ}$

$\mathrm{q}=\frac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{Bn} \mathrm{A} \cos \theta_{1}-\mathrm{Bn} \mathrm{A} \cos \theta_{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$

$=\frac{\operatorname{Bn} \mathrm{A}(\cos 0-\cos 60)}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{BnA}(1-0.5)}{\mathrm{R}}$

$=\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{50}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-5}}{50}$

$=1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{C} \quad$ (Charge induced in a coil)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Eddy currents are produced when

a metal is kept in steady magnetic field
a circular coil is placed in a magnetic field

questions.solution: Eddy can be created when a conductor is moving through a magnetic field, or when the magnetic field surrounding a stationary conductor is varying. Option A

questions.q: Two coaxial solenoids are made by winding thin insulated wire over a pipe of cross-sectional area $\mathrm{A}=10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and length $=20 \mathrm{~cm} .$ If one of the solenoid has 300 turns and the other 400 turns, their mutual inductance is $\left(\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{TmA}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} M &=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{~N}_{1} \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{~A}}{\ell} \\ &=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 300 \times 400 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}}{0.2} \\ &=2.4 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{H} \end{aligned}$

#

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of secondary and primary turns of step-up transformer is $4: 1 .$ If a current of 4 A is applied to the primary, the induced current in secondary will be
questions.solution: $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{4} \times 4=1 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The laws of electromagnetic induction have been used in the construction of a
questions.solution:
Hint:
The laws of Electromagnetic Induction give us idea about the various ways in which we can produce Electric Current using Electric or Magnetic field.
Correct Option is D.
Explanation for correct answer:
- Galvanometer is a device which detects current in the circuit.
- Voltmeter is a device which measures potential difference between two points in the circuit.
- Electric motor is a device which converts Electrical Energy into Mechanical Energy.
- Electric Generator is an device which produces Electric Current from Mechanical energy, Chemical energy, or by using Magnetic or Electric Fields. The laws if Electromagnetic Induction are used in Electric Generator which produces Electric Current from Magnetic or Electric Fields.

questions.q: The back emf induced in a coil, when current changes from 1 ampere to zero in one milli-second, is 4 volts, the self inductance of the coil is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}$
But $\mathrm{e}=4 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{0-1}{10^{-3}}=-1 / 10^{-3}$
$\therefore \frac{-1}{10^{-3}}(-\mathrm{L})=4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=4 \times 10^{-3}$ henry

questions.solution:
From $L=\frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{1} \alpha \frac{N^{2}}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor consists of two circular plates of radius $R=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$. They are separated by a short distance. If electric field between the capacitor plates changes as $\frac{d E}{d t}=6 \times 10^{13} \frac{\mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{~m} \times \mathrm{s}}$, then the value of displacement current is
questions.solution:
Given, area of plates, $A=\pi R^2=3.14 \times(0.1)^2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ and $\frac{d E}{d t}=6 \times 10^{13} \frac{\mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{~m} \times \mathrm{s}}$
Displacement current between the plates,
$\begin{aligned}
I_d & =\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi}{d t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad I_d & =\varepsilon_0 A\left(\frac{d E}{d t}\right) \quad\left[\because \frac{d \phi}{d t}=A \frac{d E}{d t}\right] \\
& =8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.14 \times(0.1)^2 \times 6 \times 10^{13} \\
\Rightarrow \quad I_d & =16.67 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electromagnetic waves travel in a medium with speed of $1.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The relative permeability of the medium is 2.0 . The relative permittivity will be :
questions.solution:
$v=\frac{C}{\sqrt{\mu_{\mathrm{r}} \epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}}$
$\Rightarrow 1.5 \times 10^8=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2 \times \epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \epsilon_{\mathrm{T}}=4 \Rightarrow \epsilon_{\mathrm{T}}=2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}}$ represent electric field and propagation vectors of the EM waves in vacuum, then magnetic field vector is given by: ( $\omega$ - angular frequency) :
questions.solution:
For an EM wave
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{\omega}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of average electric energy density and total average energy density of electromagnetic wave is :
questions.solution:
We have $\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}}{2 \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\left[\because \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \in_0 \mathrm{E}_0^2=\frac{\mathrm{B}_0^2}{2 \mu_0}\right]$

questions.q: An electromagnetic wave is propagating along $X$-axis. At $x=1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $t=18 \mathrm{~s}$, its electric vector $|E|=8 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, then the magnitude of its magnetic vector is

As we know that, $\frac{E}{B}=c$
$$

An electromagnetic wave passes through space and its equation is given by $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{0} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx})$
where $\mathrm{E}$ is electric field. Energy density of electromagnetic wave in space is

$$
=\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{rm}}^{2}=\varepsilon_{0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field in a travelling electromagnetic wave has a peak value of $20 \mathrm{nT}$. The peak value of electric field strength is

$$
\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{0}\right|=\left|\mathrm{B}_{0}\right| \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}|=20 \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^{8}=6 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}
$$
