questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An oil drop of radius $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ is held stationary under a constant electric field of $3.65 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$ due to some excess electrons presence on it. If the density of oil drop is $1.26 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$, then number of excess electrons on the oil drop approximately are
[Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ ]
questions.solution:
$E=3.65 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}, r=1 \mu \mathrm{~m}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text {oil }} & =1.26 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3 \\
& =1.26 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3
\end{aligned}$
Since, droplet is stationary hence weight of droplet $=$ force due to electric field
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \cdot \rho_{\text {oil }} \cdot g=q E...(i)$
If $n$ be the number of excess electrons in the oil drop, then
$q=n e$
Hence, from Eq. (i), $\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_{\text {oil }} \cdot g=n e E$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow n & =\frac{4 \pi r^3 \rho_{\text {oill }} g}{3 e E} \\
& =\frac{4 \times 3.14 \times\left(10^{-6}\right)^3 \times 1.26 \times 10^3 \times 10}{3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3.65 \times 10^4} \\
& =9.03 \simeq 9
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential of a large liquid drop when eight liquid drops are combined is 20 V , then the potential of each single drop was
questions.solution:
Volume of eight drops = Volume of a big drop
$\therefore \quad\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right) \times 8=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 r=R$...(i)
According to charge conservation,
$8 q=Q$...(ii)
Potential of one small drop, $V^{\prime}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$
Similarly, potential of big drop, $V=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 R}$
Now, $\quad \frac{V^{\prime}}{V}=\frac{q}{Q} \times \frac{R}{r}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{V^{\prime}}{20}=\frac{q}{8 q} \times \frac{2 r}{r} \quad$ [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
$\therefore \quad V^{\prime}=5 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A dust particle of mass $4 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{mg}$ in suspended in air under the influence of an electric field of $50 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$ directed vertically upwards. How many electrons were removed from the neutral dust particle? [Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ${ }^2$ ]
questions.solution:
Mass of dust particle, $m=4 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{mg}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =4 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} \\
& =4 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} \\
& =4 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~kg}
\end{aligned}$
Electric field, $E=50 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$
Weight of dust particle, $W=m g$
$=4 \times 10^{-18} \times 10=4 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~N}$
Electric force experienced by dust particle,
$\begin{aligned}
& F_e=q E \\
& F_e=n e \cdot E=n \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 50
\end{aligned}$
where, $n$ is the number of electrons removed from neutral dust particle.
$\therefore$ At balance condition,
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Electric force }=\text { Weight of dust particle } \\
& n \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 50=4 \times 10^{-17} \\
& n=\frac{4 \times 10^{-17}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 50} \\
& =\frac{400}{80}=5
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field at point $(30,30,0)$ due to a charge of $0.008 \mu \mathrm{C}$ placed at origin will be, (coordinates are in cm )
questions.solution:
$E=\frac{K q}{r^2} \cdot \hat{r}=\frac{K q}{r^3} \cdot r$
Here,
$\begin{aligned}
r & =\sqrt{\left(x_2-x_1\right)^2+\left(y_2-y_1\right)^2+\left(z_2-z_1\right)^2} \\
& =\sqrt{(30)^2+(30)^2+0^2} \\
& =30 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm} \\
& =30 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
$\text { and } q=8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$
Also, $\quad \mathbf{r}=(30 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+30 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned}
\text { So, } \mathrm{E} & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6}}{\left(30 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-2}\right)^3} \times(30 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+30 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \times 10^{-2} \\
& =\frac{9 \times 8 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-11}}{27 \times 2 \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^3} \times 30(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \\
& =\frac{9 \times 8 \times 10^2 \times 3}{27 \times 2 \sqrt{2}}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \\
& =200 \sqrt{2}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If two charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ are separated with distance ' $d$ ' and placed in a medium of dielectric constant $K$. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force ?
questions.solution:
As. $F$(air)=$F$(medium)
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{~d}_{\text {air }}^2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}} \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{~d}^2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d}_{\text {air }}=\mathrm{d} \sqrt{\mathrm{K}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Electric potential at a point 'P' due to a point charge of $5 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$ is 50 V . The distance of ' P ' from the point charge is:
(Assume, $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^{+9} \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
Electric potential at a point P due to a point charge, $\left(\because \mathrm{K}=9 \times 10^9\right)$
$V_P=\frac{K Q}{r}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 50=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{r}=\frac{45}{50}=\frac{9}{10}=0.9 \mathrm{~m}=90 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five charges $+q,+5 q,-2 q,+3 q$ and $-4 q$ are situated as shown in the figure, The electric flux due to this configuration through the surface S is :
questions.solution:
Using Gauss's law, $\phi=\frac{q}{\epsilon_0}$
Here, $\mathrm{q}=$ charge inside the closed surface
$\therefore \phi=\frac{q+(-2 q)+5 q}{\epsilon_0}$
$\Rightarrow \phi=\frac{4 q}{\epsilon_0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a cuboid of dimension $2 \mathrm{~L} \times 2 \mathrm{~L} \times \mathrm{L}$, a charge $q$ is placed at the centre of the surface ' $S$ ' having area of $4 \mathrm{~L}^2$. The flux through the opposite surface to ' $\mathrm{S}$ ' is given by
questions.solution:
After placing similar cubic at the bottom, we get cube of side $2 \mathrm{~L}$ having change $\mathrm{q}$ at its centre.

From the Gauss's law electric flux through whole


Flux passing through shaded face
$\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q} / \varepsilon_0}{6}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force between two small charged spheres having charges of $1 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}$ and $2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}$ placed $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ apart in air is
questions.solution:
Here, $q_1=1 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}, q_2$ and $2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}$, $r=20 \mathrm{~cm}=20 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$F=\frac{q_1 q_2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}{\left(20 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2}$
$=4.5 \times 10^{-3} N$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which one of the following graphs represents the variation of electric potential with distance $r$ from the centre of a non-conducting charged sphere of radius $R$ ?
questions.solution:
Electric potential at centre $R=0$ $$ V_{\text {centre }}=1.5 V_{\text {surface }} $$
where, $$ \begin{aligned} V_0 & =\frac{k Q}{R} \\ V_{\text {inside }} & =\frac{k Q}{2 R}\left(3-\frac{r^2}{R^2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric potential $V$ is given as a function of distance $\times($ metre $)$ by $V=\left(5 x^{2}+10 x-4\right)$ volt. Question: Value of electric field at $x=1 \mathrm{~m}$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{V}=5 x^{2}+10 x-4$

$\mathrm{E}=\frac{-\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-(10 x+10)$

At $x=1 m, \mathrm{E}=-20 \mathrm{~V} / m$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $Q$ is placed at each of the opposite corners of a square. A charge $q$ is placed at each of the other two corners. If the net electrical force on $Q$ is zero, then $\frac{Q}{q}$ equals:
questions.solution: It is given that $\vec{F}_{A}+\vec{F}_{B}+\vec{F}_{D}=0$ Where $\vec{F}_{A}, \vec{F}_{B}$ and $\vec{F}_{D}$ are the forces applied by charges placed at $A, B$ and $D$ on the charge placed at $C$. $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{B}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{D}}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{A}} \\ \left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{B}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{D}}\right|=\sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{KqQ}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{A}}\right|=\frac{\mathrm{KQ}^{2}}{2 a^{2}} \\ \therefore \sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{KqQ}}{a^{2}}=-\frac{\mathrm{KQ}^{2}}{2 a^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{q}=-2 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charge q, Q and 4q are placed in a straight line of length $l$ at points distant $0, \frac{1}{2}$ and $l$ respectively from one end. In order to make the net froce on q zero, the charge Q must be equal to
questions.solution:
$\left(\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}\right)_{\mathrm{q}}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{Qq}}{\left(\frac{\ell}{2}\right)^{2}}+\mathrm{k} \frac{4 \mathrm{q}^{2}}{\ell^{2}}=0$
$\begin{array}{l} \text { where } \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \\ \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{Qq}+4 \mathrm{q}^{2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}=-\mathrm{q} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $+q$ is at a distance $L / 2$ above a square of side $\mathrm{L}$. Then what is the flux linked with the surface?
questions.solution: m{L} The given square of side $L$ may be considered as one of the faces of a cube with edge $L$. Then given charge q will be considered to be placed at the centre of the cube. Then according to Gauss's theorem, the magnitude of the electric flux through the faces (six) of the cube is given by $\phi=\mathrm{q} / \varepsilon_{0}$ Hence, electric flux through one face of the cube for the given square will be $\phi^{\prime}=\frac{1}{6} \phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the following figures, which figure represents the variation of the electric field with distance from the centre of a uniformly charged non-conducting spheres of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> ?
questions.solution:
The electric field intensity at a point lying outside the sphere (non-conducting) is

E=14πε0·qr2

i.e.  E∝1r2    …i

The electric field intensity at the surface (r=R),

E=14πε0qR2

⇒E∝1R2

where, R being the radius of sphere,

The electric field intensity inside the sphere is

E=qr4πε0R3

⇒E∝r

At the centre of sphere, r=0, ∴E=0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two metal plates having a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> apart. It is found that a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>96</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> remains suspended in the region between the plates. The charge on the particle must be (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi><mo>=</mo></math> elementary charge)
questions.solution:
V=800 V

E=Vd=8002×10-2 V m-1

=4×104  V m-1

At equilibrium electrostatic force must be equal to force due to weight.

qE=mg

⇒ q=mgE

=1.96×10-15×9.84×104 coulomb

=1.96×10-15×9.84×104×1.6×10-19 elementary charges

=3 e

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges are placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a as shown in the figure. The force experienced by the charge placed at the vertex A in a direction normal to BC is
questions.solution:
Resultant force

F'=F2+F2+2FFcos120°=F   ∵cos120°=-12

Now, from figure

F=F'2+F''2+2F'F''cos90°

Now, the force normal to BC at vertex A is

F''=F2-F'2=0   ∵F'=F

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface charge density of a thin charged disc of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is $\sigma$. The value of the electric field at the centre of the disc is $\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}$. With respect to the field at the centre, the electric field along the axis at a distance $\mathrm{R}$ from the centre of the $\operatorname{disc}$
questions.solution:
Electric field intensity at the centre of the disc.

\(\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}} \text { (given) }\)

Electric field along the axis at any distance \(x\) from the centre of the disc

\(\mathrm{E}^{\prime}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}}-\mathrm{R}^{2}}\right)\)

From question, \(\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{R}\) (radius of disc)

\(\begin{aligned}

&\therefore E^{\prime}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}\left(1-\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+R^{2}}}\right) \\

&=\frac{\sigma}{2 \epsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} R-R}{\sqrt{2} R}\right) \\

&=\frac{4}{14} E

\end{aligned}\)

\(\therefore\) \% reduction in the value of electric field

\(=\frac{\left(\mathrm{E}-\frac{4}{14} \mathrm{E}\right) \times 100}{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{1000}{14} \%=70.7 \%\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical charged spheres suspended from a common point by two massless strings of lengths I, are initially at a distance d $(\mathrm{d}<<1)$ apart because of their mutual repulsion. The charges begin to leak from both the spheres at a constant rate. As a result, the spheres approach each other with a velocity $v$. Then $v$ varies as a function of the distance $\mathrm{x}$ between the spheres, as:
questions.solution: From figure $\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{mg}} \simeq \theta$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{kq}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2} \mathrm{mg}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \ell} $$ or $x^{3} \propto q^{2} \ldots$ (1) or $x^{3 / 2} \propto q \ldots(2)$ Differentiating eq. (1) w.r.t. time $3 \mathrm{x}^{2} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto 2 \mathrm{q} \frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{dt}}$ but $\frac{\mathrm{dq}}{\mathrm{dt}}$ is constant $$ \text { so } \mathrm{x}^{2}(\mathrm{v}) \propto \mathrm{q} \text { Replace } \mathrm{q} \text { from eq. }(2) $$ $\mathrm{x}^{2}(\mathrm{v}) \propto \mathrm{x}^{3 / 2} \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{v} \propto \mathrm{x}^{-1 / 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere of radius $R$ has a charge $Q$ distributed in its volume with a charge density $\rho$ $=k r^{a}$, where $k$ and $a$ are constants and $r$ is the distance from its centre. If the electric field at $r=$ $\frac{R}{2}$ is $\frac{1}{8}$ times that at $r=R,$ the value of $a$ is
questions.solution:
Using Gauss's law, we have

$$

\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{A}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int(\rho d v)=\frac{1}{\epsilon_{0}} \int_{0}^{R} k r^{a} \times 4 \pi r^{2} d r

$$

or $E \times 4 \pi R^{2}=\left(\frac{4 \pi k}{\epsilon_{0}}\right) \frac{R^{(a+3)}}{(a+3)}$

$$

\therefore \quad E_{1}=\frac{k R^{(a+1)}}{\epsilon_{0}(a+3)}

$$

For $r=\frac{R}{2} \cdot E_{2}=\frac{k\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{a+1}}{\epsilon_{0}(a+3)}$

Given, $E_{2}=\frac{E_{1}}{8}$

or $\frac{k\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{a+1}}{\epsilon_{0}(a+3)}=\frac{1}{8} \frac{k R^{(a+1)}}{\epsilon_{0}(a+3)}$

$$

\begin{array}{ll}

\therefore & \quad \frac{1}{a+1}=\frac{1}{8} \\

\text { or } & a=2 .

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the corners of an equilateral triangle of side a (1 metre), three point charges are placed (each of $0.1 \mathrm{C}$ ). If this system is supplied energy at the rate of $1 \mathrm{kw},$ then calculate the time required to move one of the mid-point of the line joining the other two.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A spherically symmetric gravitational system of particles has a mass density $\rho=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\rho_{0} & \text { for } \mathrm{r} \leq \mathrm{R} \\ 0 & \text { for } \mathrm{r}>\mathrm{R}\end{array}\right.$

where $\mathrm{r}_{0}$ is a constant. A test mass can undergo

circular motion under the influence of the gravitational field of particles. Its speed $\mathrm{V}$ as a function of distance $r(0<r<\infty)$ from the centre of the system is represented by
questions.solution: Given mass density \(P=\left\{P_{0}\right.\) for \(r \leq R O\) for \(\left.r>R\right\}\) Since gravitational depends on mass, which is zero for \(r>R\), hence it only acts for \(r \leq R\). \(\begin{aligned} &\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\text {graw }}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\text {circular }} \\ &\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\text {sphere }} \times \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{e})^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ &\Rightarrow \mathrm{V}^{2} \alpha \frac{\mathrm{M}_{\text {sphere }}}{\mathrm{R}} \text { since } \mathrm{r} \leq \mathrm{R} \\ &\Rightarrow \mathrm{V}^{2} \alpha \frac{\mathrm{R}^{3}}{\mathrm{R}} \alpha \mathrm{R}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V} \alpha \mathrm{R} \quad \text { straight line }+\mathrm{ve} \text { slope } \end{aligned}\) Beyond \(\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{R}\), mass density \(=0 \Rightarrow\) acceleration due to gravity \(=0 \Rightarrow\) velocity stays constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point dipoles $\mathrm{p} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ are located at (0,0,0) and $(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0,2 \mathrm{~m})$ respectively. The resultant electric field due to the two dipoles at the point $(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0)$ is
questions.solution: The given point is at axis of $\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}}{2}$ dipole and at equatorial line of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}$ dipole so that field at given point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field in theregion is given by $\mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{x}^{3}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$, then the correct expression for the potential in the region is [assume potential at infinity is zero]
questions.solution: $V=-\int_{\infty}^{x} E \cdot d r=-\int_{\infty}^{x} \frac{M}{x^{3}} d x=\frac{M}{2 x^{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two long coaxial and conducting cylinders of radius a and $\mathrm{b}$ are separated by a material of conductivity $\sigma$ and a constant potential difference $\mathrm{V}$ is maintained between them, by a battery. Then the current, per unit length of the cylinder flowing from one cylinder to the other is -
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{V}=\int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \ell=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}} \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}\right)$

Now, $I=\sigma \int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d A}=\sigma \int \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r} \cdot 2 \pi d r=\frac{\sigma \lambda}{\varepsilon_{0}}$

From (1)$: I=\frac{2 \sigma \pi \varepsilon_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0} \ln (b / a)}=\frac{2 \pi \sigma}{\ln (b / a)} v$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three equal charges (q) are placed at corners of an equilateral triangle of side $a$. The force on any charge is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}=\sqrt{\mathrm{F}^{2}+\mathrm{F}^{2}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{2} \cos 60^{\circ}}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a region of space having a uniform electric field $\mathrm{E}$, a hemispherical bowl of radius $\mathrm{r}$ is placed. The electric flux $\phi$ through the bowl is
questions.solution: $\phi=\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{ds}) \cos \theta=\mathrm{E}\left(2 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}\right) \cos 0^{\circ}=2 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{E}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric field intensity just sufficient to balance the earth's gravitational attraction on an electron will be: (given mass and charge of an electron respectively are $9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \times$ $\left.10^{-19} \mathrm{C} .\right)$
questions.solution:
$-\mathrm{eE}=\mathrm{mg}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=-5.6 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an uniformly charged sphere of total charge Q and radius $\mathrm{R},$ the electric field $\mathrm{E}$ is plotted as function of distance from the centre. The graph which would correspond to the above will be
questions.solution:
Electric field inside the uniformly charged sphere varies linearly, $E=\frac{k Q}{R^{3}} r,(r \leq R)$ while outside the sphere, it varies as inverse
square of distance, $E=\frac{k Q}{r^{2}} ;(r \geq R)$ which is correctly represented in option (c).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $\mathrm{Q}_{1}$ exerts some force on a second charge $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$. If a 3 rd charge $\mathrm{Q}_{3}$ is brought near, then the force of $\mathrm{Q}_{1}$ exerted on $\mathrm{Q}_{2}-$
options: will increase if $\mathrm{Q}_{3}$ is of the same sign as $\mathrm{Q}_{1}$ and will decrease if $\mathrm{Q}_{3}$ is of opposite sign.
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow metal sphere of radius $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is charged such that the potential on its surface is $10 \mathrm{~V}$. The potential at a distance of $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the sphere is
questions.solution: Potential at any point inside the sphere $=$ potential at the surface of the sphere $=10 \mathrm{~V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $+q$ is at a distance $L / 2$ above a square of side $\mathrm{L}$. Then what is the flux linked with the surface?
questions.solution: The given square of side $\mathrm{L}$ may be considered as one of the faces of a cube with edge $\mathrm{L}$. Then given charge $\mathrm{q}$ will be considered to be placed at the centre of the cube. Then according to Gauss's theorem, the magnitude of the electric flux through the faces (six) of the cube is given by $$ \phi=\mathrm{q} / \varepsilon_{0} $$ Hence, electric flux through one face of the cube for the given square will be $\phi^{\prime}=\frac{1}{6} \phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two metallic spheres of radii $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ are given charges of $-1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{C}$ and $5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. If these are connected by a conducting wire, the final charge on the bigger sphere is
questions.solution:
$\quad \frac{k x}{1 \mathrm{~cm}}=\frac{k(Q-x)}{3 \mathrm{~cm}}$
$3 x=Q-x \Rightarrow 4 x=Q$
$x=\frac{Q}{4}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-2}}{4} C=1 \times 10^{-2}$
$Q^{\prime}=\mathrm{Q}-x=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a region, the potential is represented by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z})=6 \mathrm{x}-8 \mathrm{xy}-8 \mathrm{y}+6 \mathrm{yz},$ where $\mathrm{V}$ is in volts and $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z}$ are in metres. The electric force experienced by a charge of 2 coulomb situated at point (1,1,1) is :
questions.solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=-\frac{\partial \mathrm{V}}{\partial \mathrm{x}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\partial \mathrm{V}}{\partial \mathrm{y}} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\frac{\partial \mathrm{V}}{\partial \mathrm{z}} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$=-[(6-8 \mathrm{y}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-8 \mathrm{x}-8+6 \mathrm{z}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(6 \mathrm{y}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}]$
$\mathrm{At}(1,1,1), \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+10 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}})=\sqrt{2^{2}+10^{2}+6^{2}}=\sqrt{140}=2 \sqrt{35}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=2 \times 2 \sqrt{35}=4 \sqrt{35}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If an electron has an initial velocity in a direction different from that of an electric field, the path of the electron is
questions.solution: The path is a parabola, because initial velocity can be resolved into two rectangular components, one along $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ and other $\perp$ to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$. The former decreases at a constant rate and latter is unaffected. The resultant path is therefore a parabola.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If on combining two charged bodies, the current does not flow then
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One main scale division of a vernier caliper is equal to $m$ units. If ${m}^{\text {th }}$ division of main scale coincides with $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale, the least count of the vernier caliper is:
questions.solution:
Given 1 Main scale division = m
$\mathrm{n} M S D=(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{VSD}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{MSD}$
Least count of vernier caliper $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L . C=m-m\left(\frac{n}{n+1}\right)=\left(1-\frac{n}{n+1}\right) m$
$=m\left(\frac{n+1-n}{n+1}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{n+1}\right) m \Rightarrow L \cdot C=\left(\frac{m}{n+1}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the shown arrangement of the experiment of the meter bridge if $A C$ corresponding to null deflection of galvanometer is $\mathrm{x},$ what would be its value if the radius of the wire $\mathrm{AB}$ is doubled?
questions.solution: At null point $\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{R_{3}}{R_{4}}=\frac{x}{100-x}$ If radius of the wire is doubled, then the resistance of $A C$ will change and also the resistance of $C B$ will change. But since $\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}$ does not change so, $\frac{R_{3}}{R_{4}}$ should also not change at null point. Therefore the point $C$ does not change.
