questions.q: Which of the following is most accurate?
options: A screw gauge of least count $0.001 \mathrm{~mm}$.
A screw gauge having pitch $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and 50 divisions on circular scale.
A vernier callipers of least count $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$.
Vernier callipers having 20 divisions on the sliding scale (vernier scale) coinciding 19 divisions on the main millimetre scale.
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body which is initially at rest at a height $R$ above the surface of the Earth of radius R, falls freely towards the Earth, then its velocity on reaching the surface of the Earth is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance between Sun and Earth is $R$. The duration of year if the distance between Sun and Earth becomes $3 R$ will be:
questions.solution:
Basically 1 year is equal to time period of earth revolution around sun.
So, $T_i=1$ year
Now,
$T^2 \propto R^3$
$\therefore\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^3$
$\Rightarrow T_2=\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{3 / 2} T_1=\left(\frac{3 R}{R}\right)^{3 / 2} \times 1$
$=3 \sqrt{3}$ years.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a particle inside a uniform spherical shell, the gravitational force on the particle is
questions.solution: Various regions of spherical shell attract the point mass inside it in various directions. These forces cancel each other completely. Therefore the gravitational force on the particle is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy of a satellite in its orbit around the earth is E . What should be the kinetic energy of the satellite so as to enable it to escape from the gravitational pull of the earth?
questions.solution:
We know that $v_e=\sqrt{2} v_0$, where $\mathrm{v}_0$ is orbital velocity.
K.E. in the orbit, $E=\frac{1}{2} M v_0{ }^2$
K.E. to escape $E=\frac{1}{2} M v_e^2=\frac{1}{2} M\left(2 v_0{ }^2\right)$
$=\frac{1}{2} M v_0^2 \times 2=2 E$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical particles each of mass ' $m$ ' go round a circle of radius $a$ under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The angular speed of each particle will be:
questions.solution:
Radius of circle, $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{a}$
Centrifugal force, $F=m \omega^2 r$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{Gmm}}{(2 \mathrm{a})^2}=\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{a} \\
& \left(\because \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{G} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{M}_2}{\mathrm{~d}}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Here $M_1=m ; M_2=m$ and $d=2 a$
$\Rightarrow$ angular speed, $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{G m}{4 a^3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{V}$ is the gravitational potential due to sphere of uniform density on it's surface, then it's value at the center of sphere will be:
questions.solution:
Potential due to sphere
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{2 \mathrm{R}^3}\left(3 \mathrm{R}^2-\mathrm{r}^2\right)$
At surface $r=R \Rightarrow V=\left(\frac{G M}{R}\right)$
At centre, $r=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_0=\frac{3 \mathrm{GM}}{2 \mathrm{R}}=\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{~V}}{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two planets $A$ and $B$ of radii $R$ and $1.5 R$ have densities $\rho$ and $\rho / 2$ respectively. The ratio of acceleration due to gravity at the surface of $B$ to A is :
questions.solution:
Acceleration due to gravity,
$\mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{G} \rho \mathrm{R}$
$\therefore \frac{g_2}{g_1}=\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 1.5=\frac{3}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the law of gravitational attraction suddenly changes and becomes an inverse cube law i.e. F $\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}$, but still remaining a central force. Then
options: Kepler's law of area still holds
Kepler's law of period still holds
Kepler's law of area and period still holds
neither the law of area, nor the law of period still holds
questions.solution:
According to kepler's law of area $\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$

For central forces, torque $=0$

$\therefore \mathrm{L}=$ constant

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{dt}}=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point particle is held on the axis of a ring of mass $m$ and radius $r$ at a distance $r$ from its centre C. When released, it reaches $\mathrm{C}$ under the gravitational attraction of the ring. Its speed at $C$ will be
questions.solution:
Let ' $\mathrm{M}$ ' be the mass of the particle

Now, $E_{\text {initial }}=E_{\text {final }}$

i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\sqrt{2} r}+0=\frac{\mathrm{GM} m}{r}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MV}^{2}$

or, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MV}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{GM} m}{r}\left[1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~V}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{G} m}{r}\left[1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ or,

$\mathrm{V}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{Gm}}{r}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The escape velocity from a planet is $v_{e}$. A tunnel is dug along a diameter of the planet and a small body is dropped into it at the surface. When the body reaches the centre of the planet, its speed will be
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{e}}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}} ; \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gR}}$

In tunnel body will perform SHM at centre $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{max}}=\mathrm{A\omega}$ (see chapter on SHM)

$=\frac{\mathrm{R} 2 \pi}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{R} / \mathrm{g}}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{gR}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the the earth is at one-fourth of its present distance from the sun, the duration of year will be
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a satellite of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and revolving at a height $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}$ above the surface of earth where $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}=$ radius of earth, is
questions.solution:
At a height h above the surface of earth the gravitational potential energy of the particle of mass m is $\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{h}}=-\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}+\mathrm{h}}$

Where $M_{e} \& R_{e}$ are the mass $\&$ radius of earth respectively. In this question, since $\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}$ 

So $U_{h-R_{e}}=-\frac{G M_{e} m}{2 R_{e}}=\frac{-m g R_{e}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The escape velocity from the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> A body is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> With what constant velocity will it move in the inter planetary space?
questions.solution:
If v be the velocity in the inter planetary space and Ui, Uf initial and final PE

Then, by the law of conservation of energy,

Ui+Ki=Uf+Kf

-GMemRe+12m3ve2=0+12mv2

-GMeRe+92ve2=12v2

⇒  -2GMeRe+9Ve2=v2

⇒  -2gRe2Re+9ve2=v2  ∵GMe=gRe2

-2gRe+9×2gRe=v2     ∵ve2=2gRe

16 gRe=v2
⇒  v=8·2gRe=22ve

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An asteroid of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is approaching earth, initially at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> It hite oarth with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>f</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mo>,</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close=""><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are radius and mass of earth<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>, then
questions.solution:
Initial energy of the asteroid is
Ei=Ki+Ui=12mvi2-GMθm10Re

Final energy of the asteroid,
Ef=12mvf2-GMθmRθ

According to law of conservation of energy, Ei=Ef

12mvi2-GMθm10Rθ=12mvf2-GMθmRθ

vf2-2GMθRθ=vi2-2GMθ10Rθ

⇒ vf2=vi2+2GMθRθ1-110

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherically symmetric gravitational systom of particles has mass density ρ=ρ0 for r≤R0 for r>R where, ρ0 is a constant. A test mass can undergo circular motion under the influence of the gravitational field of particles. Its speed v as a function of distance r(0<r<∞) from the centre of the system is represented by
questions.solution:
When r≤R, then force on the test mass n at the surface of the sphere =mg

Force on the test mass at distance r from the centre of sphere is

If r<R, then F=GMmR2·rR=GMmR3·r

∴mv2r=GMmR3·r

∴v α r

If r>R then F=GMmr2

∴Mv2r=GMmr2

∴ v α 1r

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A geostationary satellite orbits around the earth in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. Then, the time period of a sky satellite orbiting a few <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above the earth's surface <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>64000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> will approximately be
questions.solution:
By Kepler's law of planetary motion,

T2∝r3, hence T12∝r13

Similarly T22∝r23

∴ T12∝360003

T22∝6400+h3

Therefore, 

T22=T126400+h360003

>T12640036003

>2428453

∴ T2>24×8×845×45

>1.8 h

So, T2≅2 h

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What should be the velocity of rotation of earth due to rotation about its own axis so that the weight of a person becomes $\frac{3}{5}$ of the present weight at the equator. Equatorial radius of the earth is $6400 \mathrm{~km}$.
questions.solution:
True weight at equator, $W=m g$ Observed weight at equator,

$$

\mathrm{W}^{\prime}=\mathrm{mg}^{\prime}=\frac{3}{5} \mathrm{mg}

$$

At equator, latitude $\lambda=0$ Using the formula,

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{mg}^{\prime}=\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{mR} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \lambda \\

\frac{3}{5} \mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{mR} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} 0=\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{mR} \omega^{2} \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{mR} \omega^{2}=\mathrm{mg}-\frac{3}{5} \mathrm{mg}=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{mg} \\

\therefore \quad \omega=\left(\frac{2}{5} \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^{1 / 2} \\

=\left(\frac{2 \times 9.8}{5 \times 6.4 \times 10^{6}}\right)^{1 / 2}=7.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Kepler's third law states that square of period of revolution (T) of a planet around the sun, is proportional to third power of average distance $\mathrm{r}$

between sun and planet i.e. $\mathrm{T}^{2}=\mathrm{Kr}^{3}$ here $\mathrm{K}$ is constant. If the masses of sun and planet are $\mathrm{M}$ and $\mathrm{m}$ respectively then as per Newton's law of gravitation force of attraction between them is

$\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$, here $\mathrm{G}$ is gravitational constant. The relation between $\mathrm{G}$ and $\mathrm{K}$ is described as
questions.solution:
As we know, orbital speed, $V_{\text {orb }}=\sqrt{\frac{\text { GM }}{\text { r }}}$ 

Time period $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{v}_{\text {orb }}}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{GM}}} \sqrt{\mathrm{r}}$

Squarring both sides,

$\mathrm{T}^{2}=\left(\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r} \sqrt{\mathrm{r}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{GM}}}\right)^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{\mathrm{GM}} \cdot \mathrm{r}^{3}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{T}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}=\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{\mathrm{GM}}=\mathrm{K} \Rightarrow \mathrm{GMK}=4 \pi^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An artificial satellite is moving in a circular orbit around the earth with a speed equal to half the magnitude of the escape velocity from the earth. The height $(h)$ of the satellite above the earth's surface is (Take radius of earth as $R_{e}$ )
questions.solution:
The escape velocity from earth is given by $v_{e}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~g} R_{e}} \ldots$ (i)

The orbital velocity of a satellite revolving around earth is given by

$$

v_{0}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{G} M_{e}}}{\left(R_{e}+h\right)}

$$

where, $M_{e}=$ mass of earth, $R_{e}=$ radius of earth, $h=$ height of satellite from surface of earth.

By the relation $\mathrm{G} M_{e}=\mathrm{g} R_{e}^{2}$

$$

\text { So, } \quad v_{0}=\frac{\sqrt{g R_{e}^{2}}}{\left(R_{e}+h\right)} \ldots

$$

Dividing equation (i) by (ii), we get

$$

\frac{v_{e}}{v_{0}}=\frac{\sqrt{2\left(R_{e}+h\right)}}{\left(R_{e}\right)}

$$

Given, $v_{0}=\frac{v_{e}}{2}$

$$

\frac{2 v_{e}}{v_{e}}=\frac{\sqrt{2\left(R_{e}+h\right)}}{R_{e}}

$$

Squaring on both side, we get

$$

4=\frac{2\left(R_{e}+h\right)}{R_{e}}

$$

or $R_{e}+h=2 R_{e} \quad$ i.e., $h=R_{e}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There is a shell of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and density of the shell is uniform. The work done to take a point mass from point $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{B}$ is $[\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{r}]$
questions.solution: Gravitaional field inside the shell is zero, so no work required.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the radius of the earth were to shrink by one per cent, its mass remaining the same, the value of g on the earth's surface would
questions.solution:
$\quad \mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dg}}{\mathrm{g}}=-2 \frac{\mathrm{dR}}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{dR}}{\mathrm{R}}=-1 \% \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dg}}{\mathrm{g}}=2 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man of mass m starts falling towards a planet of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$. As he reaches near to the surface, he realizes that he will pass through a small hole in the planet. As he enters the hole, he sees that the planet is really made of two pieces a spherical shell of negligible thickness of mass $2 \mathrm{M} / 3$ and a point mass $\mathrm{M} / 3$ at the centre. Change in the force of gravity experienced by the man is
questions.solution:
$\quad$ Change in force of gravity
$$
=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{G} \frac{\mathrm{M}}{3} \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}
$$
(only due to mass $\mathrm{M} / 3$ due to shell gravitational field is zero (inside the shell))
$$
=\frac{2 \mathrm{GMm}}{3 \mathrm{R}^{2}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Geo-stationary satellite is one which
options: remains stationary at a fixed height from the earth's surface
revolves like other satellites but in the opposite direction of earth's rotation
revolves round the earth at a suitable height with same angular velocity and in the same direction as earth does about its own axis
questions.solution:
Geo-stationary satellites are also called synchronous satellite. They always remain about the same path on equater, i.e., it has a period of exactly one day $(86400 \mathrm{sec})$ So orbit radius $\left(\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{r}^{3}}{\mathrm{GM}}}\right)$ comes out
to be $42400 \mathrm{~km}$, which is nearly equal to the circumference of earth. So height of Geostationary satellite from the earth
$$
\text { surface is } 42,400-6400=36,000 \mathrm{~km} \text { . }
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the gravitational force varies inversely as the nth power of distance. Then the time period of a planet in circular orbit of radius ' $\mathrm{R}$ ' around the sun will be proportional to
questions.solution:
$$
\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{KR}^{-\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{MR} \omega^{2} \Rightarrow \omega^{2}=\mathrm{KR}^{-(\mathrm{n}+1)}
$$
or $\omega=\mathrm{K}^{\prime} \mathrm{R}^{\frac{-(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}}$
[where $\mathrm{K}^{\prime}=\mathrm{K}^{1 / 2}$, a constant $]$
$\frac{2 \pi}{T} \alpha R^{\frac{-(n+1)}{2}} \quad \therefore T \alpha R^{\frac{(n+1)}{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two planets $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ have the same material density. If the radius of $\mathrm{A}$ is twice that of $\mathrm{B}$, then the ratio of the escape velocity $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}} / \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}$ is
questions.solution:
$v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g R}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R^{2}}} R=\sqrt{\frac{2 G d \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}{R^{2}}} R$
$=\mathrm{R} \sqrt{2 \mathrm{Gd} \frac{4}{3} \pi}$
as $v_{e} \propto R$ for same density, $\frac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}=2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rotation of the earth having radius $\mathrm{R}$ about its axis speeds upto a value such that a man at latitude angle $60^{\circ}$ feels weightless. The duration of the day in such case will be:
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a mixture of gases, the average number of degree of freedoms per molecule is 6 . The rms speed of the molecule of the gas is $c$, then the velocity of sound in the gas is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of an ideal gas is increased from 200 K to 800 K . If r.m.s. speed of gas at 200 K is $v_0$. Then, r.m.s. speed of the gas at 800 K will be:
questions.solution:
RMS speed,
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{mm}} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{T}}$
Here, $\mathrm{T}_{\text {initiol }}=200 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{fing1}}=800 \mathrm{~K}$
Initial RMS speed $=\mathrm{v}_0$
$\therefore \frac{v_0}{v_{\mathrm{rms}}}=\sqrt{\frac{200}{800}} \Rightarrow v_{\mathrm{ms}}=2 v_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two vessels $A$ and $B$ are of the same size and are at same temperature. A contains 1 g of hydrogen and $B$ contains 1 g of oxygen. $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $P_B$ are the pressures of the gases in $A$ and $B$ respectively, then $\frac{P_A}{P_B}$ is:
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mol}, \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{32} \mathrm{~mol}$
By ideal gas equation,
$P V=n R T \Rightarrow P \propto n$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{32}{2}=16$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A flask contains hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio of $2: 1$ by mass at temperature $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of hydrogen and oxygen respectively is :
questions.solution:
Average kinetic energy for diatomic gases
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{kT} \\
& \therefore \frac{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{av}}\right)_{\mathrm{H}}}{\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{av}}\right)_{\mathrm{O}}}=\frac{(27+273)}{(27+273)}=1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bicycle tyre is filled with air having pressure of $270 \mathrm{kPa}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The approximate pressure of the air in the tyre when the temperature increases to $36^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
From the ideal gas equation $\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}$ Here, volume is constant $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_2}$
Here, $T_1=27+273=300 \mathrm{~K}$
$P_1=270 \mathrm{kPa}$
$\mathrm{T}_2=36+273=309 \mathrm{~K}$
$\Rightarrow P_2=\frac{P_1}{T_1} \times T_2=\frac{270 \times(309)}{300}=278 \mathrm{kPa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monoatomic gas of molar mass $m$ is kept in a insulated container. Container is moving with velocity $v$. If the container is suddenly stopped, then the change in the temperature of the gas is
questions.solution:
Let the insulated container has $n$ moles of the monoatomic gas. The loss of kinetic energy of the gas,
$$
\Delta E_K=\frac{1}{2}(m n) v^2
$$
If the change in temperature of the gas is $\Delta T$, then heat gained by the gas is,
$$
\Delta Q=\frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T
$$
Now, according to question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q & =\Delta E_K \\
\frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T & =\frac{1}{2} m n v^2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \Delta T & =\frac{m v^2}{3 R}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of an ideal diatomic gas undergoes a process as shown in the figure. The molar specific heat of the gas in the process is
questions.solution:
In the curve, $V \propto \frac{1}{T}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow V=m \frac{1}{T} \text { where, } m=\text { slope of curve. } \\
& \Rightarrow V T=\text { constant } \\
& \therefore \quad T d V+V d T=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad d V=-\frac{V}{T} d T \\
& \text { As, } Q=\Delta U+W \\
& C d T=C_V d T+p d V \\
& C d T=C_V d T-\frac{p}{T} V d T \\
& \Rightarrow \quad C=C_V-\frac{p V}{T} \\
& =C_V-R \quad\left(\because \frac{p V}{T}=R\right) \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
For diatomic gas, $C_V=\frac{5}{2} R$
$$
\therefore \quad C=C_V-R=\frac{5}{2} R-R=\frac{3 R}{2}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of an ideal gas is increased from $120 \mathrm{~K}$ to $480 \mathrm{~K}$. If at $120 \mathrm{~K}$, the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules is $v$, at $480 \mathrm{~K}$ it becomes
questions.solution: \(\frac{\mathrm{v}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}} \therefore \mathrm{v}_{2}=2 \mathrm{v}_{1}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ther.m.s. velocity of oxygen molecule at $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $474 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. The r.m.s. velocity in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ of hydrogen molecule at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
$v_{\operatorname{oxg} .}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 289}{32}}\left(v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{\mathrm{M}}}\right)$

$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{H}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{R} \times 400}{2}}$ so $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{H}}=2230.59 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas has molar heat capacity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>37</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, in this process <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo></math> constant. The number of degree of freedom of the molecule of gas is
questions.solution:
Given, C=37.55 J mol-1 K-1

Also, PT= constant K

According to ideal gas equation, PV=RT

⇒   p=RTV      .....i

Putting the value of p in Eq. (i), we get

RTV×T=K   ⇒   V=RT2K     .....ii

On differentiating above equation both sides, we get

dVdT=2RTK....iii
But TK=1p     fromEq.  i
Hence, Eq. iiibecomes

dVdT=2 Rp
So, C=CV+pdVdT
or C=CV+p×2 Rp=CV+2 R
or CV=C-2 R      ...iv

As CV=nR2, where n= number of degrees of freedom.
Putting the value of CV in Eq. (iv), we get

nR2=C-2 R

n=2 C-2 RR=2 37.55-2×8.38.3=5.048≈5

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A balloon is filled at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> pressure by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>He</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> pressure, the volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>He</mi></math> will be
questions.solution:
We have, P1V1T1=P2V2T2

or V2=P1V1T2P2T1

=1×500×273-30.5×273+27=1×500×2700.5×300

V2=900 m3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The molecules of a given mass of a gas have

r.m.s. velocity of $200 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{5}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ pressure. When the temperature and pressure of the gas are respectively, $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $0.05 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2},$ the r.m.s. velocity of its molecules in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ is :
questions.solution:
Here $v_{1}=200 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$; temperature $\mathrm{T}_{1}=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=27+273=300 \mathrm{k}$

temperature $\mathrm{T}_{2}=127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=127+273=400 \mathrm{k}$

$\mathrm{V}=?$

R.M.S. Velocity, $\mathrm{V} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{T}}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v}{200}=\sqrt{\frac{400}{300}} \\

\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{200 \times 2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\

\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{v}=\frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the kinetic theory of gases, which of these statements is/are true?

(i) The pressure of a gas is proportional to the mean speed of the molecules.

(ii) The root mean square speed of the molecules is proportional to the pressure.

(iii) The rate of diffusion is proportional to the mean speed of the molecules.

(iv) The mean translational kinetic energy of a gas is proportional to its kelvin temperature.
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The temperature of an ideal gas is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then percentage increase in $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}$

is
questions.solution:
We know, $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{mms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}$

$\Rightarrow \%$ increase in

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{R} \mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}}}-\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{1}}{\mathrm{M}}}} \times 100 \\

=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{2}}-\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{1}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{1}}} \times 100 \\

=\frac{\sqrt{400}-\sqrt{300}}{\sqrt{300}} \times 100 \\

=\frac{20-17.32}{17.32} \times 100=15.5 \%

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The average translational kinetic energy of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ (molar mass 32) molecules at a particular temperature is $0.048 \mathrm{eV}$. The translational kinetic energy of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ (molar mass 28 ) molecules in $\mathrm{eV}$ at the same temperature is
questions.solution:
Given,
\(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=0.048 \mathrm{eV}\)
We know that,
Translational kinetic energy,
\(\mathrm{E}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{KT}\)
where, \(\mathrm{k}=\) Boltzmann constant
\(\mathrm{T}=\) Temperature
\(\therefore \mathrm{E} \propto \mathrm{T}\)
Since, the temperature is same for both oxygen and nitrogen
therefore, \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=0.048 \mathrm{eV}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a gas if ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and volume is $\gamma$ then value of degrees of freedom is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \gamma=1+\frac{2}{f}, \Rightarrow \gamma-1=\frac{2}{f} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{f}{2}=\frac{1}{\gamma-1} \Rightarrow f=\frac{2}{\gamma-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The molecules of a given mass of gas have a root mean square velocity of $200 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ pressure. When the temperature is $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the pressure $0.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2},$ the root mean square velocity in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$, is
questions.solution:
$\frac{c_{2}}{c_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{400}{300}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$
$$
\Rightarrow c_{2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times 200=\frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 3 moles of an ideal gas at a temperature of $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are mixed with 2 moles of an ideal gas at a temperature $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, determine the equilibrium temperature of the mixture, assuming no loss of energy.
questions.solution:
Energy possessed by the ideal gas at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
$$
E_{1}=3\left(\frac{3}{2} R \times 300\right)=\frac{2700 R}{2}
$$
Energy possessed by the ideal gas at $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
$$
\mathrm{E}_{2}=2\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{2} \times 500\right)=1500 \mathrm{R}
$$
If $\mathrm{T}$ be the equilibrium temperature, of the mixture, then its energy will be
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}=5\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{R} \mathrm{T}}{2}\right)
$$
Since, energy remains conserved,
$$
\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}=\mathrm{E}_{1}+\mathrm{E}_{2}
$$
or $\quad 5\left(\frac{3 R T}{2}\right)=\frac{2700 R}{2}+1500 R$
or $\quad \mathrm{T}=380 \mathrm{~K}$ or $107^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses $\mathrm{m}_1$ and $\mathrm{m}_2$ (where $m_2 \gt m_1$). If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}}$, then the ratio of the masses $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$ is:
questions.solution:
Acceleration is given as:
$a=\left(\frac{m_2-m_1}{m_1+m_2}\right) g$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\sqrt{2}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_2-\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2}\right) \mathrm{g}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}\left(m_2-m_1\right)=m_1+m_2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}+1}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass 1 kg is pushed up a surface inclined to horizontal at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ by a force of 10 N parallel to the inclined surface as shown in figure. When the block is pushed up by 10 m along inclined surface, the work done against frictional force is : $\left[g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right]$
questions.solution:
Mass of block, $m=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
Force of parallel inclined surface, $\mathrm{F}=10 \mathrm{~N}$
Work done against frictional force
$=\mu_2 \mathrm{~N} \times 10=\mu \mathrm{Mg} \cos 60 \times 10=0.1 \times 5 \times 10=5 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person of mass 60 kg is inside a lift of mass 940 kg and presses the button on control panel. The lift starts moving upwards with an acceleration $1.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the tension in the supporting cable is
questions.solution: Total mass $=(60+940) \mathrm{kg}=1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ Let T be the tension in the supporting cable, then $T-1000 g=1000 \times 1$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=1000 \times 11=11000 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At any instant the velocity of a particle of mass $500 \mathrm{~g}$ is $\left(2 t \hat{i}+3 t^2 \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$. If the force acting on the particle at $t=1 s$ is $(\hat{i}+x \hat{j}) N$. Then the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be:
questions.solution:
Mass of particle, $\mathrm{m}=500 \mathrm{~g}=0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$
velocity of a particle, $\vec{v}=2 t \hat{i}+3 t^2 \hat{j}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \mathrm{t} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
at $\mathrm{t}=1, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
Force acting on the particle,
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{ma}=0.5(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}})=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+x \hat{\mathrm{j}} \quad$ Hence $x=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the diagram shown below, both the strings $A B$ and $C D$ are made of same material and have same cross-section. The pulleys are light and fictionless. If the speed of wave in string $A B$ is $v_1$ and in $C D$ is $v_2$, then $\frac{v_1}{v_2}$ is
questions.solution: Let the speed of wave in string be $v$ and tension in string be $T$. $$ \begin{gathered} v \propto \sqrt{T} \\ \frac{v_{A B}}{v_{C D}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{2 T}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{gathered} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What will be the acceleration due to gravity at a depth $d$, where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth?
questions.solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at the surface of earth,
$$
g=\frac{G M}{R^2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \rho G R
$$
$$
\left[\because M=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \cdot \rho\right]
$$
Where, $\rho=$ density of earth.
Acceleration due to gravity at depth $d$ from the surface of earth,
$$
g^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \rho G(R-d)
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
g^{\prime}=g\left[1-\frac{d}{R}\right]
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is projected with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. At its highest point, it explodes into two pieces of equal mass, one of the piece continue to move on the original trajectory, then the velocity of second piece is
questions.solution:
According to the law of conservation of linear momentum at the highest point,
$$
m v \cos \theta=\frac{m}{2}(v \cos \theta)+\frac{m}{2} v^{\prime}
$$
$\Rightarrow \quad m v \cos \theta-\frac{m}{2} v \cos \theta=\frac{m}{2} v^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow \quad v^{\prime}=2\left(v \cos \theta-\frac{v \cos \theta}{2}\right)=v \cos \theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of weight $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended as shown in the figure. The tension $\mathrm{T}_{1}$ in the horizontal string (in $\mathrm{kg}$ wt ) is
questions.solution:
The tension in the above string can be divided into two components where the component opposite to weight will balance the weight while the component of this tension opposite to \(\mathrm{T}_{1}\) will be equal to \(\mathrm{T}_{1}\).

\(\mathrm{T} \sin \theta=2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{wt}\)

\(\mathrm{T} \cos \theta=\mathrm{T}_{1}\)

The first equation gives

\(\mathrm{T}=4 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{wt}\)

So \(\mathrm{T}_{1}\) will be,

\(\mathrm{T}_{1}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{wt}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball of mass $0.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is thrown vertically upwards by applying a force by hand. If the hand moves $0.2 \mathrm{~m}$ while applying the force and the ball goes upto $2 \mathrm{~m}$ height further, find the magnitude of the force. (Consider $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$.
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of the ball just when it leaves the hand is $u$ then applying,

$\begin{array}{l}

v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a \text { for upward journey } \\

\Rightarrow-u^{2}=2(-10) \times 2 \\

\Rightarrow u^{2}=40

\end{array}$

Again applying $v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s$ for the upward journey of the ball, when the ball is in the hands of the thrower,

$\begin{array}{l}

v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s \\

\Rightarrow 40-0=2(a) 0.2 \\

\Rightarrow a=100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \\

\therefore \mathrm{F}=m a=0.2 \times 100=20 \mathrm{~N} \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}-m g=20 \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=20+2=22 \mathrm{~N}

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The upper half of an inclined plane with inclination $\phi$ is perfectlysmooth while the lower halfis rough. A body starting from rest at the top will again come to rest at the bottom if the coefficient of friction for the lower half is given by
questions.solution:
Acceleration of block while sliding down upper half $=\mathrm{g} \sin \phi$

retardation of block while sliding down lower half $=-(\mathrm{g} \sin \phi-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \phi)$

For the block to come to rest at the bottom, acceleration in I half = retardation in II half.

$\mathrm{g} \sin \phi=-(\mathrm{g} \sin \phi-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \phi)$

$\Rightarrow \mu=2 \tan \phi$

Alternative method: According to work-energy theorem, $\mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{K}=0$

(Since initial and final speeds are zero)

$\therefore$ Work done by friction $+$ Work done by gravity $=0$ i.e., $-(\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \phi) \frac{\ell}{2}+\mathrm{mg} \ell \sin \phi=0$

or $\frac{\mu}{2} \cos \phi=\sin \phi$ or $\mu=2 \tan \phi$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of friction between the rubber tyres and the road wayis $0.25 .$ The maximum speed with which a car can be driven round a curve of radius $20 \mathrm{~m}$ without skidding is $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\mu \mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{mv}^{2} / \mathrm{r}$ or $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{r}}$

or $v=\sqrt{(0.25 \times 9.8 \times 20)}=7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is placed on a horizontal surface having kinetic friction 0.4 and static friction $0.5 .$ If the force applied on the body is $2.5 \mathrm{~N},$ then the frictional force acting on the body will be $\left[\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right]$
questions.solution:
Limiting friction $=0.5 \times 2 \times 10=10 \mathrm{~N}$

The applied force is less than force of friction, therefore the force of friction is equal to the applied force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a straight line with momentum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>. Starting at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi></math> acts in the same direction on the moving particle during time interval of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. So that its momentum changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given, F=at

when, t=0, then linear momentum =p

when, t=T, then linear momentum =2p

According to Newton's law of motion, applied force, F=dpdt

or dp=Fdt

dp=atdt

or ∫p2pdp=a∫0Ttdt

2p-p=a·T22

⇒2pa=T2⇒T=2pa

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A maximum of 3 N force can be applied on a block, down the incline, that would still not move the block which is placed on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. The maximum external force up the inclined plane that does not move the block is 12 N. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is Take g=10 m s-2
questions.solution:
Block does not move upto a maximum applied force of 3 N down the inclined plane.

Equation of motion is 3+mg sin30°=F

3+mg sin30°=μ mg cos30°            ....(i)

Similarly, block also does not move upto a maximum applied force of 12 N up the plane.

Now, equation of motion is

mg sin30°+F=12

or, mg sin30°+μ mg cos30°=12           ...(ii)

Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

mg sin30°=92 and μ mg cos30°=152

Hence, dividing

∴ 1μtan30°=9/215/2=35

⇒μ=533

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force on a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. If it starts from rest, then the position of the body at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is
questions.solution:
F=20 i^+10 j^

∴ Fx=20 N and Fy=10 N

∴ Acceleration,

ax=Fxm=201
ax=20 m s-2

and ay=Fym=101=10 m s-2

∴ Displacement of body in x -direction in 2 s.

x=ux t+12ax t2

=0+12×20×22
=40 m

Displacement of body in y-direction in 2 s.

y=uy t+12 ay t2

=0+12×10×22

=20 m

∴ Position of the body at t=2 s

r=x i^+y j^
r=40 i^+20 j^

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An automobile moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> reaches an upward inclined road of angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo></math> its engine becomes switch off. If the coefficient of friction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then how much distance will automobile move before coming to rest?
questions.solution:
Given, initial speed, u=36 km h-1

=36×100060×60=10 m s-1

θ=30°, μ=0.1, s=?

Here, work done in moving up the inclined road= KE of the vehicle

mg sin θ+F s=12mu2

mg sin θ+μR×s=12mu2

mg sin θ+μ mg cos θ×s=12mu2

s=12mu2mg sin θ+μ cos θ=u22 g sin θ+μ cos θ

=10×102×10× sin 30°+0.1 cos 30°=8.53 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> times as much time to slide down a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> rough inclined plane as it takes to slide down a perfectly smooth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the rough plane and the object is
questions.solution:
s=ut+12at2,a=gsinθ,u=0

Then,   s=0+12at2

or  t=2sa=2sgsinθ [for smooth plane]

For the rough plane. the effective value of acceleration along the incline is
a'=gsinθ-μkgcosθ

and t'=2sa=2sgsinθ-μkgcosθ

Now,   t't=n=gsinθgsinθ-μkgcosθ

=sin45°sin45°-μkcos45°

=11-μk

⇒ 1n2=1-μk⇒μk=1-1n2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two blocks A and B are placed one over the other on a smooth horizontal surface. The maximum horizontal force that can be applied on lower block A, so that A and B move without separation is 49 N. The coefficient of friction between A and B is
questions.solution:
Equation of motion of lower block is

F=μMA+MB·g

μ=FMA+MBg

=4910×9.8=12=0.5

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block A of mass 100 kg rests on another block B of mass 200 kg and is tied to a wall as shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction between A and B is 0.2 and that between B and ground is 0.3. The minimum force required to move the block B is g=10 m s-2
questions.solution:
Friction force between A and B and between block B and surface S will oppose F

∴ F=FAB+FBS=μABMAg+μBSmA+mBg

=0.2×100×10+0.3100+200×10

=200+900=1100 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Block A of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and block $\mathrm{B}$ of mass $2 \mathrm{~m}$ are placed on a fixed triangular wedge by means of a massless, inextensible string and a frictionless pulley as shown in figure.
The wedge is inclined at $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal on both the sides. If the coefficient of friction between the block $\mathrm{A}$ and the wedge is $2 / 3$ and that between the block $\mathrm{B}$ and the wedge is $1 / 3$ and both the blocks $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are released from rest, the acceleration of A will be
questions.solution:
Total maximum friction force,\(f_{t}=f_{A}+f_{B}\)

\(\mu_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{mg} \cos 45^{\circ}+\mu_{\mathrm{B}} 2 \mathrm{mg} \cos 45^{\circ}\)

or, \(f_{t}=m g\left(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \times 2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\)

\(=\frac{4}{3 \sqrt{2}} \mathrm{mg}\)

The net pulling force, \(F=2 m g \sin 45^{\circ}-m g \sin 45^{\circ}\) \(=\mathrm{mg} \sin 45^{\circ}\)

\(=\frac{m g}{\sqrt{2}}\)

\(\because \mathrm{F}<\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{t}}\)

\(\therefore\) The blocks will not move.

So, acceleration of block A is Zero.

\(\therefore\) option (D) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A frictionless wire $\mathrm{AB}$ is fixed on a sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$. A very small spherical ball slips on this wire. The time taken by this ball to slip from A to $\mathrm{B}$ is
questions.solution:
Acceleration of body along $\mathrm{AB}$ is g $\cos \theta$ Distance travelled in time $\mathrm{t}$ sec $=$

$A B=\frac{1}{2}(g \cos \theta) t^{2}$

From $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}, \mathrm{AB}=2 \mathrm{R} \cos \theta$

Thus, $2 \mathrm{R} \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} g \cos \theta t^{2}$

$\Rightarrow t^{2}=\frac{4 R}{g} \Rightarrow t=2 \sqrt{\frac{R}{g}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In figure, two blocks are separated by a uniform strut attached to each block with frictionless pins. Block $A$ weighs $400 \mathrm{~N}$, block $B$ weighs $300 \mathrm{~N}$, and the strut $A B$ weigh $200 \mathrm{~N}$. If $\mu=0.25$ under $B,$ determine the minimum coefficient of friction under $A$ to prevent motion.
questions.solution:
Consider FBD of structure.
Applying equilibrium equations, $A v+B v=200 \mathrm{~N}$ $A_{H}=B_{H} \ldots$ (ii) From FBD of block $B$,
$B_{H}+F_{B} \cos 60^{\circ}-N_{B} \sin 60^{\circ}=0$ $N_{B} \cos 60^{\circ}-B_{V}-300+F_{B} \sin 60^{\circ}=0$ $F_{B}=0.25 N_{B}$ $B_{H}-0.74 N_{B}=0$ (iii) $-B_{V}+0.71 N_{B}=300$ FBD of block $A$
$F_{A}-A_{H}=0$ $N_{A}-A_{V}=400 \quad \ldots$ $F_{A}=\mu_{A} N_{A}$ $\therefore \mu_{A} N_{A}-A_{H}=0 \quad \ldots$ on solving the above equation $N_{A}=650 \mathrm{~N}, F_{A}=260 \mathrm{~N}, F_{A}=\mu_{A} N_{A}$ $\therefore \mu_{A}=\frac{260}{250}=0.4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The masses of blocks $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{M}$ respectively. Between $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, there is a constant frictional force $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ can slide on a smooth horizontal surface. A is set in motion with velocity while $\mathrm{B}$ is at rest. What is the distance moved by A relative to B before they move with the same velocity?
questions.solution:
For the blocks A and B FBD as shown below
Equations of motion $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{M}}(\text { in }-\mathrm{x} \text { direction }) \\ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{M}}(\text { in }+\mathrm{x} \text { direction) } \end{array} $$ Relative acceleration, of A w.r.t. B, $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}} &=\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{B}}=-\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}-\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{M}} \\ &=-\mathrm{F}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{Mm}}\right)(\text { along }-\mathrm{x} \text { direction }) \end{aligned} $$ Initial relative velocity of A w.r.t. B, $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{v}_{0}$ using equation $v^{2}=u^{2}+2$ as $$ 0=v_{0}^{2}-\frac{2 \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{Mm}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=\frac{\mathrm{Mmv}_{0}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M})} $$ i.e., Distance moved by A relative to B $$ \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{Mmv}_{0}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M})} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A load of mass m falls from a height h on to the scale pan hung from the spring as shown in the figure. If the spring constant is $\mathrm{k}$ and mass of the scale pan is zero and the mass $\mathrm{m}$ does not bounce relative to the pan, then the amplitude of vibration is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A person with his hand in his pocket is skating on ice at the rate of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and describes a circle of radius $50 \mathrm{~m}$. What is his inclination to vertical:

$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
Since surface (ice) is frictionless, so the centripetal force required for skating will be provided by inclination of boy with the vertical and that angle is given as $\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{rg}}$ where $\mathrm{v}$ is speed of skating $\&$

i is radius of circle in which he moves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) with which a car driver must traverse a flat curve of radius $150 \mathrm{~m}$ and coefficient of friction 0.6 to avoid skidding is
questions.solution:
The condition to avoid skidding,

$$

\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mu \mathrm{rg}}=\sqrt{0.6 \times 150 \times 10}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bob is hanging over a pulley inside a car through, a string. The second end of the string is in the hand of a person standing in the car. The car is moving with constant acceleration 'a' directed horizontallyas shown in figure. Other end of the string is pulled with constant acceleration 'a' vertically. The tension in the string is equal to -
questions.solution: Applying Newton's law along string $$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}-\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\mathrm{g}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2}}=\mathrm{ma} $$ or $\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{\mathrm{g}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2}}+\mathrm{ma}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass m is placed on a smooth inclined wedge $A B C$ of inclination $\theta$ as shown in the figure. The wedge is given an acceleration 'a' towards the right. The relation between a and $\theta$ for the block to remain stationary on the wedge is
questions.solution:
Let the mass of block is m. It will remains stationary if forces acting on it are in equilibrium. i.e., $m a \cos \theta=m g \sin \theta$ $\Rightarrow a=g \tan \theta$
Here $\mathrm{ma}=$ Pseudo force on block, $\mathrm{mg}=$ weight

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object experiences a net force and accelerates from rest to its final position in $16 \mathrm{~s}$. How long would the object take to reach the same final position from rest if the object's mass was four times larger ?
questions.solution: When the mass increases by a factor of 4 the acceleration must decrease by a factor of four if the same force is applied. The question asks about position so we need to relate acceleration and time to position. We can do this by the equation $: x_{f}-x_{i}=v_{x i} t+1 / 2 a_{x} t^{2}$ We want the change in position to stay the same. The initial velocity is zero so in order for the change in position to remain constant the term $(1 / 2)$ at $^{2}$ must remain the same. If the acceleration is reduced by a factor of 4 you can see that the time must be increased by a factor of 2 in order for the term to remain the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $\mathrm{m}_{1}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{3}$ are connected by massless strings, as shown, on a frictionless table. They are pulled with a force $\mathrm{T}_{3}$ $=40 \mathrm{~N}$. If $m_{1}=10 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}=6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{3}=4 \mathrm{~kg},$ the tension $\mathrm{T}_{2}$ will be
questions.solution:
For equilibrium of all 3 masses,
$$
a=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{3}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3}}
$$
For equilibrium of $\mathrm{m}_{1} \& \mathrm{~m}_{2}$ $\mathrm{T}_{2}=\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{a}$ or, $\mathrm{T}_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{T}_{3}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\mathrm{m}_{3}}$
Given $m_{1}=10 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}=6 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~m}_{3}=4 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\mathrm{T}_{3}=40 \mathrm{~N}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{2}=\frac{(10+6) \cdot 40}{10+6+4}=32 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two blocks are connected over a massless pulley as shown in fig. The mass of block $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and the coefficient of kinetic
friction is 0.2 . Block A slides down the incline at constant speed. The mass of block $\mathrm{B}$ in $\mathrm{kg}$ is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $\mathrm{M}$ is pulled along a horizontal frictionless surface by a rope of mass $\mathrm{M} / 2$. If a force $2 \mathrm{Mg}$ is applied at one end of the rope, the force which the rope exerts on the block is -
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The straight wire $A B$ carries a current $I$. The ends of the wire subtend angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ at the point $P$ as shown in figure. The magnetic field at the point $P$ is
questions.solution: The magnetic field at a point $P$ due to a current carrying straight wire, such that the ends of the wires, makes angle $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (see figure) is given by $B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi d}(\sin \alpha-\sin \beta)$ where, $d$ is the distance between the point $P$ and wire. In the given problem, $\alpha=\theta_1$ and $\beta=\theta_2$ $\therefore \quad B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \pi d}\left(\sin \theta_1-\sin \theta_2\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current 1 . The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}$ and 2a from axis of the wire is:
questions.solution:
Magnetic field due to straight wire,
$B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$
Magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ is,
$\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{a} / 2}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{a} / 2}}{\mathrm{~B}_{2 \mathrm{a}}}=\frac{1}{1}=1: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electrostatic force $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_1\right)$ and magnetic force $\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_2\right)$ acting on a charge q moving with velocity v can be written:
questions.solution:
Electrostatic force, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_1=\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$
Magnetic force, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_2=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils A and B of radius $r_A=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $r_B=20 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively are equal if: (Where $\mathrm{N}_A, \mathrm{I}_A$ and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}}, \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}$ are number of turn and current of $A$ and $B$ respectively)
questions.solution:
The magnetic moment associated with circular coil,
$\begin{aligned}
& M=N I A \\
& M_A=M_B \\
& \therefore N_A I_A A_A=N_B I_B A_B \\
& \therefore N_A I_A \pi(0.1)^2=N_B I_B \pi(0.2)^2\left(\because A=\pi r^2\right) \\
& \therefore N_A I_A=4 N_B I_B
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid of 1200 turns is wound uniformly in a single layer on a glass tube $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long and $0.2 \mathrm{~m}$ in diameter. The magnetic intensity at the center of the solenoid when a current of 2 A flows through it is:
questions.solution:
Given that number of turns, $n=1200$
Current, $\mathrm{I}=2 \mathrm{~A}$
Magnetic field at centre inside the solenoid is given by,
$\mathrm{B}=\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}$
So magnetic intensity at centre of the solenoid,
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\mu_0}=\mathrm{nI}=\left(\frac{1200}{2}\right)(2) \quad\left(\because \mathrm{B}=\mu_0 \mathrm{H}\right) \\
& \mathrm{H}=1.2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{Am}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge particle is moving in a uniform magnetic field $(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \mathrm{T}$. If it has an acceleration of $(\alpha \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the value of $\alpha$ will be :
questions.solution:
Given that uniform magnetic field, $\vec{B}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) T$
Acceleration $\vec{a}=(\alpha \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ We know that
$F=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \Rightarrow m \vec{a}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$
Here, $\vec{a} \perp \vec{B}$, so, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{B}=0$
$(\alpha \hat{i}-4 \hat{j})(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})=0 \Rightarrow 2 \alpha-12=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic moment of an electron (e) revolving in an orbit around nucleus with an orbital angular momentum is given by:
questions.solution:
As $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{IA}$
$\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}|=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{v}}} \pi \mathrm{R}^2$ $\left[\because \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{V}}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow|\vec{M}|=\frac{1}{2} e v R \Rightarrow|\vec{M}|=\frac{\mathrm{mvR}}{1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{e}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
$\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}|=\frac{\mathrm{eL}}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}|=-\frac{\mathrm{e} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
[ $\because$ Here $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}$ will always be opposite]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton moving with a velocity $3 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ enters a magnetic field of $0.3$ tesla at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the field. The radius of curvature of its path will be $(\mathrm{e} / \mathrm{m}$ for proton $=10^{8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$ )
questions.solution:
$r=\frac{m v \sin \theta}{\mathrm{B} e}=\frac{3 \times 10^{5} \sin 30^{\circ}}{0.3 \times 10^{8}}$

$\frac{3 \times 10^{5} \times \frac{1}{2}}{3 \times 10^{7}}=0.5 \times 10^{-2}$

$m=0.5 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid is $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long and $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ in diameter. It has 5 layers of winding of 1000 turns each and carries a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. What is the magnetic field at its centre ? Use the standard value of $\mu_{0}$.
questions.solution:
Length of solenoid, $l=2 \mathrm{~m}$

Total number of turns, $\mathrm{N}=5 \times 1000=5000$

Number of turns per unit length,

$\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{l}=\frac{5000}{2}=2500 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$

Current, I = 5A

Now, B $=\mu_{0} \mathrm{nI}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2500 \times 5 \mathrm{~T}$

$=1.57 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron moves in a circular arc of radius 10 $\mathrm{m}$ at a contant speed of $2 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ with its plane of motion normal to magnetic flux density of $10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. What will be the value of specific charge of the electron?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Bqv}=\mathrm{mv}^{2} / \mathrm{r}$ or $\mathrm{q} / \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{rB}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron revolves in a circular orbit of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> with angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. The magnetic field at the centre of electron orbit is
questions.solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of circular loop.

B=μ0l2r    ...i

Period of revolution of electron, where ω= angular velocity of electron

T=2πω

where, ω= angular velocity of electron.

Current produced due to movement of electron,

I=eT=e2πω=eω2π

On substituting the value of I in Eq. (i), we get

B=μ02r·eω2π

⇒B=μ0eω4πr

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two electrons are moving with equal speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> parallel to each other. The ratio of the electrostatic to the magnetic force between them is
questions.solution:
Given, speed of electrons, v=5×105 m s-1

Electrostatic force between the electrons,

Fe=k·e2r2   ...i

Magnetic field produced due to moving electron with velocity v,

B=μ04π·evr2   ...ii

Magnetic force between the electrons,

Fm=Bev=μ04π·evr2·ev   (from Eq. (ii))

Fm=μ04π·e2v2r2

∴ From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have

FeFm=ke2/r2μ0e2v24π·r2=k·4πμ0v2

=9×109×4×3.144π×10-75×1052=3.6×105

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at the centre O of the current-carrying square loop shown in the figure is
questions.solution:
Given, current through the square loop is 2 A.

I=2 A

The given situation is shown in the figure. Magnetic field due to wire AB at the centre O is

BAB=μ04π·Irsin45°+sin45°

=10-7×22×10-212+12

=2×10-5 T [Downward to the plane of square loop]

Since, magnetic field due to side AB, BC, CD and DA is same in magnitude and direction.

Hence, net magnetic field at O

B=4×BAB=42×10-5 T

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton is revolving on a circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> with frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Magnetic dipole moment associated with proton is
questions.solution:
Given, charge on proton, q=e
=1.6×10-19 C

Radius,   r=2 mm=2×10-3 cm

Frequency, f=10 Hz

Current associated by proton is given by

I=qt                       [T→ time period ]

=qf                            ∵T=1f

=1.6×10-19×10=1.6×10-18 A

∴ Magnetic dipole moment, M=IA

=I·πr2=1.6×10-18×3.14×2×10-32
=2×10-24 A-m2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current i flows through a loop as shown in figure. The magnetic field at the Centre O is
questions.solution:
The angle, subtended by arc DE at O is π2 and FC at O=3π2 the effective magnetic field at O is

B=BDE+BFC

=μ0i4π3r×π2+μ0i4πr×3π2

=μoi8r13+3

=5μoi12r acting downwards

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of angular momentum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> to the atomic dipole moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> for hydrogen like atoms and ions is
options: always constant and is equal to the ratio of mass to the charge of electron
always constant and is equal to twice the ratio of mass to the charge of electron to the principal quantum number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>
proportional to the principal quantum number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>
questions.solution:
The electron moving with a speed v in the circular Bohr orbit of radius r constitutes a current of magnitude.

i=eT. where T is the orbital period of the electron.

T=2πr/v

i=ev2πr

By definition of atomic dipole moment μi, its magnitude is given by

μi=iA=eV2πr·πr2=evr2

∴ Angular momentum, L=mvr
∴ Lμi=2mvrevr=2me= constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper rod of mass m slides under gravity on two smooth parallel rails l distance apart and set an angle θ to the horizontal. At the bottom, the rails are joined by a resistance R in figure. There is a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to the plane of the rails. The terminal velocity of rod is
questions.solution:
Terminal velocity of the rod is attained when magnetic force on the rod (Bil) balances the component of weight of the rod(mgsinθ), as in figure.

So, Bil=mgsinθ

BeRl=mgsinθ

BleR=mgsinθ

BlB/VTR=mgsinθ

vT=mgsinθRB2l2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To increase the current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>%</mo><mo>,</mo></math> its resistance is increased, so that new resistance becomes twice its initial resistance, by what factor does its voltage sensitivity change?
questions.solution:
New, current sensitivity, Is'=Is+50100Is

=150100Is=32Is

New resistance, R'=2 R

Initial voltage sensitivity, Vs=IsR

Now, new voltage sensitivity.

Vs=is'R'=32 Ic2 R=34Vs

% decrease in voltage sensitivity

=Vs-Vs'Vs×100

=1-VsVs×100

=1-34×100=25 %

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positive charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is projected in magnetic field of width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>q</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Then, the time taken by charged particle to emerge from the magnetic field is
questions.solution:
Referring the diagram, 

width of magnetic field w=mv2qB

Also we know that when a charge is projected in perpendicular magnetic field then radius of path is 

R=mvqB

Now in triangle OAB,

sinθ=wR=mv2qBmvqB

=12

⇒ sinθ=sin45°

=sinπ4

Time period for angle 2π is T=2πmqB

∴ Time by charged particle for θ=π4in order to emerge from magnetic field is 

T=πm4qB.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is in plane of the coil. If due to a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi></math> in the triangle, a torque <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>τ</mi></math> rests on it, the side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> of the triangle is
questions.solution:
Torque acting on equilateral triangle in a magnetic field B is

τ=i AB sin θ

Area of triangle LMN,

A=32l2 and θ=90°

Substituting the given values in expression for torque, we get

τ=i×34 l2 B sin 90°

τ=34il2B    ∵  sin 90°=1

l2=4τ3 Bi

Here, l=2 τ3 Bi12
