The kinetic energy of the proton that describes a circular orbit of radius 0.5 metre in the same plane with the same $\mathrm{B}$ is
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire carrying current I has the shape as shown in adjoining figure. Linear parts of the wire are very long and parallel to $\mathrm{X}$ -axis while semicircular portion of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is lying in Y-Z plane. Magnetic field at point $\mathrm{O}$ is:
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field and losses $4 \%$ of its kinetic energy. The radius of curvature of its path changes by
questions.solution:
As we know $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qvB}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{Bq}}$

And $\mathrm{KE}=\mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}$

$\therefore \mathrm{mv}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~km}}$

$\therefore \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~km}}}{\mathrm{qB}}$

$\Rightarrow r \propto \sqrt{k}$ or $r=c^{1 / 2}(c$ is a constant $)$ $\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\mathrm{c} \frac{\mathrm{dk}^{1 / 2}}{\mathrm{dr}}$ or $\frac{\mathrm{c} \Delta \mathrm{k}}{\Delta \mathrm{r}}=2 \sqrt{\mathrm{k}}$

$\frac{\Delta r}{r}=\frac{\mathrm{c} \Delta \mathrm{k}}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{c} \sqrt{\mathrm{k}}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{k}}{2 \mathrm{k}}$

Therefore percentage changes in radius of path,

$\frac{\Delta r}{r} \times 100=\frac{\Delta k}{2 k} \times 100=2 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ having equal charges, after being accelerated through the same potential difference, enter a region of uniform magnetic field and describe circular paths of radii $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$, respectively. The ratio of masses of $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ is
questions.solution:
When a charge particle is allowed to move in a uniform magnetic field, then it describes spiral or circular path

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\text { Centripetal force, } \frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}=\mathrm{qvB} \quad \therefore \\

\mathrm{v}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{R}\right) \\

\text { Hence, } \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{m}}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{qB}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \mathrm{R}\left[\because \mathrm{V}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}}\right] \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{q}}\right)^{1 / 2} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}} \\

\text { or, } \mathrm{m} \propto \mathrm{R}^{2}

\end{array}

$$

$[\because \mathrm{V}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are constant $]$

$$

\text { or, } \frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)^{2}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A long straight wire along the $Z$ -axis carries a current I in the negative $Z$ -direction. The

magnetic vector field $\vec{B}$ at a point having coordinates $(x, y)$ in the $Z=0$ plane is
questions.solution:
The wire carries a current $I$ in the negative $z$ -direction. We have to consider the magnetic vector field $\vec{B}$ at $(x, y)$ in the $z=0$ plane.
Magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $O P$ $\therefore \vec{B}=B \sin \theta \hat{i}-B \cos \theta \hat{j}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \sin \theta=\frac{y}{r}, \cos \theta=\frac{x}{r} B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} \\ \therefore \quad \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r^{2}}(y \hat{i}-x \hat{j}) \end{array} $$ or $\quad \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} I(y \hat{i}-x \hat{j})}{2 \pi\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A narrow beam of protons and deuterons, each having the same momentum, enters a region of uniform magnetic field directed perpendicular to their direction of momentum. The ratio of the radii of the circular paths described by them is
questions.solution:
Since, the radius of circular path of a charged

particle in magnetic field is $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\rho}{\mathrm{qB}}$

Now, the radius of circular path of charged particle of given momentum $\rho$ and magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ is given by $\mathrm{r} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{q}}$

But charge on both charged particles, protons and deuterons, is same. Therefore,

$$

\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{D}}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{D}}}{\mathrm{q}_{\rho}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{l}}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two parallel conductors carry current in opposite directions as shown in figure. One conductor carries a current of $10.0 \mathrm{~A}$. Point $\mathrm{C}$ is a distance $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}$ to the right of the $10.0 \mathrm{~A}$ current. If $\mathrm{d}=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ and I is adjusted so that the magnetic field at $\mathrm{Cis}$ zero, the value of the current $I$ is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at $\mathrm{C}$ due to first conductor

is $\mathrm{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{3 \mathrm{~d} / 2}$ (since, point $\mathrm{C}$ is separated

by $\mathrm{d}+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~d}}{2}$ from 1 st conductor). The direction of field is perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed outwards. The magnetic field at $\mathrm{C}$ due to second conductor is $\mathrm{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{10}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}$ (since, point $\mathrm{C}$

is separated by $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}$ from 2 nd conductor) The direction of field is perpendicular to the plane of paper and directed inwards. Since, direction of $\mathrm{B}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{2}$ at point $\mathrm{C}$ is in opposite direction and the magnetic field at C is zero, therefore,

$$

\begin{array}{c}

\mathrm{B}_{1}=\mathrm{B}_{2} \\

\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{3 \mathrm{~d} / 2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{10}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}

\end{array}

$$

On solving $\mathrm{I}=30.0 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform electric field and uniform magnetic field are acting along the same direction in a certain region. If an electron is projected in the region such that its velocity is pointed along the direction of fields, then the electron
options: will turn towards right of direction of motion
will turn towards left direction of motion
questions.solution: $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ are in same direction so that magnetic force on electron becomes zero, only electric force acts. But force on electron due to electric field is opposite to the direction of velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal overhead powerline is at height of $4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the ground and carries a current of $100 \mathrm{~A}$ from east to west. The magnetic field directly below it on the ground is $\left(\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} \mathrm{A}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field is $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{r}}=10^{-7} \times \frac{2 \times 100}{4}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$
According to right hand palm rule, the magnetic field is directed towards south.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $m$ is magnetic moment and $B$ is the magnetic field, then the torque is given by
questions.solution: $\vec{\tau}=\vec{m} \times \vec{B}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is $\mathrm{M}$. The work done to turn the magnet by $90^{\circ}$ of magnet in direction of magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ will be
questions.solution:
Work done, $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{MB}(1-\cos \theta)$
$\theta=90^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{MB}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steady current is set up in a cubic network composed of wires of equal resistance and length d as shown in figure. What is the magnetic field at the centre $\mathrm{P}$ due to the cubic network
questions.solution: Bysymmetry, the magnetic field at the centre Pis zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $M$ is magnetic moment and $B$ is the magnetic field, then the torque is given by
questions.solution: Torque, $\tau=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at the origin due to the current flowing in the wire is $-$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{OD}}=0, \mathrm{~B}_{\mathrm{OB}}=0$
$\mathrm{~B}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}}\left[\cos 45^{\circ}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\cos 45^{\circ} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right]$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{8 \pi \mathrm{a}}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop of radius $r$ carries a constant current $i .$ It is placed in a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}$ such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}$ is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The magnetic force acting on the loop is
questions.solution: The magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Let us consider two diametrically opposite elements. By Fleming's Left hand rule on element $\mathrm{AB}$ the direction of force will be Leftwards and the magnitude will be $$ \mathrm{dF}=\operatorname{Idl} \mathrm{B} \sin 90^{\circ}=\mathrm{IdIB} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The free space inside a current carrying toroid is filled with a material of susceptibility $2 \times 10^{-2}$. The percentage increase in the value of magnetic field inside the toroid will be
questions.solution:
Given,
Susceptibility of material, $\chi_m=2 \times 10^{-2}$
Using $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}=1+\chi_{\mathrm{m}}=1+0.02=1.02$
$\mathrm{B}_{\text {final }}=\mu_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{B}_0$ (here, $\mathrm{B}_0=$ initial magnetic field)
$\%$ increase in magnetic field
$=\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\text {final }}-\mathrm{B}_0}{\mathrm{~B}_0} \times 100=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{B}_0-\mathrm{B}_0 \times 100}{\mathrm{~B}_0}$
$=\frac{(\chi+1)-1 \times 100}{1}=0.02 \times 100=2 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the $B-H$ curves of two samples of $X$ and $Y$ of iron are as shown below, then which one of the following statement is correct?
options: Both $X$ and $Y$ are suitable for making electromagnets.
Both $X$ and $Y$ are suitable for making permanent magnet.
$X$ is suitable for making permanent magnet and $Y$ for making electromagnet.
$X$ is suitable for making electromagnet and $Y$ is suitable for permanent magnet.
questions.solution: For permanent magnet we prefer a material with high retentivity and high coercivity. For electromagnet, we prefer high saturated magnetism, low coercivity and least possible area of hystersis loop. Therefore, $X$ is suitable for making permanent magnet and $Y$ for making electromagnet.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angle of dip at a place is $37^{\circ}$ and the vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is $6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The earth's magnetic field at this place is $\left(\tan 37^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{3}{4}\left[\because \tan 37^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}\right]$

$\therefore \mathrm{V}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{H}$

$\mathrm{V}=6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$

$\mathrm{H}=\frac{4}{3} \times 6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}=8 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$

$\therefore \mathrm{B}_{\text {total }}=\sqrt{\mathrm{V}^{2}+\mathrm{H}^{2}}=\sqrt{(36+64)} \times 10^{-5}$

$=10 \times 10^{-5}=10^{-4} \mathrm{~T} .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The susceptibility of a magnetism at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is $1.2 \times 10^{-5}$. The temperature at which the susceptibility increases to $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \chi=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{T}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\chi_{1}}{\chi_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{300} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{2} \Rightarrow \frac{12}{18} \times 300=200 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At the magnetic north pole of the earth, the value of horizontal component of earth's magnetic field and angle of dip are, respectively
questions.solution:
At the magnetic north pole, the magnetic needle will point vertically. There is no component of earth's magnetic field in the horizontal direction and the angle of dip (the angle that the resultant magnetic field at the place makes with the horizontal) is $90^{\circ}$.
$$
\mathrm{H}=0, \delta=90^{\circ}(\text { maximum })
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: You measure two quantities as $A=1.0 \mathrm{~m} \pm 0.2 \mathrm{~m}$, $B=2.0 \mathrm{~m} \pm 0.2 \mathrm{~m}$. We should report correct value for $\sqrt{A B}$ as
questions.solution:
Given, $A=1.0 \mathrm{~m} \pm 0.2 \mathrm{~m}, B=2.0 \mathrm{~m} \pm 0.2 \mathrm{~m}$
Let $Y=\sqrt{A B}=\sqrt{(1.0)(20)}=1.414 \mathrm{~m}$
Rounding off to two significant digits
$\begin{aligned}
Y & =1.4 \mathrm{~m} \\
\frac{\Delta Y}{Y} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\Delta A}{A}+\frac{\Delta B}{B}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{0.2}{1.0}+\frac{0.2}{2.0}\right] \\
& =\frac{0.6}{2 \times 20} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \Delta Y & =\frac{0.6 Y}{2 \times 20}=\frac{0.6 \times 1.4}{2 \times 20}=0.212
\end{aligned}$
Rounding off to one significant digit,
$\Delta Y=0.2 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two resistances are given as $R_1=(10 \pm$ $0.5) \Omega$ and $R_2=(15 \pm 0.5) \Omega$. The percentage error in the measurement of equivalent resistance when they are connected in parallel is
questions.solution:
Equivalent resistance in parallel combination is given by
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{R_{\text {eq }}}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2} \\
& \Rightarrow R_{\text {eq }}=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}=\frac{10 \times 15}{10+15}=6
\end{aligned}$
Differentiating both sides, we get
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\Delta R_{\text {eq }}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}^2}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_1^2}+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_2^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_1^2}+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_2^2}\right) \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}} \\ & =\left(\frac{0.5}{100}+\frac{0.5}{225}\right) \cdot 6=\left(\frac{6 \times 0.5}{25}\right)\left(\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{9}\right)=\frac{13}{300} \\ & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}} \times 100=\frac{13}{3}=4.33 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The heat generated in a circuit is given by $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{I}^{2} \mathrm{Rt},$ where I is current, $\mathrm{R}$ is resistance and tis time. If the percentage errors in measuring I. $\mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{t}$ are $2 \%, 1 \%$ and $1 \%$ respectively, then the maximum error in measuring heat will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \frac{\Delta Q}{Q} \times 100 &=\frac{2 \Delta I}{I} \times 100+\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100+\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100 \\ &=2 \times 2 \%+1 \%+1 \%=6 \% \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity of a projectile at the initial point A is 2i^-3j^ m s-1. Its velocity (in m s-1) at point B is
questions.solution: From the figure, the X-component remains unchanged while the Y-component is reverse. Then, the velocity at point B is 2i^+3j^ m s-1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The two vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ are drawn from a common point and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}},$ then angle
between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ is -
(1) $90^{\circ}$ if $\mathrm{C}^{2}=\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{B}^{2}$
(2) greater than $90^{\circ}$ if $\mathrm{C}^{2}<\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{B}^{2}$
(3) greater than $90^{\circ}$ if $\mathrm{C}^{2}>\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{B}^{2}$
(4) less than $90^{\circ}$ if $\mathrm{C}^{2}>\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{B}^{2}$
Correct options are -
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{B}^{2}=\mathrm{R}^{2}$. The angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ is
questions.solution:
$\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}-\mathrm{A}^{2}-\mathrm{B}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~B}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}-\mathrm{R}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{AB}}=0$
$\therefore \theta=\pi / 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Pressure inside two soap bubbles are 1.01 and 1.02 atmosphere, respectively. The ratio of their volumes is :
questions.solution:
According to question, pressure inside, 1st soap bubble,
$\Delta P_1=P_1-P_0=0.01=\frac{4 T}{R_1}$    .....(i)
And $\Delta P_2=P_2-P_0=0.02=\frac{4 T}{R_2}$     .....(ii)
Dividing, equation (ii) by (i),
$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1} \Rightarrow R_1=2 R_2$
Volume $V=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \Rightarrow \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{R_1^3}{R_2^3}=\frac{8 R_2^3}{R_2^3}=\frac{8}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cube of ice floats partly in water and partly in kerosene oil. The radio of volume of ice immersed in water to that in kerosene oil (specific gravity of Kerosene oil $=0.8$, specific gravity of ice $=0.9)$
questions.solution:
Let $V_1$ be, the volume immersed in water.
$V_2$ be the volume immersed in oil.
In equilibrium condition,
$v_1 \rho_w g+v_2 \rho_0 g=\left(v_1+v_2\right) \rho_e g$
$v_1+\frac{v_2 \rho_0}{\rho_w}=\left(v_1+v_2\right) \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_w}$
$\Rightarrow v_1+0.8 v_2=0.9 v_1+0.9 v_2 \Rightarrow 0.1 v_1=0.1 v_2$
$\Rightarrow v_1: v_2=1: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Eight equal drops of water are falling through air with a steady speed of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. If the drops coalesce, the new velocity is:
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{r}$ be the radius of small drops of water.
$\mathrm{R}=$ radius of big drop formed as volume remain same.
$\therefore 8 . \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3-\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{r}$
Terminal velocity,
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2}{9 \eta}(\rho-\sigma) \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{~g} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}} \propto \mathrm{r}^2 \\
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{10}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2\left(\because \mathrm{v}_1=10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \text { given }\right) \\
& \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_2=40 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If 1000 droplets of water of surface tension 0.07 $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. having same radius $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ each, combine to from a single drop. In the process the released surface energy is( Take $\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
questions.solution:
We have $V_f=V_i$
$\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \pi r_{\mathrm{f}}^3=1000 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}^3=1000 \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^3$
$\Rightarrow r_f=10 r_i$
So, released energy $=$ Initial surface energy - final surface energy
$=1000 \times \mathrm{T} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^2-\mathrm{T} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}^2$
$=4 \pi \mathrm{T}\left(1000 \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^2-\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}^2\right)$
$=4 \pi \times 0.07\left(1000 \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^2-100 \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}^2\right)$
$=4 \pi \times 0.07 \times 900 r_i^2$
$=4 \pi \times 63 \times 10^{-6}=7.92 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capillary tube is attached horizontally to a constant heat arrangement. If the radius of the capillary tube is increased by $25 \%$, then the rate of flow of liquid will change nearly by
questions.solution:
Liquid volume coming out of the tube per second
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad V=\frac{p \pi r^4}{8 \eta l} \\
& \Rightarrow \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{V_2}{V_1}=\left[\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right]^4 \Rightarrow V_2=V_1\left[\frac{125}{100}\right]^4=V_1\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)^4 \\
& \Rightarrow \\
& \text { So, now } \frac{\Delta V}{V}=\frac{V_2-V_1}{V_1}=\frac{(244) V_1-V_1}{V_1}=144 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A U - shaped tube contains a liquid of density rho and it is rotated about the left dotted line as shown in the figure. Find the difference in the levels of liquid column.
questions.solution: Weight of liquid of height \(\mathrm{H}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4} \times \mathrm{H} \rho \times \mathrm{g} \rightarrow\) (i) Let us consider a mass dm situated at a distance \(x\) from \(A\) as shown in the figure. The centripetal force required for the mass to rotate \(=(\mathrm{dm}) \omega^{2}\) \(\therefore\) The total centripetal force required for the mass of length \(L\) to rotate \(=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\rho \mathrm{x} \frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4}\right) \mathrm{xdx} \times\left(\mathrm{x} \omega^{2}\right)=\rho \times \frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4} \times \omega^{2} \times \frac{1^{2}}{2} \rightarrow\) (ii) This centripetal force required is provided by the weight of liquid of height 'H' From (i) and (ii) \(\begin{aligned} & \frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4} \times \mathrm{H} \times \rho \times \mathrm{g}=\rho \times \frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4} \times \omega^{2} \times \frac{1^{2}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=\frac{\omega^{2} \mathrm{l}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical drop of radius $R$ is divided into eight equal droplets. If surface tension is $\mathrm{T}$, then the work done in this process is
questions.solution:
Volume of 8 small droplets $=$ Volume of 1 big drop $\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \times 8=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3} \Rightarrow r=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{(8)^{1 / 3}}$

Work done $=($ Change in area $) \times$ surface tension $=\left(4 \pi r^{2} n-4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}\right) \mathrm{T}$

$\left\{4 \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}}{(8)^{2 / 3}}\right) \times 8-4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}\right\} \mathrm{T}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \mathrm{~T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of density $\rho^{\prime}$ is dropped from rest at a height h into a lake of density $\rho$ where $\rho>\rho^{\prime}$ neglecting all dissipative forces, calculate the maximum depth to which the body sinks before returning to float on the surface:
questions.solution:
The effective acceleration of the body $\mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\left(1-\frac{\rho}{\rho^{\prime}}\right) \mathrm{g}$
Now, the depth to which the body sinks $\mathrm{h}^{\prime}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}^{\prime}}\right)=\frac{2 \mathrm{gh}}{2 \mathrm{~g}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{g}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mathrm{h} \times \rho^{\prime}}{\rho-\rho^{\prime}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel with water is placed on a weighing pan and it reads $600 \mathrm{~g}$. Now a ball of mass $40 \mathrm{~g}$ and density $0.80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ is sunk into the water with a pin of negligible volume, as shown in figure keeping it sunk. The weighing pan will show a reading
questions.solution:
Volume of ball $=\frac{40}{0.8}=50 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$

Downthrust on water $=50 \mathrm{~g}$. Therefore reading is $650 \mathrm{~g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two separate soap bubbles of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively, formed of same liquid (surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) come together to form a double bubble. The radius of interface of double bubble is
questions.solution:
Excess pressure inside first bubble of radius r1

p1=p0+4Tr1

Excess pressure inside second bubble of radius r2
p2=p0+4Tr2

Excess pressure inside double bubble,

p=p2-p1

p=4Tr1-r2r1r2

If R be the radius of double bubble, then

p=4TR

or 4Tr1-r2r1r2=4TR

⇒R=r1r2r1-r2=3×10-3×2×10-33-2×10-3=6×10-3 m

R=6×10-3 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of wood floats in water with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mi>th</mi></msup></math> of its volume submerged. If the same block just floate in a liquid, the density of liquid in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questions.solution:
Let V be the volume of the block. When block floats in water, then Vblock g=45vρwater g

or Pblock=45ρwater     .....i

When block floats in liquid.

Vρblock g=Vρliquidg

ρblock =ρliquid 

ρliquid =45ρwater  [from Eq. (i)]

=45×103 kg m-3

=800 kg m-3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a drop of liquid breaks into smaller droplets, it results in lowering of temperature of the droplets. Let a drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, break into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> small droplets each of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then decrease (drop) in temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> (given, specific heat of liquid drop<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mi>S</mi></math> and surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mi>T</mi></math> )
questions.solution:
since, volume remains unchanged, during this phenomenon, so

43πR3=N×43πr3

N=R3r3

Now, change in surface area =4πR2-N4πr2

=4πR2-Nr2

Energy released (ΔU)=T× change in surface area

=T×4πR2-Nr2

Here, all this energy released is al the cost of lowering the temperature and mass of the big drop of liquid =43πR2ρ

Now, change in temperature, Δθ=ΔUms

=T×4πR2-Nr243πR3ρS

=3TμS1R-Nr2R3

=3TρS1R-R3×r2r3×R3

=3TρS1R-1r

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ice-berg of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kgm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is floating in water of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kgm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The percentage of volume of ice-berg outside the water is
questions.solution:
Let the volume of ice-berg is V and its density is ρ. If this ice-berg floats in water with volume Vin inside it, then 

Vinσg=Vρg

σ= density of water

⇒ Vin=ρσ·V

⇒ Vout=V-Vin

=σ-ρσ V=1000-9001000 V

=V10

⇒ Vout /V=0.1=10 %

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Work done in increasing the size of a soap bubble from radius of (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>) <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> is nearly (surface tension of soap solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
Work done = Change in surface energy

r2=5 cm

r1=3 cm

⇒ W=2T×4πr22-r12

=2×0.03×4π52-32×10-4

=0.4π mJ

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The approximate depth of an ocean is $2700 \mathrm{~m}$. The compressibility of water is $45.4 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~Pa}^{-1}$ and density of water is $10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. What fractional compression of water will be obtained at the bottom of the ocean?
questions.solution:
Compressibility of water, $\mathrm{K}=45.4 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~Pa}^{-1}$

density of water $\mathrm{P}=10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$

depth of ocean, $\mathrm{h}=2700 \mathrm{~m}$ We have to find $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=?$

As we know, compressibility, $\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{(\Delta \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{V})}{\mathrm{P}}(\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{Pgh})$

So, $(\Delta \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{V})=\mathrm{KPgh}$

$=45.4 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{3} \times 10 \times 2700$

$=1.2258 \times 10^{-2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capillary tube of radius $\mathrm{r}$ is immersed vertically in a liquid such that liquid rises in it to height $\mathrm{h}$ (less than the length of the tube). Mass of liquid in the capillary tube is $\mathrm{m}$. If radius of the capillary tube is increased by $50 \%$, then mass of liquid that will rise in the tube, is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water is flowing on a horizontal fixed surface, such that its flow velocity varies with $y$ (vertical direction) as $v=k\left(\frac{2 y^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{3}}{a^{3}}\right) .$ If coefficient of viscosity for water is $\eta$, what will be shear stress between layers of water at $y=a$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass capillary tube of internal radius $\mathrm{r}=0.25$ $\mathrm{mm}$ is immersed in water. The top end of the tube projected by $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ above the surface of the water. At what angle does the liquid meet the tube? Surface tension of water $=0.7 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done in blowing a soap bubble of surface tension $0.06 \times \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ from $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ radius to $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ radius is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{W} &=\mathrm{S} \times \Delta \mathrm{A}=0.06 \times 4 \pi\left(\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2}-\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}\right) \\ &=0.003168 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $\quad$ A ball whose density is $0.4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ falls into water from a height of $9 \mathrm{~cm}$. To what depth does the ball sink?
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ice block floats in a liquid whose density is less than water. A part of block is outside the liquid. When whole of ice has melted, the liquid level will
questions.solution: Ice is lighter than water. When ice melts, the volume occupied by water is less than that of ice. Due to which the level of water goes down.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large drop of oil (density $0.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and viscosity $\eta_{0}$ ) floats up through a column of another liquid (density $1.2 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ and viscosity $\eta_{\mathrm{L}}$ ). Assuming that the two liquids do not mix, the velocity with which the oil drop rises will depend on:
questions.solution: Forces of viscosity arise in a fluid when different sections of the fluid move with different velocities. Here, there is no such motion inside the oil drop and hence the viscosity of oil does not come into play.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Viscosity is the property of a liquid due to which it :
options: opposes relative motion between its adjacent layers
tends to regain its deformed position
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An air bubble of volume $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ is released by a fish at a depth $\mathrm{h}$ in a lake. The bubble rises to the surface. Assume constant temperature and standard atmospheric pressure above the lake. The volume of the bubble just before touching the surface will be (density) of water is $\rho$
questions.solution:
As the bubble rises the pressure gets reduced for constant temperature, if $\mathrm{P}$ is the standard atmospheric pressure, then $(\mathrm{P}+\rho \mathrm{gh}) \mathrm{V}_{0}=\mathrm{PV}$
$$
\text { or } \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_{0}\left(1+\frac{\rho \mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{P}}\right)
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical ball is dropped in a long column of a viscous liquid. The speed (v) of the ball as a function of time (t) may be best represented by
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two mercury drops (each of radius $\mathrm{r}$ ) merge to form a bigger drop. The surface energy of the bigger drop, if $\mathrm{T}$ is the surface tension, is
questions.solution:
$\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=2 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=2^{1 / 3} \mathrm{r}$
Surface energy of bigger drop,
$\mathrm{E}=4 \pi R^{2} T=4 \times 2^{2 / 3} \pi r^{2} T=2^{8 / 3} \pi r^{2} T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two circular plates of radius $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ each, have a 0.01 mm thick water film between them. Then what will be the force required to separate these plate (S.T. of water $=73$ dyne/cm)?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{F}=& \frac{2 \mathrm{AT}}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{2 \times \pi \times(0.05)^{2} \times 73 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01 \times 10^{-3}} \\=& 36.5 \pi \approx 115 \text { newton } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A liquid is flowing through a non-sectional tube with its axis horizontally. If two points $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ on the axis of tube has a sectional area $2.0 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ and $25 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ respectively then find the flow velocity at $Y$ when the flow velocity at $X$ is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.
questions.solution:
According to principle of continuity
$$
v_{y}=\frac{v_{x} A_{x}}{A_{y}}=\frac{10(m / s) \times 2\left(c m^{2}\right)}{25 \times 10^{-2}\left(c m^{2}\right)}=80 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires of the same material (Young's modulus $=Y$ ) and same length $L$ but radii $R$ and $2 R$ respectively, are joined end to end and a weight $w$ is suspended from the combination as shown in the figure. The elastic potential energy in the system is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta l_1=\frac{w L}{\left(4 \pi R^2\right) Y}, \Delta l_2=\frac{w L}{\pi R^2 Y} \\ & \therefore U=\frac{1}{2} K_1\left(\Delta l_1\right)^2+\frac{1}{2} K_2\left(\Delta l_2\right)^2 \\ &=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{Y\left(4 \pi R^2\right)}{L} \times\left[\frac{w L}{4 \pi R^2 Y}\right]^2+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{Y\left(\pi R^2\right)}{L} \\ & \times\left[\frac{w L}{\pi R^2 Y}\right]^2\left(\because K=\frac{Y A}{L}\right) \\ &=\frac{5 w^2 L}{8 \pi R^2 Y}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: With rise in temperature, the Young's modulus of elasticity:
questions.solution:
Young's modulus, $\mathrm{Y}=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}$
If the temperature increases, strain also increases. Hence young's modulus decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Young's modules of materials of a wire of Length ' L ' and cross-sectional area A is Y . If the length of the wire is doubled and cross-sectional area is halved then Young's modules will be:
questions.solution: Young's modulus depends on the material not depends on length and cross sectional area. So Young's modulus remains constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Young's moduli of the material of wires A and B are in the ratio of $1: 4$, while its area of cross sections are in the ratio of $1: 3$. If the same amount of load is applied to both the wires, the amount of elongation produced in the wires $\mathrm{A}$ and $B$ will be in the ratio of
[Assume length of wires A and B are same]
questions.solution:
Elongation produced in the wire is given b
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\mathrm{AY}}$
Here, $L=$ length of wire
$A=$ area of cross-section of wire
$\mathrm{Y}=$ Young's moduli of material of wire.
$\because \mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{L}$ is same and
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{4} \quad \text { (given) } \\
& \frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{3} \quad \text { (given) } \\
& \therefore \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}} \frac{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{3}{1} \times \frac{4}{1}=12
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \mathrm{~m}$ long wire having cross-sectional area $6.25 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and Young's modulus is $10^{10}$ $\mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ is subjected to a load of $250 \mathrm{~N}$, then the elongation in the wire will be :
questions.solution:
$\Delta \ell=\frac{F \ell}{Y A}=\frac{250 \times 100}{10^{10} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}=40 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$
$=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are two wire of same material and same length while the diameter of second wire is two times the diameter of first wire, then the ratio of extension produced in the wires by applying same load will be
questions.solution:
$\because$ Both wires are same materials so both will have same Young's modulus, and let it be Y. $Y=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}=\frac{F}{\text { A. }(\Delta L / L)}, F=$ applied force

$\mathrm{A}=$ area of cross-section of wire

Now,

$\mathrm{Y}_{1}=\mathrm{Y}_{2} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\left(\mathrm{A}_{1}\right)\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{1}\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{FL}}{\left(\mathrm{A}_{2}\right)\left(\Delta \mathrm{L}_{2}\right)}$

Since load and length are same for both

$\begin{array}{l}

\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \Delta \mathrm{L}_{1}=\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2} \Delta \mathrm{L}_{2},\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{1}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}_{2}}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{1}}\right)^{2}=4 \\

\Delta \mathrm{L}_{1}: \Delta \mathrm{L}_{2}=4: 1

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel wire of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has elastic limit of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The maximum upward acceleration that can be given to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> elevator supported by this steel wire if the stress is to exceed one-fourth of the elastic limit is [Take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
questions.solution:
Given, Y=2.2×108 m s-2

Area of cross-section of steel wire,

A=4 cm2=4×10-4 m2

Mass of elevator, m=1000 kg

The maximum tension in the steel wire that can support elevator is given by

T=14× stress × area of cross-section 

=14×2.2×108×4×10-4

=2.2×104 N m-2

If f be the maximum upward acceleration of the elevator, then

T=m g+f

2.2×104=1000 10+f

⇒  f=12 m s-2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rubber cord has a cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and total unstretched length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It is stretched to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and then released to project a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>. The velocity of projection is [Given, Young's modulus of rubber, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
questions.solution:
Force constant K of rubber is given by

K=YAl=5×108×10-60.1=5×103 N m-1

Now, from conservation of energy,

elastic potential energy of cord = kinetic energy of particle

i.e. 12K∆l2=12mv2

⇒v=Km·∆l

=5×1035×10-30.125-0.1

=103×0.025=25 m s-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Breaking stress of a steel wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and the density of steel is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math>. The greatest length of steel wire that can hang vertically without breaking is
questions.solution:
Maximum stress = Maximum weight  Cross-sectional area 

Now, maximum weight of steel wire

= Volume × Density ×g=Alρg

where, l is the maximum length of steel wire that can hang vertically without breaking. ρ is the density of steel and A is the cross-sectional area of steel wire.
∴ Maximum stress, p=AlρgA=lρg

∴ l=pρg

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mild steel wire of length 2 L and cross-sectional area A is stretched, well with in the elastic limit, horizontally between two pillars as shown in figure. A mass m is suspended from the mid-point of the wire strain in the wire is
questions.solution:
Increase in length 

Δ L=BO+OC-BC

=2BO-2BD

⇒ ΔL=2BO-2 L

=2 L2+x212-2 L

or ΔL=2 L 1+x2L212-2 L [∵ using Bionomial theorem]

⇒ ΔL≈2 L 1+12x2L2-1

=x2L   ∵ x<<L

∴ Strain =ΔL2 L

=12 L×∆L=12 L×x2L=x22 L2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire 2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F$, how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
questions.solution: As shown in the figure, the wires will have the same Young's modulus (same material) and the length of the wire of area of crosssection $3 A$ will be $\ell / 3$ (same volume as wire 1 ). For wire $1, \quad Y=\frac{F / A}{\Delta x / \ell}$ For wire $2, Y=\frac{F^{\prime} / 3 A}{\Delta x /(\ell / 3)}$ From (i) and (ii) $, \frac{F}{A} \times \frac{\ell}{\Delta x}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{3 A} \times \frac{\ell}{3 \Delta x}$ $\Rightarrow F^{\prime}=9 F$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The load versus elongation graph for four wires is shown. The thinnest wire is
questions.solution:
Using \(\quad \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{L} / \mathrm{L}}\)

Cross-section area of the wire \(\quad \mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{R}^{2}\) where \(\quad \mathrm{R}\) is the radius of the wire \(\therefore \quad \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\pi \mathrm{R}^{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}}\)

\(\Longrightarrow \mathrm{F}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{L}} \Delta \mathrm{L}\)

Thus Slope of load vs elongation graph, \(\mathrm{s}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \mathrm{Y}}{\mathrm{L}}\)

\(\Longrightarrow \quad \mathrm{s} \propto \mathrm{R}^{2}\)

Now from the graph shown, OA has the least slope and hence the least radius. Thus \(\mathrm{OA}\) represents the thinnest wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cube is subjected to a uniform volume compression. If the side of the cube decreases by $2 \%$ the bulk strain is
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} \frac{\Delta a}{d} &=2 \% \\ v &=a^{3} \\ \frac{\Delta v}{v} &=3 \frac{\Delta a}{d} \\ &=6 \% \\ &=0.06 \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Young's modulus of a perfectly rigid body is
questions.solution: For a perfectly rigid body strain produced is zero for the given force applied, so $\mathrm{Y}=$ stress/strain $=\infty$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One end of a long metallic wire of length $\mathrm{L}$ tied to the ceiling. The other end is tied with a massless spring of spring constant $\mathrm{K}$. Amass hangs freely from the free end of the spring. The area of cross section and the young's modulus of the wire are A and Y respectively. If the mass slightly pulled down and released, it will oscillate with a time period T equal to:
questions.solution: Step 1: Calculation of spring constant of wire Young's modulus of wire, \(\mathrm{Y}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \mathrm{L} / \mathrm{L}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{F}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A}}\) Due to elasticity of the wire, it behaves as equivalent spring \(\left(\mathrm{K}_{\text {spring }}=\mathrm{F} / \Delta \mathrm{x}\right)\). Hence, Spring constant for Wire, \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{F}}{\Delta \mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{Y} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{L}}\) Step 2: Calculation of Equivalent spring constant Let a force \(\mathrm{F}\) be applied to the end of the spring. The wire will extend. Total extension \(\Delta \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{wire}}+\mathrm{x}_{\text {spring }}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}}+\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{K}}\) But \(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{w}} \quad\) (By Newton's, third Law, Both will apply equal and opposite forces at their joint A ) \(\therefore \Delta \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{w}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{w}}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}\right)\) So, Equivalent spring constant \(\left(\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}\right)\) is given by: \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\Delta \mathrm{S}}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{w}}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}}\) \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{(\mathrm{K})\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}\right)}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{w}}+\mathrm{K}}=\frac{\mathrm{KY} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{Y} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{L}}+\mathrm{K}}=\frac{\mathrm{KYA}}{\mathrm{YA}+\mathrm{KL}}\) Step 3: Calculation of Time period Angular frequency \(\omega\) for spring block system is given by: \(\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}}{\mathrm{m}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{KYA}}{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{YA}+\mathrm{KL})}}\) \(\therefore\) Time period, \(\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(\mathrm{YA}+\mathrm{KL}) \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{KYA}}}\) Hence, Option B is correct

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However wire 1 has crosssectional area $A$ and wire 2 has cross-sectional area $3 A$. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force $F,$ how much force is needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount?
questions.solution: As shown in the figure, the wires will have the same Young's modulus (same material) and the length of the wire of area of crosssection $3 A$ will be $\ell / 3$ (samevolume as wire 1 ). For wire $1, \quad Y=\frac{F / A}{\Delta x / \ell}$ For wire $2, Y=\frac{F^{\prime} / 3 A}{\Delta x /(\ell / 3)}$ From (i) and (ii) $, \frac{F}{A} \times \frac{\ell}{\Delta x}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{3 A} \times \frac{\ell}{3 \Delta x}$ $\Rightarrow F^{\prime}=9 F$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron rod of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ and cross-sectional area of $50 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ stretched by $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$, when a mass of $250 \mathrm{~kg}$ is hung from its lower end. Young's modulus of iron rod is
questions.solution:
$Y=\frac{\mathrm{F} / \mathrm{A}}{\Delta \ell / \ell}=\frac{\frac{250 \times 9.8}{50 \times 10^{-6}}}{\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}}$
$=\frac{250 \times 9.8}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \times \frac{2}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \Rightarrow 19.6 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The upper end of a wire of diameter $12 \mathrm{~mm}$ and length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is clamped and its other end is twisted through an angle of $30^{\circ} .$ The angle of shear is
questions.solution: $r \theta=\ell \phi \Rightarrow \phi=\frac{r \theta}{\ell}=\frac{6 \mathrm{~mm} \times 30^{\circ}}{1 \mathrm{~m}}=0.18^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic rod breaks when strain produced is $0.2 \% .$ The Young's modulus of the material of the rod is $7 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2} .$ What should be its area of cross-section to support a load of $10^{4} \mathrm{~N}$ ?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A particle is moving in a straight line. The variation of position ' $x$ ' as a function of time ' $t$ ' is given as $x=\left(t^3-6 t^2+20 t+15\right) m$.
The velocity of the body when its acceleration becomes zero is:
questions.solution:
Displacement, $x=t^3-6 t^2+20 t+15$
$\therefore$ Velocity, $v=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2-12 \mathrm{t}+20$
$\therefore$ Acceleration, $a=\frac{d v}{d t}=6 t-12$
When $a=0$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{t}-12=0 \Rightarrow t=2 \mathrm{~s}$
At $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}, v=3(2)^2-12(2)+20=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest and moving with an acceleration $\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, in the third second is:
questions.solution:
Distance travelled in the nth second is given by
$\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{u}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}(2 \mathrm{n}-1)$
put $\mathrm{u}=0, \mathrm{a}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}, \mathrm{n}=3$
$\therefore \quad d=0+\frac{4}{3 \times 2}(2 \times 3-1)=\frac{4}{6} \times 5=\frac{10}{3} m$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at $90 \mathrm{~km} /$ $h$ observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for $8 \mathrm{~s}$. If the velocity of the train B is $54 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, then length of train B is :
questions.solution:
Velocity of train A
\(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=90 \frac{\mathrm{km}}{\mathrm{hr}}=90 \times \frac{5}{18}=25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
Velocity of train B
\(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=54 \frac{\mathrm{km}}{\mathrm{hr}}=54 \times \frac{5}{18}=15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
Velocity of train B w.r.t. train
\(\begin{aligned}
A=\vec{V}_B & -\vec{V}_A \\
& =15-(-25) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}\)
Time of crossing \(=\frac{\text { length of train }}{\text { relative velocity }}\)
\((8)=\frac{\ell}{40}\)
\(\ell=8 \times 40=320\) meter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object moves with speed $v_1, v_2$ and $v_3$ along a line segment $A B, B C$ and $C D$ respectively as shown in figure. Where $A B=B C$ and $A D=3 A B$, then average speed of the object will be:
options: $\frac{3 v_1 v_2 v_3}{\left(v_1 v_2+v_2 v_3+v_3 v_1\right)}$
questions.solution:
Consider,
$\mathrm{AB}=x$
$\mathrm{BC}=x$


$2 x+\mathrm{CD}=3 x \Rightarrow \mathrm{CD}=3 x-2 x=x$
Average speed of the object $ < \mathrm{v}>$
$=\frac{\text { Total distance }}{\text { Total time }}$
$ < \mathrm{v}>=\frac{3 x}{\frac{x}{v_1}+\frac{x}{v_2}+\frac{x}{v_3}}=\frac{3 v_1 v_2 v_3}{v_2 v_3+v_1 v_3+v_1 v_2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A balloon is rising vertically up with a velocity of $29 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. A stone is dropped from it and it reaches the ground in 10 seconds. The height of the balloon when the stone was dropped from it, was $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
questions.solution:
For stone to be dropped from rising balloon of velocity $29 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{u}=-29 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{sec} .$

$\begin{array}{l}

\therefore \mathrm{h}=-29 \times 10+\frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times 100 \\

=-290+490=200 \mathrm{~m} .

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From a $200 \mathrm{~m}$ high tower, one ball is thrown upwards with speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and another is thrown vertically downwards at the same speeds simultaneously. The time difference of their reaching the ground will be nearest to
questions.solution: The ball thrown upward will lose velocity in ls. It return back to thrown point in another $1 \mathrm{~s}$ with the same velocity as second. Thus the difference will be $2 \mathrm{~s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the acceleration, velocity and displacement of a tennis ball as its falls to the ground and bounces back. Directions of which of these change in the process?
questions.solution: When a tennis ball falls on the ground and bounces back, its velocity and displacement changes in reverse direction while direction of acceleration remains same.

The time interval of passing between the two walls is
questions.solution:
2h=ut

ux=2hΔt ∵u=ux,t=Δt....i

h=uyt-12gt2

gt2-2uyt+2h=0

t1=2uy+4uy2-8gh2g

t2=2uy-4uy2-8gh2g

Δt=t1-t2=4uy2-8ghg

g24Δt4-2ghΔt2+4h2=0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is dropped vertically from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> above the ground. It hits the ground and bounces up vertically to a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Neglecting subsequent motion and air resistance, its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> varies with height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> above the ground as
questions.solution:
For the uniformly accelerated/decelcrated motion,

v2=u2±2gh

So. from this equation. we can say v-h paragraph is parabola.

Initially, velocity is downwards (-ve) after collision, it reverses the direction with smaller magnitude and velocity is upwards (-ve).

So. graph (a) satisfies these conditions.

Also, When t=0, h=d Velocity increases downwards (U→A)

When t-1 velocity reverses its direction A'→B

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aeroplane is flying in a horizontal direction with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. When it is vertically below a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the ground a food packet is released from it. The packet strikes the ground at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given R=3 km=3000 m

Range, R=u2hg

⇒ u=Rg2hor u=3000×102×2000

=3000×120

=150 m s-1

=150×185 km h-1

=540 km h-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis has acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math>, at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> given by<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>t</mi><mi>T</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> are constants. The particle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and the instant when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the particle's velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>x</mi></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
Acceleration

f=dvdt=f01+tT

or dv=f01-tT·dt     .........  Eq(i)

Integrating Eq. (i) on both sides, we get

v=f0t-f0T·t22+C

After applying boundary conditions 

v=0 at t=0

We get, C=0

⇒ v=f0t-f0T·t22    ..........  Eq(ii)   

As f=f01-tT

When f0=0,t=T

Substituting t=T in Eq. (ii), then velocity

vx=f0T-f0T·T22=12f0T

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is thrown vertically upwards from A, the top of the tower, reaches the ground in time $t_{1}$. If it is thrown vertically downwards from A with the same speed, it reaches the ground in time $\mathrm{t}_{2}$. If it is allowed to fall freely from $\mathrm{A},$ then the time it takes to reach the ground is given by
questions.solution:
Let the body is projected vertically upwards from A with a speed $u_{0}$ Using equation, $\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{ut}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^{2}$

For case $(1)-\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{u}_{0} \mathrm{t}_{1}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}_{1}^{2} \quad \ldots(1)$

For case (2) $-\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{u}_{0} \mathrm{t}_{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}_{1}^{2}$

Subtracting eq (2) from (1), we get

$$

\begin{array}{l}

0=\mathrm{u}_{0}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}+\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}\right) \\

\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}_{0}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}-\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)

\end{array}

$$

Putting the value of $\mathrm{u}_{0}$ in eq (2), we get

$$

-\mathrm{h}=-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{g}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}-\mathrm{t}_{2}\right) \mathrm{t}_{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{gt}_{2}^{2}

$$

$$

\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}\right)

$$

For case $3, \mathrm{u}_{0}=0, \mathrm{t}=?$

$$

-\mathrm{h}=0 \times \mathrm{t}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{gt}^{2}

$$

$\mathrm{h}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{gt}^{2}$

Comparing eq. (4) and $(5),$ we get

$$

\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2} \quad \therefore \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train accelerating uniformly from rest attains a maximum speed of $40 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ in $20 \mathrm{~s}$. It travels at the speed for $20 \mathrm{~s}$ and is brought to rest with uniform retardation in further $40 \mathrm{~s}$. What is the average velocity during the period ?
questions.solution:
(i) $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+$ at $_{1}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

40=0+a \times 20 \\

a=2 m / s^{2} \\

v^{2}-u^{2}=2 a s \\

40^{2}-0=2 \times 2 s_{1} \\

\text { (ii) } s_{1}=400 \mathrm{~m} \\

\text { (iii) } v=u+a t \\

\quad 0=40+a \times 40 \\

a=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \\

0^{2}-40^{2}=2(-1) \mathrm{s}_{3} \\

\mathrm{~s}_{3}=800 \mathrm{~m} \\

\text { Total distance travelled }=\mathrm{s}_{1}+\mathrm{s}_{2}+\mathrm{s}_{3} \\

=400+800+800=2000 \mathrm{~m} \\

\text { Total time taken }=20+20+40=80 \mathrm{~s} \\

\text { Average velocity }=\frac{2000}{80}=25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}

\end{array}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man runs at a speed of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to overtake a standing bus. When he is $6 \mathrm{~m}$ behind the door at $\mathrm{t}=0,$ the bus moves forward and continuous with a constant acceleration of $1.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. The man reaches the door in time $\mathrm{t}$. Then,
questions.solution:
Let us draw the figure for given situation,
$\Rightarrow \quad 4 \mathrm{t}=6+\frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times \mathrm{t}^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 4 \mathrm{t}=6+0.6 \mathrm{t}^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body moves with uniform acceleration, then which of the following graph is correct?
questions.solution: An object is said to be moving with a uniform acceleration, if its velocity changes by equal amount in equal intervals of time. The velocity-time graph of uniformly accelerated motion is a straight line inclined to time axis. Acceleration of an object in a uniformly accelerated motion in one dimension is equal to the slope of the velocity-time graph with time axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is moving in a straight line with initial velocity and uniform acceleration a. If the sum of the distance travelled in $t^{\text {th }}$ and $(t+1)^{\text {th }}$ seconds is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$, then its velocity after $t$ seconds, in $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{is}$
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The position $\mathrm{x}$ of a particle varies with time $(\mathrm{t})$ as $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \mathrm{t}^{2}-\mathrm{B} \mathrm{t}^{3}$. The acceleration at time $\mathrm{t}$ of the particle will be equal to zero. What is the value of $t ?$
questions.solution:
Given that $x=A t^{2}-B t^{3}$
$$
\therefore \text { velocity }=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{t}-3 \mathrm{~B} \mathrm{t}^{2}
$$
and acceleration 
$$ 
=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=2 \mathrm{~A}-6 \mathrm{Bt}
$$
For acceleration to be zero $2 \mathrm{~A}-6 \mathrm{Bt}=0$.
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~A}}{6 \mathrm{~B}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{3 \mathrm{~B}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle starts moving rectilinearly at time $\mathrm{t}=0$ such that its velocity $\mathrm{v}$ changes with time $\mathrm{t}$ according to the equation $v=t^{2}-t$ where $t$ is in seconds and $v$ is in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Find the time interval for which the particle retards.
questions.solution:
Acceleration of the particle $a=2 t-1$ The particle retards when acceleration is opposite to velocity.
$$
\Rightarrow \text { a } \cdot v<0 \Rightarrow(2 t-1)\left(t^{2}-t\right)<0 \Rightarrow t(2 t-1)
$$
$(\mathrm{t}-1)<0$
Now t is always positive
$$
\therefore(2 t-1)(t-1)<0
$$
or $2 \mathrm{t}-1<0$ and $\mathrm{t}-1>0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}<\frac{1}{2}$ and $\mathrm{t}>1$.
This is not possible or $2 \mathrm{t}-1>0 \& \mathrm{t}-1<0 \Rightarrow 1 / 2<\mathrm{t}<1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A projectile is projected with velocity of $40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. If $R$ be the horizontal range covered by the projectile and after $t$ seconds, its inclination with horizontal becomes zero, then the value of $\cot \theta$ is
[Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ]
questions.solution:
At maximum height, inclination with horizontal becomes zero.
Time (taken by) projectile to reach maximum height,
$\begin{aligned}
 \quad t & =\frac{u \sin \theta}{g} \\
\Rightarrow \quad u & =\frac{g t}{\sin \theta}\ldots(i)
\end{aligned}$
Since,
$R=u \cos \theta \times(2 t)$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{R}{2 u t} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{R}{2 \cdot \frac{g t}{\sin \theta} \cdot t} \\
& =\frac{R \sin \theta}{2 g t^2}(Using(i))
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cot \theta=\frac{R}{2 g t^2}=\frac{R}{2 \times 10 t^2}=\frac{R}{20 t^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rigid body rotates about a fixed axis with variable angular velocity equal to $\alpha-\beta t$, at the time $t$, where $\alpha, \beta$ are constants. The angle through which it rotates before it stops is
questions.solution:
$\omega=\alpha-\beta t$. Comparing with $\omega=\omega_0-\alpha t$
Initial angular velocity $=\alpha$
Angular'retardation $=\beta$
$\therefore$ Angle rotated before it stops is $\frac{\alpha^2}{2 \beta}$. [using
$\left.\omega^2=\omega_0^2+2 \beta \theta\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The range of the projectile projected at an angle of $15^{\circ}$ with horizontal is 50 m . If the projectile is projected with same velocity at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with horizontal, then its range will be :
questions.solution:
Range of projectile
$R=\frac{v^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g}(\because R \propto \sin (2 \theta))$
$\frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{\sin \left(2 \theta_1\right)}{\sin \left(2 \theta_2\right)}=\frac{\sin (2 \times 15)}{\sin (2 \times 45)}=\frac{\sin 30^{\circ}}{\sin 90^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{50}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_2=100 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is projected with a velocity ' $u$ ' making an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about the point of projection when the particle is at its maximum height $h$ is:
questions.solution:
Angular momentum, $L=m v H=m u \cos 30 H$
$=m u \cos 30^{\circ} \times \frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}\left[\because H=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}\right]$
$=\frac{m u^3}{2 g} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{\sqrt{3} m u^3}{16 g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is thrown with a velocity of 9.8 ms making an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. It will hit the ground after a time
questions.solution:
Time of flight $=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}$
$=\frac{2 \times 9.8 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{9.8}=2 \times \frac{1}{2}=1 \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is projected at $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal with initial velocity $40 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The velocity of the projectile at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ from the start will be: (Given $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
Given,
Initial velocity of projectile, $u=40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Angle, $\theta=30^{\circ}$
Time of flight
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \times 40 \times 1}{10 \times 2}=45\left(\because \mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
It means projectile is at maximum height at $t=$ $2 \mathrm{~s}$. At maximum height vertical component of velocity is zero.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Velocity at } \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta=40 \cos 30^{\circ} \\
& =20 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \text {. } \\
&
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is projected with the velocity of $(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. The horizontal range of the projectile will be
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathbf{v}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$ $$ \therefore \quad v=|\mathrm{v}|=\sqrt{3^2+4^2} \Rightarrow v=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$ From figure,
$\sin \theta=\frac{4}{5}$ and $\cos \theta=\frac{3}{5}$ $\because \quad R=\frac{v^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g}$ $=\frac{v^2 \cdot 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta}{g}$ $=\frac{5 \times 5 \times 2 \times \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{3}{5}}{10}=24 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile A is thrown at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal from point P. At the same time, another projectile $\mathrm{B}$ is thrown with velocity $\mathrm{v}_{2}$ upwards from the point $Q$ vertically below the highest point. For $\mathrm{B}$ to collide with $\mathrm{A}, \frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}$ should be
questions.solution:
This happen when vertical velocity of both are same.

$\therefore v_{2}=v_{1} \sin 30^{\circ} \text { or } \frac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation of a projectile is $y=\sqrt{3} x-\frac{g x^{2}}{2}$ The angle of projection is given by
questions.solution:
Comparing the given equation with

$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \tan \theta-\frac{\mathrm{gx}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{u}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}, \text { we get }$

$\tan \theta=\sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel is rotating at $900 \mathrm{r}$.p.m. about its axis. When power is cut offit comes to rest in 1 minute. The angular retardation in $\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
Angular retardation,

$\begin{aligned} \alpha &=\frac{\omega_{2}-\omega_{1}}{t}=\frac{2 \pi\left(\mathrm{n}_{2}-\mathrm{n}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{t}} \\ &=\frac{2 \pi(0-900 / 60)}{60}=\frac{\pi}{2} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is given an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is along the ground and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> is along the vertical. Then, the equation of the path of projectile is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo></math>Take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Velocity of projectile at time t is given by

v=u-g t j^

v=i^+3 j^-10 t j^

∴  r=∫v dt

=∫ i^+3 j^-10 t j^ dt

=t i^+3 t j^-10t22j^

=t i^+3 t j^-5 t2 j^

r=t i^+3 t-5 t2 j^

or x i^+y j^=t i^+3 t-5 t2 j^

On comparing, x=t          ...(i)

and y=3 t-5 t2          ...(ii)

From Eqs (i) and (ii), we get

y=3 x-5 x2

questions.solution:
Given, H=R

sinθ×sinθ2=2sinθ×cosθ

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The horizontal range and maximum height attained by a projectile are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> respectively. If a constant horizontal acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>g</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is imparted to the projectile due to wind, then its horizontal range and maximum height will be
questions.solution:
T=2Uyg

H=Uy22g

Here maximum height will be same as H because there is no acceleration change in vertical direction.

and R=Ux×T

When a horizontal acceleration is also given to the projectile Uy,T and H will remain unchanged while range will become

R'=Ux×T+12aT2

=R+12g44Uy2g2=R+H  ∵a=g4

Therefore horizontal range & maximum height will be R+H & H.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone projected with a velocity a at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal reaches maximum height $\mathrm{H}_{1}$. When it is projected with velocity u at an angle $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ with the horizontal, it reaches maximum height $\mathrm{H}_{2}$. The relation between the horizontal range $\mathrm{R}$ of the projectile, heights $\mathrm{H}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ \text { and } \mathrm{H}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin ^{2}\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ \mathrm{H}_{1} \mathrm{H}_{2}=& \frac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \times \frac{u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=\frac{\left(u^{2} \sin 2 \theta\right)^{2}}{16 g^{2}}=\frac{R^{2}}{16} \\ \therefore R=4 \sqrt{H_{1} H_{2}} \end{aligned}$

If the projectile was launched at an angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, then $\theta$ is $\left(\right.$ take $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$\

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is projected at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal with a velocity of $20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. After $1 \mathrm{~s}$, explosion takes place and the particle is broken into two equal pieces. As a result of explosion, one part comes to rest. The maximum height from the ground attained by the other part is
questions.solution:
Given : Initial velocity $u_{0}=20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} ;$ angle of projection $\theta=45^{\circ}$

Therefore horizontal and vertical components of initial velocity are $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=20 \sqrt{2} \cos 45^{\circ}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

and $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sqrt{2} \sin 45^{\circ}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

After $1 \mathrm{~s}$, horizontal component remains unchanged while the vertical component becomes $v_{y}=u_{y}-g t$

Due to explosion, one part comes to rest. Hence, from the conservation of linear momentum, vertical component of second

part will become $v_{y}^{\prime}=20 m / s$ Therefore, maximum height attained by the second part will be $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{h}_{1}+\mathrm{h}_{2}$

Using, $\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{ut}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^{2}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}_{1}=(20 \times 1)-\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(1)^{2}=15 \mathrm{~m}$

$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$

$\mathrm{h}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{\prime 2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=\frac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 10}=20 \mathrm{~m}$

$\mathrm{H}=20+15=35 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2 \mathrm{~m}$ wide truck is moving with a uniform speed $v_{0}=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ along a straight horizontal road. $\mathrm{A}$ pedestrain starts to cross the road with a uniform speed $v$ when the truck is 4 m away from him. The minimum value of $v$ so that he can cross the road safely is
questions.solution:
Let the man starts crossing the road at an angle $\theta$ as shown in figure. For safe crossing the condition is that the man must cross the road by the time the truck describes the distance 4 । $A C$ or 4 । $2 \cot \theta$. $\therefore \frac{4+2 \cot \theta}{8}=\frac{2 / \sin \theta}{v}$ or $v=\frac{8}{2 \sin \theta+\cos \theta}$ For minimum $v, \frac{d v}{d \theta}=0$
or $\frac{-8(2 \cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{(2 \sin \theta+\cos \theta)^{2}}=0$ or $2 \cos \theta-$ $\sin \theta=0$ or $\tan \theta=2$ From equation (i), $$ v_{\min }=\frac{8}{2\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)+\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}}=\frac{8}{\sqrt{5}}=3.57 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is fired with a velocity u making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. What is the magnitude of change in velocity when it is at the highest point -
questions.solution:
Initially $\mathrm{u}=\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{u} \sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{j}}$.

At highest point $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}$

$\therefore$ difference is $\mathrm{u} \sin \theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity and acceleration vectors of a particle undergoing circular motion are $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ respectively at an instant of time. The radius of the circle is -
questions.solution:
It can be observed that component of acceleration perpendicular to velocity is $\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$

$$

\therefore \text { radius }=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{(2)^{2}}{4}=1 \text { metre }

$$

questions.q: A body is projected, making an acute angle with the horizontal. If angle between velocity $\vec{v}$ and acceleration $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{g}}$ is $\theta,$ then
questions.solution: Here velocity is acting upwards when projectile is going upwards and acceleration is downwards. The angle $\theta$ between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text { a }}$ is more than $0^{\circ}$ and less than $180^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile can have the same range $\mathrm{R}$ for two angles of projection. If $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ be the times of flight in two cases, then what is the product of two times of flight?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ and
$\mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin (90-\theta)}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2} &=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}^{2} \cos \theta \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}^{2}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{~g}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}\right] \\ &=\frac{2}{\mathrm{~g}} \mathrm{R}, \end{aligned}$
where $\mathrm{R}$ is the range. 
Hence $t_{1} t_{2} \propto R$

questions.solution: As $a_{\mathrm{CM}}=0 \quad\left[\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{CM}}=\right.$ constant $]$, Tangential acceleration of each point $\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{AB}}\right|=\frac{2 \mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$

questions.solution: $1.5=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 30}{\mathrm{~g}} ; \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 90}{\mathrm{~g}}=3 \mathrm{~km}$

questions.solution: Body moves with constant speed it means that tangential acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{T}}=0 \& \mathrm{conly}$ centripetal acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{C}}$ exists whose direction is always towards the centre or inward (along the radius of the circle).
