questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given cycle $A B C D A$, the heat required for an ideal monoatomic gas will be
questions.solution:
In the given cycle, heat is supplied in process $D$ to $A$ at constant volume and in process $A$ to $B$ at constant pressure.
$\therefore$ Heat supplied, $Q=n C_V(\Delta T)_{D A}+n C_p(\Delta T)_{A B}$
For an ideal monoatomic gas,
$\begin{aligned}
C_V & =\frac{3}{2} R \text { and } C_p=\frac{5}{2} R \\
\Rightarrow \quad Q & =\frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T+\frac{5}{2} n R \Delta T \quad[\because n R \Delta T=p \Delta V] \\
& =\frac{3}{2}\left(p_0 V_0\right)+5\left(p_0 V_0\right) \\
& =\frac{13}{2} p_0 V_0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas can be taken from A to B via two different processes $A C B$ and $A D B$. When path $A C B$ is used 60 J of heat flows into the system and 30J of work is done by the system. If path ADB is used work done by the system is 10 J . The heat Flow into the system in path ADB is :
questions.solution: $\triangle \mathrm{U}$ remains same for both paths ACB and ADB $\Delta Q_{A C B}=\Delta W_{A C B}+\Delta U_{A C B}$ $\Rightarrow 60 \mathrm{~J}=30 \mathrm{~J}+\Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{ACB}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{ACB}}=30 \mathrm{~J}$ As change in internal energy depends only on initial and final point $\therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{ADB}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{ACB}}=30 \mathrm{~J}$ $\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{ADB}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{ADB}}+\Delta \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{ADB}}=10 \mathrm{~J}+30 \mathrm{~J}=40 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A source supplies heat to a system at the rate of 1000 W . If the system performs work at a rate of 200 W . The rate at which internal energy of the system increases
questions.solution:
Given,
Rate of heat supplied, $\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1000 \mathrm{~W}$
Rate of work performed, $\frac{\mathrm{dw}}{\mathrm{dt}}=200 \mathrm{~W}$
Using first law of thermodynamics
$d Q=d U+d w$
\(\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dU}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}-\frac{\mathrm{dw}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dU}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1000-200=800\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On celcius scale the temperature of body increases by $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The increase in temperature on Fahrenheit scale is:
questions.solution:
Since $\frac{F-32}{9}=\frac{C}{5}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{C}=\frac{5}{9} \Delta \mathrm{~F}$
$\Rightarrow 40=\frac{5}{9} \Delta \mathrm{~F} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{~F}=72^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hypothetical gas expands adiabatically such that its volume changes from 08 litres to 27 litres. If the ratio of final pressure of the gas to initial pressure of the gas is $\frac{16}{81}$. Then the ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}$ will be.
questions.solution:
In adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{PV}^\gamma=\text { constant } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1^\gamma=\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2^\gamma \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_1}\right)^\gamma \Rightarrow \frac{81}{16}=\left(\frac{27}{8}\right)^\gamma \\
& \Rightarrow\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^2=\left(\frac{9 \times 3}{4 \times 2}\right)^\gamma \Rightarrow\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^{2-\gamma}=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^\gamma \\
& \Rightarrow\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^{2-\gamma}=\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)^{\gamma / 2} \Rightarrow 2-\gamma=\frac{\gamma}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow 2=\frac{3}{2} \gamma \Rightarrow \gamma=\frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thermodynamic system is taken through cyclic process. The total work done in the process is :
questions.solution:
Work done $=$ Area under the curve
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} \times(4-2) \times(400-100)=\frac{1}{2}(2) \times 300$
$\mathrm{W}=300 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas at atmospheric pressure is adiabatically compressed so that its density becomes 32 times of its initial value. If the final pressure of gas is 128 atmospheres, the value of ' $\gamma$ ' of the gas is :
questions.solution:
Volume $v=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{d}$ and using $\mathrm{PV}^{\gamma}=$ constant

$\begin{array}{l}

\frac{\mathrm{P}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{d}}\right)^{\gamma} \\

\text { or } 128=(32)^{\gamma} \\

\therefore \gamma=\frac{7}{5}=1.4

\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The compressibility of water is $4 \times 10^{-5}$ per unit atmospheric pressure. The decrease in volume of $100 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ of water under a pressure of 100 atmosphere will be
questions.solution:
Compressibility $=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{P}}$.

Here, $\mathrm{P}=100 \mathrm{~atm}$

Compressibility $=4 \times 10^{-5}$ and $\mathrm{V}=100 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$

Hence, $\Delta \mathrm{V}=0.4 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an adiabatic process, the pressure is increased by $\frac{2}{3} \% .$ If $\gamma=\frac{3}{2},$ then the volume decreases by nearly
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{PV}^{3 / 2}=\mathrm{K}$

$\log \mathrm{P}+\frac{3}{2} \log \mathrm{V}=\log \mathrm{K}$

$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}+\frac{3}{2} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=0$

$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=-\frac{2}{3} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}}$ or $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}=\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)=-\frac{4}{9}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal monoatomic gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> expands adiabatically to twice its volume. The final temperature of gas is
questions.solution:
For an ideal monatomic gas, the ratio of specific heat capacity at constant pressure to specific heat capacity at constant volume is

γ=53

In an adiabatic process,

TVγ-1= constant

i.e. TfVfγ-1=TiViγ-1⇒Tf=TiViVfγ-1=3001253-1

=300122/3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The pressure versus temperature graph of an ideal gas is shown in the figure below. If the density of gas at point A is ρ0, then the density of the gas at point B will be
questions.solution:
Given, density of gas at point A, ρA=ρ0

General equation for an ideal gas

pV=nRT

⇒pV=mMRT  [m→mass, M→ molecular mass]

⇒p=mV·RM·T

⇒p=ρRMT   ...i    [where density ρ=mV]

Applying Equation. (i) at point A and B, respectively.

p=ρARMT0   ...ii

2p=ρBRM3T0   ...iii

From Equations. (ii) and (iii), we get

12=ρAρB·13

⇒ρB=23ρA=23ρ0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure shows the graph of pVT versus p for 2×10-4  kg of hydrogen gas at two different temperatures, where p, V and T represents pressure, volume and temperature respectively.Then, the value of pVT, where the curve meet on the vertical axis, is
questions.solution:
Number of moles in 2×10-4 kg

μ= Mass of hydrogen in gram  Molecular mass 

=2×10-4×1032

=0.1 mole

∴  pV=μRT

pVT=μR=0.1×R=R10

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A reservoir is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>827</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and Carnot's engine takes a thousand <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kilo</mi><mi>calorie</mi></math> of heat from it and exhausts it to a sink at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. What is the amount of work and the efficiency of the engine?
questions.solution:
Given, Q1=106 cal

T1=827 °C=(827+273)=1100 K,

T2=27 °C=(27+273)=300 K.

As,   Q1T1=Q2T2

∴  Q2=T2T1Q1=3001100×106=2.72×105 cal
work done, W=Q1-Q2=106-2.72×105=7.27×105 cal

The efficiency of the engine,
η=1-T2T1×100

⇒η=1-3001100×100

=72.72 %

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Helium gas goes through a cycle ABCDA (consisting of two isochoric and two isobaric lines) as shown in figure. The efficiency of this cycle is approximately
questions.solution:
Helium is mono atomic gas, for which
CV=32R.Cp=52R

Work done by the gas in one complete cycle W= area ABCDA=p0V0

From A to B, Heat given to gas =nCVΔT

=1×32R×ΔT=32V0Δp=32V0p0

From B to C, heat given to gas =nCpΔT

=1×52R×ΔT=522p0ΔV=5p0V0

Efficiency of cycle = Work done by the gas/cycle  Total heat given to gas/cycle 

=p0V032p0V0+5p0V0=213

Efficiency %=213×100=15.4 %

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Figure below shows two paths that may be taken by a gas to go from a state $A$ to a state $(c)$
In process $\mathrm{AB}, 400 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat is added to the system and in process $\mathrm{BC}, 100 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat is added to the system. The heat absorbed by the system in the process AC will be
questions.solution:
In cyclic process ABCA $\mathrm{Q}_{\text {cycle }}=\mathrm{W}_{\text {cycl }}$

$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}+\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{CA}}=$ ar. of $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}$

$+400+100+\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)\left(4 \times 10^{4}\right)$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}}=-460 \mathrm{~J}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{A} \rightarrow C}=+460 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel of volume 20L contains a mixture of hydrogen and helium at temperature of $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure 2 atm. The mass of mixture is $5 \mathrm{~g}$. Assuming the gases to be ideal, the ratio of mass of hydrogen to that of helium in the given mixture will be
questions.solution:
Let there are $\mathrm{n}_{1}$ moles of hydrogen and $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ moles of helium in the given mixture. As $\mathrm{Pv}$ $=\mathrm{nRT}$

Then the pressure of the mixture

$$

\begin{array}{r}

P=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}}+\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}}=\left(\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{V}} \\

2 \times 101.3 \times 10^{3}=\left(\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}\right) \times \frac{(8.3 \times 300)}{20 \times 10^{-3}}

\end{array}

$$

or,

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\left(\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}\right)=\frac{2 \times 101.3 \times 10^{3} \times 20 \times 10^{-3}}{(8.3)(300)} \\

\text { or, } \mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}=1.62

\end{array}

$$

The mass of the mixture is (in grams)

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{n}_{1} \times 2+\mathrm{n}_{2} \times 4=5 \\

\Rightarrow \quad\left(\mathrm{n}_{1}+2 \mathrm{n}_{2}\right)=2.5 \quad \ldots

\end{array}

$$

Solving the eqns. (1) and (2), we get $\mathrm{n}_{1}=0.74$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}=0.88$

Hence, $\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{H}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{He}}}=\frac{0.74 \times 2}{0.88 \times 4}=\frac{1.48}{3.52}=\frac{2}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ gas having a volume equal to 22.4 Litres at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atmospheric pressure in compressed isothermally so that its volume reduces to 11.2 litres. The work done in this process is-
questions.solution:
Work done in adiabatic process, \(\mathrm{W}=\mu \mathrm{RT} \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}\right)\)

\(\mathrm{W}=1 \times 8.314 \times 273 \times \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{11.2}{22.4}\right)\)

\(\mathrm{W}=8.314 \times 273 \times \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\)

\(\mathrm{W}=8.314 \times 273 \times\left[\log _{\mathrm{e}}(1)-\log _{\mathrm{e}}(2)\right]\)

\(\mathrm{W}=8.314 \times 273 \times(0-0.69)\)

\(\mathrm{W}=-1566.1 \mathrm{~J}\)

questions.q: In a thermodynamic process, the pressure of a fixed mass of a gas is changed in such a manner that the gas releases $20 \mathrm{~J}$ of heat and $8 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done on the gas. If the initial internal energy of the gas was $30 \mathrm{~J}$, then the final internal energy will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two balloons are filled one with pure he gas and other with air respectively. If the pressure and temperature of these balloons are same, then the number of molecules per unit volume is
options: more in He gas filled balloon
questions.solution: Assuming the balloons have the same volume, as $\mathrm{pV}=\mathrm{nRT}$. If $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{V}$ and $\mathrm{T}$ are the same, $\mathrm{n}$ the number of moles present will be the same, whether it is He or air. Hence, number of molecules per unit volume will be same in both the balloons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thermodynamical system is changed from state $\left(\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{~V}_{1}\right)$ to $\left(\mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}\right)$ by two different process, the quantity which will remain same will be
questions.solution:
For all process $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\Delta \mathrm{Q}-\Delta \mathrm{W}$

does not change as it depends on initial final states.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A Carnot's heat engine works between the temperatures $427^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What amount of heat should it consume per second to deliver mechanical work at the rate of $1.0 \mathrm{~kW} ?$
questions.solution:
The efficiency of the heat engine is

$$

\eta=1-\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=1-\left(\frac{273+27 K}{273+427 K}\right)=\frac{4}{7}

$$

But $\quad \eta=\frac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{Q}_{1}}$

$$

\therefore \mathrm{Q}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{W}}{\eta}=\frac{1.0 \mathrm{~kW}}{4 / 7}=1.75 \mathrm{~kW}=0.417 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{s}

$$

Thus, the engine would require 417 cal of heat per second, to deliver the requisite amount of work.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel containing 1 more of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ gas (molar mass  32) at temperature $\mathrm{T}$. The pressure of the gas is p. An identical vessel containing one mole of he gas (molar mass 4 ) at temperature $2 \mathrm{~T}$ has a pressure of
questions.solution:
Applying gas equation, $\mathrm{pV}=\mathrm{nRT}$ We can write, $\mathrm{p}_{1} \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{RT}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{p}_{2} \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{RT}_{2}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{p}_{2}}{\mathrm{p}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}} \times \frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}=\frac{1}{1} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{~T}}=2$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{2}=2 \mathrm{p}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two gases occupy two containers $A$ and $B$ the gas in $\mathrm{A},$ of volume $0.10 \mathrm{~m}^{3},$ exerts a pressure of $1.40 \mathrm{MPa}$ and that in $\mathrm{B}$ of volume $0.15 \mathrm{~m}^{3}$ exerts a pressure $0.7 \mathrm{MPa}$. The two containers are united by a tube of negligible volume and the gases are allowed to intermingle. Then if the temperature remains constant, the final pressure in the container will be (in MPa)
questions.solution:
We know that $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{RT}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{RT}$

$\begin{array}{ll} & \text { and } P_{f}\left(V_{A}+V_{B}\right)=\left(n_{A}+n_{B}\right) R T \\ & P_{f}\left(V_{A}+V_{B}\right)=P_{A} V_{A}+P_{B} V_{B} \\ \therefore & P_{f}=\left(\frac{P_{A} V_{A}+P_{B} V_{B}}{V_{A}+V_{B}}\right) \\ & =\frac{1.4 \times 0.1+0.7 \times 0.15}{0.1+0.15} \mathrm{MPa}=0.98 \mathrm{MPa}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A solid body of constant heat capacity $1 \mathrm{~J} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is being heated by keeping it in contact with reservoirs in two ways:
(i) Sequentially keeping in contact with 2 reservoirs such that each reservoir supplies same amount of heat.
(ii) Sequentially keeping in contact with 8 reservoirs such that each reservoir supplies same amount of heat. 
In both the cases body is brought from initial temperature $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to final temperature $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Entropy change of the body in the two cases respectively is:
questions.solution: The entropy change of the body in the two cases is same as entropy is a state function.

questions.solution:

$$
\mathrm{K}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}} ; \quad 5=\frac{260}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}-260}
$$
or $\mathrm{T}_{1}-260=52 ; \quad \mathrm{T}_{1}=312 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.solution: For an ideal gas $\mathrm{PV}=$ constant i.e., $\mathrm{PV}$ does not vary with $\mathrm{V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula of latent heat is:
questions.solution:
Heat, $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{mL} \quad$ where, $\mathrm{L}=$ latent heat
$\therefore \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensions of coefficient of self inductance are
questions.solution:
Energy stored in an inductor, $\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{LI}^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{L}=\frac{2 \mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{I}^2}$
$[\mathrm{L}]=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{A}]^2}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a temperature scale ' $X$ '. The boiling point of water is $65^{\circ} \mathrm{X}$ and the freezing point is $-15^{\circ} \mathrm{X}$. Assume that the $\mathrm{X}$ scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to $-95^{\circ} \mathrm{X}$ on the Farenheit scale would be:
questions.solution:
Boiling point of water, $=$ UFP $65^{\circ}$
Freezing point of water, $=\mathrm{LFP}-15^{\circ}$
$\frac{\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{LFP}}{\mathrm{UFP}-\mathrm{LFP}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{F}}-32}{212-32}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-95-(-15)}{65-(-15)}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{F}}-32}{180}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{-80}{80}=\frac{T_F-32}{180} \Rightarrow T_F=-180+32$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{F}}=-148^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The frequency $(v)$ of an oscillating liquid drop may depend upon radius $(r)$ of the drop, density $(\rho)$ of liquid and the surface tension $(s)$ of the liquid as :
$v=r^a \rho^b s^c$. The values of $a, b$ and $c$ respectively are
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & v=\mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{a}} \rho^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{s}^{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow[v]=[\mathrm{r}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\rho]^{\mathrm{b}}[\mathrm{s}]^{\mathrm{c}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]=\left[\mathrm{L}^1\right]^{\mathrm{a}}\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^{-3}\right]^{\mathrm{b}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{MLT}}{\mathrm{L}}\right]^{-2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}^{-1}=\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}} \cdot \mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{a}-3 \mathrm{~b}} \cdot \mathrm{T}^{-2 c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=0, \mathrm{a}-3 \mathrm{~b}=0,-2 \mathrm{c}=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=-\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{a}-3 \mathrm{~b}=0 ; \\ & \mathrm{a}+\frac{3}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field in a certain region is given by $\vec{E}=\left(\frac{A}{x^2} \hat{i}+\frac{B}{y^3} \hat{j}\right)$. The SI unit of $A$ and $B$ are:
questions.solution:
Electric field in a certain region is given by,
$\vec{E}=\frac{A}{x^2} \hat{i}+\frac{B}{y^3} \hat{j}$
$\left[\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{x}^2}\right]=\mathrm{NC}^{-1} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{A}]=\mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$
$\left[\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\mathrm{y}^3}\right]=\mathrm{NC}^{-1} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{B}]=\mathrm{Nm}^3 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If force $(F)$, length $(L)$ and time $(T)$ are assumed to be fundamental units, then the dimensional formula of the mass will be
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{KF}^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{c}}$

Substituting the dimensions of

$[\mathrm{F}]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right],[\mathrm{L}]=[\mathrm{L}]$ and $[\mathrm{T}]=[\mathrm{T}]$ and comparing both side, we get $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{FL}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The engine of a truck moving along a straight road delivers constant power. The distance travelled by the truck in time $t$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{ms}^{2}}{\mathrm{t}^{3}}$

since both $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{m}$ are constants

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{s}^{2}}{\mathrm{t}^{3}}=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></msqrt></math> is
questions.solution:
We know that, velocity of light c is terms of ϵ0 and μ0 is given by

c=1μ0ε0⇒c2=1μ0ε0

c2=1μ02μ0ε0⇒c=1μ0μ0ε0⇒μ0ε0=cμ0   ...i

Dimension of the magnetic field, B=τNIA=ML2 T-2AL2

=MA-1T-2

Again, B=μ0nI

∴ μ0=Bnl=MA-1T-2L-1A=MLA-2T-2

μ0=MLA-2T-2    ...ii

and C=LT-1    ...iii

From Equations. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

μ0ϵ0=LT-1MLA-2T-2=ML2T-3A-2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimension of which base quantity corresponds to that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mfrac></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mo>?</mo></math>
questions.solution:
As, gravitational constant,

G=M-1 L3 T-2

Planck's constant, h=ML2 T-1

Hence, Gh=M-1L3T-2ML2T-1

=M0L5 T-3

Velocity of light, [c]=LT-1

Now, Ghc31/2=L5T-31/2L3T-31/2=L21/2=L

Hence [L]= length

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The frequency of vibration of string is given by

$\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2 l}\left[\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}\right]^{1 / 2}$

Here $\mathrm{p}$ is number of segments in the string and $l$ is the length. The dimensional formula for $\mathrm{m}$ will be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{2 \ell}\left[\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}\right]^{1 / 2}$

$v^{2}=\frac{p}{4 \ell^{2}} \frac{F}{m} \Rightarrow m=\frac{p^{2} F}{4 \ell^{2} v^{2}}$

Now, dimensional formula of R.H.S.

$=\frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}}{\mathrm{~L}^{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}\right)^{2}}$

[p will have no dimension as it is an integer] $=\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{0}$

So, dimensions of $\mathrm{m}$ will be $\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{0}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
For the equation $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{A}^{a} \mathrm{v}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{d}^{\mathrm{c}},$ where $\mathrm{F}$ is the force,

A is the area, $v$ is the velocity and d is the density, the values of $a, b$ and $c$ are respectively
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{L}^{2 \mathrm{a}}\right] \times\left[\mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{T}^{-\mathrm{b}}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{L}^{-3 \mathrm{c}}\right]$

$$

=\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{L}^{2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-3 \mathrm{c}} \mathrm{T}^{-\mathrm{b}}\right]

$$

Comparing powers of $\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{L}$ and $\mathrm{T},$ on both sides, we get

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\mathrm{c}=1,2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-3 \mathrm{c}=1,-\mathrm{b}=-2 \text { or } \mathrm{b}=2 \\

\text { Also, } 2 \mathrm{a}+2-3(1)=1 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}=2 \text { or } \mathrm{a}=1

\end{array}

$$

$\therefore$ This is 1,2,1

questions.q: Wave pulse can travel along a tense string like a violin spring. Aseries of experiments showed that the wave velocity $\mathrm{V}$ of a pulse depends on the following quantities, the tension T of the string, the cross-section area A of the string and then as per unit volume $\rho$ of the string. questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the force is given by $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{at}+\mathrm{bt}^{2}$ with $\mathrm{t}$ as time. The dimensions of a and b are
questions.solution:
Dimension of at = Dimension of F 
$[a t]=[F] \Rightarrow[a]=\left[\frac{F}{t}\right]$
$[b]=\left[\frac{M L T^{-2}}{T}\right] \Rightarrow[a]=\left[M L T^{-3}\right]$
Dimension of $\mathrm{bt}^{2}=$ Dimension of $\mathrm{F}$
$\left[\mathrm{bt}^{2}\right]=[\mathrm{F}] \Rightarrow[\mathrm{b}]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{t}^{2}}\right]$
$[\mathrm{b}]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{ML} \mathrm{T}^{-4}}{\mathrm{~T}^{2}}\right] \Rightarrow[\mathrm{b}]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-4}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{3}}{3 \mathrm{Yq}}}$ then find the dimensions of q. Where $\mathrm{T}$ is the time period of bar of mass $\mathrm{M}$ length $\mathrm{L}$ and Young modulus $\mathrm{Y}$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{3}}{3 \mathrm{Yq}}},$ writing dimensions of both
the sides, we get $[\mathrm{T}]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{3}}{\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{q}}\right]^{1 / 2}$
or $\quad q=\left[L^{4}\right]$

questions.q: If $P$ represents radiation pressure, c represents speed of light and $\mathrm{Q}$ represents radiation energy striking a unit area per second, the non-zero integers $x, y$ and $z$ such that $P^{x} Q^{y} c^{z}$ is dimensionless, are.

questions.q:
In the relation:
$\mathrm{P}$ is pressure, $\mathrm{Z}$ is distance, $\mathrm{k}$ is Boltzmann constant and $\theta$ is the temperature. The
dimensional formula of $\beta$ will be
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Young's double slit experiment is performed in a medium of refractive index of 1.33 . The maximum intensity is $I_0$. The intensity at a point on the screen, where path difference between the light coming out from slits is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$, is
questions.solution:
Given, path difference, $\Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{4}$
Phase difference, $\phi=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right) \times$ (Path difference)
$=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}...(i)$
It is given that, maximum intensity is $I_0$. So, intensity at a point on the screen, where phase difference $\phi$ is given by
$\begin{aligned}
I & =I_0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \\
& =I_0 \times \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi / 2}{2}\right) \quad[\because \text { using Eq.(i) }] \\
& =I_0 \times\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 \\
& =\frac{I_0}{2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In YDSE, monochromatic light falls on a screen 1.80 m from two slits separated by 2.08 mm . The first and second order bright fringes are separated by 0.553 mm . The wavelength of light used is
questions.solution:
Position of first bright fringe,
$y_1=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$
Position of second bright fringe,
$\begin{aligned}
y_2 & =\frac{2 \lambda D}{d} \\
y_2-y_1 & =\frac{2 \lambda D}{d}-\frac{\lambda D}{d} \\
y_2-y_1 & =\frac{\lambda D}{d} \\ or
\lambda & =\frac{\left(y_2-y_1\right) d}{D}
\end{aligned}$
Substituting given values, we have
$\begin{aligned}
\lambda & =\frac{0.553 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.08 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8} \\
& =639 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m} \\ or 
\lambda & =639 \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A microwave of wavelength 2.0 cm falls normally on a slit of width 4.0 cm . The angular spread of the central maxima of the diffraction pattern obtained on a screen 1.5 m away from the slit, will be:
questions.solution:
Condition for minima is $\mathrm{d} \sin \theta=\mathrm{n} \lambda$
For Ist minimad $\sin \theta=\lambda$
$\sin \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2} \quad \therefore \theta=30^{\circ}$
Angular spread $=2 \theta=60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The property of light which cannot be explained by Huygen's construction of wavefront is
questions.solution: The property of light which cannot be explained by Huygen's construction of wave front is origin of spectra.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a light ray incidents on the surface of a medium, the reflected ray is completely polarized. Then the angle between reflected and refracted rays is
questions.solution:
According to Brewester's law if the reflected ray is completely polarized, then the reflected and refracted ray is perpendicular to each other.
Thus, $\theta=90^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A single slit of a width $a$ is illuminated by a monochromatic light of wavelength $600 \mathrm{~nm}$. The value of ' $a$ ' for which first minimum appears at $\theta$ $=30^{\circ}$ on the screen will be :
questions.solution:
Wavelength of monochromatic light,
$\lambda=600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$
As fir first minima a $\sin \theta=\lambda$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{a} \sin 30^{\circ}=600 \times 10^{-9} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=1200 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}=1.2 \mu \mathrm{m}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of intensities at two points $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ on the screen in a Young's double slit experiment where phase difference between two wave of same amplitude are $\frac{\pi}{3}$ and $\frac{\pi}{2}$, respectively are
questions.solution:
Intensity at a point in Young's double slit experiment is given by
$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2+2 \sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1 \mathrm{I}_2} \cos \phi$
Here $\mathrm{I}_1=\mathrm{I}_2=\mathrm{I}_0$ (say)
At $P$
$\therefore \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{I}_0+\mathrm{I}_0+2 \mathrm{I}_0 \cos \frac{\pi}{3}=2 \mathrm{I}_0+2 \mathrm{I}_0 \times \frac{1}{2}=3 \mathrm{I}_0$
At $Q$
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\mathrm{I}_0+\mathrm{I}_0+2 \mathrm{I}_0 \cos 90^{\circ}=2 \mathrm{I}_0$
$\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{Q}}}=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Young's double slit experiment, the intensities at two points, for the path difference $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ and $\frac{\lambda}{3}$ ( $\lambda$ being the wavelength of light used) are $I_1$ and $I_2$ respectively. If $I_0$ denotes the intensity produced by each one of the individual slits, then $\frac{\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2}{\mathrm{I}_0}=\ldots \ldots$
questions.solution:
Resultant intensity in Young's double slit experiment
$\mathrm{I}=4 \mathrm{I}_0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\Delta \phi}{2}\right)$
For path difference $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ phase difference,
$\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{I}_1=4 \mathrm{I}_0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=2 \mathrm{I}_0$
For path difference $\frac{\lambda}{3}$
$I_2=4 I_0 \cos ^2\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3}\right)=I_0$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2}{\mathrm{I}_0}=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a young's double slit arrangement frings are produced using light of wavelength $4000 Å$. One slit is covered by a thin plate of glass of refractive index $1.4$ and the other with another glass plate of same thickness but of refractive index 1.7. By doing so the central bright shifts to original sixth fringe from centre. Thickness of glass plate is
questions.solution:
As, shift $\Delta x=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}(\mu-1) t$
Shift due to one plate $\Delta x_1=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}\left(\mu_1-1\right) t$ Shift due to another path $\Delta x_2=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}\left(\mu_2-1\right) t$ Net shift $\Delta x=\Delta x_2-\Delta x_1=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}\left(\mu_2-\mu_1\right) t$ Also, it is given that $\Delta x=6 \beta$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Hence, } 6 \beta=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}\left(\mu_1-\mu_2\right) t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{6 \lambda}{\left(\mu_1-\mu_2\right)}=\frac{6 \times 4000}{(1.7-1.4)}-80000 Å \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t=8 \times 10^{-6}=8 \mu \mathrm{m} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the optical system shown in Fig. The point source of light $S$ is having wavelength equal to $\lambda$. The light is reaching screen only after reflection. For point $P$ to besecond maxima, the value of $\lambda$ would be $(\mathrm{D}>>d$ and $d>>\lambda)$
questions.solution: $\text { At } \mathrm{P}, \Delta x=\frac{(8 d) \times 3 d}{\mathrm{D}}$ For 2 nd maxima, $\Delta x=2 \lambda$ $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \frac{24 d^{2}}{\mathrm{D}}=2 \lambda \\ \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{12 d^{2}}{\mathrm{D}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin sheet of glass $(\mu=1.5)$ of thickness 6 micron introduced in the path of one of the interfering beams in a double slit experiment shifts the central fringe to a position previously occupied by fifth bright fringe. Then the wavelength of light used is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n} \lambda=(\mu-1) \mathrm{t} ;$

$\therefore \lambda=\frac{(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{(1.5-1) \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{5}=6000 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double-slit experiment, the distance between slits and screen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and distance between slits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. A light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> is used to find fringes on the screen. If the screen moves with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then first maxima will move with a speed of:
questions.solution:
Given, D=2 m, d=0.25 mm=2.5×10-4 m

Velocity of screen, dDdt=5 m s-1

Speed of first maxima =dβdt=?

In Young's double-slit experiment, fringe width β=Dλd

On differentiating with respect to t, we get

dβdt=λd·dDdt

=800×10-92.5×10-4×5

=16×10-3 m s-1=16 mm s-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two monochromatic light waves are travelling with same frequency and constant phase difference. If both waves interfere, then
options: there is a gain of energy
there is a loss of energy
the energy is redistributed and the distribution remains constant in time
the energy is redistributed and the distribution changes with time
questions.solution: When two waves with same frequency and constant phase difference interfere to each other, then after interference, intensity of resultant wave is distributed such that it is maximum at some points [constructive interference or bright fringes] and minimum at another points. [destructive interference or dark fringes]. Hence, energy is distributed and remains constant [in the form of bright and dark fringes]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, intensity at a point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> of the maximum intensity. Angular position of this point is
questions.solution:
I=Imaxcos2ϕ2

Imax4=Imaxcos2ϕ2

cosϕ2=12

ϕ2=π3

⇒ ϕ=2π3=2πλ×Δx....i

where, Δx=dsinθ

Putting the value of Δx in Eq. (i), we get

sinθ=λ3d

θ=sin-1λ3d

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, the slits are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> apart and are illuminated by photons of two wavelengths <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>12000</mn><mo>Å</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10000</mn><mo>Å </mo></math>. At what minimum distance from the common central bright fringe on the screen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the slit will a bright fringe from one interference pattern coincide with a bright fringe from the other?
questions.solution:
Given, λ1=12000Å 

and λ2=10000Å 

D=2 cm

d=2 mm=2×10-3 m

We have, 

λ1λ2=n2n1=1200010000=65

as X=n1λ1Dd   n1=5

=5×12000×10-10×22×10-3

=5×1.2×104×10-10×103=6 mm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of intensity at the centre of a bright fringe to the intensity at a point distance one-fourth of the distance between two successive bright fringes will be
questions.solution:
Intensity at centre of bright fringe,

I0=I+I+2II·cos0

I0=2I+2I    ∵ cos0°=1

=4I

Similarly Intensity at point P4

(with phase difference =2π4=π2)

I'=I+I+2IIcosπ2

=2I+2II×0

=2I

∴ I0I'=4I2I=2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path difference is $\lambda$ is $\mathrm{K},(\lambda$ being the wave length of light used). The intensity at a point where the path difference is $\lambda / 4,$ will be :
questions.solution:
For path difference $\lambda,$ phase difference $=2 \pi \mathrm{rad}$

For path difference $\frac{\lambda}{4},$ phase difference

$$

=\frac{\pi}{2} \mathrm{rad}

$$

As $\mathrm{K}=4 \mathrm{I}_{0}$ so intensity at given point where

path difference is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$

$\mathrm{K}^{\prime}=4 \mathrm{I}_{0} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\cos 45^{\circ}\right)$

$=2 \mathrm{I}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Calculate the wavelength of light used in an interference experiment from the following data:

Fringe width $=0.03 \mathrm{~cm}$. Distance between the slits and eyepiece through which the interference pattern is observed is $1 \mathrm{~m}$. Distance between the images of the virtual source when a convex lens of focal length $16 \mathrm{~cm}$ is used at a distance of $80 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the eyepiece is $0.8 \mathrm{~cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a YDSE, the light of wavelength $\lambda=5000 Ã$ is used, which emerges in phase from two slits a distance $\mathrm{d}=3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$ apart. Atransparent sheet of thickness $\mathrm{t}=1.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$ refractive index $\mu=1.17$ is placed over one of the slits. what is the new angular position of the central maxima of the interference pattern, from the centre of the screen? Find the value of $y$.
questions.solution:
The path difference when transparent sheet is introduced $\Delta x=(\mu-1) t$

If the central maxima occupies position of nth fringe, then $(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n} \lambda=\mathrm{d} \sin \theta$

$$

\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{(1.17-1) \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-7}}=0.085

$$

Therefore, angular position of central maxima

$$

\theta=\sin ^{-1}(0.085)=4.88^{\circ} \approx 4.9

$$

For small angles, $\sin \theta=\theta=\tan \theta$

$$

\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta=\frac{y}{D}

$$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{d}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{D}(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{d}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular size of the central maxima due to a single slit diffraction is $\mathrm{a} \rightarrow$ slit width $)$
questions.solution:
Angular size of central maxima is

$$

2 \times\left(\frac{\lambda}{a}\right)=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mica slit of thickness t and refractive index $\mu$ is introduoed in theray from the first source S$_{1}$. By how much distance of fringes pattern will be displaced?
questions.solution:
Hint:
The laws of Electromagnetic Induction give us idea about the various ways in which we can produce Electric Current using Electric or Magnetic field.
Correct Option is D.
Explanation for correct answer:
- Galvanometer is a device which detects current in the circuit.
- Voltmeter is a device which measures potential difference between two points in the circuit.
- Electric motor is a device which converts Electrical Energy into Mechanical Energy.
- Electric Generator is an device which produces Electric Current from Mechanical energy, Chemical energy, or by using Magnetic or Electric Fields. The laws if Electromagnetic Induction are used in Electric Generator which produces Electric Current from Magnetic or Electric Fields.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Young's double slit experiment the angular width of a fringe formed on a distant screen is $1^{\circ}$. The wavelength fo the light used is $6280 \AA$. What is the distance between the two coherent sources?
questions.solution:
The angular fringe width is given by $\alpha=\frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}$
where $\lambda$ is wavelength and $\mathrm{d}$ is the distance between two coherent sources. Thus
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{d}=\frac{\lambda}{\alpha} \\
\text { Given, } \lambda=6280 \AA, \alpha=1^{\circ}=\frac{\pi}{180} \mathrm{radian}
\end{array}$
Thus $\mathrm{d}=\frac{6280 \times 10^{-10}}{3.14} \times 180$
$=3.6 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}=0.036 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the distance between the first maxima and fifth minima of a double slit pattern is $7 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the slits are separated by $0.15 \mathrm{~mm}$ with the screen 50 $\mathrm{cm} .$ from the slits, then the wavelength of the light used is:
questions.solution:
There are three and a half fringes from first maxima to fifth minima as shown.
$\Rightarrow \beta=\frac{7 \mathrm{~mm}}{3.5}=2 \mathrm{~mm} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\beta \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}=600 \mathrm{nm}$

questions.q: When a mica sheet of thickness 7 microns and $\mu=1.6$ is placed in the path of one of interfering beams in the biprism experiment then the central fringe gets at the position of seventh bright fringe. The wavelength of light used will be
questions.solution:
$\lambda=\frac{(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{n}}$
According to question
$$
\mathrm{n}=7, \mu=1.6, \mathrm{t}=7 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~meter}
$$
From eqs. (1) and $(2), \lambda=6 \times 10^{-7}$ meter

questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The speed of sound in oxygen at S.T.P. will be approximately:
(Given, $\left.\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}, \gamma=1.4\right)$
questions.solution:
At STP
Temperature, $T=273 \mathrm{~K}$
Molecular mass of oxygen, $\mathrm{M}=32 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$
Speed of sound is given by
$\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.3 \times 273}{32 \times 10^{-3}}}$
$=314.8541 \simeq 315 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plane progressive wave is given by $y=2 \cos$ $2 \pi(330 t-x) \mathrm{m}$. The frequency of the wave is:
questions.solution:
Equation of wave is, $y=2 \cos 2 \pi(330 t-x)$ m
$y=A \cos (\omega t-k x)$
On comparing $\omega=2 \pi \times 330$
$2 \pi \mathrm{f}=2 \pi \times 330 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=330 \mathrm{~Hz}$
$[\because \omega=2 \pi \mathrm{~F}]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The engine of a train moving with speed $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ towards a platform sounds a whistle at frequency $400 \mathrm{~Hz}$. The frequency heard by a passenger inside the train is (neglect air speed. Speed of sound in air $330 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution: The relative velocity of a passenger with respect to source of sound (engine of train) is 0 . So there will be no Doppler's effect. So frequency heard is $400 \mathrm{~Hz}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of speed of sound in hydrogen gas to the speed of sound in oxygen gas at the same temperature is:
questions.solution:
Given $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2}=2 ; \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_2}=32$
Speed of sound, $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad v \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{H}_2}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{32}{2}}=4: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A submarine $A$ travelling at $17 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is being chased along the line of its velocity by another submarine $B$ travelling at $34 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. B sends a sonar signal of $600 \mathrm{~Hz}$ to detect $A$ and receives a reflected sound of frequency $v$. The of $v$ is
[Speed of sound in water $=1500 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ ]
questions.solution:
$\therefore$ Given, velocity of submarine $A, v_A=17 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Velocity of submarine $B, v_B=34 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Signal sent by submarine $B$ is detected by submarine $A$ can be shown as
Freqency of the signal, $f_0=600 \mathrm{~Hz}$ Speed of sound in water $v_s=1500 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ Frequency received by submarine $A$ is $$ f_1=\left(\frac{v_{\mathrm{S}}-v_A}{v_{\mathrm{S}}-v_B}\right) f_0=\left[\frac{1500-17}{1500-34}\right] \times 600 \mathrm{~Hz} $$ $$ f_1=\frac{1483}{1466} \times 600 $$ Frequency received by submarine $B$ is $$ f_2=\left(\frac{v_{\mathrm{S}}+v_B}{v_s+v_A}\right) f_1 $$ Substituting given values and $f_1$ value from Eq. (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned} f_2 & =\left(\frac{1500+34}{1500+17}\right) \times\left[\frac{1483}{1466} \times 600\right] \\ f_2 & =1.0112 \times 1.0115 \times 600 \\ f_2 & =613.7 \mathrm{~Hz} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Transverse waves of the same frequency are generated in two steel wires $A$ and $B$. The diameter of $A$ is twice that of $B$ and the tension in $A$ is half that in $B$. The ratio of the velocities of the waves in $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
The velocity of transverse waves is given by $v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$ where, $T=$ Tension and $m=$ mass per unit length of the wire. If $r$ is the radius of the wire and $\rho$ its density then,
$m=\pi r^2 \rho$
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{\pi r^2 \rho}}$
$\therefore \quad v_A=\frac{\sqrt{T_A}}{r_A \sqrt{\pi \rho}}$
and $\quad v_B=\frac{\sqrt{T_B}}{r_B \sqrt{\pi \rho}}$
Now, $\quad \frac{v_A}{v_B}=\sqrt{\frac{T_A}{T_B}} \cdot \frac{r_B}{r_A}$
$\because \quad r_A=2 r_B$ and $T_A=\frac{1}{2} T_B$
Hence, $\quad \frac{v_A}{v_B}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A direct current of $6 \mathrm{~A}$ is superimposed on an alternating current $I=10 \sin \omega t$ flowing through a wire. The effective value of the resulting current will be
questions.solution:
Given, $I=6+10 \sin \omega t$
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\mathrm{eft}} & =\left[\frac{\left.\int_0^T I^2 d t\right]^{1 / 2}}{\int_0^T d t}=\left[\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T(6+10 \sin \omega t)^2 d t\right]^{1 / 2}\right. \\
& =\left[\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T\left(36+120 \sin \omega t+100 \sin ^2 \omega t\right) d t\right]^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$
But as, $\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin \omega t d t=0$ and $\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sin ^2 \omega t=\frac{1}{2}$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad I_{\mathrm{eff}}=\left[36+\frac{1}{2} \times 100\right]^{1 / 2}=9.27
$$
Thus, $\quad I_{\text {eff }}=9.27 \mathrm{~A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of whistle of an engine appears to be $(4 / 5)^{\text {th }}$ of initial frequency when it crosses a stationary observer. If the velocity of sound is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then the speed of engine will be
questions.solution:
$n^{\prime}=\frac{n v}{v-v_{s}} \ldots \ldots \ldots(1)$

$n^{\prime \prime}=\frac{n v}{v+v_{s}} \ldots \ldots . .(2)$

From (1) and (2), $\frac{n^{\prime}}{n^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{v+v_{s}}{v-v_{s}} \ldots \ldots . .(3)$

According to question, $\frac{n^{\prime}}{n^{\prime}}=\frac{5}{4}$

$v_{s}=? v=330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \ldots \ldots \ldots .(4)$

From eq. (3) and (4)

$\frac{5}{4}=\left[\frac{330+v_{s}}{330-v_{s}}\right] \Rightarrow 9 v_{s}=330$

$\therefore v_{s}=36.6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.q: The displacement y of a particle in a medium can be expressed as, $y=10^{-6} \sin \left(100 t+20 x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) m$ where $t$ is in second and $x$ in meter. The speed of the wave is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An organ pipe, open from both end produces 5 beats per second when vibrated with a source of frequency $200 \mathrm{~Hz}$. The second harmonic of the same pipes produces 10 beats per second with a source of frequency $420 \mathrm{~Hz}$. The fundamental frequency of organ pipe is
questions.solution:
Let the fundamental frequency of organ pipe be $\mathrm{f}$ Case I : $f=200 \pm 5=205 \mathrm{~Hz}$ or $195 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Case II : frequency of 2 nd harmonic of organ pipe $=2 \mathrm{f}$ (as is clear from the second figure) $2 \mathrm{f}=420 \pm 10$ or $\mathrm{f}=210 \pm 5$ or $\mathrm{f}=205$ or 215 Hence fundamental frequency oforgan pipe $=205 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A whistle of frequency $1000 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is sounded on a car travelling towards a cliff with velocity of 18 $\mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ normal to the cliff. If $\mathrm{c}=330 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, then the apparent frequency of the echo as heard by the car driver is nearly
questions.solution: By the concept of accoustic, the observer and source are moving towards each other, each with a velocity of $18 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ $\therefore \mathrm{v}^{\prime}=\frac{330+18}{330-18} \times 1000 \approx 1115 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two sources of sound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are moving towards and away from a stationary observer with the same speed respectively. If the frequency of the sound produced by both the sources is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, then the speed of sources (approximately) if the observer detects 4 beats per second, is [Given, speed of sound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
questions.solution:
Given, frequency of sound produced by sources A and B, fA=fB=400 Hz

Speed of sound in air, v=340 m s-1

Apparent frequency heard by the observer when source A is moving towards him,

fA'=vv-vS×fA=340340-vS×400

Apparent frequency heard by the observer when source B is moving away from him,

fB'=vv+vs×fB=340340+vs×400

Given, Beats =fA'-fB'=4

340340-vs×400-340340+vs×400=4

⇒34000×2vS3402-vS2=1

34000×2vS3402=1   ∵ vS2<<3402

⇒vs=1.7 m s-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If sound travels in air with the speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the number of tones present in an open organ pipe of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> for a maximum frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, are
questions.solution:
Given, speed of sound, v=340 m s-1
Length of open organ pipe, l=2 m

Frequency, f=1200 Hz
For open organ pipe,

Fundamental frequency, f0=v2l=3402×2=85

∴ Number of tones present in the open organ pipe
=ff0=120085=14.11≃14

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train moves towards a stationary observer with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The train blows whistle and its frequency is registered by the observer as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ν</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If the train's speed is reduced to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>17</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the frequency registered is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ν</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the speed of sound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>ν</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>ν</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
questions.solution:
According to Doppler's effect, the approximate frequency heard by the stationary observer,

ν=CC-vsν0

Case (I) vs=34 m s-1, where C=Speed of sound in air,

vs=Speed of source, and ν0= frequency of the source.

∴  ν1=340340-34ν0

=340306ν0          ....i

Case (ii) vs=17 m s-1
∴   ν2=340340-17ν0=340323ν0     ....ii

From Eq. (i) and (ii), we get

∴   ν1ν2=340/306340/323=323306=1918

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A whistle of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mi>Hz</mi></math> tied to the end of a string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> revolves at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rev</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>min</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A listener standing some distance away in the plane of rotation of whistle hears frequencies in the range. (Speed of sound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
vs=rω=1.2×2πf   ∵ω=2πf

=1.2×2×3.14×40060=50 m s-1

vmin=vv+vs·v

=340340+50×500

=436 Hz

vmax=vv-vs·v

=340340-50×500

=586 Hz

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A transverse wave propagating on a stretched string of linear density 3×10-4 kgm-1 is represented by the equation

y=0.2sin(1.5x+60t)

where, x in metres and t is in seconds. The tension in the string (in Newton) is
questions.solution:
Equation of wave, 

y=0.2sin[1.5x+60t]

Comparing with standard equation, we get 

y=Asin[kx+ωt]

k=1.5=2πλ

and ω=60=2πT

∴ Velocity of wave, v=ωk

=601.5=40 m s-1

Velocity of wave in stretched string, v=Tm

where, m is the linear density, T is tension in the string.

So, T=v2m=(40)2×3×10-4

=0.48 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A string of length $\ell$ is fixed at both ends. It is vibrating in its $3^{\text {rd }}$ overtone with maximum amplitude'a'. The amplitude at a distance $\ell / 3$ from one end is
questions.solution:
For a string vibrating in its $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ overtone $(\mathrm{n}+1)^{\text {th }}$ harmonic) $\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{~A} \sin \left(\frac{(\mathrm{n}+1) \pi \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{L}}\right) \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$
$$ \begin{array}{l} \text { For } \left.x=\frac{\ell}{3}\right] 2 A=a \& n=3 \\ y=\left[a \sin \left(\frac{4 \pi}{\ell} \cdot \frac{\ell}{3}\right)\right] \cos \omega t \\ =a \sin \frac{4 \pi}{3} \cos \omega t=-a\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \cos \omega t \end{array} $$ i.e. at $x=\frac{\ell}{3},$ the amplitude is $\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A source of sound S emitting waves of frequency $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and an observor $\mathrm{O}$ are located at some distance from each other. The source is moving with a speed of $19.4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the source observer line as shown in the figure. The observor is at rest. The apparent frequency observed by the observer is (velocity of sound in air $330 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution:
Here, original frequency of sound, $\mathrm{f}_{0}=100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ Speed of source $V_{s}=19.4 \cos 60^{\circ}=9.7$
From Doppler's formula $\mathrm{f}^{\mathrm{l}}=\mathrm{f}_{0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{V}_{0}}{\mathrm{~V}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}}\right)$ $\mathrm{f}^{\mathrm{l}}=100\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}-0}{\mathrm{~V}-(+9.7)}\right)$ $\mathrm{f}^{\mathrm{l}}=100 \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}\left(1-\frac{9.7}{\mathrm{~V}}\right)}$ $\mathrm{f}^{1}=100\left(1+\frac{9.7}{330}\right)=103 \mathrm{~Hz}$ Apparent frequency $\mathrm{f}^{1}=103 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two tuning forks with natural frequencies 340 Hz each move relative to a stationary observer. One fork moves away from the observer, while the other moves towards the observer at the same speed. The observer hears beats offrequency $3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. Find the speed of the tuning forks.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of a sonometer wire is $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$. When the weights producing the tensions are completely immersed in water, the frequency becomes $80 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and on immersing the weights in a certain liquid, the frequency becomes $60 \mathrm{~Hz}$. The specific gravity of the liquid is
questions.solution:
As we know, frequency $f \propto \sqrt{m g}$ or $f \propto \sqrt{g}$

In water, $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{w}}=0.8 \mathrm{f}_{\text {air }}$

$$

\begin{array}{l}

\frac{\mathrm{g}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{g}}(0.8)^{2}=0.64 \\

\Rightarrow 1-\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{m}}}=0.64 \\

\Rightarrow \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{m}}}=0.36

\end{array}

$$

In liquid, $\frac{g^{\prime}}{g}=(0.6)^{2}=0.36$

$$

1-\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{\mathrm{m}}}=0.36 \frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{\mathrm{m}}}=0.64

$$

From eq. (1) and (2) $\frac{\rho_{l}}{\rho_{n}}=\frac{0.64}{0.36} \therefore \rho_{l}=1.77$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sound source, emitting sound of constant frequency, moves with a constant speed and crosses a stationary observer. The frequency (n) of sound heard by the observer is plotted against time (t). Which of the following graphs represents the correct variation?
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
When a string is divided into three segments of length $l_{1}, l_{2},$ and $l_{3}$, the fundamental frequencies of these three segments are $v_{1}, v_{2}$ and $v_{3}$ respectively. The original fundamental frequency

(v) of the string is
questions.solution:
Fundamental frequency is given by

$$

v=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow v \propto \frac{1}{l} \Rightarrow P \propto \frac{1}{v}

$$

Since, $P$ divided into $l_{1}, l_{2}$ and $l_{3}$ segments Here $l=l_{1}+l_{2}+l_{3}$

So $\quad \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}+\frac{1}{v_{2}}+\frac{1}{v_{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two waves of wavelengths $99 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ both travelling with velocity $396 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ are made to interfere. The number of beats produced by them per second is
questions.solution:
Velocity of wave $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{n} \lambda$ where $n=$ frequency of wave $\Rightarrow n=\frac{v}{\lambda}$

$\mathrm{n}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{396}{100 \times 10^{-2}}=396 \mathrm{~Hz}$

no. of beats $=\mathrm{n}_{1}-\mathrm{n}_{2}=4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If equation of transverse wave is $y=x_{0} \cos$ $2 \pi\left(\mathrm{nt}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\lambda}\right) .$ Maximum velocity of particle is twice of wave velocity, if $\lambda$ is-
questions.solution:
$y=x_{0} \cos 2 \pi\left(\right.$ nt $\left.-\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)$

$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}_{0} \cos \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(\mathrm{vt}-\mathrm{x}) \quad[\because \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{n} \lambda]$

$\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\max }=\mathrm{x}_{0} \times \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \mathrm{v}=2 \mathrm{v}($ given $) \therefore \lambda=\pi \mathrm{x}_{0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The transverse displacement $y(x, t)$ of a wave on a string is given by 
$y(x, t)=e^{-\left(a x^{2}+b t^{2}+2 \sqrt{a b}\right.}$ xt $)$
This represents a
questions.solution:
$y(x, t)=e^{-\left(a x^{2}+b t^{2}+2 \sqrt{a b} x t\right)}=e^{-(\sqrt{a x}+\sqrt{b t})^{2}}$
It is a function of type $y=f(\omega t+k x)$ $\therefore y(x, t)$ represents wave travelling along $-x$ direction.
$\text { Speed of wave }=\frac{\omega}{k}=\frac{\sqrt{b}}{\sqrt{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{b}{a}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sound souorce is moving towards stationary listener with $\frac{1}{10}$ th of the speed of sound. The ratio of apparent to read frequency is
questions.solution:
$v_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{10} \quad \mathrm{n}^{\prime}=\mathrm{n} \frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{n}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\left(\mathrm{v}-\frac{\mathrm{v}}{10}\right)}=\frac{10}{9}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe is $300 \mathrm{~Hz}$. The first overtone of this pipe has same frequency as first overtone of a closed organ pipe. If speed of sound is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then the length of closed organ pipe is
questions.solution:
For open pipe, $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \ell},$ where $\mathrm{n}_{0}$ is the
fundamental frequency of open pipe. $\therefore \ell=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{n}}=\frac{330}{2 \times 300}=\frac{11}{20}$
As freq. of 1 st overtone of open pipe $=$ freq. of 1 st overtone of closed pipe
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad 2 \frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \ell}=3 \frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \ell^{\prime}} \Rightarrow \ell^{\prime} &=\frac{3 \ell}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{11}{20} \\ &=41.25 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wave $y=a \sin (\omega t-k x)$ on a string meets with another wave producing a node at $\mathrm{x}=0 .$ Then the equation of the unknown wave is
questions.solution:
Equation of a wave $y_{1}=a \sin (\omega t-k x)$....(i)
Let equations of another wave may be, $y_{2}=a \sin (\omega t+k x)$....(ii)
$y_{3}=-a \sin (\omega t+k x) \quad \ldots .$ (iii)
If Eq. (i) propagate with Eq. (ii), we get $\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{a} \cos \mathrm{kx} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$
If Eq. (i), propagate with Eq. (iii), we get $y=-2 a \sin k x \cos \omega t$
At $x=0, y=0,$ wave produce node 
So, Eq.(iii) is the equation of unknown wave

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A source of sound produces waves of wavelength $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ when it is stationary. If the speed of sound in air is $320 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and source moves with speed $20 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1},$ the wavelength of sound in the forward direction will be nearest to
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two tuning forks $A$ and $B$ sounded together give 6 beats per second. With an air resonance tube closed at one end, the two forks give resonance when the two air columns are $24 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 25 $\mathrm{cm}$ respectively. Calculate the frequencies of forks.
questions.solution:
Let the frequency of the first fork be $\mathrm{f}_{1}$ and that of second be $\mathrm{f}_{2}$
We then have, $\mathrm{f}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \times 24}$ and $\mathrm{f}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \times 25}$
We also see that $\mathrm{f}_{1}>\mathrm{f}_{2}$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{f}_{1}-\mathrm{f}_{2}=6
$$
and $\frac{\mathrm{f}_{1}}{\mathrm{f}_{2}}=\frac{24}{25}$
Solving (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{f}_{1}=150 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $\quad \mathrm{f}_{2}=144 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force of $F - 0.5 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on lower block as shown in figure. The work done by lower block on upper block for a displacement of 3 m of the upper block with respect to ground is (Take, $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Maximum acceleration of 1 kg block may be
$a_{\max }=\mu g=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Common acceleration without relative motion between two blocks may be,
$a=\frac{0.5}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Since, $a \lt a_{\max }$
There will be no relative motion and blocks will move with acceleration $\frac{0.5}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.
Force of friction by lower block on upper block,
$\begin{aligned}
f & =m a=(1)\left(\frac{0.5}{3}\right)=\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{~N}(\text { towards right }) \\
\therefore \quad W & =f \times s \\
& =\frac{1}{6} \times 3=0.5 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum of mass 1 kg and length $l=1 \mathrm{~m}$ is released from rest at angle $\theta=60^{\circ}$. The power delivered by all the forces acting on the bob at angle $\theta=30^{\circ}$ will be $\left(\right.$ Take, $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution: Power of tension $=0$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { Power of } m g=(m g)(v) \cos 60^{\circ} \\ & \text { Here, } \quad v=\sqrt{2 g h} \\ & \text { and } \quad h=l\left(\cos 30^{\circ}-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)=0.36 \mathrm{~m} \\ & v=\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.36} \\ & =2.68 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \text { Power }=(m g)(v) \cos 60^{\circ} \\ & =(1 \times 10)(2.68) \times \frac{1}{2} \\ & =1.34 \times 10=13.4 \mathrm{~W} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal massless spring $S$ can be compressed 1 m by a force of 100 N in equilibrium. The same spring is placed at the bottom of a frictionless inclined plane inclined at $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. A 10 kg block $M$ is released from rest at the top of the incline and is brought to rest momentarily after compressing the spring by 2 m . If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, what is the speed of mass just before it touches the spring?
questions.solution:
$F=k x$
$\therefore \quad k=\frac{F}{x}=\frac{100}{1} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}=100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
Now, from energy conservation, between natural length of spring and its maximum compression state.
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2+m g h=\frac{1}{2} k x_{\max }^2$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad v & =\sqrt{\frac{k x_{\max }^2}{m}-2 g h} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{(100)(2)^2}{10}-(2)(10)\left(\frac{2}{2}\right)}=\sqrt{20} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical ball of mass 20 kg is stationary at the top of a hill of height 100 m . It rolls down a smooth surface to the ground, then climbs up another hill of height 30 m and finally rolls down to a horizontal base at a height of 20 m above the ground. The velocity attained by the ball is
questions.solution: Using conservation of energy, $\mathrm{m}(10 \times 100)=\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{v}^2+10 \times 20\right)$ or $\frac{1}{2} v^2=800$ or $v=\sqrt{1600}=40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass 2 kg is on a smooth horizontal table and moves in a circular path of radius 0.6 m . The height of the table from the ground is 0.8 m . If the angular speed of the particle is $12 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, the magnitude of its angular momentum about a point on the ground right under the centre of the circle is
questions.solution: Angular momentum $L_0=\mathrm{mvr} \sin 90^{\circ}$ $=2 \times 0.6 \times 12 \times 1 \times 1$ $\left[\right.$ As $V=r \omega$, $\left.\operatorname{Sin} 90^{\circ}=1\right]$ So, $L_0=14.4 \mathrm{kgm}^2 / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A machine gun fires 300 bullets per $\mathrm{min}$ if the mass of each bullet is $10 \mathrm{~g}$ and the velocity of the bullets is $600 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the power (in $\mathrm{kW}$ ) of the gun is
questions.solution:
Work done by the gun $=$ total kinetic energy of the bullets
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =n \frac{1}{2} m v^2=300 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times(600)^2 \\
& =150 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 600
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Power of gun } & =\frac{\text { work done }}{\text { Time taken }} \\
& =\frac{150 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 600}{60 \mathrm{~s}} \\
& =9 \mathrm{~kW} \quad(\because 1 \text { minute }=60 \text { seconds })
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A boy pushes a toy box $2.0 \mathrm{~m}$ along the floor by means of a force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ directed downward at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The work done by the boy is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{F} \mathrm{s} \cos \theta=10 \times 2 \cos 60^{\circ}=10 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is moving unidirectionally under the influence of a source of constant power. The square of its displacement in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is proportional to
questions.solution:
By work-energy theorem,

W=P×t=12mv2

⇒v2=2Ptm

v=2Ptm1/2

or dsdt=2Ptm1/2

∫ds=∫2Ptm1/2dt

s=2Pm1/2·23t3/2

i.e. s∝t3/2

s2∝t3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An elastic string of unstretched length $\mathrm{L}$ and force constant $\mathrm{k}$ is stretched by a small length $\mathrm{x}$. It is further stretched by another small length y. The work done in the second stretching is
questions.solution:
In the string elastic force is conservative in nature. $\therefore \mathrm{W}=-\Delta \mathrm{U}$

Work done by elastic force of string,

$$

\begin{aligned}

\mathrm{W} &=-\left(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{F}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{F}} \\

\mathrm{W} &=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{x}^{2}-\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{2} \\

&=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{x}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+2 \mathrm{xy}\right) \\

&=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{x}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{y}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k}(2 \mathrm{xy}) \\

&=-\mathrm{k} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{y}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \mathrm{y}^{2}

\end{aligned}

$$

Therefore, the work done against elastic force

$\mathrm{W}_{\text {external }}=-\mathrm{W}=\frac{\mathrm{K} y}{2}(2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small block of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is kept on a rough inclined surface of inclination $\theta$ fixed in a elevator. The elevator goes up with a uniform velocity $\mathrm{v}$ and the block does not slide on the wedge. The work done by the force of friction on the block in time t will be:
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{f} \sin \theta \mathrm{S}\)

\(=(\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta)(\sin \theta)(\mathrm{vt})\)

\(=\mathrm{mg} \sin ^{2} \theta \mathrm{vt}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $3.628 \mathrm{~kg}$ freight car moving along a horizontal rail road spur track at $7.2 \mathrm{~km} /$ hour strikes a bumper whose coil springs experiences a maximum compression of $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ in stopping the car. The elastic potential energy of the springs at the instant when they are compressed $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned} 7.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{ram} &=\frac{7.2 \times \frac{5}{18} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}}{7 / 2^{2}} \\ &=\frac{72 \times 5}{10 \times 18}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}\)

firt case EC \(\frac{1}{2} k x^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\) \(\Rightarrow \frac{1}{7} k\left(30 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3.628 \times(2)^{2}\) \(k=\frac{4 \times 3.628}{9 \times 10^{-2}}\)

\(\begin{aligned} \text { EC } & \frac{1}{2} k x^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2} k\left(30 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 3.628 \times(2)^{2} \\ k &=\frac{4 \times 3.628}{9 \times 10^{-2}} \end{aligned}\)

energy potential enrgy when conpressed at \(15 \mathrm{~cm}\) 

\(\begin{aligned}\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2} k\left(15 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2} \\=& \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4 \times 3.628}{9 \times 10^{-2}} \times 225 \times 10^{-4} \\=& \frac{1639 \times 10^{-2}}{9} \\=& 182.206 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}\)

\(\begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} k\left(15 \times 10^{2}\right. \\ &=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\alpha \times 3.628}{9 \times 10^{-2}} \times 225 \times 10^{-4} \\ &=\frac{1639 \times 10^{-2}}{9} \\ &=182.206 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light inextensible string that goes over a smooth fixed pulley as shown in the figure connects two blocks of masses $0.36 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $0.72 \mathrm{~kg}$. Taking $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2},$ find the work done $(\mathrm{in}$ joules) by the string on the block of mass $0.36 \mathrm{~kg}$ during the first second after the system is released from rest.
questions.solution:
Given $m=0.36 \mathrm{~kg}, M=0.72 \mathrm{~kg}$. The figure shows the forces on $m$ and $M$. When the system is released, let the acceleration be $a$. Then $T-m g=m a$ $M g-T=M a$ $$ a=\frac{(M-m) g}{M+m}=g / 3 $$ and $T=4 \mathrm{mg} / 3$ For block m: $$ u=0, a=g / 3, t=1, s=? $$
$$ s=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^{2}=0+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{g}{3} \times 1^{2}=g / 6 $$ Work done by the string on $m$ is $$ \bar{T} \bar{s}=T s=4 \frac{m g}{3} \times \frac{g}{6}=\frac{4 \times 0.36 \times 10 \times 10}{3 \times 6}=8 \mathrm{~J} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the unit of force and length be each increased by four times, then the unit of energy is increased by
questions.solution: Since unit of energy $=$ (unit of force). (unit of length) so if we increase unit of length and force, each by four times, then unit of energy will increase by sixteen times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following must be known in order to determine the power output of an automobile?
options: Mass and amount of work performed
Force exerted and distance of motion
Work performed and elapsed time of work
questions.solution: Power is defined as the rate of doing work. For the automobile, the power output is the amount of work done (overcoming friction) divided by the length of time in which the work was done.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A massless platform is kept on a light elastic spring as shown in fig. When a sand particle of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is dropped on the pan from a height of $0.24 \mathrm{~m},$ the particle strikes the pan and spring is compressed by $0.01 \mathrm{~m}$. From what height should the particle be dropped to cause a compression of $0.04 \mathrm{~m}$.
questions.solution: No Solution available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy for a force field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ is given by $U(x, y)=\cos (x+y)$. The force acting on a particle at position given by coordinates $(0, \pi / 4)$ is $-$
questions.solution:
$\quad \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}=\sin (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}=\sin (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}) \\
\left.\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{x}}=\sin (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})\right]_{(0, \pi / 4)}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\left.\mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{y}}=\sin (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})\right]_{(0, \pi / 4)}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\therefore \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}]
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long string is stretched by $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the potential energy is $\mathrm{V}$. If the spring is stretched by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$, its potential energy will be
questions.solution:
$\quad V=\frac{1}{2} k(x)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} k(2)^{2}$ or $k=\frac{2 V}{4}=\frac{V}{2}$
$$
\mathrm{V}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k}(10)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right)(10)^{2}=25 \mathrm{~V}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A chain of mass $\mathrm{M}$ is placed on a smooth table with $1 / \mathrm{n}$ of its length $\mathrm{L}$ hanging over the edge. The work done in pulling the hanging portion of the chain back to the surface of the table is
questions.solution:
$\quad \mathrm{W}=$ change in $\mathrm{PE}$ of $\mathrm{COM}$ of hanging part
$$
=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{g} \frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{MgL}}{2 \mathrm{n}^{2}}
$$
